handbooks.title: [De Gruyter Textbook] Darvas F., Dorman G., Hessel V., Ley S.V. (ed.) - Flow Chemistry_ Volume 1 Flow Chemistry – Fundamentals (2021, Walter de Gruyter) [10.1515_9783110693676] - libgen.li
Thomas Wirth - Microreactors in Organic Synthesis and Catalysis (2008, Wiley-VCH) - libgen.li
[Green chemistry series No. 62] García-Verdugo, Eduardo_ Luis, Santiago V - Flow chemistry_ integrated approaches for practical applications (2020, Royal Society of Chemistry)

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use the formula: tres = V/v = S*L/v, where V is reactor volume, v is flow rate, S is cross-section, and L is length
rules.reasoning: Residence time in flow reactors is determined by reactor volume and bulk flow rate, unlike batch reactors where time is determined by how long the vessel is held at temperature

rules.condition: WHEN needing to increase residence time in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Either decrease the flow rate or increase the length of the reactor
rules.reasoning: Residence time is inversely proportional to flow rate and directly proportional to reactor length

rules.condition: WHEN defining stoichiometry in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use the concentration of reagents and the ratio of their flow rates instead of molar quantities
rules.reasoning: Flow rate is the volume of fluid that passes through a given channel per unit time, making flow rate ratios more relevant than absolute quantities
rules.exceptions: In batch processes, use concentration and ratio of molar quantities

rules.condition: WHEN measuring reaction progress in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Measure distance traveled in the channel rather than time spent in flask
rules.reasoning: In flow reactors, spatial position corresponds to reaction time, unlike batch reactors where progress is measured by time in flask

rules.condition: WHEN comparing steady-state characteristics between batch and flow
rules.recommendation: Expect uniform concentration at each position within batch flask, but steady but different concentration at each position throughout flow reactor length
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors have spatial gradients in concentration while batch reactors have temporal changes but spatial uniformity

rules.condition: WHEN changing stoichiometry in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Alter a single flow rate (v1 or v2) while keeping all other parameters constant to change stoichiometry, final concentration, and residence time
rules.reasoning: Flow rates directly control the ratio of reagents and overall residence time in the system

rules.condition: WHEN designing microfluidic devices with constant flow rate
rules.recommendation: Understand that reactant concentration decays exponentially with distance along the reactor, similar to how it decays with time in flask reactors
rules.reasoning: Time in a flask reactor equates with distance in a flow reactor, where reaction time is defined as residence time between reagent inlet and quencher position
rules.quantitative: Rate = -d[A]/dt = k[A] for first order, Rate = -d[A]/dt = k[A][B] or k[A]² for second order

rules.condition: WHEN conducting flash chemistry reactions
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors to conduct extremely fast reactions involving short-lived highly reactive intermediates without deceleration
rules.reasoning: Flash chemistry requires precise control of reaction windows between activation and optimal quenching time, which flow reactors can provide through controlled residence times

rules.condition: WHEN defining reaction time in flow chemistry with quenching agents
rules.recommendation: Define reaction time as the residence time between reagent inlet and the position of the quencher inlet
rules.reasoning: This provides high-resolution reaction time control for producing desired compounds with high selectivity
rules.quantitative: Reaction time = residence time between reagent inlet and quencher inlet position

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time in packed-bed reactors for liquid/solid or gas/liquid/solid reactions
rules.recommendation: Consider the dead or void volume of the reactor - the difference between total reactor volume and volume of stationary phase
rules.reasoning: Gas solubility in liquid phase, particle size, and system pressure influence flow rate and residence time, complicating calculations
rules.quantitative: Dead volume = Total reactor volume - Stationary phase volume

rules.condition: WHEN residence time calculations become complex in packed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Measure residence time by injecting a dye solution manually and monitoring its appearance at the end
rules.reasoning: This is often the simplest way when theoretical calculations are complicated by multiple factors

rules.condition: WHEN sizing particles for packed-bed microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use particle diameter dp < l/25 to avoid residence time distribution (axial dispersion)
rules.reasoning: Prevents axial dispersion that would broaden residence time distribution
rules.quantitative: dp < l/25, where dp = particle diameter, l = catalyst bed length

rules.condition: WHEN sizing particles for packed-bed microreactors to avoid wall effects
rules.recommendation: Use particle diameter dp < dt/10 to avoid wall effect
rules.reasoning: Prevents reactant molecules from flowing through without contacting active sites when particles are too large
rules.quantitative: dp < dt/10, where dp = particle diameter, dt = tube reactor diameter

rules.condition: WHEN calculating pressure drop in packed-bed microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use Darcy's law: ΔP/L = μQ/κA
rules.reasoning: High resistance for fluid flow results in high pressure drop over the packed bed
rules.quantitative: ΔP/L = μQ/κA, where ΔP = pressure drop, L = bed length, μ = fluid viscosity, Q = volumetric flow rate, κ = bed permeability, A = cross section

rules.condition: WHEN operating packed-bed microreactors
rules.recommendation: Expect pressure drop to increase linearly with increasing flow rates and lengths of packed bed
rules.reasoning: Direct relationship from Darcy's law between pressure drop and these parameters
rules.quantitative: Linear relationship: ΔP ∝ Q and ΔP ∝ L

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow microreactors for molecular-level reactions
rules.recommendation: Use reactor sizes ranging from 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ m with timescales of milliseconds to seconds
rules.reasoning: Chemical reactions occur at 10⁻¹³-10⁻¹² s timescale, and there's a correlation between reaction time and reaction environment size
rules.quantitative: Reactor size: 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ m; Timescale: milliseconds to seconds

rules.condition: WHEN comparing space-time yield between reactor types
rules.recommendation: Expect batch reactors STY < 1 kg m⁻³ s⁻¹, plug-flow reactors STY < 500 kg m⁻³ s⁻¹
rules.reasoning: Space-time yield represents mass of product formed per volume of reactor per time
rules.quantitative: Batch reactors: STY < 1 kg m⁻³ s⁻¹; Plug-flow reactors: STY < 500 kg m⁻³ s⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN size is reduced by 100-fold in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Expect surface-to-volume ratio to increase 100-fold, enabling rapid heat transfer
rules.reasoning: Volume scales as length cubed, surface area as length squared, so miniaturization dramatically increases surface-to-volume ratio

rules.condition: WHEN using microreactors for temperature control
rules.recommendation: Leverage miniaturization and large surface area for rapid heat transfer (slow reactions) or heat removal (exothermic reactions)
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratio enables fast cooling/heating and precise temperature control

rules.condition: WHEN operating in laminar flow regime with small dimensions (0.05-0.5 cm)
rules.recommendation: Recognize this as plug flow reactor (PFR) regime where radial diffusion is faster than convective mass transport
rules.reasoning: In small dimensions with laminar flow, radial mixing is much faster than axial convection, creating ideal PFR conditions

rules.condition: WHEN running microreactors in laminar flow regime
rules.recommendation: Achieve complete mixing in microseconds compared to seconds or longer in classical reactors
rules.reasoning: Small dimensions enable rapid homogeneous mixing due to short diffusion distances
rules.quantitative: Mixing time: microseconds (microreactors) vs seconds+ (classical reactors)

rules.condition: WHEN operating in laminar flow at steady state with very low Reynolds numbers
rules.recommendation: Rely only on diffusion for mass transfer between adjacent fluid layers
rules.reasoning: In pure laminar flow, convection only transports mass tangent to velocity, vertical concentration gradients require diffusion
rules.quantitative: Reynolds number must be particularly low for pure laminar flow

rules.condition: WHEN running fast reactions in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Carry out reactions at room temperature with short residence times without reducing reaction rate
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry enables precise control of short residence times while maintaining reaction efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN designing microfluidic channels for mixing
rules.recommendation: Use smaller channel dimensions to reduce diffusion time - diffusion time scales with the square of the diffusion path length (td = L²/D)
rules.reasoning: Molecules in microfluidic channels can reach the wall in seconds versus hours in batch reactors due to shorter diffusion paths
rules.quantitative: td = L²/D, where L is diffusion distance and D is diffusion coefficient. Examples: 1 μm = 0.0005s, 10 μm = 0.005s, 100 μm = 5s, 1 mm = 500s (for D = 1 × 10⁻⁵ cm² s⁻¹)
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when other mass transfer limitations dominate

rules.condition: WHEN determining reaction control regime in laminar flow using Damkoehler number
rules.recommendation: Calculate Da = (kr·C₀^(n-1))/[2D·AR/x²] where kr is reaction rate constant and C₀ is initial concentration
rules.reasoning: Damkoehler number shows if reaction is under diffusion or kinetic control - expresses reaction rate relative to mass transport rate
rules.quantitative: Da < 1: reaction rate limited, Da > 1: mass transport limited
rules.exceptions: Formula applies specifically to nth order reactions in microreactors

rules.condition: WHEN Da < 1 in consecutive reactions
rules.recommendation: Expect mostly desired product C to form with good homogeneity
rules.reasoning: Low Damkoehler number indicates reaction is slower than mixing, allowing uniform concentration distribution

rules.condition: WHEN Da > 1 in consecutive reactions
rules.recommendation: Expect formation of side product S due to local concentration gradients and insufficient mixing
rules.reasoning: High Damkoehler number means reaction is faster than mixing, creating local hot spots where side reactions occur
rules.exceptions: Can be mitigated by enhanced mixing strategies

rules.condition: WHEN needing to improve mixing in laminar flow
rules.recommendation: Apply three strategies: 1) use narrower channels for larger concentration gradients, 2) use longer channels or slower flow for more diffusion time, 3) use fluids with higher diffusion coefficients
rules.reasoning: These approaches increase the driving force for diffusion, provide more time for mixing, or enhance the diffusion process itself
rules.quantitative: Strategies target the diffusion equation parameters
rules.exceptions: Limited by practical constraints of channel fabrication and flow rates

rules.condition: WHEN laminar flow mixing is insufficient for fast reactions (Da > 1)
rules.recommendation: Use convective diffusion enhancement through turbulent flow regime with helical, curved, split-recombine, or packed-bed reactor structures
rules.reasoning: Turbulent flow creates convective mixing that is much faster than pure molecular diffusion
rules.quantitative: Needed when Da > 1 for diffusion-controlled reactions

rules.condition: WHEN designing passive micromixers
rules.recommendation: Use mixer geometry to generate effective contact between reagents through structures like T-junction, Y-junction, cross-junction, co-flow junction, split-recombine, multilamination, helical/curved, or periodic structures
rules.reasoning: Passive mixers rely on channel geometry rather than external energy to create mixing
rules.exceptions: May be insufficient for very fast reactions requiring active mixing

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up mixing from microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use static mixers with tube inserts such as blades/helices or fluid stretching to generate turbulence and radial mixing
rules.reasoning: Static mixers split fluid into layers and recombine in new sequences, creating turbulent mixing at larger scales
rules.exceptions: Design must be optimized for specific flow rates and fluid properties

rules.condition: WHEN targeting reaction acceleration through temperature
rules.recommendation: Use temperatures >200°C combined with small reactor dimensions for rapid heat exchange
rules.reasoning: Small dimensions allow rapid heat exchange, and high temperature provides significant rate acceleration according to Arrhenius equation k = Ae^(-Ea/RT)
rules.exceptions: Limited by thermal stability of reactants and materials

rules.condition: WHEN seeking reaction acceleration
rules.recommendation: Combine high temperature with high pressure to reach supercritical conditions for further reaction acceleration
rules.reasoning: Supercritical fluids have unique properties that can accelerate many chemical reactions beyond what temperature or pressure alone can achieve
rules.exceptions: Requires specialized equipment and safety considerations

rules.condition: WHEN performing heterogeneous gas-liquid-solid triphasic reactions
rules.recommendation: Use immobilized catalysts in cartridges (fixed-bed or packed-bed reactors) separated from reaction media
rules.reasoning: This configuration minimizes catalyst poisoning and side reactions due to immediate product removal from reactor zone, while providing high surface-to-volume ratio
rules.quantitative: Requires large interfacial areas (high surface-to-volume ratio)

rules.condition: WHEN handling reactive systems
rules.recommendation: Use small dimensions and short diffusion paths to prevent reagent accumulation and increase safety
rules.reasoning: Small dimensions enable rapid mixing and mass transfer, preventing dangerous accumulation of reactive intermediates
rules.exceptions: Safety benefits may be offset by other factors in specific reaction systems

rules.condition: When using heterogeneous catalysts in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use catalytic capillary reactors (narrow tubes, capillaries, channels) for homogeneous-like behavior with heterogeneous catalysts
rules.reasoning: Catalytic capillaries allow flow through narrow channels while maintaining catalyst contact, combining benefits of homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis

rules.condition: When using heterogeneous catalysts in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use packed-bed column reactors for heterogeneous catalyst powders or particles
rules.reasoning: Packed beds provide good contact between flowing reactants and solid catalyst particles while maintaining continuous flow

rules.condition: When using structured catalysts in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use honeycomb reactors with piled narrow channels that work as catalyst/catalyst bed, or monolith reactors with uniform material and microporous structure
rules.reasoning: Structured catalysts provide high surface area and controlled flow patterns while minimizing pressure drop

rules.condition: When designing heterogeneous catalytic flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use solid-supported reagents, catalysts, and scavengers together with in-line analytics for single line flow synthesis
rules.reasoning: This approach supports continuous multistep synthesis without intermediate isolation, improving efficiency and reducing waste

rules.condition: When designing multistep flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use continuous reactor networks to circumvent the need for isolation of intermediate products (telescoping)
rules.reasoning: Single line continuous reactor networks eliminate purification steps between reactions, improving efficiency and reducing complexity

rules.condition: When calculating flow reactor performance
rules.recommendation: Calculate residence time as reactor volume divided by bulk flow rate
rules.reasoning: Residence time determines how long reactants stay in the reactor zone and affects conversion and selectivity
rules.quantitative: Residence time = reactor volume / bulk flow rate

rules.condition: When defining flow reactor stoichiometry
rules.recommendation: Define stoichiometry by the concentration of reagents and the ratio of their flow rates
rules.reasoning: Flow stoichiometry is controlled by concentration and flow rate ratios rather than absolute amounts

rules.condition: When designing flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Consider that surface-to-volume ratio is the area per unit volume of the reactor
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratio improves heat and mass transfer, which is critical for reactor performance

rules.condition: In laminar flow regime
rules.recommendation: Rely on diffusion for radial mixing, as mixing occurs strictly due to diffusion
rules.reasoning: In laminar flow, there is no convective mixing, so radial mass transfer depends entirely on molecular diffusion
rules.quantitative: Use Damkoehler number (Da) to determine if reaction is under diffusion or kinetic control

rules.condition: When working with flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Understand that dispersion along the flow line emerges due to axial and radial diffusion and convection (pressure-driven fluid delivery)
rules.reasoning: Dispersion affects residence time distribution and can impact reaction selectivity and conversion

rules.condition: When conducting flash chemistry in flow
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors for extremely fast reactions involving short-lived highly reactive intermediates to achieve high selectivity through high-resolution reaction control
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry provides precise control over reaction time and conditions, essential for managing highly reactive intermediates

rules.condition: When operating microfluidic channels
rules.recommendation: Expect Reynolds numbers less than 2000, resulting in laminar flow domination
rules.reasoning: Low Reynolds numbers in microchannels ensure laminar flow regime where mass transfer occurs transversely between parallel flow profiles

rules.condition: When designing channels with length much greater than diameter for laminar, incompressible, viscous flow
rules.recommendation: Use the Hagen-Poiseuille equation: ΔP = 12ηLQ/πd⁴
rules.reasoning: This equation describes pressure drop for viscous flow in narrow channels, essential for pump sizing and system design
rules.quantitative: ΔP = 12ηLQ/πd⁴, where ΔP is pressure drop, η is viscosity, L is length, Q is flow rate, d is diameter
rules.exceptions: Only applies to laminar, incompressible, viscous flow in channels where length >> diameter

rules.condition: When fluid properties become increasingly controlled by viscous forces in miniaturized reactors
rules.recommendation: Account for Reynolds number determining flow regimes and describing the ratio between inertial and viscous forces
rules.reasoning: Miniaturization shifts the balance from inertial to viscous forces, fundamentally changing flow behavior and mixing mechanisms
rules.quantitative: Re = ρvL/μ = vL/ν, where ρ is density, v is velocity, L is characteristic length, μ is dynamic viscosity, ν is kinematic viscosity

rules.condition: When determining flow regimes
rules.recommendation: Use Reynolds number thresholds: truly laminar when Re < 100, transient when 100 < Re < 2,500, turbulent when Re > 2,500
rules.reasoning: These thresholds define different flow regimes that determine mixing mechanisms and pressure drop behavior
rules.quantitative: Re < 100: laminar; 100 < Re < 2,500: transient; Re > 2,500: turbulent

rules.condition: WHEN Reynolds number Re < 10 in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider flow as truly laminar
rules.reasoning: At very low Reynolds numbers, viscous forces completely dominate over inertial forces, resulting in purely laminar flow

rules.condition: WHEN Reynolds number is in the range 100-1000 in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider flow as intermediate flow regime, not typically laminar
rules.reasoning: Most flow chemistry operates within this range where flow exhibits characteristics between laminar and turbulent

rules.condition: WHEN Reynolds number Re > 2500 in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Expect turbulent flow regime with random fluid motion facilitating convective mass transport
rules.reasoning: High Reynolds numbers indicate turbulent flow where fluid elements exhibit random motion, enhancing mixing through convective transport

rules.condition: WHEN designing macroscale mixing systems
rules.recommendation: Induce turbulent flow regime by generating mesoflow through disturbing the laminar flow
rules.reasoning: At macroscale, mixing is typically achieved by creating turbulent conditions that enhance mass transfer

rules.condition: WHEN using packed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Use modified Reynolds number formula: Re = ρdpU0/μ(1-ε)
rules.reasoning: Packed beds require accounting for particle diameter (dp), porosity (ε), and superficial velocity (U0) to properly characterize flow regime
rules.quantitative: Re = ρdpU0/μ(1-ε) where dp = particle diameter, ε = porosity

rules.condition: WHEN operating packed-bed microreactors
rules.recommendation: Identify flow regimes: laminar (RePB < 10), transitional (10 < RePB < 300), turbulent (RePB > 300)
rules.reasoning: Different flow regimes in packed beds have distinct boundaries that affect mass transfer and mixing characteristics
rules.quantitative: Laminar: RePB < 10, Transitional: 10 < RePB < 300, Turbulent: RePB > 300

rules.condition: WHEN designing packed-bed reactors with isotropic porosity
rules.recommendation: Calculate interstitial velocity using U = U0/ε
rules.reasoning: Particles reduce available cross-sectional area, so actual fluid velocity within the bed is higher than superficial velocity
rules.quantitative: U = U0/ε where U0 = superficial velocity, ε = porosity

rules.condition: WHEN porosity calculation is needed
rules.recommendation: Calculate porosity as the ratio of void volume to total volume: Porosity = Vv/VT
rules.reasoning: Porosity defines the fraction of volume available for fluid flow in packed systems
rules.quantitative: Porosity = Vv/VT where Vv = void volume, VT = total volume

rules.condition: WHEN flow regime is transient or turbulent (Reynolds > 1000)
rules.recommendation: Expect convection to dominate over axial or radial molecular diffusion
rules.reasoning: In higher Reynolds number flows, convective mixing becomes much more significant than diffusive transport

rules.condition: WHEN axial dispersion is not measurable in laminar flow
rules.recommendation: Consider the microflow reactor as operating as an ideal PFR (plug flow reactor)
rules.reasoning: In ideal laminar flow with negligible axial dispersion, the reactor behaves as a perfect plug flow system
rules.exceptions: Only applies when axial dispersion is negligible

rules.condition: WHEN evaluating axial dispersion in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Calculate Bodenstein number using Bo = UL/D* where U = average velocity, L = reactor length, D* = dispersion coefficient
rules.reasoning: Bodenstein number relates axial convective forces to backmixing from axial dispersion

rules.condition: WHEN Bodenstein number Bo > 100
rules.recommendation: Expect small deviations from plug flow behavior
rules.reasoning: High Bo values indicate convection dominates over dispersion, approaching ideal plug flow

rules.condition: WHEN Bodenstein number Bo < 100
rules.recommendation: Expect large deviations from plug flow behavior
rules.reasoning: Low Bo values indicate significant dispersion effects that cause deviation from ideal plug flow

rules.condition: WHEN Bodenstein number Bo > 1000
rules.recommendation: Approximate system as having plug flow behavior
rules.reasoning: Very high Bo values indicate negligible dispersion effects, allowing plug flow approximation

rules.condition: WHEN characterizing axial dispersion by convection
rules.recommendation: Calculate Peclet number using Pe = Nconv/Ndiff = UL/DAB where Pe > 500 indicates high axial dispersion due to convection
rules.reasoning: Peclet number expresses the ratio of convection to diffusion, with high values indicating convection-dominated dispersion
rules.quantitative: Pe = UL/DAB, Pe > 500 for high axial dispersion

rules.condition: WHEN high residence time (low flow rate) and small tube diameter are used
rules.recommendation: Expect ideal laminar (plug) flow conditions
rules.reasoning: Long residence times and small diameters favor laminar flow conditions with minimal dispersion

rules.condition: WHEN using fast reactions and short tubing length
rules.recommendation: Expect convection to be the primary driver for mixing and mass transport
rules.reasoning: Fast reactions in short reactors don't allow time for diffusive mixing, making convection the dominant transport mechanism

rules.condition: WHEN designing multiflow systems with sequential reagent injection
rules.recommendation: Match reagent plug concentration and stoichiometry to obtain high conversions and selectivities
rules.reasoning: Sequential injection requires careful matching of concentration and stoichiometry to maintain reaction efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN using segmented (slug) flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use T-mixer to enhance phase mixing where segments are segregated by immiscible solvent
rules.reasoning: T-mixers help create well-defined segments in slug flow systems where different reaction combinations can occur

rules.condition: When using gas-liquid or liquid-liquid segmented flow
rules.recommendation: Use segmented flow to achieve rapid mixing and reduced residence time distribution compared to continuous flow
rules.reasoning: Internal vortex circulation within each fluid segment increases mixing compared to molecular diffusion in laminar flow, and no cross contamination occurs between reaction slugs
rules.exceptions: Sequential addition of reagents is difficult in segmented flow

rules.condition: When using continuous flow systems
rules.recommendation: Expect wide residence time distribution and large particle distribution
rules.reasoning: Continuous flow inherently has broad residence time distribution which can affect reaction selectivity and reproducibility

rules.condition: When operating microreactors in Taylor flow regime
rules.recommendation: Use Taylor flow to achieve ideal PFR behavior with minimal axial dispersion and excellent residence time distribution
rules.reasoning: Taylor flow shows minimal axial dispersion and provides excellent residence time distribution, making it behave like an ideal plug flow reactor (PFR)

rules.condition: When using Taylor flow in gas-liquid systems
rules.recommendation: Exploit internal circulations in both liquid and gas bubbles for fast mass transfer between phases
rules.reasoning: Internal circulation results in homogeneous mixing and facilitates fast mass transfer between the two phases

rules.condition: When selecting pumping methods for microchannels
rules.recommendation: Choose from three types: hydrodynamic (pressure-driven), electrokinetic, or centrifugal pumping
rules.reasoning: Different pumping mechanisms are available for different applications and channel requirements

rules.condition: When using positive displacement pumps
rules.recommendation: Use pressure-oriented pumps that force a fixed volume of fluid from inlet to discharge zone
rules.reasoning: Positive displacement pumps operate by forcing fluid volume rather than relying on flow energy conversion

rules.condition: When using negative displacement (centrifugal) pumps
rules.recommendation: Use flow-oriented pumps where flow energy converts to kinetic energy then pressure energy
rules.reasoning: Centrifugal pumps work by converting flow energy through kinetic energy to pressure energy

rules.condition: When using electroosmotic flow (EOF)
rules.recommendation: Apply only to aqueous or polar systems with low flow rates and small channel dimensions
rules.reasoning: EOF requires ionic solutions and specific conditions to function properly
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for non-polar systems, high flow rates, or large channels

rules.condition: When calculating EOF velocity
rules.recommendation: Use the formula: veof = -Eεε0ζ/η where E is electric field, ε is dielectric constant, ε0 is permittivity of free space, ζ is zeta potential, η is viscosity
rules.reasoning: This formula relates EOF velocity to the physical and electrical properties of the system

rules.condition: When selecting solvents for EOF
rules.recommendation: Use solvents with high dielectric constant and low viscosity for higher flow rates
rules.reasoning: Flow rate is directly proportional to field strength and inversely related to viscosity

rules.condition: When selecting solvents for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Choose solvents that are low viscosity, odorless (nonvolatile), anhydrous, non-corrosive, and biodegradable
rules.reasoning: These properties ensure safe operation, equipment compatibility, and environmental responsibility

rules.condition: When characterizing flow kinetics
rules.recommendation: Consider kinematic viscosity, heat conductivity, and Prandtl number (Pr = ν/α = cpμ/k)
rules.reasoning: Prandtl number relates momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity, important for heat and mass transfer

rules.condition: WHEN selecting organic solvents for flow chemistry at different temperatures
rules.recommendation: For toluene, use kinematic viscosity ratios of 0.24 at 295K, 0.89 at 235K; thermal diffusivity ratios of 0.22 at 295K, 0.25 at 235K; Prandtl number ratios of 0.38 at 295K, 1.23 at 235K relative to water at 20°C
rules.reasoning: Different solvents have significantly different transport properties that affect heat and mass transfer in microreactors
rules.exceptions: Values are specific to 22°C reference conditions

rules.condition: WHEN selecting ethanol for flow chemistry at different temperatures
rules.recommendation: Use kinematic viscosity ratios of 1.63 at 295K, 6.56 at 235K; thermal diffusivity ratios of 0.28 at 295K, 0.31 at 235K; Prandtl number ratios of 2.50 at 295K, 7.44 at 235K relative to water
rules.reasoning: Ethanol shows much higher viscosity ratios than toluene, particularly at lower temperatures, affecting flow patterns and mixing
rules.exceptions: Values are specific to 22°C reference conditions

rules.condition: WHEN selecting THF for flow chemistry at different temperatures
rules.recommendation: Use kinematic viscosity ratios of 0.55 at 295K, 1.44 at 235K; thermal diffusivity ratios of 0.23 at 295K, 0.45 at 235K; Prandtl number ratios of 1.29 at 295K, 1.35 at 235K relative to water
rules.reasoning: THF shows intermediate transport properties between toluene and ethanol, providing moderate viscosity effects
rules.exceptions: Values are specific to 22°C reference conditions

rules.condition: WHEN evaluating flow regimes in microchannels
rules.recommendation: Calculate Reynolds number as the ratio of momentum (inertial) forces to viscous forces to determine flow regime
rules.reasoning: Reynolds number determines whether flow is laminar or turbulent, which affects mixing, heat transfer, and mass transfer

rules.condition: WHEN designing segmented flow systems for multiple reactions
rules.recommendation: Use segmented flow to deliver pulses of reactants segregated by immiscible solvent, with each segment containing different reactant combinations
rules.reasoning: Segmented flow allows sequential delivery of different reactant combinations for multi-step reactions

rules.condition: WHEN axial dispersion occurs in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Evaluate using Bodenstein number (Bo) - small Bo values indicate large deviation from ideal plug flow
rules.reasoning: Axial dispersion causes backmixing, reducing the efficiency of plug flow reactors
rules.quantitative: Small Bodenstein number = large deviation from plug flow
rules.exceptions: Can use inline infrared monitoring during multistep flow reactions to monitor dispersion effects

rules.condition: WHEN designing Taylor flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use gas bubbles separated by liquid slugs with thin liquid film on channel walls to achieve internal circulations for homogeneous mixing and fast mass transfer
rules.reasoning: Internal circulation in Taylor flow bubbles facilitates homogeneous mixing and enhances mass transfer between phases

rules.condition: WHEN using hydrodynamic pumping systems
rules.recommendation: Use conventional or micro-scale syringe-type pumps to deliver solutions around the channel network
rules.reasoning: Hydrodynamic pumping provides reliable fluid delivery for microfluidic systems

rules.condition: WHEN using electroosmotic flow (EOF)
rules.recommendation: Apply electrical potential across microchannel to induce fluid flow via ion migration and electrical double layer formation
rules.reasoning: EOF creates fluid flow through electrokinetic effects when ions migrate to opposite charges, lining the channel wall

rules.condition: WHEN controlling reaction time for consecutive or multiple elementary steps
rules.recommendation: Adjust residence time to a particular step by changing channel length or flow rate to isolate intermediates or major products
rules.reasoning: High-resolution reaction time control allows isolation of specific products by focusing residence time on particular chemical steps
rules.quantitative: Residence time inversely correlates with flow rate

rules.condition: WHEN designing selective hydrogenation reactions (example: 3-methyl-1-pentyn-3-ol to 3-methyl-1-penten-3-ol)
rules.recommendation: Use reaction window concept - operate within residence time range of 15-25 seconds to maximize desired product P1 while minimizing overhydrogenation to P2
rules.reasoning: Optimal residence time window allows selective formation of intermediate product before further reaction to undesired overhydrogenated product
rules.quantitative: Optimal residence time window: 15-25 seconds for this specific reaction
rules.exceptions: Specific to the example reaction system shown

rules.condition: WHEN comparing liquid-liquid extraction in batch vs flow
rules.recommendation: In flow systems, use simple laminar flow with high interfacial surface area instead of rigorous shaking required in batch processes
rules.reasoning: Small dimensions in microflow reactors facilitate increased mass transfer, making vigorous mixing less necessary
rules.exceptions: For fast reactions, may still need convective mass transport and mixing

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are fast and diffusion-limited
rules.recommendation: Increase rate of molecular diffusion by using convective mass transport and mixing
rules.reasoning: Fast reactions require enhanced mass transfer beyond simple molecular diffusion to prevent mass transfer limitations
rules.exceptions: Not needed for extraction processes with sufficient interfacial area

rules.condition: WHEN designing heat transfer systems
rules.recommendation: Consider that area per unit volume (surface-to-volume ratio) is the crucial factor for heat transfer effectiveness
rules.reasoning: Heat transfer occurs via the reactor surface, so surface area relative to volume determines heat transfer efficiency
rules.quantitative: Q/t = -kS(T1-T2)/d, where Q=heat transfer, t=time, k=thermal conductivity, S=surface area, d=distance

rules.condition: WHEN using standard microreactors with 1 mm channel/capillary dimensions
rules.recommendation: Expect very fast heating due to high surface-to-volume ratio, especially with steel or silicon-based materials with high thermal conductivity
rules.reasoning: Small dimensions and high thermal conductivity materials provide rapid heat transfer in microreactors
rules.quantitative: 2.5 mL microreactor reaches 250°C in <1 minute; 630 mL flask requires 8 minutes for same temperature
rules.exceptions: Performance depends on reactor material thermal conductivity

rules.condition: WHEN removing excess heat in highly exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Use heat-absorbing microreactors rather than flasks for feasible heat removal
rules.reasoning: Microreactors provide much better heat removal capability than batch flasks for controlling exothermic reactions

rules.condition: WHEN evaluating heat generation vs removal in exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Consider rate of heat generated (-rΔHrxn), rate of heat removed through conduction/convection to reactor wall, and rate of heat removed from reactor wall to surroundings
rules.reasoning: Heat balance requires considering all heat generation and removal pathways to prevent temperature runaway
rules.quantitative: Heat generation rate = -rΔHrxn; Multiple heat removal pathways must be considered

rules.condition: WHEN comparing heat generation and removal capabilities
rules.recommendation: Use the ratio of heat of reaction (ΔHrxn), adiabatic temperature rise (ΔTad), and reactor diameter (dt) to evaluate heat management
rules.reasoning: These parameters help determine whether heat generation can be managed by heat removal in the reactor design
rules.quantitative: Consider ΔHrxn, ΔTad, and dt in heat generation/removal analysis

rules.condition: WHEN calculating adiabatic temperature rise
rules.recommendation: Calculate ΔTad as the temperature increase when no heat transfer occurs to or from the reactor
rules.reasoning: Adiabatic temperature rise indicates the maximum possible temperature increase if heat removal is insufficient
rules.quantitative: ΔTad = temperature increase with no heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN designing heat removal for flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Ensure the β value (ratio of heat generated to heat removed) is less than 1 using the formula βᴃ = -τΔHᵣₓₙdᶠ²/(4ΔTₐdₖ), where dᶠ is channel diameter and k is thermal conductivity
rules.reasoning: Heat removal can only be achieved in microreactors when the β value is less than 1, unlike in batch reactors where this is more challenging
rules.exceptions: Not applicable to batch reactors which have different heat transfer limitations

rules.condition: WHEN using microreactors for temperature control
rules.recommendation: Utilize high surface-to-volume ratios (95 for 2ml microreactor vs 1 for 100ml flask) to achieve efficient heat input/removal and nearly constant reaction temperatures
rules.reasoning: Microreactors achieve efficient heat transfer because of their high surface-to-volume ratios, enabling rapid equilibration
rules.exceptions: Does not apply to larger volume reactors with lower surface-to-volume ratios

rules.condition: WHEN performing rapid cooling in multistep flow synthesis to avoid side reactions
rules.recommendation: Cool reaction flow from 80-120°C to -25°C within residence time of 1.2 minutes using integrated cooling systems
rules.reasoning: Accelerated heat transfer allows rapid cooling that cannot be achieved in batch reactor systems, preventing unwanted side reactions
rules.quantitative: Temperature range: 80-120°C to -25°C, residence time: 1.2 minutes
rules.exceptions: Such rapid cooling cannot be achieved in batch reactor systems

rules.condition: WHEN performing flash chemical reactions with highly reactive intermediates
rules.recommendation: Use reaction times in the milliseconds to seconds range for highly exothermic reactions that are diffusion-controlled
rules.reasoning: Highly reactive intermediates react with substrates within milliseconds to seconds, making miniaturized flow devices ideal due to excellent mixing and heat transfer
rules.exceptions: Does not apply to slower reactions or those not diffusion-controlled

rules.condition: WHEN running highly exothermic flash reactions typically performed at -78°C in batch
rules.recommendation: Carry out reactions at ambient or moderately low temperatures in microreactors due to excellent heat transfer properties
rules.reasoning: Miniaturized flow devices provide excellent mixing and heat transfer, allowing temperature moderation compared to batch requirements
rules.quantitative: Batch temperature: -78°C, Flow temperature: ambient or moderately low
rules.exceptions: Does not apply to batch reactors which require much lower temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN seeking selective activation in competing reaction pathways sensitive to temperature
rules.recommendation: Use microreactors with narrow temperature distributions instead of batch reactors with broad temperature profiles to restrict reactions to target pathways
rules.reasoning: Batch reactors provide broad temperature profiles allowing access to multiple pathways, while narrow temperature distribution in microreactors restricts reaction to target product pathway
rules.quantitative: Microreactor: narrow temperature distribution, Batch: broad temperature distribution
rules.exceptions: Does not apply when multiple reaction pathways are desired

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with slow reactions requiring significant rate acceleration (reaction time >30 min)
rules.recommendation: Apply Novel Process Windows (NPW) approach operating at conditions beyond usual limits to speed up conversion rates while maintaining selectivity
rules.reasoning: Slow reactions require systematic rate acceleration approaches that NPW provides through process condition intensification
rules.exceptions: Not needed for already fast reactions

rules.condition: WHEN implementing Novel Process Windows for rate acceleration
rules.recommendation: Use combined high-temperature and high-pressure flow regime (>200°C and >50 bar) as one main direction for process intensification
rules.reasoning: High-temperature/high-pressure conditions shift routine processes to harsher but achievable conditions with microreactors, enabling supercritical water generation
rules.exceptions: May not be suitable for temperature or pressure sensitive substrates

rules.condition: WHEN working with supercritical conditions in NPW
rules.recommendation: Utilize supercritical fluids to bring all reactants (solvents and gases) into one phase for complete mixing
rules.reasoning: Supercritical fluids are gases that mix completely with other gases like hydrogen, eliminating mass transfer resistance across phase boundaries
rules.quantitative: All reactants in single supercritical phase
rules.exceptions: Does not apply to reactions requiring phase separation

rules.condition: WHEN applying Process Intensification (PI) to obtain equivalent or better results
rules.recommendation: Increase parameters such as temperature and pressure to achieve enhanced throughput with reduced time period while maintaining purity, selectivity, and yield
rules.reasoning: PI enables substantially smaller, cleaner, safer and more energy efficient technology by intensifying operating conditions
rules.quantitative: Maintain or improve: purity, selectivity, yield; Reduce: time period; Increase: throughput
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for reactions already operating under optimal kinetic control

rules.condition: WHEN applying process intensification to microreactors for slow reactions
rules.recommendation: Reduce reaction times from minute level to second level by exploiting faster kinetics termed 'intensified intrinsic kinetics'
rules.reasoning: Microreactors enable exploitation of much faster kinetics through improved heat and mass transfer, allowing significant time reduction
rules.exceptions: Limited by intrinsic reaction kinetics that cannot be further accelerated

rules.condition: WHEN operating flow chemistry reactions under novel process windows
rules.recommendation: Consider operating at pressures between 1E+02 to 1E+04 Pa to access supercritical and superheated conditions that shift reactions toward high temperature/pressure realization
rules.reasoning: Novel process windows enable access to supercritical and superheated conditions that can dramatically increase reaction rates while maintaining safety through small reactor volumes
rules.quantitative: Pressure range: 1E+02 to 1E+04 Pa, Temperature range: 100-1000 K
rules.exceptions: Standard atmospheric conditions may be sufficient for some reactions

rules.condition: WHEN selecting solvents for high-pressure flow chemistry applications
rules.recommendation: Use critical temperature and pressure data: Acetic acid (322°C, 58 bar), Acetone (236°C, 48 bar), Methanol (240°C, 80 bar), Water (374°C, 221 bar), etc.
rules.reasoning: Operating near or above critical conditions can provide enhanced mass transfer and reaction kinetics
rules.quantitative: Critical temperatures range from 218°C (Trifluoroacetic acid) to 376°C (DMF); Critical pressures range from 25 bar (n-Octane) to 221 bar (Water)
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for reactions that degrade under high temperature/pressure conditions

rules.condition: WHEN designing process intensification for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Target residence times: μ-Mixing (10^-2 s), μ-Heat exchange (10^-1 s), Conv-Mixing (1-10 s), Conv-Heat Mixing (10-100 s), Many reactions (100-1000 s), Process protocol times in organic textbooks (1000-10,000 s)
rules.reasoning: Different unit operations require specific time scales to achieve optimal performance, from microsecond mixing to extended reaction times
rules.quantitative: Time scales: 10^-2 to 10^4 seconds depending on operation type
rules.exceptions: Some specialized reactions may require longer or shorter times outside these ranges

rules.condition: WHEN implementing on-demand flow synthesis manufacturing
rules.recommendation: Use modular, reconfigurable platforms that combine continuous-flow synthesis, multistep sequence telescoping, reaction engineering equipment, and real-time formulation
rules.reasoning: On-demand manufacturing enables just-in-time production, reduces transport/storage risks, and allows production at remote locations
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for products requiring large-scale batch production

rules.condition: WHEN producing hazardous reagents in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Generate hazardous reagents on-site and on-demand from benign precursors using the chemical generator concept
rules.reasoning: This approach improves safety risk, especially on large scale, by eliminating transport, storage, and handling of hazardous materials
rules.exceptions: May not be feasible for all types of hazardous reagents

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow reactors for hazardous reagent generation
rules.recommendation: Design as modular systems that integrate multiple reaction steps, in-line purification procedures, and real-time reaction control using PAT tools
rules.reasoning: Modular design provides flexibility and safety while enabling continuous processing with integrated purification and control
rules.exceptions: Simple reactions may not require complex modular designs

rules.condition: WHEN performing chemistry in space environments
rules.recommendation: Account for very poor mixing due to microgravitation conditions, which fundamentally affects chemical reaction performance
rules.reasoning: The absence of gravitational forces dramatically reduces natural convection and mixing efficiency compared to Earth-based conditions
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for Earth-based flow chemistry

rules.condition: WHEN implementing automated flow chemistry platforms
rules.recommendation: Use single computer control for all reactor components, in-line analysis systems (pumps, heaters, gas chromatograph), and optimization algorithms for iterative parameter adjustment
rules.reasoning: Centralized control enables real-time optimization and remote operation capabilities
rules.exceptions: Simple manual systems may be adequate for routine operations

rules.condition: WHEN performing chemical reactions in space applications
rules.recommendation: Use green reactions and avoid explosible or inflammable solvents due to increased danger in space environments
rules.reasoning: There is strong preference for green reactions in space due to the increased danger associated with explosive or flammable solvents in space environments

rules.condition: WHEN conducting microgravity experiments
rules.recommendation: Limit experiments to shorter than 8 minutes duration for freefall microgravity conditions
rules.reasoning: Microgravity produced by freefall can only be sustained for limited time periods on special aircraft

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow chemistry systems for space applications
rules.recommendation: Use fully automated, remote-controlled systems suitable for very effective mixing
rules.reasoning: Space chemistry laboratories operate under local or remote control and need to provide effective mixing without human intervention

rules.condition: WHEN characterizing mixing versus reaction rate in laminar flow
rules.recommendation: Use Damkoehler number (Da) to determine if reaction is under diffusion or kinetic control
rules.reasoning: Damkoehler number expresses the reaction rate relative to the mass transport rate (diffusion)
rules.quantitative: Da = reaction rate / mass transport rate

rules.condition: WHEN controlling multistep reaction sequences
rules.recommendation: Adjust the residence time to control each particular step using high-resolution reaction time control
rules.reasoning: The principle of high-resolution reaction time control rests on adjusting residence time for each step in multistep sequences

rules.condition: WHEN operating under Novel Process Windows (NPW)
rules.recommendation: Use process conditions beyond usual conditions to speed up conversion rates while maintaining selectivity
rules.reasoning: NPW operates at process conditions that considerably speed up conversion rates while maintaining selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN measuring adiabatic temperature rise
rules.recommendation: Calculate ΔTad as the increase in temperature of reaction mixture when there is no heat transfer to or from the environment
rules.reasoning: Adiabatic temperature rise (ΔTad) represents the temperature increase under conditions of no heat exchange with surroundings
rules.quantitative: ΔTad = temperature increase under adiabatic conditions

rules.condition: WHEN controlling reaction time in flow microreactors
rules.recommendation: Control residence time by adjusting reactor length or flow speed. Residence time = (reactor length)/(flow speed)
rules.reasoning: In flow reactors, reaction time corresponds to residence time, which is the time the solution spends in the reactor
rules.quantitative: For 10 mm length at 1 mm/s flow speed: residence time = 10 s

rules.condition: WHEN needing to reduce residence time in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Reduce reactor length or increase flow speed
rules.reasoning: Residence time is directly proportional to reactor length and inversely proportional to flow speed
rules.quantitative: Reducing 10 mm reactor to 5 mm reduces residence time from 10 s to 5 s at same flow speed

rules.condition: WHEN needing to increase residence time in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Increase reactor length or decrease flow speed
rules.reasoning: Longer residence time allows more complete reaction
rules.quantitative: Increasing 10 mm reactor to 20 mm increases residence time from 10 s to 20 s at same flow speed

rules.condition: WHEN adjusting flow speed to control residence time
rules.recommendation: Increasing flow speed to 1 m/s in a 10 mm pipe reduces residence time to 10 ms
rules.reasoning: Higher flow speeds dramatically reduce residence time for precise control of fast reactions
rules.quantitative: 1 m/s flow speed in 10 mm reactor = 10 ms residence time

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with extremely fast reactions
rules.recommendation: Use microreactors instead of macroreactors for suitable control
rules.reasoning: Only microreactors can provide the fast mixing and precise time control needed for extremely fast reactions
rules.quantitative: Physical length between inlets typically 1-10 mm in microreactor
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for slower reactions where macroreactors are sufficient

rules.condition: WHEN the mixing time is longer than the reaction time
rules.recommendation: Ensure mixing time is much shorter than reaction time, preferably much less than 1 s if reaction time is 1 s
rules.reasoning: If mixing time exceeds reaction time, the solution will not be homogeneous during reaction, preventing adequate kinetic control
rules.quantitative: For 1 s reaction time, mixing time should be much less than 1 s

rules.condition: WHEN comparing batch vs flow reactors for fast reactions
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors with microstructures for rapid mixing that cannot be achieved in batch reactors
rules.reasoning: Conventional batch reactors cannot achieve rapid mixing by stirring, and flow macroreactors also have limitations
rules.exceptions: Suitable for slower reactions where batch mixing is adequate

rules.condition: WHEN designing reactors for time-sensitive reactions
rules.recommendation: Use reactor length between inlets of 1-10 mm to control reaction initiation and termination points
rules.reasoning: Physical distance between reagent and quencher inlets defines the reaction zone and enables precise time control

rules.condition: WHEN human manual control is required for reactions
rules.recommendation: Avoid reaction times shorter than 200 ms as they are beyond human response capability
rules.reasoning: Humans cannot react fast enough to manually control reactions on timescales shorter than 200 ms
rules.quantitative: Human reaction time >200 ms, walking pace ~1 m/s
rules.exceptions: When automated control systems are used

rules.condition: WHEN comparing flow vs batch chemistry capabilities
rules.recommendation: Use flow chemistry to enable reactions impossible in batch reactors due to time control limitations
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry allows control of time lengths that cannot be controlled in batch chemistry, enabling previously impossible reactions
rules.quantitative: Control precision down to millisecond timescales
rules.exceptions: When batch chemistry provides adequate time control

rules.condition: WHEN considering flow velocity distribution in tubes/channels
rules.recommendation: Account for non-uniform flow - faster at center than near walls, creating residence time distribution
rules.reasoning: Flow speed varies across the cross-section, being faster near the center and slower near walls
rules.quantitative: Distribution narrows with smaller diameter channels/tubes
rules.exceptions: For convenience, mean residence time is often used in calculations

rules.condition: WHEN reaction rate constants are k₁ = 10 s⁻¹ and k₂/k₁ = 0.01 for fast reactions with competing side reactions
rules.recommendation: Control reaction time between 0.3 and 0.6 seconds to achieve yields higher than 90%
rules.reasoning: The quenching agent must be added and mixed within a very narrow time window to prevent side reactions and maintain high yield
rules.quantitative: Reaction time window: 0.3-0.6 seconds for >90% yield when k₁ = 10 s⁻¹ and k₂/k₁ = 0.01

rules.condition: WHEN reaction time control shorter than one second is required
rules.recommendation: Use flow microreactor system equipped with micromixers instead of batch microreactor
rules.reasoning: Time lengths shorter than a second cannot be controlled manually and precise reaction-time control cannot be achieved in flask chemistry

rules.condition: WHEN working with aryllithium compounds bearing electrophilic functional groups
rules.recommendation: Use flow microreactors for reactions that are not viable in batch mode due to functional group incompatibility
rules.reasoning: Phenyllithium species react very rapidly with carbonyl groups, causing internal functional group incompatibility that requires multiple steps and protecting groups in batch synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN performing I/Li exchange reactions with organolithium species bearing reactive pendant groups
rules.recommendation: Conduct reactions at very low temperatures (e.g., -70°C) to minimize decomposition
rules.reasoning: Aryllithium compounds bearing electrophilic functional groups are usually conducted at very low temperatures, and reactions with electrophiles are performed at very low temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN performing I/Li exchange reaction of o-pentanoyliodobenzene at -70°C
rules.recommendation: Keep residence time in first reactor (R1) at approximately 0.003 seconds or less for complete consumption of starting material
rules.reasoning: All o-pentanoyliodobenzene is consumed within 0.003 s, and longer residence times lead to decreased yield due to dimerization
rules.quantitative: 0.003 seconds residence time for complete conversion

rules.condition: WHEN performing sequential reactions with unstable intermediates like phenyllithium species
rules.recommendation: Use flow microreactor system with two micromixers (M1 and M2) and two microtube reactors (R1 and R2) in series
rules.reasoning: Allows precise control of reaction sequence: first reaction in R1 after mixing in M1, then quenching reaction in R2 after mixing with second reagent in M2
rules.quantitative: Two-stage reactor system with intermediate mixing

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing reactions in flow microreactors
rules.recommendation: Create temperature-residence time maps by collecting product solutions under various conditions and analyzing by GC or HPLC
rules.reasoning: Maps consisting of 30-40 data points can be made in a day or two and serve as powerful tools for analyzing mechanisms and optimizing reaction conditions

rules.condition: WHEN using temperature-residence time maps for reaction optimization
rules.recommendation: Test various temperatures and residence times to find conditions that maximize yield while minimizing side reactions
rules.reasoning: Temperature affects reaction rate and reaction profile, and maps reveal appropriate temperature and residence times for obtaining desired products at good yields
rules.quantitative: Temperature range tested: -75°C to 0°C in example, residence times: 10⁻¹⁵ to 10⁰⁵ seconds

rules.condition: WHEN choosing electrophiles for reactions with aryllithium intermediates
rules.recommendation: Select stronger electrophiles over weaker ones to outcompete dimerization reactions
rules.reasoning: CH₃I is a much weaker electrophile than methyl triflate, giving only 22% yield vs 77% because the reaction is not fast enough to compete with intermediate dimerization
rules.quantitative: Methyl triflate: 77% yield, Methyl iodide: 22% yield

rules.condition: WHEN working with alkyl lithiobenzoates of different substitution patterns
rules.recommendation: Expect higher stability and wider operating windows for t-butyl > isopropyl > ethyl esters, and o-lithiobenzoates > p- and m-lithiobenzoates
rules.reasoning: Steric bulkiness of t-butyl group retards attack on carbonyl group making intermediates highly stable, and coordination of carbonyl group to Li makes o-species more stable
rules.quantitative: Stability order: t-butyl > isopropyl > ethyl; o-position > p-, m-positions

rules.condition: WHEN performing transformations impossible in conventional macro-batch reactors due to unstable intermediates
rules.recommendation: Use precise control of short residence times in flow microreactors to enable these transformations
rules.reasoning: Flow microreactors provide precise control that makes transformations possible that would be impossible in batch due to intermediate instability

rules.condition: WHEN controlling isomerization of aryllithium species in halogen/lithium exchange reactions
rules.recommendation: Use short residence times (0.06 s) to favor kinetically preferred reactive intermediates (nonisomerized), or long residence times (63 s) to favor thermodynamically preferred intermediates (isomerized)
rules.reasoning: Li can migrate to another position on the ring creating an isomer. Short residence times prevent migration, while longer times allow lithium migration to thermodynamically preferred positions
rules.quantitative: 0.06 s for kinetic products, 63 s for thermodynamic products with >99% isomeric purity

rules.condition: WHEN performing sequential deprotonations of 1,2-dichloroethene for pathway switching
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 0.055 s in R1 with 1.05 equivalents of t-BuLi to favor 1,2-dichloroethene substituted product, or 50.2 s to favor alkyne product
rules.reasoning: Short residence times allow 1,2-dichlorovinyllithium to react with electrophile before elimination, while longer times allow elimination to chloroacetylene intermediate
rules.quantitative: 0.055 s residence time, 1.05 equiv t-BuLi, 2.10 equiv t-BuLi for switching pathways

rules.condition: WHEN performing space integration of reactions using unstable organolithium intermediates
rules.recommendation: Use very short residence times (8.1 ms) for highly unstable organolithium species bearing multiple metal atoms and halogen atoms
rules.reasoning: Organolithium intermediates with Li, Sn, F, and I atoms are extremely unstable and require minimal residence time to prevent decomposition
rules.quantitative: 8.1 ms residence time in R3 reactor

rules.condition: WHEN designing integrated flow microreactor systems for multi-step synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use space integration with components added at different locations in flow system to enable sequential reactions with stepwise addition of reagents/catalysts
rules.reasoning: Space integration allows wider choice of reagents and catalysts compared to time integration, and enables use of short-lived unstable intermediates
rules.exceptions: Time and space integration is easier to carry out but more limited in reagent choice

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing TAC-101 using integrated flow microreactor system
rules.recommendation: Use system with six micromixers (M1-M6) and six microtube reactors (R1-R6) with total residence time of approximately 13 s to achieve 100-200 mg/min product output
rules.reasoning: Sequential space-integrated Br/Li exchange reactions followed by electrophile reactions require multiple mixing and reaction stages
rules.quantitative: Total residence time ~13 s, throughput 100-200 mg/min

rules.condition: WHEN performing halogen/lithium exchange reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Apply flow microreactor systems to enable generation and control of reactions with fast-reacting electrophilic organolithium species including aryllithiums with cyano, benzyl, pyridyl, oxyranyl, aziridyl, vinyl, propargyl, perfluoroalkyl groups and alkoxysulfonium ions
rules.reasoning: Flow systems provide precise control over reaction conditions and residence times needed for these reactive species

rules.condition: WHEN designing reaction sequences
rules.recommendation: Consider three types of reaction integration: (a) time and space integration where all components are mixed at once, (b) time integration where components are added at intervals in one pot, and (c) space integration where components are added at different locations in a flow system
rules.reasoning: Different integration strategies offer different advantages - space integration provides wider reagent choice and enables use of unstable intermediates

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing TAC-101 methyl ester analogs with two different silyl groups
rules.recommendation: Use flow-microreactor method instead of conventional batch methods
rules.reasoning: Conventional batch methods make it very difficult to synthesize compounds selectively with two different silyl groups due to dilithiation occurring in the first step. Flow-microreactor method is selective, efficient, versatile, and practical

rules.condition: WHEN performing multistep synthesis in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider both linear integration and convergent integration approaches
rules.reasoning: Linear synthesis is suitable for iterative reactions, including repetition of similar reactions or sets of reactions, and is frequently used for synthesizing peptides and oligosaccharides. Convergent synthesis improves efficiency by synthesizing fragments separately then combining them

rules.condition: WHEN using linear integration for multistep synthesis
rules.recommendation: Be aware that overall yield quickly drops with increase in step number
rules.reasoning: The drawback of linear synthesis is that overall yield decreases as more steps are added in sequence
rules.exceptions: In convergent synthesis, fragments are synthesized separately which improves efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN distinguishing between linear and convergent integration
rules.recommendation: Use the structure of the integrated flow system to identify the synthesis type
rules.reasoning: The two types can be easily distinguished by the structure of the integrated flow system used for the synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN performing three-component coupling with organolithium intermediates
rules.recommendation: Optimize reaction conditions using temperature-residence time mapping
rules.reasoning: Precise optimization of the reaction conditions using temperature-residence time mapping is responsible for the success of three-component coupling

rules.condition: WHEN performing carbolithiation of benzyne with aryllithiums
rules.recommendation: Generate organolithiums separately at different temperatures, then integrate at -70°C
rules.reasoning: Different organolithiums can be separately generated and then integrated. 2-bromophenyllithium decomposes at -30°C to generate benzyne without affecting the functional aryllithium

rules.condition: WHEN performing chemoselective three-component couplings with rapid reactions
rules.recommendation: Use rapid micromixing in flow to achieve desired chemoselectivity at high flow rates
rules.reasoning: Chemoselective nucleophilic reactions of difunctional electrophiles are straightforward if reactivity of one functional group is higher than the other. For very rapid reactions, rapid micromixing offers the desired compound with high selectivity at high flow rates
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for very rapid reactions without proper micromixing

rules.condition: WHEN performing Pd-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in integrated flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use Pd catalysts containing carbene ligands (PEPPSI-IPr and PEPPSI-SIPr) instead of conventional Pd catalysts
rules.reasoning: Conventional Pd catalysts such as Pd(PPh3)4 and Pd (Pt-Bu3)2 are inadequate. Pd catalysts containing carbene ligands make the cross-coupling reaction much faster than the side reactions, offering the desired products at good yields

rules.condition: WHEN integrating Br/Li exchange and cross-coupling reactions
rules.recommendation: Use three micromixers and three microtube reactors with specific conditions for each step
rules.reasoning: Successful integration requires proper temperature and residence time control at each step
rules.quantitative: M1 and R1 at 0°C with 2.62 s residence time in R1; use 0.05 equiv of PEPPSI-SIPr catalyst in M2

rules.condition: WHEN performing organolithium reactions that produce alkyl halide byproducts
rules.recommendation: Be aware that byproducts like n-BuBr can react with organolithium species and cause side reactions
rules.reasoning: The n-BuBr byproduct reacts with p-methoxyphenyllithium to give p-methoxybutylbenzene. Also, reactions between aryllithiums and alkyl halides complete within minutes at 0°C, which can interfere with desired reactions
rules.exceptions: Problem can be avoided by using two equivalents of tert-butyllithium, though this is impractical for large-scale production

rules.condition: WHEN using t-BuLi in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Avoid using excess t-BuLi for large-scale synthesis due to safety concerns
rules.reasoning: t-BuLi ignites spontaneously in air, even at room temperature, making it impractical for large-scale laboratory synthesis and industrial production
rules.exceptions: May be acceptable for small-scale research applications

rules.condition: WHEN performing cross-coupling reactions after halogen/Li exchange in integrated flow systems
rules.recommendation: Carry out the cross-coupling reaction for 94 s at 50°C
rules.reasoning: This residence time and temperature provides sufficient time for the Pd-catalyzed cross-coupling to occur effectively while maintaining the integrity of the intermediate species

rules.condition: WHEN performing multi-step integrated flow reactions with halogen/Li exchange followed by cross-coupling
rules.recommendation: Complete the overall transformation in less than 2 min
rules.reasoning: Rapid processing minimizes side reactions and decomposition of reactive intermediates

rules.condition: WHEN integrating different reaction types in flow microreactor systems
rules.recommendation: Ensure the desired second reaction is faster than the side reactions of the intermediate
rules.reasoning: This prevents unwanted side reactions and maximizes yield of the desired product

rules.condition: WHEN performing integrated multi-step reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use different temperatures for the first and subsequent reactions as needed
rules.reasoning: Different reaction steps may have different optimal temperature requirements for maximum efficiency
rules.quantitative: Example: 0°C for lithiation (2.6 s), 50°C for cross-coupling (94 s)

rules.condition: WHEN performing lithiation reactions in integrated flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use residence times of 0.083 s to 4.9 s depending on the specific reaction
rules.reasoning: Short residence times are sufficient for fast lithiation reactions and prevent decomposition

rules.condition: WHEN performing borylation reactions in integrated flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use residence times of 2.1 s for borylation steps
rules.reasoning: This provides sufficient time for the borylation reaction to proceed to completion

rules.condition: WHEN performing Suzuki-Miyaura coupling in integrated flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 44 s for the coupling reaction
rules.reasoning: This allows the cross-coupling reaction to proceed to high conversion (up to 97%)
rules.quantitative: 44 s residence time, up to 97% yield

rules.condition: WHEN performing anionic polymerization of living polymers in flow
rules.recommendation: Avoid using polar solvents such as THF at temperatures around -78°C or lower
rules.reasoning: Living polymer ends decompose readily in polar solvents, and maintaining such low temperatures is environmentally and economically burdensome

rules.condition: WHEN performing anionic polymerization of alkyl methacrylates in flow microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use controlled temperatures: MMA at -28°C, BuMA at 0°C, t-BuMA at 24°C
rules.reasoning: Precise temperature control enables successful polymerization with controlled molecular weight distribution under more accessible temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow anionic polymerization from laboratory to industrial scale
rules.recommendation: Continuously run the reaction for extended periods - about 1 kg of polymer can be produced over 3 h reaction time
rules.reasoning: Flow microreactor systems allow easy scale-up by continuous operation, achieving high molecular weight (Mn = 8,000) with narrow distribution (Mw/Mn = 1.1)
rules.quantitative: 1 kg polymer in 3 h, Mn = 8,000, Mw/Mn = 1.1

rules.condition: WHEN performing sequential anionic polymerization with residence time control
rules.recommendation: Use residence times of 0.825-2.95 s for the first polymerization step
rules.reasoning: This residence time range allows control over the degree of polymerization while preventing significant decomposition of living polymer ends

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing residence time for sequential anionic polymerization to minimize decomposition
rules.recommendation: Use 2.95 s for MMA and 0.825 s for BuMA in the first reactor
rules.reasoning: These specific residence times allow sequential polymerization without significant decomposition of the living polymer end

rules.condition: WHEN controlling molecular weight in anionic polymerization
rules.recommendation: Adjust residence time in the first reactor with fixed flow rate to control molecular weight distribution
rules.reasoning: Longer residence time increases molecular weight but also increases polydispersity due to decomposition
rules.quantitative: Example: tR = 2.95 s gives Mn = 8600, Mw/Mn = 1.29; tR = 5.89 s gives Mn = 8800, Mw/Mn = 1.30

rules.condition: WHEN designing a flow reactor system
rules.recommendation: Understand the role of each individual component and how they can be combined to deliver the required reaction conditions
rules.reasoning: Flow reactor design requires understanding component integration just like batch reactors, but with different spatial arrangements

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between mixers for flow operations
rules.recommendation: Deploy different mixers (flow) or stirrers (batch) depending on the miscibility and viscosity of the reacting fluids (liquid or gas)
rules.reasoning: Physical properties of reactants dictate the appropriate mixing technology

rules.condition: WHEN designing continuous-flow processes
rules.recommendation: Uncouple and spatially separate mixing, heating, cooling, and quenching operations rather than integrating them in the same reaction volume
rules.reasoning: This affords the chemist greater control and flexibility to potentially fine-tune each individual step toward a desired outcome

rules.condition: WHEN handling solid particles (e.g., catalyst particles) in flow
rules.recommendation: Handle as slurry in a packed bed but be aware that clogging of reaction channels can be an issue
rules.reasoning: Solids can cause blockages in narrow flow channels

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with gas-liquid reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use vapor-liquid-pressure diagrams to control the phases of a reaction
rules.reasoning: Phase control is critical for gas-liquid reactions to ensure proper contact

rules.condition: WHEN selecting materials for flow reactor construction
rules.recommendation: Ensure chemical compatibility of materials for the wettable parts, with solvents being especially important
rules.reasoning: Material compatibility prevents degradation and contamination of the reaction

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with gases in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Decide whether the gas will be dissolved in the solvent (pre-saturate) or remain in a different phase (segmented flow, bubble column, etc.)
rules.reasoning: Gas handling strategy affects mass transfer and reaction efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN considering flow chemistry for reactions
rules.recommendation: Note that slow reactions do not generally benefit from flow
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry advantages are most apparent for fast reactions where precise control is needed

rules.condition: WHEN selecting mixers for flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Base the choice of mixers on the reaction kinetics
rules.reasoning: Mixing efficiency requirements depend on how fast the reaction proceeds

rules.condition: WHEN designing exothermic or photochemistry reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Maximize surface-to-volume ratio of the reactor for better heat management/photon flux
rules.reasoning: Higher surface area improves heat transfer and light penetration

rules.condition: WHEN elevated temperatures and/or pressure are involved in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use a back-pressure regulator (BPR) to maintain a steady flow, which is critical for controlling residence times
rules.reasoning: BPR ensures consistent flow rates and residence times under varying conditions

rules.condition: WHEN using gas-liquid reactions, reactions in supercritical CO2, or reactions under inert atmosphere
rules.recommendation: Use a BPR to control and maintain the requisite vapor-liquid equilibrium
rules.reasoning: BPR is obligatory for maintaining proper phase equilibrium in these systems

rules.condition: WHEN the reaction operates above the boiling point of solvents or reagents
rules.recommendation: Use a BPR to maintain proper phase control
rules.reasoning: Prevents unwanted phase changes that would disrupt the reaction

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pumps for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider that pump choice may influence the selection of BPR, as all pumps have an effective upper limit to the backpressure they can work against
rules.reasoning: Pump and BPR must be compatible for system operation

rules.condition: WHEN using piston pumps with insufficient back-pressure
rules.recommendation: Avoid piston pumps as they will deliver inaccurate or variable flow rates when insufficient back-pressure is applied
rules.reasoning: Piston pumps are designed to work against back pressure for accurate flow delivery

rules.condition: WHEN carrying out reactions with two or more reagents in separate streams
rules.recommendation: Use additional pumps and mixers to ensure good contact between the reagents, particularly if they are of different viscosities or immiscible phases
rules.reasoning: Multiple streams require proper mixing for effective reaction

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with exo- or endothermic processes in flow
rules.recommendation: Cool or heat the reactor to dissipate excess heat or provide energy to initiate and maintain the reaction
rules.reasoning: Temperature control is essential for reaction management and safety

rules.condition: WHEN heating or cooling reaction zones in laboratory settings
rules.recommendation: Use traditional oil bath/oven for heating or ice-bath for subambient temperatures
rules.reasoning: Simple, effective temperature control methods for lab-scale operations

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pumps for continuous-flow experiments in synthetic laboratory
rules.recommendation: Use displacement method pumps (syringe, piston, or gear pumps) as the primary choice for flow chemistry applications
rules.reasoning: Most pumping technologies employed in synthetic laboratory for continuous-flow experiments are based on the displacement method

rules.condition: WHEN requiring very low flow rates with high precision
rules.recommendation: Use syringe pumps for microliter range flow rates
rules.reasoning: Syringe pumps provide constant and pulsation-free flow at very low flow rates with high precision, typically few microliters to milliliter per minute
rules.quantitative: Few microliters to milliliter per minute

rules.condition: WHEN requiring high-pressure applications with syringe pumps
rules.recommendation: Use specialized syringe pumps that can deliver liquids at high pressures up to a few hundred bar
rules.reasoning: While many laboratory syringe pumps are only designed for low-pressure applications, specialized versions are available for high-pressure work
rules.quantitative: Up to a few hundred bar
rules.exceptions: Standard laboratory syringe pumps are only for low-pressure applications

rules.condition: WHEN needing to overcome limited reservoir capacity and lack of mixing possibilities
