rules.recommendation: Deploy dual syringe pump systems consisting of two electronically driven syringes programmed to work synchronously
rules.reasoning: One syringe can be recharging fluid from reservoir while the other delivers fluid into reaction zone, or combine with switch valves for multi-point delivery

rules.condition: WHEN working with piston pumps in continuous-flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use piston pumps for high-pressure regimes up to ~140 bar
rules.reasoning: Piston pumps work at high-pressure regimes and are a popular choice for continuous-flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN requiring even higher pressures than standard piston pumps
rules.recommendation: Use plunger pumps made from steel, stainless steel, nickel-molybdenum alloys (Hastelloy), or ceramics for pressures up to 2,070 bar
rules.reasoning: These materials provide good resistance against corrosive chemical reagents and can achieve much higher pressures

rules.condition: WHEN experiencing pulsating flow with piston pumps
rules.recommendation: For very low flow rates, prefer syringe pumps over piston pumps
rules.reasoning: Piston pumps generate pulsating flow, and pulsation may still be problematic at low flow rates despite employing two pistons to smooth the flow

rules.condition: WHEN processing highly viscous materials
rules.recommendation: Avoid piston/plunger pumps as processing is difficult
rules.reasoning: The mechanism of piston/plunger pumps makes processing of highly viscous materials difficult

rules.condition: WHEN processing slurries or suspensions
rules.recommendation: Avoid piston/plunger pumps as processing is difficult or impossible
rules.reasoning: Particulates impede the operation of most nonreturn check valves in piston/plunger pumps

rules.condition: WHEN using piston/plunger pumps
rules.recommendation: Avoid airlocks at all costs as they can disrupt flow
rules.reasoning: Airlocks in piston/plunger pumps can disrupt the flow and should be avoided

rules.condition: WHEN handling gas-liquid mixtures
rules.recommendation: Use diaphragm pumps with reciprocating flexing membrane and Teflon membranes for aggressive chemicals
rules.reasoning: Diaphragm pumps are suitable for gas-liquid mixtures and Teflon membranes provide chemical resistance
rules.exceptions: Working pressure is typically lower than piston/plunger pumps due to compressed air drive

rules.condition: WHEN using gear pumps for continuous-flow processes
rules.recommendation: Use gear pumps for pressures up to ~200 bar, particularly suitable for viscous fluids
rules.reasoning: Gear pumps can work at pressures up to ~200 bar and are particularly suitable for transfer of viscous fluids

rules.condition: WHEN working with highly corrosive liquids
rules.recommendation: Design gear pumps to withstand highly corrosive liquids
rules.reasoning: Gear pumps can be designed to withstand highly corrosive liquids

rules.condition: WHEN handling slurries and viscous liquids
rules.recommendation: Use peristaltic pumps as they are particularly suitable for these applications
rules.reasoning: Peristaltic pump closely resembles gastrointestinal movement and is particularly suitable for handling slurries and viscous liquids

rules.condition: WHEN needing versatile chemical compatibility
rules.recommendation: Use peristaltic pumps as they offer the most versatile chemical compatibility
rules.reasoning: Only the tubing contacts the fluid, and tubing is available in wide range of materials and dimensions, making changing between applications trivial and cheap

rules.condition: WHEN pressure requirements are low
rules.recommendation: Consider peristaltic pumps, but note they deliver quite low pressure (<10 bar) compared to other pump types
rules.reasoning: The pressure delivered by peristaltic pumps is quite low in comparison with other pumps described

rules.condition: WHEN controlling fluid flow to set points in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use mass-flow controllers (MFC) with thermal sensors consisting of two resistance thermometers wrapped around a small tube
rules.reasoning: MFCs measure and control flow of liquids and gases by detecting temperature difference created when gas flows through heated tube
rules.quantitative: Temperature range 2-4°C for heating the tube
rules.exceptions: Do not confuse with BPRs (back-pressure regulators)

rules.condition: WHEN needing precise pressure maintenance under controlled fluid flow conditions
rules.recommendation: Use back-pressure regulators (BPRs) which are nonreturn valves for upstream pressure control
rules.reasoning: BPRs are essential for gas-liquid reactions, gas evolution during reaction, and maintaining volatile reactants in liquid phase when reaction temperature exceeds boiling point

rules.condition: WHEN needing simple and cheap pressure control for laboratory-scale continuous-flow operations
rules.recommendation: Use fixed or static BPRs with nonreturn check valve with fixed set point
rules.reasoning: Being the simplest and cheapest type, they maintain upstream pressure just below the limit by opening to release excess pressure

rules.condition: WHEN needing manual or electronic control of working pressures
rules.recommendation: Use variable or adjustable BPRs with spring-loaded pressure relief valves or dome-loaded devices
rules.reasoning: These allow varying the pressure at which the valve opens and offer working pressures up to ~1,034 bar

rules.condition: WHEN operating back pressure regulators (BPR)
rules.recommendation: Operate at pressures up to 689 bar (10,000 psi)
rules.reasoning: BPRs can handle high pressures and can be constructed entirely using polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) or Hastelloy for excellent chemical compatibility

rules.condition: WHEN constructing back pressure regulators for chemical compatibility
rules.recommendation: Use polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) or Hastelloy materials
rules.reasoning: These materials offer excellent chemical compatibility for flow chemistry applications

rules.condition: WHEN heating flow reactors with water bath
rules.recommendation: Use water bath temperatures up to 95°C
rules.reasoning: Water baths provide common approach for heating reactor tubes, coils, or chips

rules.condition: WHEN heating flow reactors with oil bath
rules.recommendation: Use oil bath temperatures up to ~250°C for precise temperature control
rules.reasoning: Oil baths can achieve higher temperatures than water baths while maintaining precise control with thermostats

rules.condition: WHEN cooling flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use ice-salt mixtures down to -20°C, or liquid N2/dry ice with solvents down to -78°C
rules.reasoning: These cooling methods provide effective temperature control for low-temperature reactions
rules.quantitative: down to -20°C (ice-salt), down to -78°C (liquid N2/dry ice)

rules.condition: WHEN requiring cryostatic conditions for experiments lasting several hours
rules.recommendation: Use electronically controlled refrigerated circulators (e.g., Julabo, Huber, Heidolph) down to -95°C
rules.reasoning: These provide consistent cooling at cryostatic conditions and are good for in-house reactor setups despite being bulky and requiring circulatory cooling liquid

rules.condition: WHEN using HPLC column thermostats for temperature control
rules.recommendation: Achieve temperature range from -10 to 200°C without cooling fluids using Peltier elements
rules.reasoning: HPLC column thermostats allow very precise temperature maintenance and temperature programming (ramping and holding times)

rules.condition: WHEN using GC ovens for heating/cooling
rules.recommendation: Utilize temperature range from -80 to 450°C depending on manufacturer
rules.reasoning: GC ovens offer much wider temperature range making them the heating/cooling device of choice for in-house continuous-flow reactors, though they have sizeable footprint
rules.exceptions: Occupy significant bench space in synthetic laboratory

rules.condition: WHEN designing reactor connections and tubing
rules.recommendation: Consider thermal resistance of materials to minimize temperature gradients and excessive heat loss through reactor walls
rules.reasoning: Important to maintain consistent temperature throughout the reactor system

rules.condition: WHEN working with microreactors (<1 mm, typically μm scale)
rules.recommendation: Rely on rapid diffusion for mixing due to high surface-to-volume ratio
rules.reasoning: In microreactors, the high surface-to-volume ratio enables mixing to be achieved through rapid diffusion

rules.condition: WHEN working with larger reaction channels where diffusion is less efficient
rules.recommendation: Deploy static (passive) or dynamic (active) mixers, particularly for multiphase reaction mixtures
rules.reasoning: In larger reaction channels, mixing by diffusion is generally less efficient, particularly for multiphase reaction mixtures

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between static and dynamic mixers
rules.recommendation: Use static mixers for energy efficiency and absence of moving parts; use dynamic mixers for highly turbulent flow and longer residence times
rules.reasoning: Static mixers are more energy efficient and safer with no electrical components or moving parts. Dynamic mixers achieve highly turbulent flow (high Reynolds numbers) preferred for longer residence times or highly immiscible liquids

rules.condition: WHEN using Y-piece, T-piece, or cross-piece mixers
rules.recommendation: Mix solutions at angles of 60°, 90° (cross-flow), or 180° (opposing flow) depending on application
rules.reasoning: The extent of mixing depends on dimension, speed (flow rate), and direction of merging fluids

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with miscible fluids with similar viscosities for undemanding applications
rules.recommendation: Use simple union pieces (Y-piece, T-piece, cross-piece) for effective mixing
rules.reasoning: These union pieces can create effective mixing for relatively undemanding applications

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are biphasic/multiphasic or only slow flow rates can be used
rules.recommendation: Deploy additional mixers before the reactor or incorporate mixers into the reaction chamber
rules.reasoning: More demanding reactions require additional mixing to ensure good mixing between reactants or immiscible phases during reaction

rules.condition: WHEN using inert packings for mixing enhancement
rules.recommendation: Pack reactor internal volume with sand, silica gel, silica carbide, or glass beads
rules.reasoning: Packing reduces reactor volume leading to shorter residence time, so void volume determination is important for residence time calculations
rules.exceptions: Important to determine void volume for residence time calculations

rules.condition: WHEN using packed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Determine void volume experimentally by weighing reactor with dry bed and again when saturated with liquid of known density
rules.reasoning: Packing reduces reactor volume, leading to shorter residence time, making void volume determination crucial for accurate residence time calculations

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with very fine particles in packed beds
rules.recommendation: Pass material through a sieve to remove fine particles to avoid clogging
rules.reasoning: Very fine particles can cause clogging or unacceptable pressure buildup in the system

rules.condition: WHEN selecting materials for exothermic reactions or fast flow rates
rules.recommendation: Use metal (titanium, nickel), plastic (PTFE), or ceramic foams as alternatives to packed bed reactors
rules.reasoning: The porosity and rigidity of these foams can be optimized to afford high surface area to volume for efficient heat transfer while preventing unacceptable pressure drops

rules.condition: WHEN using structured mixers for precise flow control
rules.recommendation: Place structured static mixers (wave, spiral, baffles) directly inside reactor tube to achieve excellent radial mixing while maintaining plug-like axial flow profile
rules.reasoning: Structured mixers provide more precise control of flow patterns compared to particles and foams

rules.condition: WHEN using gas-liquid contactors (bubble column reactors)
rules.recommendation: Use porous media (frit or microfiltration membrane) made of titanium, stainless steel, or PTFE to generate small gas bubbles
rules.reasoning: Creates large interfacial contact between gas and liquid phases, providing good mixing and heat transfer with precise control of bubble size through pore size and gas flow rate

rules.condition: WHEN achieving turbulent flows in microchannels is impossible
rules.recommendation: Modify reaction channel structure into twisted pipes to induce radial mixing through centrifugal forces
rules.reasoning: As fluid flows around bends, pressure differential from centrifugal forces causes molecules to move toward outside of bend, creating radial mixing
rules.quantitative: high Reynolds numbers required for turbulent flow

rules.condition: WHEN designing split-and-recombine (SAR) systems
rules.recommendation: Continually separate and merge reaction mixture to achieve chaotic flow
rules.reasoning: This approach enhances radial mixing of viscous liquids while allowing high surface area for effective heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN designing continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTRs) for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use small internal volumes (e.g., 2 mL) to achieve efficient mixing and maintain plug flow-like processing
rules.reasoning: Small internal volumes allow efficient mixing to be achieved and create a more uniform, plug flow-like processing environment
rules.quantitative: 2 mL internal volume for lab-scale CSTR

rules.condition: WHEN controlling mixing in continuous stirred tank reactors
rules.recommendation: Control mixing by varying revolutions per minute (RPM) of the magnetic stir bar
rules.reasoning: Magnetic stirring provides controllable agitation similar to flask-based synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up CSTR operations for better residence time distribution
rules.recommendation: Use a larger number of lower volume reactors instead of a single CSTR of the same combined volume
rules.reasoning: Multiple smaller reactors give a better residence time distribution than a single large CSTR
rules.quantitative: Multiple units vs single combined volume

rules.condition: WHEN implementing oscillatory flow for enhanced mixing
rules.recommendation: Use time-pulsating flow with pressure gradients to achieve back-mixing and introduce constrictions (baffle disks with orifices) along the tube
rules.reasoning: Oscillating pump creates eddies on upstroke and downstroke, creating highly turbulent flow for enhanced mixing
rules.quantitative: Time-pulsating flow with baffle disk orifices

rules.condition: WHEN fabricating microfluidic reactors
rules.recommendation: Use transparent materials such as acrylic, glass, silicon, or polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) for micro-structured channels
rules.reasoning: These materials allow for etching or molding of microchannels and provide optical transparency for monitoring

rules.condition: WHEN designing microfluidic reactors for multiple operations
rules.recommendation: Integrate multiple units of operation (mixing, heating/cooling, quenching, product extraction) in uninterrupted continuous flow using 'lab on a chip' technique
rules.reasoning: Integration allows for complete reaction processes without interruption and efficient use of space

rules.condition: WHEN mixing in narrow microchannels
rules.recommendation: Utilize diffusion in laminar flow for efficient mixing, which cannot be achieved in larger (meso-scale) channels
rules.reasoning: Narrow channels enable efficient mixing through diffusion in laminar flow regime
rules.exceptions: Larger channels cannot achieve this type of mixing

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactors for small-scale applications
rules.recommendation: Use internal volumes as small as picoliters (pL) for applications with very limited material quantities
rules.reasoning: Miniaturized features are highly desirable for bioanalytical sciences, bioassays, and synthetic chemistry optimization with limited material

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactors for heat transfer
rules.recommendation: Utilize the large inner surface area-to-volume ratio (several tens of thousands m² m⁻³) to achieve very high heat-transfer coefficients of up to 20-30,000 W m⁻² K⁻¹
rules.reasoning: High surface area to volume ratio provides very efficient heating/cooling for safe performance of highly exothermic reactions
rules.quantitative: Surface area-to-volume ratio: tens of thousands m² m⁻³; Heat transfer coefficients: 20-30,000 W m⁻² K⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN performing highly exothermic reactions with hazardous and reactive intermediates
rules.recommendation: Use microreactors with high heat-transfer coefficients to perform reactions safely
rules.reasoning: Very efficient heating/cooling allows safe handling of hazardous and reactive intermediates in exothermic reactions
rules.quantitative: Heat transfer coefficients up to 20-30,000 W m⁻² K⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN conducting gas-liquid biphasic reactions requiring high surface area contact
rules.recommendation: Use annular flow regime to afford high surface area contact between gaseous and liquid phases within a microchannel
rules.reasoning: Annular flow provides maximum interfacial contact area beneficial for mass-transfer limited reactions
rules.exceptions: High gas flow rate required to maintain annular flow, which may not be practical for long residence time reactions

rules.condition: WHEN conducting biphasic reactions in microreactors with long residence time requirements
rules.recommendation: Use segmented flow instead of annular flow for biphased reactions
rules.reasoning: Segmented flow is often favored because annular flow requires high gas flow rates that may not be practical for long residence times
rules.exceptions: When high surface area contact is more important than residence time

rules.condition: WHEN controlling contact between two phases in segmented flow
rules.recommendation: Control contact between phases by adjusting the lengths of the gas and liquid slugs
rules.reasoning: Slug length adjustment provides control over phase contact time and mixing characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN designing liquid-liquid segmented flow microchannels
rules.recommendation: Ensure the cross section of each segment is smaller than its length to avoid emulsion formation
rules.reasoning: Proper geometry prevents unwanted emulsion formation during liquid-liquid segmented flow
rules.quantitative: Cross section < length for each segment

rules.condition: WHEN designing liquid-liquid segmented flow microchannels
rules.recommendation: Consider the material used in microchannel construction as it affects interfacial tension and surface energies between the liquid and reactor wall
rules.reasoning: Material choice impacts the physics of segmented flow through interfacial tension and surface energy effects
rules.quantitative: Material-dependent interfacial tension and surface energies

rules.condition: WHEN using PDMS chip reactors
rules.recommendation: Keep pressure drop below 2-3 bar
rules.reasoning: Higher pressure drops can cause strain on reactor walls and fittings, which can be dangerous, and affects gas solubility
rules.quantitative: ≤ 2-3 bar pressure drop for PDMS chip reactors

rules.condition: WHEN reactions involve highly viscous liquids and high flow rates in narrow channels
rules.recommendation: Monitor for problematic pressure drops and implement pressure monitoring
rules.reasoning: Narrow channels are prone to fouling and blockages which can cause dangerous pressure buildups

rules.condition: WHEN there is potential for blockage events in tubular reactors
rules.recommendation: Monitor reactor pressure and configure system to automatically shut down if blockage is detected
rules.reasoning: Clogging can cause dangerous pressure buildups in the system

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up microreactor processes
rules.recommendation: Use reactor stacking (scaling-out) or parallelization (numbering up) of microreactors rather than simple scale-up
rules.reasoning: Achieving even flow distributions between reactors is challenging but necessary for consistent results

rules.condition: WHEN selecting reactor materials for cost-effectiveness
rules.recommendation: Use glass reactors as the cheapest and most popular choice
rules.reasoning: Although other materials like polymers, metals, and ceramics are available, glass offers the best cost-performance ratio
rules.exceptions: When chemical compatibility or extreme conditions require other materials

rules.condition: WHEN using coil reactors with internal diameters ≤2 mm
rules.recommendation: No additional mixers are needed for homogeneous reactions
rules.reasoning: Small internal diameters provide sufficient mixing through the coil geometry

rules.condition: WHEN using 1/16" stainless steel tubing
rules.recommendation: Can withstand pressures up to 12,000 psi (827 bar) depending on wall thickness
rules.reasoning: Stainless steel has high pressure tolerance for high-pressure reactions
rules.quantitative: Up to 12,000 psi (827 bar) for 1/16" stainless steel tubing

rules.condition: WHEN performing reactions at elevated temperatures up to 500°C
rules.recommendation: Use metal coils for good heat transfer with high surface-to-volume ratio
rules.reasoning: Metal coils provide excellent heat transfer capabilities at high temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN using chemically aggressive reagents with extreme pH values
rules.recommendation: Prefer polymer tubings over metal tubings to eliminate leaching of catalytic metals
rules.reasoning: Metal leaching can influence reaction outcomes and contaminate products
rules.exceptions: When high temperature tolerance is required

rules.condition: WHEN using PEEK tubing (1/16")
rules.recommendation: Can withstand up to 6,000 psi (413 bar) pressure but limited to 143°C due to glass transition temperature
rules.reasoning: PEEK offers good chemical and pressure tolerance but has temperature limitations

rules.condition: WHEN designing packed-bed reactors for heterogeneous catalysis
rules.recommendation: Use particle size ratio dp < L/25 and dp < dc/10 where L is bed length and dc is bed diameter
rules.reasoning: Optimal particle size ensures proper mass transfer and avoids excessive pressure drops

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing catalyst performance in packed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Use smaller particles to facilitate better mass transfer due to increased surface area
rules.reasoning: Smaller particles increase surface area and improve mass transfer rates
rules.exceptions: Fine particles can cause unfavorable pressure drops

rules.condition: WHEN setting up fixed-bed reactors in laboratory
rules.recommendation: Fill chromatography column or stainless steel tube with catalyst particles and cap with sinters or filters, pump mobile phase upward to expel dead volumes
rules.reasoning: Upward flow helps eliminate dead volumes and ensures proper catalyst bed packing

rules.condition: WHEN catalyst deactivation occurs in fixed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Regenerate catalyst by calcinating under oxidizing atmosphere for coking issues
rules.reasoning: Calcination can restore catalyst activity by removing carbon deposits
rules.exceptions: Only applicable to certain types of catalyst poisoning

rules.condition: WHEN using solid-phase-supported reagents or inline scavengers in continuous flow
rules.recommendation: Use liquid chromatographic columns (such as those produced by Omnifit) instead of traditional fixed-bed reactors due to the large volume requirements
rules.reasoning: The volume of fixed-bed reactor required for solid-phase-supported reagents or inline scavengers - both employed in stoichiometric amounts, often in excess - needs to be quite large

rules.condition: WHEN designing tube-in-tube reactors for gas-liquid reactions
rules.recommendation: Use Teflon tube made of semipermeable AF-2400 membrane to introduce gaseous reactant through the walls into a concentric tube containing the reactant in liquid phase
rules.reasoning: Achieves rapid gas/liquid contact along the direction of the flow

rules.condition: WHEN operating tube-in-tube reactors
rules.recommendation: Match the rate of gas permeation across the membrane by the rate of its uptake by the liquid (through dissolution and consumption) to avoid formation of gas bubbles
rules.reasoning: Formation of gas bubbles must be avoided to maintain steady state in the reactor and ensure more even distribution of gaseous concentration along the reaction volume

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up falling film reactors
rules.recommendation: Scale out by stacking the reactor layers rather than increase the surface area
rules.reasoning: The geometry of a falling film reactor is characterized by its small thickness and large interfacial area, which inherently places an upper limit on its scalability
rules.quantitative: Reactors with dimensions of up to 1 m exist

rules.condition: WHEN performing highly exothermic biphasic gas-liquid reactions
rules.recommendation: Use falling film reactors to achieve good mass and heat transfer for good selectivity
rules.reasoning: The large interfacial area of falling film reactors provides excellent heat and mass transfer capabilities

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use falling film reactors to allow good transmission of photons across the glass and into the thin film
rules.reasoning: The large surface area of the falling film reactor is highly beneficial for photochemical reactions

rules.condition: WHEN using microwave reactors for single-mode operation
rules.recommendation: Use a frequency of 2.45 GHz (corresponding to wavelength of 12.2 cm) and ensure reactor diameter is narrower than the standing wave
rules.reasoning: This places an upper limit to the diameter of a reactor to achieve effective and homogeneous heating

rules.condition: WHEN needing to overcome diameter limitations in microwave reactors
rules.recommendation: Align several modes along the flow reactor tube to effectively extend the range of microwave irradiation
rules.reasoning: This overcomes the limitation imposed by the standing wave requirement for effective heating

rules.condition: WHEN designing large-scale continuous-flow microwave reactors
rules.recommendation: The largest available reactor can be operated up to 250°C and 30 bar, with productivity up to 2 ton h⁻¹
rules.reasoning: These are the current industrial scale limits for microwave flow reactors
rules.quantitative: Max temperature: 250°C, max pressure: 30 bar, max productivity: 2 ton h⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN constructing simple continuous-flow photoreactors
rules.recommendation: Wrap transparent tubing around a light source
rules.reasoning: This simple design takes advantage of the large surface-area-to-volume ratio afforded by flow reactors for photon delivery

rules.condition: WHEN selecting tubing for compression fittings in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use rigid tubes made of metal or hard polymers (e.g., PEEK, nylon, Teflon, Kynar, or polyethylene) that can resist compression
rules.reasoning: These materials can resist the compression applied by the ferrule to form a tight seal

rules.condition: WHEN selecting fittings for medium pressure applications
rules.recommendation: Use 1/4-28 UNF threaded flat-bottomed connections for 1/8" and 1/16" tubing up to 20-30 bar pressure
rules.reasoning: These are standard for medium pressure applications and widely available
rules.quantitative: Pressure range: up to 20-30 bar, tubing sizes: 1/8", 1/16"

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow systems for high pressure operation
rules.recommendation: Use coned ports instead of flat-bottomed connections for pressures >30 bar
rules.reasoning: Coned ports are generally rated to higher pressures than similarly sized flat-bottomed connections

rules.condition: WHEN selecting standard fittings for HPLC systems
rules.recommendation: Use 10-32 UNF thread standard employed by many HPLC manufacturers
rules.reasoning: This is a common standard for high pressure liquid chromatography systems

rules.condition: WHEN selecting tubing sizes for flow systems
rules.recommendation: Standard HPLC accessories are available for tubes with 1/8", 1/16", and 1/32" OD
rules.reasoning: These are standard sizes with readily available fittings and accessories

rules.condition: WHEN threading compression nuts on fittings
rules.recommendation: Do not under- or over-tighten fittings, follow manufacturer's recommended procedures
rules.reasoning: Improper tightening can result in leaks, and installation of compression fittings differs between manufacturers and designs

rules.condition: WHEN using high-pressure flow reactor applications
rules.recommendation: Exercise extreme caution before intermixing and interchanging tube fittings from different manufacturers
rules.reasoning: Different manufacturers have dimensional and metallurgical differences that affect swaging behavior, making intermixing unsafe

rules.condition: WHEN using 1/4-28 flat-bottomed connections in low to medium pressure applications
rules.recommendation: Most 1/4-28 flat-bottomed connections are generally interchangeable when ferrule and nut are matched
rules.reasoning: Standardization allows interchangeability at lower pressures

rules.condition: WHEN reconnecting fittings multiple times
rules.recommendation: Always inspect fitting components for signs of damage and wear before reconnection, replace ferrules and/or fitting body if there are doubts about condition
rules.reasoning: Ensures leak-tight seal and prevents catastrophic failures

rules.condition: WHEN using polymer tubings in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Replace periodically to avoid catastrophic failures
rules.reasoning: Polymer tubings are generally mechanically less robust than SS tubes and can become brittle over time from exposure to chemical reagents, heat or UV light

rules.condition: WHEN selecting parts for 1/4-28 flat bottomed connections that need frequent disassembly
rules.recommendation: Select two-piece ferrules instead of single-piece ferrules
rules.reasoning: Single-piece 1/4-28 fitting ferrules tend to become stuck to the nut during installation and can result in twisting of tubing with potential to damage tubing wall. Two-piece ferrules have stainless steel locking ring allowing free rotation

rules.condition: WHEN installing pressure regulator in flow system
rules.recommendation: Install pressure regulator at start of system (between pump and reactor) with valve normally opened to regulate inlet pressure
rules.reasoning: Regulates inlet pressure to the system

rules.condition: WHEN installing BPR (Back Pressure Regulator) in flow system
rules.recommendation: Install BPR at end of flow process (usually before collection vessel) with valve normally closed to provide obstruction to flow and regulate upstream pressure
rules.reasoning: Keeps process under pressure and prevents outgassing and solvent evaporation

rules.condition: WHEN using piston pumps in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Install BPRs after pumps to work against pressure and help mechanical action of pump valves, prevent gravity feeding mobile phase through pump faster than intended
rules.reasoning: Helps mechanical action and prevents uncontrolled flow rates

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring flow system pressure
rules.recommendation: Install pressure sensor between pump and BPR, and include pressure sensor before end BPR to monitor pressure drop
rules.reasoning: Monitoring pump outlet helps troubleshoot pump faults like air locks and valve failures; pressure drop monitoring detects system blockages and leakages

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pump type for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider pressure range: Syringe (typically low, specialized can go very high), Peristaltic (low to medium, typically <10 bar), Piston (low to very high)
rules.reasoning: Different pump types have different pressure capabilities that affect reactor design
rules.exceptions: Specialized syringe pumps can achieve very high pressure

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pump type for flow rate requirements
rules.recommendation: Consider flow rate capabilities: Syringe (very low to medium), Peristaltic (medium to very high), Piston (low to high)
rules.reasoning: Different pump types have different flow rate capabilities

rules.condition: WHEN needing to dispense small volumes
rules.recommendation: Use syringe pumps which are good for small volumes down to picoliters
rules.reasoning: Syringe pumps excel at precise small volume dispensing

rules.condition: WHEN needing to move high volumes of liquid
rules.recommendation: Use peristaltic pumps which are good for moving high volumes of liquid
rules.reasoning: Peristaltic pumps excel at high volume liquid handling

rules.condition: WHEN considering chemical compatibility of pumps
rules.recommendation: Syringe pumps have good tolerance for most chemicals; Peristaltic pumps depend on tubing used (chemically resistant formulations available but expensive); Piston pumps have more wetted parts requiring chemical compatibility checks
rules.reasoning: Different pump types have different chemical exposure profiles
rules.exceptions: Specialized piston pumps with chemically resistant components are available

rules.condition: WHEN considering pump maintenance costs
rules.recommendation: Syringe and peristaltic pumps have low maintenance costs; piston pumps have high maintenance costs
rules.reasoning: Different pump types have different maintenance requirements and associated costs

rules.condition: WHEN handling fluids of different viscosities
rules.recommendation: Syringe pumps handle low-to-high viscosity liquids; peristaltic pumps are good for moving undissolved solids; piston pumps find viscous fluids challenging
rules.reasoning: Different pump mechanisms have different capabilities with fluid properties

rules.condition: WHEN performing regular pump maintenance
rules.recommendation: Perform regular maintenance including cleaning of check valves, replacement of gaskets and O-rings, and periodic recalibration
rules.reasoning: Pump failure can be expensive and potentially dangerous, especially where accurate stoichiometric control is required for chemical safety reasons

rules.condition: WHEN setting up telescoped processes with multiple reaction steps
rules.recommendation: Connect different reactors sequentially, particularly when different temperatures are required for each step
rules.reasoning: Allows direct introduction of reaction mixture from one reactor to another without intermediate purification
rules.exceptions: Can be achieved within one microfluidic device in some cases

rules.condition: WHEN adding reagents or solvents in telescoped processes
rules.recommendation: Consider that volumetric flow is additive - when two or more reactants are combined, resultant flow rate will be sum of individual pump flow rates
rules.reasoning: Combined flows result in shortened residence time and diluted reaction mixture
rules.quantitative: Resultant flow rate = sum of individual flow rates

rules.condition: WHEN reaction mixture becomes diluted in telescoped processes
rules.recommendation: Compensate by lengthening the second reactor (increase residence time) or increasing reaction temperature (to accelerate reaction rate), or introduce means of removing solvents
rules.reasoning: Dilution from added solutions must be compensated to maintain reaction efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN determining reactor volumes before flow experiments
rules.recommendation: Account for both reactor volume and associated dead volumes in fittings and connecting tubings
rules.reasoning: Dead volume affects total system volume and residence time calculations
rules.quantitative: Dead volume = total volume (end to end) - reactor volume

rules.condition: WHEN calculating flow rate and residence time
rules.recommendation: Use relationship: flow rate (volume per unit time) = reactor volume / residence time
rules.reasoning: Fundamental relationship for determining when steady state is reached and for productivity calculations
rules.quantitative: flow rate = reactor volume / residence time

rules.condition: WHEN determining when steady state is reached
rules.recommendation: Wait for residence time of reactor before product collection can commence
rules.reasoning: Ensures output has reached steady state composition

rules.condition: WHEN measuring residence time distribution for custom reactors
rules.recommendation: Introduce known tracer at reactor inlet (at t = 0) and monitor concentration at outlet over time
rules.reasoning: Necessary to understand flow characteristics of the device

rules.condition: WHEN testing solubility limits before flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Test solubility limits of reactant, reagent, product(s), and side-product(s) in reaction solvent(s) to prevent solid precipitate formation that can cause blockages
rules.reasoning: Solid formation can cause system blockages

rules.condition: WHEN testing flow conditions before full reaction
rules.recommendation: Perform small scale batch reaction using feed stock solutions proposed for flow system to check for unexpected solid formation, use batch microwave reactor for testing potential superheated flow conditions
rules.reasoning: Identifies potential issues before committing to flow system

rules.condition: WHEN testing flow system safety
rules.recommendation: Test for loose fittings and connections by pumping nontoxic liquid (water or 2-propanol) through system, leak test gaseous reactant fittings using soapy water
rules.reasoning: Safety testing prevents leaks during actual reaction

rules.condition: WHEN priming flow reactor system
rules.recommendation: Fill reactor system with reaction solvent before introducing reactants, adjust temperature and pressure to set values and allow equilibration before synchronized introduction of reactants
rules.reasoning: Gas pockets can prevent system from reaching stable back-pressure and ensures proper initial conditions

rules.condition: WHEN reaction requires specific conditions to initiate
rules.recommendation: Some reactions can only be initiated under certain conditions within reactor device (heating/cooling, ultrasonication, microwave radiation, or catalyst contact)
rules.reasoning: Certain reactions have specific initiation requirements that differ from simple mixing
rules.exceptions: Some reactions initiate with simple mixing in T-piece

rules.condition: WHEN determining steady state in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: May require a few reactor volumes of reaction mixture to pass through system before consistent output is attained, estimate collection point from total system volume or use in-line analytical technique or fraction collector
rules.reasoning: Ensures consistent product composition before collection

rules.condition: WHEN performing post-reaction workup in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Implement in-line purification techniques including pre-packed columns with scavengers, phase separation for extraction, crystallization, or automated column chromatography for continuous purification
rules.reasoning: Product purification procedures can be performed automatically in continuous flow, eliminating manual workup steps

rules.condition: WHEN shutting down a flow chemistry system after reaction completion
rules.recommendation: Flush the system with reaction solvent as it returns to ambient temperature before releasing pressure, then rinse with organic solvent such as ethanol or 2-propanol before disassembly
rules.reasoning: Ensures safe depressurization and cleaning of the system to prevent hazards during maintenance
rules.quantitative: Return to ambient temperature before pressure release
rules.exceptions: If system is not required for further use

rules.condition: WHEN using Labtrix microreactor systems for liquid phase reactions
rules.recommendation: Use glass micro reactors with syringe pumps for reagent dosing and Peltier thermal control with temperature range of -20 to 195°C
rules.reasoning: System designed for research and optimization of liquid phase reactions with precise thermal and flow control
rules.quantitative: Temperature range: -20 to 195°C, reactor volumes: 1-9.5 μL

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between commercial flow reactors and home-made setups

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pump types for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use piston pumps for HPLC equipment compatibility with flow rates up to 10 mL min⁻¹, but avoid with insoluble materials. Use peristaltic pumps for handling light slurries with good flow rate range but limited maximum pressures. Use syringe pumps for accurate low flow rates (μL min⁻¹) but with limited high-pressure tolerance and volume capacity.
rules.reasoning: Different pump types have specific capabilities and limitations regarding pressure tolerance, flow rate accuracy, and material compatibility
rules.quantitative: Piston pumps: up to 10 mL min⁻¹; Syringe pumps: μL min⁻¹ flow rates
rules.exceptions: Piston pumps should not be used with insoluble materials; syringe pumps require dual fashion operation for continuous flow

rules.condition: WHEN selecting tubing materials for flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Choose from fluorinated polymers (PFA, FEP, PTFE), polyether ether ketone (PEEK), metals (stainless steel, copper), or glass with typical diameters 1/16–1/4 inch, based on application compatibility (corrosive chemicals, temperature, pressure) and cost.
rules.reasoning: Different tubing materials have varying chemical compatibility, temperature tolerance, and pressure ratings
rules.exceptions: Selection depends on specific chemical compatibility requirements

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time (tRes) for flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Calculate residence time by dividing reactor volume (mL) by flow rate (mL min⁻¹). For mixed streams, divide total reactor volume by combined flow rate (sum of all individual flow rates).
rules.reasoning: Residence time determines how long the reaction mixture spends in the reactor, affecting conversion and selectivity
rules.exceptions: For telescoped multistep sequences, residence time in later steps depends on combined flow rates from prior processes

rules.condition: WHEN designing telescoped multistep flow sequences
rules.recommendation: Choose reactor volumes appropriately - use small volume for fast first reactor and large volume for slow second reactor to accommodate different residence time requirements.
rules.reasoning: A slow second step cannot be easily accommodated after a rapid first step unless reactor volumes are chosen appropriately
rules.quantitative: Example given: 5 mL reactor (tRes = 2.5 min, fast step) followed by 50 mL reactor (tRes = 16.67 min, slow step)

rules.condition: WHEN comparing flow vs batch reaction times
rules.recommendation: Expect flow reactions to have relatively short residence times (<1 h) compared to equivalent batch transformations (several hours) due to faster kinetics from better mass transfer.
rules.reasoning: Better mass transfer in flow (mixing of reactants and reagents/catalysts) results in faster kinetics
rules.quantitative: Flow: <1 h residence time; Batch: several hours
rules.exceptions: Total processing time for larger volumes can still be hours during scale-up runs

rules.condition: WHEN achieving mixing in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use small-diameter T- or Y-pieces, static mixers, serpentine channels, or patterned channel surfaces (often in microchips) to generate significant turbulence and homogenize different streams.
rules.reasoning: These devices create turbulence needed for effective mixing of reactant and reagent streams for faster reactions
rules.quantitative: Reynolds number determines laminar vs turbulent flow regime
rules.exceptions: Better mixing in flow regimes is independent of reaction scale, whereas batch processes suffer from ineffective mixing as vessel dimension increases

rules.condition: WHEN performing cryogenic reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Utilize the large surface-to-volume ratio of flow reactors for effective heat dissipation. Flow reactors enable higher temperatures than batch (e.g., -30°C in flow vs -78°C in batch lithiations).
rules.reasoning: Large surface-to-volume ratio allows effective dissipation of heat released, improving safety and control while reducing energy consumption
rules.quantitative: -30°C in flow vs -78°C in batch for lithiations

rules.condition: WHEN performing endergonic reactions requiring heating
rules.recommendation: Take advantage that only small reactor volume needs heating. Use back-pressure regulators to superheat low-boiling solvents at temperatures far exceeding atmospheric boiling point.
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratio enables effective heat input with small volumes, and back-pressure regulation allows use of superheated solvents
rules.quantitative: Can use solvents at >150°C when atmospheric bp is lower (see Table 4.1)
rules.exceptions: Prevents use of unfavorable high-boiling solvents like xylene, dichlorobenzene, NMP

rules.condition: WHEN using pressurized solvents in flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use back-pressure regulators to enable low-boiling solvents at temperatures exceeding their atmospheric boiling points. This favors greener solvents over high-boiling alternatives.
rules.reasoning: Pressurization allows use of environmentally friendlier solvents that are easier to separate after reaction
rules.quantitative: MeCN: 82°C at 1 bar → 149°C at 10 bar → 183°C at 25 bar; THF: 66°C at 1 bar → 126°C at 10 bar → 157°C at 25 bar

rules.condition: WHEN starting a continuous flow process
rules.recommendation: Allow the flow system to reach steady state before collecting reaction product by pumping 1-2 reactor volumes of substrate solutions through the system.
rules.reasoning: Various variables (flow rate, temperature, pressure) affect the flow process and system needs stabilization to run continuously without fouling

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring flow processes
rules.recommendation: Use in-line analysis tools combined with suitable software to capture real-time data and report issues, allowing quick intervention if necessary.
rules.reasoning: Real-time monitoring enables immediate response to process deviations and maintains process control

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with solubility in flow processes
rules.recommendation: Pay close attention to solubility issues in flow processes, as heterogeneous catalysts in packed-bed reactors use 'contact time' highlighting substrate-reagent/catalyst contact importance.
rules.reasoning: Solubility issues can affect reaction efficiency and contact time between substrates and catalysts/reagents, especially in packed-bed systems

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow processes with insoluble starting materials or precipitation of products
rules.recommendation: Avoid inappropriately high concentration of solutions to prevent fouling within flow reactors due to blockages in microchannels, connectors, and back-pressure regulators
rules.reasoning: Insoluble starting materials or precipitation of products will lead to fouling within flow reactors due to blockages in microchannels, connectors, and back-pressure regulators
rules.exceptions: Solutions that avoid clogging and fouling are available to experienced experimentalists using peristaltic pumps, agitation, optimized solvents, and temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN starting flow chemistry process development
rules.recommendation: Conduct initial experiments in batch to evaluate solubility, reactivity, and stoichiometry for a given reaction before attempting this in continuous flow mode
rules.reasoning: Initial batch experiments help establish baseline conditions and understanding before translating to continuous flow

rules.condition: WHEN developing multistep flow sequences
rules.recommendation: Break down multistep sequences and integrate in-line purification aids as the process develops
rules.reasoning: This approach helps manage complexity and ensures compatibility between process steps

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow processes for downstream operations
rules.recommendation: Keep in mind all parameters that will affect downstream operations (quenching, additional transformations, purification, etc.) to ascertain compatibility of the intermediate material with subsequent reagents
rules.reasoning: Must consider compatibility of solvents, spent reagents, side-products with subsequent reagents as well as increasing dilution and flow rates

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing homogeneous flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Assess the solubility of all components (substrates, intermediates, products including side products) within the relevant temperature window
rules.reasoning: Solubility assessment is crucial for successful homogeneous flow reactions to avoid precipitation and blockages

rules.condition: WHEN performing difluorocyclopropanation reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use THF as solvent with concentration of 0.9 M for optimal results
rules.reasoning: Solvent optimization in flow indicated that THF was the most suitable, matching batch conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing difluorocyclopropanation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use only NaI as initiator for formation of difluorocarbene
rules.reasoning: The choice of initiator was crucial, with only NaI initiating the formation of difluorocarbene

rules.condition: WHEN performing difluorocyclopropanation reactions
rules.recommendation: Heat THF solution to 120°C for optimal reaction acceleration
rules.reasoning: Superheating of the THF solution to 120°C significantly accelerated the reaction

rules.condition: WHEN performing difluorocyclopropanation reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Operate at 8 bar back-pressure to allow for superheating
rules.reasoning: 8 bar back-pressure allows for superheating of the solvent system

rules.condition: WHEN performing difluorocyclopropanation reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use a residence time of 10 minutes for optimal conversion
rules.reasoning: 10 minutes residence time yielded the desired product in 94% isolated yield

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up difluorocyclopropanation reactions
rules.recommendation: Modify conditions slightly: use 2.5 equiv. TMS-CF3 and 0.06 equiv. NaI for multigram scale
rules.reasoning: Conditions were modified slightly for scale-up to reach productivity of 27.4 mmol h⁻¹
rules.quantitative: 2.5 equiv. TMS-CF3, 0.06 equiv. NaI, productivity of 27.4 mmol h⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation of alcohols in flow
rules.recommendation: Use Cu(OTf) (5 mol%), TEMPO (5 mol%), N-methylimidazole (NMI, 10 mol%) and bipyridine (bpy, 5 mol%) as catalyst system
rules.reasoning: This catalyst system provides potentially faster reactions, lower cost, lower toxicity, and higher substrate tolerance compared to Pd-based approaches
rules.quantitative: Cu(OTf): 5 mol%, TEMPO: 5 mol%, NMI: 10 mol%, bpy: 5 mol%

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation of alcohols with oxygen gas
rules.recommendation: Use 35 bar pressure for optimal conversion
rules.reasoning: Initial batch tests revealed suitable conditions based on 35 bar pressure and temperatures of 60-100°C

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation of activated alcohols
rules.recommendation: Use temperature of 100°C for activated substrates
rules.reasoning: Elevated temperature (100°C) provided high yields for several activated substrates
rules.exceptions: Non-activated substrates showed significant amounts of decomposition at these conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation of non-activated alcohols
rules.recommendation: Lower temperature to 60°C for non-activated substrates
rules.reasoning: Non-activated substrates showed significant amounts of decomposition at higher temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation of activated alcohols
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 5 minutes for activated substrates
rules.reasoning: Short residence time of 5 minutes provided high yields for several activated substrates

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation of non-activated alcohols
rules.recommendation: Extend residence time to 30-45 minutes for non-activated substrates
rules.reasoning: Longer residence times were needed to achieve good conversion for non-activated substrates at lower temperatures

rules.condition: WHEN using Cu(OTf) catalyst with stainless steel components
rules.recommendation: Avoid using stainless steel syringes as they cause deactivation of Cu(OTf) catalyst; use HPLC pump with inert PTFE tubing instead
rules.reasoning: Appreciable deactivation of the Cu(OTf) catalyst was observed when using stainless steel syringes
rules.exceptions: Keep second stream (substrate in MeCN) in stainless-steel syringe as before

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid aerobic oxidations
rules.recommendation: Use slug flow regime with interspersing gas and liquid segments passing through heated tubular reactor followed by vapor-liquid separator and expansion chamber
rules.reasoning: This setup allows for safe handling of gas-liquid biphasic systems with proper separation and venting

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up aerobic oxidation to 100g scale
rules.recommendation: Reduce catalyst loading to 2.5 mol% Cu(OTf), 5 mol% bpy, 5 mol% NMI and 0.25 mol% TEMPO in combination with 4 M substrate concentration
rules.reasoning: These modified conditions resulted in a robust process operated for over 24 h with near quantitative yield
rules.quantitative: 2.5 mol% Cu(OTf), 5 mol% bpy, 5 mol% NMI, 0.25 mol% TEMPO, 4 M substrate concentration

rules.condition: WHEN performing precipitation reactions in flow that can cause fouling and clogging
rules.recommendation: Use Coflore ATR flow reactor with internal static mixers and constant transverse agitation at specific frequency (4 Hz) to prevent sedimentation
rules.reasoning: Static mixers provide excellent mixing while constant agitation prevents solid product sedimentation that would otherwise cause reactor fouling

rules.condition: WHEN performing deacetylation reactions requiring hot concentrated sulfuric acid (>110°C)
rules.recommendation: Use flow coils (52 mL each) at 130°C with 13 min residence time, followed by immediate quenching with water at 10°C
rules.reasoning: Flow provides superior heat transfer for safe handling of hot concentrated sulfuric acid and enables reproducible performance upon scale-up
rules.quantitative: 130°C reaction temperature, 13 min residence time, 10°C quench temperature

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up precipitation processes from batch to flow
rules.recommendation: Double flow rates and halve residence time while maintaining high yield - achieved 250 g/h production by going from 1.5 min to 44 s residence time
rules.reasoning: Flow allows for throughput optimization without yield loss, enabling higher productivity through reduced residence times
rules.quantitative: Residence time reduced from 1.5 min to 44 s, maintained 94% yield, achieved 250 g/h throughput

rules.condition: WHEN combining two reaction streams before quenching in precipitation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use T-piece mixing with flow rate of 4 mL/min for each stream, then combine with quench stream at 60 mL/min
rules.reasoning: Proper mixing ratios ensure complete reaction before precipitation occurs
rules.quantitative: 4 mL/min each reaction stream, 60 mL/min quench stream

rules.condition: WHEN performing controlled precipitation via Coflore ATR reactor
rules.recommendation: Use internal volume of 0.1-1.0 L with external cooling system to maintain constant temperature of 10°C
rules.reasoning: External cooling facilitates heat transfer and enables excellent product yield while managing exothermic precipitation

rules.condition: WHEN performing high-temperature Gould-Jacobs quinolone synthesis in flow
rules.recommendation: Operate at 100 bar pressure with Phoenix flow reactor designed for high temperature and pressure operation
rules.reasoning: High pressure prevents solvent vaporization at elevated temperatures required for cyclization reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing high-temperature reactions (250-400°C) in flow
rules.recommendation: Use stainless steel tubing reactor coil (2 mL volume, 1.0 mm inner diameter) with HPLC pump and backpressure regulator
rules.reasoning: Stainless steel withstands high temperatures and pressures while small diameter provides good heat transfer
rules.quantitative: 2 mL volume, 1.0 mm inner diameter, temperature range 250-400°C

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing Gould-Jacobs cyclization at high temperature
rules.recommendation: Use optimal conditions of 390°C with flow rate 4.0 mL/min (residence time 0.5 min) and reaction concentration 0.4 M
rules.reasoning: Elevated temperatures, higher concentrations and shorter residence times favor desired cyclization over decarboxylated side products
rules.quantitative: 390°C, 4.0 mL/min flow rate, 0.5 min residence time, 0.4 M concentration

rules.condition: WHEN performing selective synthesis of decarboxylated products in Gould-Jacobs reaction
rules.recommendation: Decrease flow rate to 4.0-0.5 mL/min and reaction concentration to 0.4-0.05 M while increasing pressure to 100-120 bar, add ca. 1 vol% water
rules.reasoning: Lower concentrations and addition of water favor bimolecular decarboxylation reaction pathway
rules.quantitative: Flow rate 4.0-0.5 mL/min, concentration 0.4-0.05 M, pressure 100-120 bar, 1 vol% water

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up high-temperature flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Prepare 18 mL stock solution and run for 4.5 min collection to isolate 1.36 g product in 82% yield, achieving productivity of 18.2 g/h
rules.reasoning: Flow enables longer run times while maintaining constant reaction conditions for effective scale-up
rules.quantitative: 18 mL stock solution, 4.5 min collection time, 1.36 g product, 82% yield, 18.2 g/h productivity

rules.condition: WHEN performing highly exothermic reactions that require cryogenic conditions in batch (below -78°C)
rules.recommendation: Use flow chemistry with high surface-to-volume ratio to safely perform these reactions without cryogenic cooling
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratio in flow enables effective heat removal from highly exothermic transformations

rules.condition: WHEN using unstable bromomethyl lithium reagent that is unstable even at -120°C
rules.recommendation: Generate the reagent in situ in flow rather than pre-forming in batch, as it must be generated in situ even at very low temperatures
rules.reasoning: In situ generation eliminates storage and handling of highly unstable organolithium reagents

rules.condition: WHEN using back pressure regulators (BPRs) in multi-step flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use several BPRs at 40 psi to act as check valves between reaction steps
rules.reasoning: BPRs prevent backflow and maintain pressure control between sequential reaction stages

rules.condition: WHEN combining neat reagents in flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Mix streams of neat reagents in T-pieces to initiate the sequence and render intermediates
rules.reasoning: T-pieces provide effective mixing for neat reagent streams before entering reactor coils

rules.condition: WHEN performing cyclocondensation reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use heated reactor coil at 85°C with 55 min residence time for complete reaction
rules.reasoning: Elevated temperature and extended residence time ensure complete cyclocondensation

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up telescoped flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Achieve throughput of 3.4 g/h with total residence time of 74 min for 56% isolated yield
rules.reasoning: Demonstrates achievable production rates for multi-step flow synthesis
rules.quantitative: 3.4 g/h throughput, 74 min total residence time, 56% yield

rules.condition: WHEN performing liquid-liquid extraction with membrane separation
rules.recommendation: Use hydrophobic membrane (e.g., PTFE) with small pore size to block polar molecules while allowing nonpolar species to pass
rules.reasoning: Enables separation of phases (aqueous-organic) by selective permeability

rules.condition: WHEN mixing methyl acetoacetate substrate with acetic acid
rules.recommendation: Mix neat substrate in T-piece with acetic acid (2.5 equiv., aq.) before combining with sodium nitrite solution
rules.reasoning: Sequential mixing ensures proper stoichiometry and reaction initiation

rules.condition: WHEN converting nitrite to nitrosonium ion in flow
rules.recommendation: Use reactor coil at 60°C with 1 min residence time for full conversion
rules.reasoning: Elevated temperature ensures complete conversion of nitrite to reactive nitrosonium ion

rules.condition: WHEN adjusting pH in flow extraction process
rules.recommendation: Use glass mixing chip (2 mL, ca. 70 s residence time) to adjust pH prior to membrane separators
rules.reasoning: Adequate mixing time needed for pH adjustment before extraction

rules.condition: WHEN performing liquid-liquid extraction in flow
rules.recommendation: Use isopropyl acetate as organic solvent at equal flow rate (1.7 mL/min)
rules.reasoning: Provides effective extraction of organic products from aqueous phase

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing extraction efficiency in flow
rules.recommendation: At pH 6, ca. 7% of acetic acid co-extracts; at pH 8.2, less than 1% co-extracts
rules.reasoning: pH controls the extraction selectivity and minimizes unwanted co-extraction

rules.condition: WHEN separation efficiency drops due to pH adjustment
rules.recommendation: Use five consecutive Zaiput extractors to maintain robust purification and achieve 93% yield and 99% purity
rules.reasoning: Multiple extraction stages compensate for reduced individual stage efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN assembling flow extraction platform
rules.recommendation: Use PFA polymer tubing (0.8 mm inner diameter), 1/4-28 thread fittings, T-pieces, and PEEK or ETFE connectors
rules.reasoning: These materials provide chemical compatibility and proper flow characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN running optimized continuous flow process with multiple feeds
rules.recommendation: Use flow rates of methyl acetoacetate (0.249 mL/min), acetic acid (0.329 mL/min), sodium nitrite (0.422 mL/min)
rules.reasoning: Optimized flow rates provide proper stoichiometry and residence times

rules.condition: WHEN performing nitration reactions at elevated temperature with acetonitrile
rules.recommendation: Replace acetonitrile with sulfolane as solvent when significant heat release occurs above intended reaction temperature
rules.reasoning: Sulfolane provides better thermal stability - significant heat release only occurs above 150°C vs immediate heat release with MeCN
rules.quantitative: Heat release threshold: >150°C for sulfolane vs immediate for MeCN

rules.condition: WHEN mixing substrate and fuming HNO3 in sulfolane
rules.recommendation: Ensure mixing process is dose-dependent with 80% of heat released during mixing step
rules.reasoning: Controlled mixing prevents thermal runaway as most heat is released during the mixing phase

rules.condition: WHEN performing kilogram-scale nitration in flow
rules.recommendation: Use plug flow reactor with substrate solution (1 M in sulfolane at 35°C) and fuming nitric acid solution (33% w/w in sulfolane)
rules.reasoning: Plug flow provides controlled mixing and heat management for large-scale exothermic nitration

rules.condition: WHEN building kilogram-scale flow reactor
rules.recommendation: Use Hastelloy C-22 coiled reactor (i.d. 0.18 cm, length 9.1 m) operated at 45°C with temperature sensor and pressure gauge
rules.reasoning: Hastelloy provides corrosion resistance for nitric acid reactions; coiled design provides efficient heat transfer
rules.quantitative: 0.18 cm inner diameter, 9.1 m length, 45°C operating temperature

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing kilogram-scale nitration flow process
rules.recommendation: Use 8.7 equiv. fuming HNO3 at 45°C for 8.5 min residence time to reach full conversion
rules.reasoning: Excess reagent and elevated temperature ensure complete conversion in short residence time

rules.condition: WHEN operating validated kilogram-scale nitration process
rules.recommendation: Achieve throughput of ~110 g/h validated to produce 2 kg of product
rules.reasoning: Demonstrates achievable production rate for industrial-scale continuous nitration

rules.condition: WHEN operating kilogram-scale flow reactor with two feed streams
rules.recommendation: Adjust flow rate of each solution to 630 mL/h (10.5 mL/min each)
rules.reasoning: Balanced flow rates ensure proper stoichiometry and residence time control

rules.condition: WHEN performing temperature-sensitive processes in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use chilled water maintained at 5-10°C for quenching reactions
rules.reasoning: Controlled low temperature quenching prevents thermal degradation and maintains product quality

rules.condition: WHEN performing reslurry protocols to remove trace impurities
rules.recommendation: Stir the solid in fresh reactor with water at 30-35°C for 1 hour
rules.reasoning: Controlled temperature and time allows for effective removal of trace impurities like sulfofane

rules.condition: WHEN drying solid products after washing
rules.recommendation: Dry in vacuo at 50-55°C for 16 hours
rules.reasoning: Ensures complete removal of residual water while preventing thermal degradation

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow processes for production
rules.recommendation: Achieve production rates of ~110 g h⁻¹ using established protocols
rules.reasoning: Demonstrates feasible production throughput for continuous flow manufacturing

rules.condition: WHEN using oxygen gas in flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use relatively low partial pressure of oxygen gas of 9% in nitrogen, equivalent to 3 bar
rules.reasoning: Reduces explosion risk while maintaining sufficient oxygen for reactions
rules.quantitative: 9% O₂ in N₂, equivalent to 3 bar

rules.condition: WHEN performing batch-based reactions above 150°C
rules.recommendation: Consider operational requirements and safety features for high-temperature applications
rules.reasoning: High temperatures require special safety considerations and equipment design

rules.condition: WHEN choosing reactor design for heat transfer in mixing-dependent reactions
rules.recommendation: Use metal plate reactors over standard T-mixers
rules.reasoning: Metal plate reactors offer better heat transfer capabilities compared to standard T-mixers

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow chemistry processes
rules.recommendation: Leverage high surface-to-volume ratios for accelerated and simplified chemical processes
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratios enable efficient heat transfer, mass transfer, and reaction control

rules.condition: WHEN performing exothermic and temperature-sensitive processes
rules.recommendation: Utilize the large surface area between solution and applied conditions for quick and homogeneous heat application or removal
rules.reasoning: Large surface area allows for rapid heat transfer without limitations imposed by Lambert-Beer law

rules.condition: WHEN performing interfacial processes (gas-liquid, solid-liquid, liquid-liquid)
rules.recommendation: Utilize high surface-to-volume ratio to significantly accelerate and simplify these chemistries
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratio eliminates need for phase-transfer catalysts in aqueous-organic Taylor flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with unstable/reactive products in flow
rules.recommendation: Use precise delivery and flow control to link reaction time with position in reactor for maximizing yield and selectivity
rules.reasoning: Space-time relationship allows for quenching inline via removal of conditions or addition of suitable solution

rules.condition: WHEN generating and using reactive/toxic intermediates
rules.recommendation: Maximize concentrations at specific point in reactor where trapping reagent can be added
rules.reasoning: Enables flash chemistry that was impossible to perform otherwise due to safety and control limitations

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid chemistries in flow
rules.recommendation: Use pressurization without headspace to accelerate chemistries and access conditions impossible or unsafe in batch
rules.reasoning: Pressurization without headspace provides better control and safety compared to batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN using flow chemistry for gaseous reagents and by-products
rules.recommendation: Apply pressurization to safely increase gas solubility in the flowing stream
rules.reasoning: Pressurization prevents loss of reactive gases to headspace and allows reactions above solvent boiling points

rules.condition: WHEN performing reactions above the boiling point of solvents
rules.recommendation: Use pressurization to access otherwise inaccessible reaction conditions
rules.reasoning: Pressurization allows reactions to be performed well above boiling point without vapor formation

rules.condition: WHEN designing a flow chemistry module
rules.recommendation: Include pumps, mixers, reactors, and backpressure regulators (BPRs) as core components
rules.reasoning: These components provide stable, controlled conditions for the flowing stream

rules.condition: WHEN designing telescoped processes with multiple modules
rules.recommendation: Make each subsequent reaction more tolerant of impurities than the previous ones
rules.reasoning: All by-products/excess reagents/unreacted materials/catalysts transfer from previous steps

rules.condition: WHEN designing late-stage modules in telescoped processes requiring long residence times
rules.recommendation: Use larger reactors than would be needed in standalone modules
rules.reasoning: Flow rate continues to increase with each additional pump/mass flow controller

rules.condition: WHEN considering flow vs batch for a process
rules.recommendation: Use flow for reactions that benefit from high surface-to-volume ratios, precision in reagent delivery and reaction time, and pressurization capability
rules.reasoning: These are flow's core attributes that provide advantages over batch processing

rules.condition: WHEN performing exothermic or temperature-sensitive chemistries
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors to leverage high surface-to-volume ratios for excellent temperature control
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratios provide superior heat transfer compared to batch reactors

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors with UV or LED light sources
rules.reasoning: High surface-to-volume ratio allows accelerated reactions and ability to leave activation zone to prevent overreaction/decomposition

rules.condition: WHEN reactions involve heterogeneous reagents/catalysts or are driven by precipitation
rules.recommendation: Avoid flow chemistry - use batch instead
rules.reasoning: These reactions are handled much easier in flasks than tubing due to clogging issues

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are completed overnight and involve safe reagents
rules.recommendation: Consider batch processing instead of flow
rules.reasoning: Flow may not provide sufficient advantage to justify the setup effort and resource investment

rules.condition: WHEN performing liquid-liquid reactions without extreme conditions or scaling issues
rules.recommendation: Consider batch mixing instead of flow
rules.reasoning: Liquid-liquid systems can be efficiently mixed in batch and do not particularly benefit from flow apparatus

rules.condition: WHEN performing liquid-liquid reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use either high flow rates or reactors packed with inert materials
rules.reasoning: Efficient mixing between phases in flow requires enhanced mixing techniques

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use higher pressures to improve gas solubility in the liquid phase
rules.reasoning: Higher pressures safely improve gas solubility where the transformation takes place

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Control gas addition stoichiometry using mass flow controllers
rules.reasoning: Avoids super-stoichiometric waste typical of batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN working with hazardous gases like phosgene and diazomethane
rules.recommendation: Generate and use gaseous reagents safely in flow systems
rules.reasoning: Flow capability enables applications that are otherwise carefully avoided due to safety concerns

rules.condition: WHEN performing solid-liquid reactions with packed-bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Pack solid material (e.g., catalyst) into a column and pass liquid evenly through it
rules.reasoning: Provides high effective molarity of heterogeneous material and combines reaction and separation in a single unit

rules.condition: WHEN using packed-bed reactors for catalysis
rules.recommendation: Expect improved catalyst lifetime due to isolation from outside environment
rules.reasoning: Catalysts often exhibit improved lifetime owing to their isolation from the outside environment

rules.condition: WHEN using nonimmobilized/packed solid reagents or partially soluble reagents
rules.recommendation: Avoid flow chemistry due to clogging risk
rules.reasoning: Solid material loosely passing through thin tubing may cause clogging of channels

rules.condition: WHEN designing reaction conditions to prevent precipitation
rules.recommendation: Choose temperature, solvents, etc. that prevent solid precipitation of complexes, products, by-products, or salts
rules.reasoning: Critical to avoid clogging in flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN considering flow for photochemical, electrochemical, gas/liquid, immobilized catalyst, fast kinetics/unstable intermediate, hazardous, extreme condition, or scale-up problematic processes
rules.recommendation: These processes are best suited for flow chemistry
rules.reasoning: These process types benefit most from flow's core advantages

rules.condition: WHEN performing fast, diffusion-limited processes with microfluidic devices
rules.recommendation: Use microfluidic devices for optimal mixing
rules.reasoning: These devices are extremely dependent on mixing, and poor mixing can cause accumulation of by-products or hazardous intermediates

rules.condition: WHEN performing flash chemistry with reaction times from milliseconds to seconds
rules.recommendation: Use flow chemistry for precise control over extremely fast transformations
rules.reasoning: Batch equipment cannot provide high-degree control over such processes, and flow enables transformations not feasible otherwise due to side-reactions and decomposition

rules.condition: WHEN performing exergonic reactions requiring slow reagent addition and/or cryogenic temperatures
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors for safe operation due to rapid heat dissipation
rules.reasoning: Rapid heat dissipation of thin tubing allows safe operation, sometimes even at room temperature

rules.condition: WHEN slow transformations need process intensification with high temperatures and pressures
rules.recommendation: Apply high temperatures and high pressures in flow systems
rules.reasoning: Flow systems can benefit from process intensification and provide narrow temperature profile windows

rules.condition: WHEN deciding whether to use flow chemistry for a reaction
rules.recommendation: Use the decision tree flowchart to systematically evaluate: safety, batch performance, optimization goals, reagent states, precipitation effects, catalyst type, emulsion involvement, reaction speed, selectivity temperature dependence, heating requirements, and photochemical/electrochemical nature
rules.reasoning: The decision tree provides a systematic approach to determine if flow chemistry offers advantages over batch processing

rules.condition: WHEN a reaction is not safe in batch
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry as an alternative approach
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry can provide better safety control through continuous processing and smaller reaction volumes

rules.condition: WHEN a reaction involves gas reagents
rules.recommendation: Flow chemistry is favorable for gas-liquid reactions
rules.reasoning: Flow systems provide better control and mixing for gas-liquid reactions compared to batch

rules.condition: WHEN using heterogeneous catalysts
rules.recommendation: Flow chemistry is advantageous
rules.reasoning: Heterogeneous catalysts can be easily integrated into flow systems for continuous operation

rules.condition: WHEN reactions involve emulsions
rules.recommendation: Scaling could be problematic - consider flow alternatives
rules.reasoning: Emulsions can be difficult to scale up in batch systems but may be better controlled in flow

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are fast (<1 hour)
rules.recommendation: Flow chemistry is favorable for fast reactions
rules.reasoning: Fast reactions benefit from the precise control and rapid mixing available in flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN selectivity is affected by temperature
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for better temperature control
rules.reasoning: Flow systems provide superior temperature control compared to batch reactors

rules.condition: WHEN heating speeds up the reaction
rules.recommendation: Flow chemistry is advantageous for heated reactions
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors provide efficient and precise heating capabilities

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are photochemically or electrochemically driven
rules.recommendation: Flow chemistry is highly recommended
rules.reasoning: Flow systems provide better light penetration and electrode contact for these specialized reactions

rules.condition: WHEN translating from batch to flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Focus on five key variables: solvent/solubility, concentration/stoichiometry, temperature, pressure, and residence time
rules.reasoning: These five variables are the most critical factors that differ between batch and flow processing

rules.condition: WHEN planning a flow reaction
rules.recommendation: Conduct solvent screening in batch first, as only homogeneous solutions should be fed into the flow system
rules.reasoning: Slurries can cause clogging in flow systems and require specialized equipment like peristaltic pumps or serial micro-batch reactors
rules.exceptions: Slurries can be used with specialized equipment

rules.condition: WHEN selecting solvents for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Identify solvents that create homogeneous mixtures and solubilize all reagents, with desired physical properties for the transformation
rules.reasoning: Complete solubility prevents precipitation and clogging in narrow flow tubing
rules.quantitative: All reagents must be completely soluble
rules.exceptions: Solvent mixtures can be tested if single solvents fail

rules.condition: WHEN no single solvent works for all reagents
rules.recommendation: Test solvent mixtures for the desired chemical transformation
rules.reasoning: Multiple solvents may be needed to achieve complete solubility of all components
