rules.exceptions: Not applicable - always follow safety protocols for diazo compounds

rules.condition: WHEN conducting flow chemistry experiments with hazardous chemicals
rules.recommendation: Conduct the entire experiment in a fume hood and consult Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS) for all chemicals
rules.reasoning: Proper ventilation and knowledge of chemical hazards are essential for safe operation
rules.quantitative: Use fume hood for all operations involving volatile or hazardous chemicals
rules.exceptions: Not applicable - always follow safety protocols

rules.condition: WHEN setting up microreactor systems for multi-step synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use three syringe pumps for solutions A, B, and Q, with mixing channel M1 (1 μL internal volume) followed by second addition point M2
rules.reasoning: Sequential addition allows for intermediate formation before final reaction step, with controlled residence times
rules.quantitative: Reactor volume = 1 μL; use 5 mL syringes for solutions A and B, 1 mL syringe for solution Q
rules.exceptions: When different reaction stoichiometry or kinetics require different setup

rules.condition: WHEN starting flow chemistry experiments
rules.recommendation: Set flow rates to relatively high value (25 μL min⁻¹) initially to fill tubing, then adjust to required rates after system equilibration
rules.reasoning: Higher initial flow rates quickly displace air and establish steady flow, reducing startup time
rules.quantitative: Initial flow rate: 25 μL min⁻¹; wait 5-10 minutes until effluent becomes colored before switching to experimental conditions
rules.exceptions: When high flow rates might damage sensitive components

rules.condition: WHEN collecting effluent for analysis in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Collect effluent over several minutes (5-10 min) to smooth out temporal variations and dilute with DCM to keep absorbance below 1
rules.reasoning: Longer collection times average out fluctuations and proper dilution ensures accurate spectroscopic measurements
rules.quantitative: Collection time: 5-10 minutes; dilute with 1 mL DCM to keep λmax absorbance < 1
rules.exceptions: When studying fast transient behavior or when dilution interferes with analysis

rules.condition: WHEN measuring unstable products like diazo compounds
rules.recommendation: Measure absorption spectrum immediately after synthesis completion due to compound decomposition over time
rules.reasoning: Diazo compounds decompose over time, leading to inaccurate measurements if delayed
rules.quantitative: Measure λmax and absorbance immediately after each synthesis run
rules.exceptions: When products are known to be stable

rules.condition: WHEN changing experimental conditions in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Allow sufficient time for system equilibration and removal of effluent corresponding to previous conditions before collecting new samples
rules.reasoning: System needs time to reach steady state at new conditions and purge previous conditions from dead volume
rules.quantitative: Wait for equilibration time after changing flow rates before collecting new samples
rules.exceptions: When studying transient behavior or startup dynamics

rules.condition: WHEN working with hydrazones that would lead to diazo compounds that are not resonance stabilized
rules.recommendation: Take care, as they may be very unstable and dangerous
rules.reasoning: Non-resonance stabilized diazo compounds have inherent instability issues that pose safety hazards

rules.condition: WHEN you have only three pumps but four reactants and want to avoid confounding effects
rules.recommendation: Use an extra pump and T-piece or Y-piece to separate solutions of reactants and DMSO, allowing independent variation of flow rates
rules.reasoning: With three pumps and four reactants, the ratio between TFAA and DMSO is always varied at the same time as the ratio of TFAA and substrate, creating confounded effects that cannot be distinguished

rules.condition: WHEN DMSO and TFAA react together in flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Ensure proper mixing is required for these reactive components
rules.reasoning: DMSO and TFAA undergo reaction, requiring adequate mixing for proper conversion, unlike DMSO and substrate combinations
rules.exceptions: This is not strictly needed for DMSO and substrate in alternative setup

rules.condition: WHEN measuring absorption spectra for diazo compound product analysis
rules.recommendation: Use wavelength λmax = 521 nm for absorbance measurements
rules.reasoning: The visible absorption spectrum of the product mixture shows a maximum at this wavelength

rules.condition: WHEN collecting effluent for temporal variation averaging in flow experiments
rules.recommendation: Collect effluent over a precalculated time interval such that the total amount of substrate that passed through the system is always equal
rules.reasoning: This approach allows temporal variations in conversion to average out and provides consistent basis for comparison

rules.condition: WHEN adding DCM to colored liquid for analysis
rules.recommendation: Add either 0.5 or 1.0 mL DCM such that the volume becomes large enough to fill a semi-micro cuvette while keeping absorbance sufficiently low to stay below 1 and in Beer-Lambert law regime
rules.reasoning: Need sufficient volume for analysis while maintaining absorbance in valid range for Beer-Lambert law

rules.condition: WHEN normalizing measured absorbances for flow chemistry analysis
rules.recommendation: Divide measured absorbances by the theoretical concentration of product in case of total conversion
rules.reasoning: This normalization accounts for concentration effects due to flow rate variations and provides a measure of relative conversion

rules.condition: WHEN fitting experimental optimization data to models
rules.recommendation: Use quadratic models and avoid incorporating terms beyond quadratic ones
rules.reasoning: Addition of higher order terms results in overfitting, and exchange of quadratic terms for higher order terms results in fits that are actually worse, although the location of optimum hardly changes

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing diazo compound synthesis at 10°C using the given concentrations and setup
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 1.75 s and ln([TFAA]/[1]) = 0.065 for optimal results
rules.reasoning: Model predicts optimal combination of factors based on experimental data fitting

rules.condition: WHEN conducting experimental optimization of the diazo synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 1.5 s and ln([TFAA]/[1]) = 0.2231 for optimal experimental results
rules.reasoning: These were the experimentally determined optimal conditions

rules.condition: WHEN conducting flow chemistry experiments for diazo synthesis

rules.reasoning: This tubing specification provides appropriate flow characteristics for the system

rules.reasoning: Gas-tight syringes prevent air ingress and maintain precise flow control

rules.condition: WHEN conducting diazo synthesis experiments
rules.recommendation: Use DCM as solvent with purity ≥99% from chemical suppliers without further purification or drying
rules.reasoning: High purity DCM provides consistent reaction conditions

rules.condition: WHEN preparing dimethyl sulfoxide and benzophenone hydrazone solution
rules.recommendation: Prepare the solution freshly on a daily basis, as after a day of standing it becomes slightly opaque and a water-soluble precipitate forms at the bottom of the flask
rules.reasoning: The solution degrades over time, forming precipitates that could affect reaction outcomes

rules.condition: WHEN performing UV analysis for relative conversion determination

rules.reasoning: This setup provides accurate quantitative measurements for conversion analysis

rules.condition: WHEN using Type I flow reactions with homogeneous catalysts that must dissolve in reaction medium
rules.recommendation: Use only reactions where all reagents including catalysts can dissolve in a reaction medium; avoid reactions using inorganic salt reagents that are insoluble
rules.reasoning: Type I flow reactions require all reagents to be dissolved, and insoluble reagents narrow the window of applicable reactions

rules.condition: WHEN using Type II flow reactions with supported reagents that may be consumed
rules.recommendation: Plan to change the reagent-packed reactor when the supported reagent is consumed; accept that overreactions may occur
rules.reasoning: Supported reagents can be consumed during reaction, requiring reactor replacement, and overreactions are a known drawback

rules.condition: WHEN using Type III flow reactions with homogeneous catalysts
rules.recommendation: Include quenching and work-up processes to remove catalyst residues after the reaction; accept that this interrupts seamless multistep flow reactions
rules.reasoning: Homogeneous catalysts require removal processes which interrupt continuous multistep operations, and catalytic reactions usually have slow reaction rates

rules.condition: WHEN designing multistep sequential continuous-flow production of high-value-added compounds
rules.recommendation: Use Type IV flow reactions with heterogeneous catalysts in column-type fixed-bed reactors
rules.reasoning: Type IV eliminates catalyst invalidation processes and contamination by catalyst residues, making it ideal for multistep continuous production

rules.condition: WHEN using Type IV catalytic flow reactions with catalyst-packed reactors
rules.recommendation: Supply reactants dissolved in mobile phase to achieve large amount of catalytic species contact, reducing reaction time to completion within residence time
rules.reasoning: Large amount of catalytic species exists in packed reactor, providing clear contrast to Type III homogeneous catalysts and batch reactions

rules.condition: WHEN comparing batch vs flow catalytic reactions in terms of [Substrate, S]/[Catalyst, C] ratio
rules.recommendation: Recognize that batch systems have disadvantageous high [S]/[C] in initial stage and low [S]/[C] in later stage; flow systems maintain consistent catalyst contact
rules.reasoning: High [S]/[C] causes undesirable interaction between excess substrate and catalyst, low [S]/[C] decreases contact frequency, requiring long reaction times in batch
rules.quantitative: [S]/[C] ratio varies with reaction progress in batch systems

rules.condition: WHEN developing heterogeneous catalysts for continuous-flow systems
rules.recommendation: Focus on finding highly active and stable heterogeneous catalysts to enable high productivity and long life-time processes
rules.reasoning: There exists a great hurdle in finding efficient, stable, and reliable catalysts for continuous-flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN performing hydrogenation reactions in flow with back pressure regulation

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring flow reactions in real-time
rules.recommendation: Use in-line IR spectroscopy with webcam monitoring for reaction progress tracking
rules.reasoning: Real-time monitoring allows for immediate reaction optimization and quality control

rules.condition: WHEN evaluating flow catalytic reactions
rules.recommendation: Use substrate/catalyst molar ratio per unit period (h) as the key parameter for yield evaluation
rules.reasoning: Flow reaction yield is considered a controllable outcome by changing the substrate/catalyst molar ratio rather than absolute quantities

rules.condition: WHEN comparing catalytic activities in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Evaluate productivity per time, productivity per catalyst, space-time yield, and TON as important outcome measures
rules.reasoning: Multiple metrics provide comprehensive assessment of catalytic efficiency in flow systems beyond simple yield measurements

rules.condition: WHEN calculating weight hourly space velocity (WHSV)
rules.recommendation: Use formula: WHSV = (C₀ × v₀)/W₀ where C₀ is initial concentration (mol/L), v₀ is flow rate (L/h), and W₀ is catalyst weight (g)
rules.reasoning: WHSV provides standardized metric for comparing reactor performance and scaling calculations

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow reactions to maintain conversion rate
rules.recommendation: To increase throughput 10-fold while maintaining conversion, increase catalyst weight by factor of 10 or decrease flow rate by factor of 10
rules.reasoning: Maintaining constant WHSV preserves reaction conversion and selectivity during scale-up
rules.quantitative: 10x throughput requires 10x catalyst weight or 0.1x flow rate

rules.condition: WHEN designing multistep one-flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Use SN1 substitution, pericyclic, addition, Wittig olefination, Jones oxidation, and hydrogenation reactions as advantageous reaction types
rules.reasoning: These reaction types are particularly well-suited for continuous flow processing due to their reaction characteristics and compatibility

rules.condition: WHEN using heterogeneous catalysts in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Prefer heterogeneous catalysts over homogeneous catalysts for flow applications
rules.reasoning: Heterogeneous catalysts allow physical separation from products, minimize waste by-products, and enable easier catalyst recovery
rules.exceptions: When homogeneous catalysts provide significantly better selectivity or activity

rules.condition: WHEN setting up gas-liquid flow reactions for laboratory use
rules.recommendation: Use a piston (HPLC pump) or syringe pump, mass flow controller (MFC), T- or Y-mixer, coil reactor, and backpressure regulator (BPR) as the most straightforward and budget-friendly setup
rules.reasoning: These components form essential parts of a flow chemistry starter pack and can be found in every laboratory equipped with HPLC instruments
rules.quantitative: Components: piston pump, MFC, T/Y-mixer, coil reactor, BPR

rules.condition: WHEN implementing gas-liquid reactions in small inner diameter tubing (0.25-0.75 mm)
rules.recommendation: Expect Taylor flow to become dominant; however, annular flow regime might be observed if there is a large difference between gas and liquid flow rates
rules.reasoning: Flow regime depends on tube diameter and relative flow rates of gas and liquid phases
rules.exceptions: Large difference between gas and liquid flow rates may cause annular flow

rules.condition: WHEN gas is highly soluble in reaction media or can be liquefied under operating pressure
rules.recommendation: Expect homogeneous reaction conditions to occur
rules.reasoning: High solubility or liquefaction eliminates the gas-liquid interface

rules.condition: WHEN using reactors with complex mixing geometry (e.g., Corning glass plates)
rules.recommendation: Bubble or bubbly flow conditions can be obtained
rules.reasoning: Complex geometry promotes different flow patterns than simple tubular reactors

rules.condition: WHEN using tube-in-tube reactors equipped with Teflon AF-2400 membrane
rules.recommendation: Expect immediate consumption of the diffused gas
rules.reasoning: The membrane allows selective gas permeation leading to rapid reaction

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid-solid reactions, especially catalytic hydrogenations

rules.reasoning: Fixed-bed configuration is typical for heterogeneous catalytic processes

rules.condition: WHEN sourcing gas from cylinders using mass flow controller
rules.recommendation: Use MFC instead of gas-tight syringe for more accurate dosing and continuous processing capability
rules.reasoning: MFC provides better accuracy and continuous operation is only limited by cylinder capacity

rules.condition: WHEN using gases with low vapor pressure (e.g., NH₃, CClF₂H)
rules.recommendation: Condense and use the gas in liquid phase or in solution
rules.reasoning: Low vapor pressure gases can be more easily handled in condensed form

rules.condition: WHEN generating hazardous gases (e.g., H₂ by electrolysis, CH₂N₂ from precursors)
rules.recommendation: Use on-demand generation from stable precursors and utilize the gas shortly after production
rules.reasoning: Reduces atom economy but allows utilization of hazardous or unstable reagents in a well-controlled and safe manner
rules.quantitative: Examples: H₂ from water electrolysis, CH₂N₂ from N-methyl-nitroso amine

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time for gas-liquid systems
rules.recommendation: Experimentally measure residence time under steady-state conditions rather than calculating from flow rates
rules.reasoning: Gas solubility, consumption during reaction, and in situ formation affect residence time calculations, making them more difficult than for homogeneous or liquid-liquid systems
rules.exceptions: Simple calculation works well for homogeneous and liquid-liquid biphasic reactions

rules.condition: WHEN reporting pressure in flow chemistry literature
rules.recommendation: Use bar or psi units (1 bar = 14.504 psi), with barg or bar(g) representing gauge pressure above ambient
rules.reasoning: Standard notation convention in flow chemistry literature

rules.condition: WHEN reporting flow rates in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Report liquid flow rates in μLmin⁻¹ or mLmin⁻¹ units, and gas flow rates in standard conditions (sccm or NmLmin⁻¹)
rules.reasoning: Standard notation conventions for flow chemistry literature
rules.quantitative: Liquid: μLmin⁻¹ or mLmin⁻¹; Gas: sccm or NmLmin⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN using flow reactors with gaseous reagents
rules.recommendation: Utilize the smaller chemical inventory compared to batch reactors with same productivity and high-pressure resistance equipment
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors have smaller inventory, and polymer tubes have high-pressure resistance, improving safety and potentially enhancing reaction rates

rules.condition: WHEN eliminating headspace in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Expect larger interfacial area leading to higher mass transfer rates
rules.reasoning: Eliminating headspace increases the contact area between phases

rules.condition: WHEN using precise control of stoichiometry in flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN performing hydrogenation in Pd-coated microreactor with annular flow conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing hydrogenations with hydrogen gas

rules.condition: WHEN using H-Cube system for gas-liquid mixing

rules.condition: WHEN operating tube-in-tube hydrogenation reactors

rules.condition: WHEN performing homogeneous hydrogenation with Crabtree's catalyst in tube-in-tube reactor

rules.condition: WHEN performing ozonolysis in flow chemistry

rules.condition: WHEN using fluoroform (CF3H) in trifluoromethylation reactions
rules.recommendation: Avoid release of fluoroform into the atmosphere and integrate in-line analytics (IR, NMR) to ensure full consumption under optimized conditions
rules.reasoning: Fluoroform is a potent greenhouse gas requiring environmental protection

rules.condition: WHEN performing trifluoromethylation with fluoroform using microflow systems
rules.recommendation: Use flow system consisting of syringe pumps for liquid, MFC for fluoroform delivery, mixer with 2-3 inlets, and stainless steel reactor tubing
rules.reasoning: Ensures sufficient residence time and proper mixing for the reaction
rules.quantitative: CF3H: 25 mL/min gas flow rate vs 1.43 mL/min liquid flow rate (11 equiv., large excess)

rules.condition: WHEN using large excess of gaseous reagent CF3H in microflow
rules.recommendation: Expect slug flow regime even with large excess of gas
rules.reasoning: Flow patterns are determined by the relative flow rates and reactor geometry
rules.quantitative: 11 equiv. CF3H, 25 mL/min gas vs 1.43 mL/min liquid still produces slug flow

rules.condition: WHEN performing trifluoromethylation of chalcones with fluoroform at -10°C
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 0.5 seconds in stainless steel mixer
rules.reasoning: Short residence time at low temperature provides efficient conversion
rules.quantitative: 0.23 mL volume, -10°C, 0.5 s residence time, 61% yield

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up processes using appropriate BPR and stoichiometric amounts of CF3H
rules.recommendation: Select appropriate BPR and use stoichiometric amount of CF3H to develop scalable process
rules.reasoning: Prevents pressure fluctuation and clogging issues identified as key challenges

rules.condition: WHEN performing multi-step synthesis of eflornithine with fluoroform
rules.recommendation: Use telescoped flow procedure with sequential temperature stages: -30°C (4 mL), 0°C (1 mL), room temperature (14 mL), 160°C (40 mL), followed by 12 bar pressure
rules.reasoning: Sequential temperature control enables multi-step synthesis with high yield

rules.condition: WHEN performing C(sp³)-H bond functionalization of light alkanes using photoredox conditions
rules.recommendation: Apply relatively high pressures to ensure liquefaction of the gaseous alkane and avoid gas-to-liquid mass transfer limitations
rules.reasoning: High pressure ensures proper phase contact and mass transfer for gas-liquid reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing Catellani-type reactions with gaseous alkenes in continuous flow
rules.recommendation: Maintain reactions in stable Taylor flow regime with precise parameter control
rules.reasoning: Taylor flow provides better mixing and mass transfer compared to other flow regimes
rules.quantitative: Batch control showed 66% vs 12% yield difference when parameters not precisely controlled

rules.condition: WHEN using stop-flow microtubing (SFMT) reactor for gas-liquid reactions
rules.recommendation: Fill microtubing reactor with reaction mixture, seal using shut-off valves, remove from system and heat in oil bath for screening conditions
rules.reasoning: Enables rapid screening of multiple conditions while saving time compared to continuous parameter screening
rules.quantitative: Results comparable to continuous flow system
rules.exceptions: Mixing efficiency expected to be smaller than in batch reactors or under Taylor flow

rules.condition: WHEN working with gaseous organofluorine compounds in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Refer to compatibility table for appropriate reagent-product combinations: CF3I→RCF3, CF3H→RCF2H/RCF3, CF2ClH→RCF2H, CF3CHN2→various products, CF3HCHN2→pyrazole products, SF6→fluorination products
rules.reasoning: Different gaseous fluorine sources enable different product types and reaction pathways
rules.quantitative: See reference numbers [76-90] for specific conditions

rules.condition: WHEN using diazomethane (CH2N2) as C1 building block
rules.recommendation: Exercise extreme caution due to explosive and toxic nature of diazomethane
rules.reasoning: Diazomethane is both explosive and toxic, requiring special safety precautions

rules.condition: WHEN performing hydrogenation reactions in flow using packed bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Use automated flow hydrogenators for O-benzyl and N-benzyloxycarbonyl protecting group removal
rules.reasoning: Automated systems provide better control and optimization of hydrogenation conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use tube-in-tube gas permeable membrane reactors for CO2 incorporation reactions
rules.reasoning: Membrane reactors enable controlled gas-liquid contact and continuous CO2 supply

rules.condition: WHEN performing hydrogenation reactions at elevated pressure in flow
rules.recommendation: Use Teflon AF-2400 for gas-liquid contact systems
rules.reasoning: Teflon AF-2400 enables effective gas-liquid contact at elevated pressures

rules.condition: WHEN performing asymmetric hydrogenation with ionic liquid catalysts
rules.recommendation: Use modified CO2 as mobile phase with supported ionic liquid phase catalysts
rules.reasoning: CO2 provides efficient mass transfer while maintaining catalyst activity

rules.condition: WHEN performing hydrogenation of aliphatic nitro compounds
rules.recommendation: Use trickle bed reactors with sponge metal catalysts
rules.reasoning: Trickle bed design provides optimal gas-liquid-solid contact for nitro reduction

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up hydrogenation processes
rules.recommendation: Implement online UV-VIS conversion control for process monitoring
rules.reasoning: Real-time monitoring enables better process control and safety during scale-up

rules.condition: WHEN performing hydrogenations requiring high surface area
rules.recommendation: Use Pd/Al2O3-coated rectangular cross-section 3D-printed static mixers
rules.reasoning: 3D-printed mixers provide enhanced surface area and mixing for heterogeneous catalysis

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid hydrogenation of nitroaromatics
rules.recommendation: Use pharmaceutically relevant pyrrolobenzo[1,4]diazepine scaffolds as substrates
rules.reasoning: These scaffolds provide efficient access to pharmaceutical intermediates

rules.condition: WHEN performing chemoselective hydrogenation
rules.recommendation: Apply flow chemistry approaches to isoindole-7-carboxylic acids and 7-oxa-bicyclo[2.2.1]heptanes
rules.reasoning: Flow conditions enable better selectivity control in complex molecular frameworks

rules.condition: WHEN performing selective hydrogenation of halogenated nitroaromatics
rules.recommendation: Use flow conditions for conversion to haloanilines in both batch and flow modes
rules.reasoning: Flow provides better control over selectivity to avoid dehalogenation

rules.condition: WHEN performing catalytic deuterodehalogenation
rules.recommendation: Use continuous-flow systems with propylene carbonate as solvent
rules.reasoning: Propylene carbonate provides suitable medium for deuterium incorporation

rules.condition: WHEN performing palladium-catalyzed carbonylation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use single and multiple tube-in-tube gas-liquid microreactors
rules.reasoning: Tube-in-tube design enables controlled CO delivery for carbonylation reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing carbonylation of nitrobenzene to phenylisocyanate
rules.recommendation: Use gas-liquid slug flow in microchannels
rules.reasoning: Slug flow provides enhanced mass transfer for gas-liquid carbonylation

rules.condition: WHEN performing Pd-catalyzed carbonylation of aryl chlorides with CO
rules.recommendation: Operate at elevated temperature and pressure
rules.reasoning: High temperature and pressure are required for activation of aryl chlorides

rules.condition: WHEN performing oxidative carbonylation using CO and O2
rules.recommendation: Use Pd-catalyzed systems at elevated temperatures and pressures
rules.reasoning: Pd catalysts are effective for oxidative carbonylation under these conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing formylation of aryl bromides
rules.recommendation: Use Pd-catalyzed continuous-flow synthesis with carbon monoxide and hydrogen
rules.reasoning: Pd catalysts enable efficient formylation under flow conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing formylation of phenol-derived aryl fluorosulfonates
rules.recommendation: Use syngas in continuous flow synthesis
rules.reasoning: Syngas provides both CO and H2 for efficient formylation reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing syngas-mediated C-C bond formation
rules.recommendation: Use flow conditions for rhodium-catalyzed hydroformylation of styrenes
rules.reasoning: Flow enables better pressure control for syngas reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing 1-octene hydroformylation
rules.recommendation: Use low syngas pressure with single-droplet screening to continuous flow synthesis
rules.reasoning: Low pressure operation is safer and more efficient for this substrate

rules.condition: WHEN performing flash carboxylation
rules.recommendation: Use fast lithiation-carboxylation sequence at room temperature in continuous flow
rules.reasoning: Room temperature operation prevents decomposition of organolithium intermediates

rules.condition: WHEN performing CO2 fixation to ethylene carbonate
rules.recommendation: Operate under supercritical conditions for continuous and selective processes
rules.reasoning: Supercritical CO2 provides enhanced mass transfer and selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN performing bromine-catalyzed conversion of CO2 and epoxides
rules.recommendation: Use continuous flow conditions for cyclic carbonate formation
rules.reasoning: Flow provides better control of the exothermic CO2 insertion reaction

rules.condition: WHEN performing photoredox activation of CO2
rules.recommendation: Use continuous flow for amino acid synthesis applications
rules.reasoning: Continuous irradiation in flow provides consistent photoactivation

rules.condition: WHEN using molecular oxygen for liquid phase aerobic oxidations
rules.recommendation: Implement continuous-flow microreactors for enhanced safety and control
rules.reasoning: Flow systems provide better oxygen handling and heat management for aerobic oxidations

rules.condition: WHEN performing Pd-catalyzed olefin cleavage using oxygen
rules.recommendation: Operate within the explosive regime using continuous-flow processes
rules.reasoning: Flow systems enable safe operation with explosive gas mixtures through better control

rules.condition: WHEN performing copper-catalyzed aerobic oxidations
rules.recommendation: Use four-electron oxidant with one-electron redox-active catalyst systems
rules.reasoning: This combination provides efficient electron transfer for aerobic oxidation

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation of primary alcohols
rules.recommendation: Use copper(I)/TEMPO catalyst system in continuous flow
rules.reasoning: Cu/TEMPO provides selective and efficient alcohol oxidation under aerobic conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use PTFE-membrane flow reactors for gas-liquid contact
rules.reasoning: PTFE membranes provide controlled oxygen delivery while maintaining safety

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic alcohol oxidation
rules.recommendation: Use heterogeneous Ru(OH)x/Al2O3 catalyst in continuous flow
rules.reasoning: Heterogeneous catalysts enable easy separation and catalyst recycling

rules.condition: WHEN performing aerobic oxidation with photocatalysis
rules.recommendation: Use decatungstate photocatalysis with C(sp3)-H functionalization in flow
rules.reasoning: Decatungstate enables selective C-H activation under mild photochemical conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing transfer hydrogenation of olefins
rules.recommendation: Use diimide generation from hydrazine and oxygen in continuous-flow
rules.reasoning: In-situ diimide generation provides clean reduction without metal catalysts

rules.condition: WHEN performing artemisinic acid reduction
rules.recommendation: Use multi-injection strategies for continuous synthesis of antimalarial drugs
rules.reasoning: Multi-injection enables precise stoichiometry control for pharmaceutical synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid ozonolysis
rules.recommendation: Use multichannel microreactors for flow distribution control
rules.reasoning: Multiple channels provide better ozone distribution and mixing

rules.condition: WHEN performing ozonolysis reactions
rules.recommendation: Use capillary reactors or laboratory scale reactors for continuous flow
rules.reasoning: Small scale reactors minimize the risk of ozonide accumulation and explosion

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical chlorination
rules.recommendation: Use microstructured reactor technology for alkylароmatic substrates
rules.reasoning: Microstructured reactors provide better light penetration and heat management

rules.condition: WHEN performing photo-radical chlorination of cycloalkanes
rules.recommendation: Use microflow systems for enhanced reaction control
rules.reasoning: Microflow provides better light distribution and prevents overchlorination

rules.condition: WHEN using hydrogen chloride gas in continuous flow
rules.recommendation: Implement solvent-free conversion of alcohols to chlorides in microflow
rules.reasoning: Solvent-free conditions reduce waste and improve safety with HCl gas

rules.condition: WHEN designing a continuous-flow apparatus
rules.recommendation: Include reagent pumps, mixing units (T-mixer), reaction zone (tube/coil or packed-bed reactors that can be heated, cooled, or irradiated), quenching inlet, back-pressure regulator and product collection vessel
rules.reasoning: This is the typical arrangement required for effective continuous-flow operation

rules.condition: WHEN using packed-bed flow reactors in heterogeneous phase reactions
rules.recommendation: Utilize high interfacial area to facilitate better mass transfer and improved heat distribution
rules.reasoning: Particle movement is mainly restricted, creating high surface area contact between phases

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are under diffusion control in laminar flow
rules.recommendation: Use Damkoehler number (Da) to determine if reaction is diffusion or kinetic controlled - reactions are diffusion limited when Da > 1
rules.reasoning: Da expresses reaction rate relative to mass transport rate (diffusion). When Da < 1, reactions are reaction rate limited. When Da > 1, rates are controlled by diffusion or mass transport
rules.quantitative: Da < 1: reaction rate limited; Da > 1: diffusion/mass transport limited

rules.condition: WHEN working in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Understand that reaction time equals residence time spent in the reaction zone, depending on flow rate and reaction volume
rules.reasoning: Unlike batch where reaction time is time under defined conditions, flow reaction time is determined by residence time in the reactor
rules.quantitative: Residence time = reaction volume / flow rate

rules.condition: WHEN scaling microreactors
rules.recommendation: Recognize that reducing size 100-fold increases surface-to-volume ratio 100-fold
rules.reasoning: Volume scales as length cubed, surface area scales as length squared. When length is shortened, surface-to-volume ratio increases proportionally

rules.condition: WHEN using microreactors with small dimensions
rules.recommendation: Achieve complete mixing in microseconds by utilizing channel or capillary diameter of 0.05-0.5 cm with laminar flow predominant
rules.reasoning: Fast mixing relies on short diffusion path in microreactors. A molecule in center of microfluidic channel can reach the wall in few seconds, compared to hours in batch flask
rules.quantitative: Channel diameter: 0.05-0.5 cm; mixing time: microseconds

rules.condition: WHEN molecular diffusion is needed in flow
rules.recommendation: Understand that time (td) needed for molecular diffusion is proportional to the square of the length of the diffusion path
rules.reasoning: Marked shortening of diffusion path in microreactors results in mixing speed unattainable in batch

rules.condition: WHEN using microreactors
rules.recommendation: Utilize rapid heat transfer due to greater surface area per unit volume than macroreactors, enabling fast cooling/heating and precise temperature control
rules.reasoning: Higher surface-to-volume ratio in microreactors enables rapid heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN determining flow regimes
rules.recommendation: Use Reynolds number ranges: truly laminar Re < 100, transient 100 < Re < 2,500, turbulent flow Re > 2,500
rules.reasoning: Reynolds number determines flow regimes in channels and is defined as ratio of momentum (inertial) forces to viscous forces
rules.quantitative: Re < 100: laminar; 100 < Re < 2,500: transient; Re > 2,500: turbulent

rules.condition: WHEN scaling flow chemistry operations
rules.recommendation: Use typical flow rates: Lab scale 1-10 mL min⁻¹, Pilot plant scale 50-150 mL min⁻¹, Industrial scale 200-600 mL min⁻¹
rules.reasoning: Standard scaling progression for flow chemistry from laboratory to industrial applications
rules.quantitative: Lab: 1-10 mL/min; Pilot: 50-150 mL/min; Industrial: 200-600 mL/min

rules.condition: WHEN constructing continuous-flow microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use channels with diameters typically 10-500 μm, constructed of glass, quartz, silicon, polymers, or stainless steel
rules.reasoning: These are the standard materials and dimensions for microreactor construction

rules.condition: WHEN conducting flash chemistry reactions
rules.recommendation: Use high-resolution reaction time control to isolate intermediate products by adjusting residence time in multistep reaction sequences
rules.reasoning: Flash chemistry involves extremely fast reactions with short-lived highly reactive intermediates conducted in highly controlled manner for high selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN operating under novel process windows (NPW)
rules.recommendation: Use combined high-temperature and high-pressure flow regime >200°C and >50 bar to speed up conversion rates while maintaining selectivity
rules.reasoning: NPW operates at conditions beyond usual conditions to considerably speed up conversion rates while maintaining selectivity through temperature, pressure, or concentration increases

rules.condition: WHEN implementing process intensification (PI)
rules.recommendation: Increase parameters such as temperature and pressure to obtain equivalent or better results in terms of purity, selectivity, and yield in reduced time period with enhanced throughput
rules.reasoning: PI is defined as ability to obtain equivalent or better results with enhanced throughput by increasing process parameters

rules.condition: WHEN designing multiple reactor configurations
rules.recommendation: Interconnect flow reactors to form multiple lines in parallel and consecutive setups, and link with in-line analytical tools (UV, IR, HPLC, GLC, mass spectrometry, NMR) for real-time analysis and feedback
rules.reasoning: Allows for rapid automated optimization of reaction parameters with real-time monitoring

rules.condition: WHEN producing hazardous starting materials
rules.recommendation: Use on-site/on-demand production where products are immediately used after generation to save transport, storage, and handling of hazardous agents
rules.reasoning: On-demand production is feasible at remote or multiple locations and eliminates safety risks of storing hazardous materials
rules.exceptions: Only feasible if products are needed or precursors are available

rules.condition: WHEN you need to deliver required equivalents of reagent B at a lower flow rate due to solubility limitations
rules.recommendation: Prepare a more concentrated solution of B (e.g. 1 mL min of a 0.15 M solution of B) or increase the length of the reaction loop (e.g. a 10 mL loop would provide 4 min residence time)
rules.reasoning: Higher concentration allows delivery of same equivalents at lower flow rates, longer loops provide extended residence time for complete reaction
rules.quantitative: Example: 1 mL/min of 0.15 M solution, 10 mL loop provides 4 min residence time
rules.exceptions: Only applicable when compound B has sufficient solubility to prepare concentrated solutions

rules.condition: WHEN performing reactions requiring elevated temperatures to accelerate kinetics
rules.recommendation: Use superheating of solvent (THF at 120°C) in contained flow environment
rules.reasoning: Contained flow environment minimizes the impact of moisture on reagents and reactive carbene intermediate, process is readily scaled to produce multigram quantities

rules.condition: WHEN working with high oxygen pressure reactions
rules.recommendation: Avoid high (partial) pressure oxygen to minimize the risk of solvent ignition, but balance safety with reaction efficiency
rules.reasoning: High oxygen pressure may give faster reactions, but creates significant safety hazards due to solvent ignition risk
rules.exceptions: When reaction efficiency requirements outweigh safety concerns with proper safety measures

rules.condition: WHEN mechanical agitation is not sufficient for mixing
rules.recommendation: Exploit sonication in flow mode for enhanced mixing
rules.reasoning: Sonication provides additional mixing energy beyond mechanical agitation

rules.condition: WHEN working with solids and slurries in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use flow systems for controlled and reproducible crystallization to give specific crystal morphology
rules.reasoning: Flow allows controlled crystallization which is important in API synthesis and offers facile product isolation

rules.condition: WHEN operating high temperature batch setups
rules.recommendation: Implement efficient insulation strategies, often requiring unattractive high-boiling solvents and monitoring (p, T) to guarantee safe operations
rules.reasoning: High temperature operations pose safety risks that must be mitigated through proper insulation and monitoring

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow processes for safety
rules.recommendation: Implement in-line temperature and pressure control as well as automated shutdown processes
rules.reasoning: Necessary in the interest of process safety to prevent dangerous conditions

rules.condition: WHEN reagents need to be mixed at low temperature to minimize degradation
rules.recommendation: Use precooling loops to ensure reagents are mixed at appropriately low temperature
rules.reasoning: Precooling loops are important to ascertain that reagents are mixed at an appropriately low temperature to minimize degradation of reactive species
rules.quantitative: Appropriately low temperature (specific temperature not given)

rules.condition: WHEN working with metals in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use plate reactors with advanced static mixers to accommodate better mixing
rules.reasoning: Metals are characterized by high heat capacity, and plate reactors can easily accommodate advanced static mixers to provide for better mixing

rules.condition: WHEN using immobilized enzymes in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Consider that immobilization allows for easy separation but may be time-consuming and reduce enzyme activity
rules.reasoning: Immobilization allows for easy separation (post reaction), increased mass transfer, reduced product inhibition, and increased stability (mechanically and chemically), but can be time-consuming and may reduce the enzyme activity

rules.condition: WHEN using packed columns with enzymes
rules.recommendation: Pack substrate in a column to increase contact between substrate and enzyme and create local excess of enzyme
rules.reasoning: Contact between substrate and enzyme is increased, thus leading to faster reactions. The substrate experiences a local excess of enzyme if packed in a column

rules.condition: WHEN setting up photochemical reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Provide short and constant distance between lamp and tubing (or chip) in combination with narrow tubing/channel diameters
rules.reasoning: Accounts for uniformity in light exposure across the reaction mixture
rules.quantitative: Narrow tubing/channel diameters, short and constant distance

rules.condition: WHEN direct irradiation of substrates is not feasible or effective
rules.recommendation: Employ a photosensitizer or photocatalyst to absorb and transfer the light energy to the substrate
rules.reasoning: Indirect light activation allows reactions to proceed when direct substrate irradiation is ineffective

rules.condition: WHEN scaling electrochemical systems from batch to flow
rules.recommendation: Address the challenge of controlling interelectrode distance, as scalability of batch systems can be inefficient and may lead to safety concerns
rules.reasoning: Control of interelectrode distance is challenging in batch, where electrolytes are often needed. Scalability of batch systems can be inefficient and may lead to safety concerns

rules.condition: WHEN using electrons and photons as reagents
rules.recommendation: Utilize these traceless reagents to obviate the need for removal as in analogous reagent-based scenarios
rules.reasoning: Electrons (and photons) are traceless reagents, obviating the need for removal as in analogous reagent-based scenarios. This increases the overall atom-economy

rules.condition: WHEN designing biomolecular reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Avoid harmful solvents in the synthesis (not the purification) to improve PMI and e-factor, but note this can lead to higher viscosity and heat dissipation issues
rules.reasoning: Faster kinetics result in biomolecular reactions. Harmful solvents are avoided in the synthesis which also improves the PMI and e-factor of the process. In flow mode, this can lead to higher viscosity (pump issues) and heat dissipation may be affected
rules.exceptions: Heat dissipation and pump viscosity issues may occur

rules.condition: WHEN designing multi-step flow processes
rules.recommendation: Ensure sufficient optimization of individual steps and evaluate whether spent reagents and impurities affect downstream reactions (need for in-line purification)
rules.reasoning: Sufficient optimization of individual steps is necessary along with evaluation of whether spent reagents and impurities affect downstream reactions. Increasing the dilution will impact kinetics and can lead to inefficient late stages

rules.condition: WHEN selecting solvents for flow chemistry considering environmental impact
rules.recommendation: Note that THF, chlorinated solvents, and toluene are classified as harmful due to their environmental impact, while ethyl acetate is more susceptible towards hydrolysis (when NaOH is used)
rules.reasoning: Environmental impact and chemical stability considerations for solvent selection in flow processes
rules.exceptions: Ethyl acetate susceptibility to hydrolysis with NaOH

rules.condition: WHEN working with fuming nitric acid
rules.recommendation: Implement effective ventilation or scrubbing due to its very corrosive nature and strong oxidant properties (e.g. toward stainless steel), and because harmful nitrous oxides are liberated
rules.reasoning: Fuming nitric acid is very corrosive and is a strong oxidant, harmful nitrous oxides are liberated and there is a need for effective ventilation or scrubbing

rules.condition: WHEN calculating flow rates and residence times for specific stoichiometry
rules.recommendation: Use the equations: Total flow rate = 5 mL/40 min = 0.125 mL min, X = 0.125/(1 + 1.5) × 1 = 0.05 mL min, Z = 0.125/(1 + 1.5) × 1.5 = 0.075 mL min
rules.reasoning: Mathematical relationships for calculating individual component flow rates based on total flow rate and stoichiometric ratios
rules.quantitative: Total flow rate = 5 mL/40 min = 0.125 mL min, X = 0.05 mL min, Z = 0.075 mL min for 1:1.5 stoichiometry

rules.condition: WHEN needing to increase residence time while maintaining stoichiometry
rules.recommendation: Replace the reactor with a bigger one (e.g. 10 mL) so that flow rates become X = 0.1 and Z = 0.15 mL min
rules.reasoning: Larger reactor volume provides longer residence time while maintaining proper stoichiometric ratios
rules.quantitative: 10 mL reactor, X = 0.1 mL/min, Z = 0.15 mL/min

rules.condition: WHEN calculating complex stoichiometric flow systems
rules.recommendation: Solve the system of equations: X + Z = 0.133 (total flow rate) and 1.5 × Z: 2 × X = 2:1 (stoichiometric ratio), giving Solution: X = 0.09 and Z = 0.24
rules.reasoning: Mathematical approach to determine individual flow rates when dealing with complex stoichiometry and dilution effects
rules.quantitative: Total flow rate = 5 mL/15 min = 0.33 mL min, X = 0.09, Z = 0.24, stoichiometry 1.5 × Z: 2 × X = 2:1

rules.condition: WHEN calculating reaction quantities in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use the formula: concentration (mmol/mL) × flow rate (mL/min) × time (min) = total amount (mmol)
rules.reasoning: Basic calculation for determining total reagent consumption over time in continuous flow

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring aldol products for quality control
rules.recommendation: Exercise caution for possible elimination of the aldol product to the corresponding α,β-unsaturated ketone during analysis
rules.reasoning: The aldol product can undergo elimination reaction to form the α,β-unsaturated ketone during analytical procedures

rules.condition: WHEN validating microlot quality in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use downstream eight-port valve to isolate a segment of product stream for analysis while allowing continuous flow through the second sample holding loop
rules.reasoning: Allows temporary isolation of microlots for analysis from integrated PAT downstream while maintaining continuous operation

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow synthesis processes involving gas reagents
rules.recommendation: Plan for gas-liquid separation after the flow synthesis since gas is used as reagent/carrier and needs separation from liquid stream
rules.reasoning: Gas serves dual purpose as reagent and carrier, requiring separation for product recovery and purification

rules.condition: WHEN implementing continuous liquid-liquid separation in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use continuous liquid-liquid separation for biphasic reactive streams, particularly organic-aqueous reactions, and for flow-based solvent extraction
rules.reasoning: Allows purification operations to be conducted in flow, enabling complete flow synthesis implementation

rules.condition: WHEN detecting liquid-liquid interfaces in gravity-based separators
rules.recommendation: Use capacitance sensor, impedance probe, or camera-based approach to detect the liquid-liquid interface
rules.reasoning: Multiple sensor options provide flexibility for interface detection in different system configurations

rules.condition: WHEN operating membrane-based liquid-liquid separators
rules.recommendation: Set lower limit as ΔPper = (8μorgQorg L)/(πR⁴mem) and upper limit as ΔPc = (2γ/Rmem)cos θ
rules.reasoning: Pressure limits ensure proper separation while preventing membrane damage and maintaining selectivity
rules.quantitative: Lower limit: ΔPper = (8μorgQorg L)/(πR⁴mem), Upper limit: ΔPc = (2γ/Rmem)cos θ, where γ is interfacial tension, Rmem is pore size, θ is wetting angle, μorg is viscosity of organic phase, Qorg is flow rate of organic phase, L is membrane thickness

rules.condition: WHEN downstream pressures fluctuate in membrane separators
rules.recommendation: Adjust backpressure regulators in real time, otherwise separation will not be completed
rules.reasoning: Fluctuating pressures can disrupt the pressure differential required for selective membrane separation

rules.condition: WHEN implementing process intensification in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use extreme/harsh process conditions (high temperature, high pressure, high concentration) within the explosive regime for intensification, but ensure robust structure and small volume under continuous flow
rules.reasoning: High surface area of microreactors facilitates heat removal, and continuous flow provides transport intensification with small volume reducing risks
rules.quantitative: High temperature, high pressure, high concentration conditions

rules.condition: WHEN heat generation is faster than heat removal in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Implement safety measures as thermal runaways can lead to accidents despite high surface area facilitating heat removal
rules.reasoning: Even with enhanced heat transfer in microreactors, exothermic reactions can still cause thermal runaway if heat generation exceeds removal capacity

rules.condition: WHEN using forbidden chemicals in flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Generate reactive intermediates in-line from less dangerous precursors, allowing small amounts to be present at any given time
rules.reasoning: In-line generation minimizes inventory of hazardous materials while enabling use of reactive intermediates
rules.quantitative: Small amounts present at a given time

rules.condition: WHEN handling specific hazardous compounds in flow
rules.recommendation: Use flow chemistry for HN₃ (azide + acid), CH₂N₂ (N-methyl-N-nitrosocompounds + base), ArLi (RLi + ArH/ArX), hydrogen peroxides, halogens (fluorine, chlorine, bromine), carbon monoxide, sulfur dioxide, and hydrazine
rules.reasoning: These compounds are particularly hazardous but can be safely handled in small quantities in flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN operating flow chemistry systems
rules.recommendation: Use appropriate PPE and CPE (personal and collective protection equipment), quench dangerous intermediates in-line to avoid accumulation, monitor reactor operating parameters (pressure, temperature, flow rate) to ensure nominal conditions and shutdown if necessary, develop clear SOPs (standard operation procedures)
rules.reasoning: Comprehensive safety measures are essential for safe operation of flow chemistry systems
rules.quantitative: Continuous monitoring of pressure, temperature, flow rate

rules.condition: WHEN reactor precipitation occurs in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use higher reaction temperature or sonicator to avoid reactor precipitation, as it can result in reactor blockage and leaks of hazardous materials
rules.reasoning: Precipitation can cause physical blockages and create safety hazards through system failure

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts in flow
rules.recommendation: Homogeneous catalysts: advantage is possibility of using batch reaction conditions, disadvantage is requirement for quenching step. Heterogeneous catalysts: advantage is no quenching required and no contamination of catalyst residue to product solution, disadvantage is requirement of immobilization study and technique
rules.reasoning: Different catalyst types have distinct advantages and challenges that affect flow process design

rules.condition: WHEN calculating catalyst performance in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use WHSV = 2 × 10⁴C₁v₁/n₁W₁ for weight hourly space velocity calculations
rules.reasoning: WHSV provides standardized measure of catalyst utilization efficiency in flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN improving catalyst performance in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Reduce C₁ by half, reduce v₁ by half, or increase W₁ by doubling it to reduce WHSV and increase contact opportunities between substrate and catalyst
rules.reasoning: Lower WHSV increases contact time and opportunities for reaction, improving conversion and yield but with possibility of overreactions
rules.exceptions: Possibility of overreactions at very low WHSV

rules.condition: WHEN measuring residence time in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use a tracer compound (e.g. some kind of dye, or a compound which can be traced by UV spectrophotometry, and TLC)
rules.reasoning: Tracer compounds provide direct measurement of actual residence time in the system

rules.condition: WHEN generating hazardous or unstable substances in flow
rules.recommendation: Use on-demand generation from benign precursors, examples include diazomethane, chlorine, carbon monoxide, and HCN
rules.reasoning: On-demand generation minimizes storage and handling risks of dangerous compounds

rules.condition: When designing microreactors for multiphase flow
rules.recommendation: Incorporate parallel plates or channels that allow fluid to flow in thin films to cause multiphase flow like bubbles and emulsions.
rules.reasoning: This structure facilitates microscale discontinuous multiphase flow, providing better control and efficiency in reactions.
rules.quantitative: Microscale dimensions usually in micrometers up to 1 mm
rules.exceptions: Designs that do not require phase separation or mixing

rules.condition: When scaling up process chemistry from lab scale to industrial scale
rules.recommendation: Use 'numbering-up' with multiple microreactors in parallel to achieve desired volume outputs. Ensure consistent residence time across microchannels.
rules.reasoning: Microreactors allow for scaling by multiplying the number of units rather than increasing size, maintaining control over reaction conditions.
rules.quantitative: System of bifurcating ducts, ensuring equal flow distribution with channel lengths after bends
rules.exceptions: Single-element microreactors without the need for increased outputs

rules.condition: When selecting materials for construction of microreactors under specific process demands
rules.recommendation: Select materials such as glass, polymers, or metals, based on their chemical and thermal compatibility with the process. Use materials that can withstand the necessary pressures and temperatures.
rules.reasoning: Different materials offer varying resistances to chemicals and temperature, impacting the microreactor's durability and effectiveness.
rules.quantitative: Selection based on specific process conditions and compatibility with UV, IR, and microwave energies

rules.condition: When designing reactors for heating/cooling-dependent reactions
rules.recommendation: Integrate heating or cooling units with microdevices for controlled reaction temperatures, potentially using coatings like palladium for specific reaction facilitation.
rules.reasoning: Thermal control is crucial for reactions that are temperature sensitive, ensuring efficiency and product stability.
rules.quantitative: Can be reused more than 20 times in some cases like the palladium coating

rules.condition: For reactions limited by diffusion in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use small diameter channels to reduce diffusion limitation, dramatically reducing reaction time as compared to flask-based reactions.
rules.reasoning: Small diameters increase the relative surface area and reduce the distance over which diffusion must occur, speeding up reactions.
rules.quantitative: Diffusion time t = x^2/D; up to 1000 times faster in 100 μm diameter reactors compared to 10 cm flasks

rules.condition: When segmenting fluid flow in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Configure the system to allow internal vortex creation within the fluid segments to speed up mixing.
rules.reasoning: The internal vortex leads to rapid mixing within segment contents, counteracting the lack of mixing typical in microscale fluid flow.
rules.exceptions: Simple single-phase continuous flow without segmentation requirement

rules.condition: When delivering fluids in microreactor systems
rules.recommendation: Utilize hydrodynamic pumping systems for high-pressure requirements but consider EO (electroosmotic) flow for reduced dispersion.
rules.reasoning: Hydrodynamic pumping achieves high pressures but can experience flow fluctuations; EO flow offers low hydrodynamic dispersion.
rules.quantitative: Use of macroscopic or microscale peristaltic or positive displacement pumps
rules.exceptions: Non-continuous processes where precise control is not required

rules.condition: When dealing with low Reynolds number geometries in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Employ complex geometries and active mixing techniques like acoustic streaming and cavitation microstreaming to enhance mixing.
rules.reasoning: Laminar flow is dominant in microreactors leading to poor mixing. Techniques like acoustic streaming can induce effective mixing by causing repeated fluid twisting and flattening.
rules.quantitative: Reynolds number generally low; specific techniques include repeated fluid twisting and flattening, acoustic streaming, resonant diaphragms.
rules.exceptions: Not required for high Reynolds number operations.

rules.condition: When active mixing is required in stopped-flow conditions
rules.recommendation: Use methodologies that enable mixing where there is no flow, such as temporarily stopped-flow conditions in microchannel reactors.
rules.reasoning: Active methodologies can induce mixing effectively even in the absence of continuous flow by using centrifugal forces or other controlled flow interventions.
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when continuous flow is maintained.

rules.condition: For microreactors handling unstable intermediates and exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Design microreactors with enhanced heat and mass transfer by maximizing surface area with features like pillared or ribbed structures.
rules.reasoning: Large surface areas afford enhanced heat and mass transfer which is crucial for controlling exothermic reactions and stabilizing intermediates.
rules.quantitative: Surface-to-volume ratios can range up to 20,000 m²/m³ compared to 1,000 m²/m³ in conventional reactors.
rules.exceptions: Not necessary for non-exothermic, stable reactions.

rules.condition: For microreactors used in photocatalytic reactions
rules.recommendation: Use materials like Pyrex, amorphous fluoropolymers, or quartz, with a preference for quartz for higher operational temperatures and lower light attenuation.
rules.reasoning: Quartz facilitates both high operational temperatures and allows better light penetration for photocatalysis.
rules.quantitative: Operational temperatures and ultraviolet light wavelength handling are key considerations.
rules.exceptions: Lower temperature and light attenuation needs may allow use of other materials.

rules.condition: When transitioning from macro to micro scale for exothermic synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use microreactors for reactions that are highly exothermic at macro scale like the synthesis of m-anisaldehyde.
rules.reasoning: Microreactors can handle the heat of reaction more efficiently due to their high surface area, reducing safety risks and improving reaction control.
rules.quantitative: Reaction temperatures were reduced notably in microreactors (performed at 273 K instead of higher temperatures in macroscale).

rules.condition: For enhancing catalytic reactions within microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use ionic liquids to dissolve catalysts in a liquid-liquid two-phase system, especially for reactions with solid/liquid heterogeneous systems.
rules.reasoning: This helps overcome the mass transport resistance and facilitates the reaction in microchannels.
rules.quantitative: Particularly useful for reactions such as Sonogashira coupling.
rules.exceptions: Not needed if catalysts are readily soluble in the primary solvent.

rules.condition: When performing the DABCO-promoted Baylis–Hillman reaction in a microreactor
rules.recommendation: Use a residence time of 2 hours for continuous flow and 9 hours and 50 minutes for stopped flow to achieve complete conversion.
rules.reasoning: Shorter residence time in continuous flow reduces reaction time compared to batch processes while maintaining efficiency.
rules.exceptions: Not applicable if batch processing is used.

rules.condition: When synthesizing N-heterocyclic compounds like Fanetizole
rules.recommendation: Use a microflow system with channels 300 µm wide and 115 µm deep at a constant temperature of 70 °C.
rules.reasoning: Utilizing specific microchannel dimensions and temperature control ensures efficient reaction conditions, high yield, and purity.
rules.quantitative: 300 µm x 115 µm channel dimensions, 70 °C temperature
rules.exceptions: Reactions that do not require high yield syntheses may not need these conditions.

rules.condition: When performing the synthesis of 2-amino-1,3-diketones using flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use a solvent system of MeOH and H2O in an 80:20 ratio.
rules.reasoning: A mixed solvent system enhances solubility and reactivity, facilitating the desired reaction outcome.
rules.exceptions: Reaction types not compatible with MeOH or H2O as solvents.

rules.condition: When using a glass capillary tube microflow system
rules.recommendation: Apply microwave irradiation at 170 W for enhanced reaction efficiency.
rules.reasoning: Microwave irradiation provides rapid and uniform heating, increasing reaction rates and product yield.
rules.exceptions: Reactions sensitive to temperature or those that do not require accelerated rates.

rules.condition: When performing the Sonogashira coupling reaction in a microreactor system
rules.recommendation: Use Pd N-heterocyclic carbene complex in the absence of a Cu cocatalyst for an efficient reaction.
rules.reasoning: The specified catalyst system enables smooth progression of the coupling reaction with high yield.
rules.quantitative: Pd NHC complex, no Cu cocatalyst
rules.exceptions: Reactions specifically requiring copper to proceed.

rules.condition: When achieving high Z/E ratios in Wittig reactions in a continuous flow system
rules.recommendation: Control the voltage and flow rate to maintain the desired Z/E ratio between 0.57 and 5.2.
rules.reasoning: Precise control of reaction parameters in flow systems ensures selectivity and product distribution.
rules.exceptions: Batch reactions with fixed reactant ratios leading inherently to different Z/E ratios.

rules.condition: WHEN integrating catalyst recycling in a continuous flow system for Mizoroki–Heck reactions
rules.recommendation: Use a continuous-microflow reactor with dual microextraction system using T-shaped micromixers (300 µm i.d.) and ionic liquid, [bmim]NTf2, to ensure smooth flow and catalyst recycling.
rules.reasoning: This setup allows for the integration of reaction, separation, and recycling steps in a continuous fashion, improving efficiency and catalyst reuse.
rules.quantitative: 300 µm i.d. micromixers, residence time: 17.8 min

rules.condition: WHEN carrying out photochemical transformations
rules.recommendation: Use microreactors with short optical path lengths for efficient and homogeneous illumination to minimize side reactions and utilize light energy effectively.
rules.reasoning: Short optical path lengths enhance the uniformity of light exposure, improving reaction outcomes and energy efficiency.
rules.quantitative: Typical effective optical path length: 50 µm

rules.condition: WHEN working with radical photopinacolization in microflow systems
rules.recommendation: Use a photomicroreactor with a width of 500 µm and a depth of 250 or 500 µm, with an online UV analysis probe for process optimization.
rules.reasoning: This setup provides high conversion rates for concentrated solutions and allows for real-time monitoring and optimization.

rules.condition: WHEN performing cycloaddition of alkenes and vinyl acetate microflow reactions
rules.recommendation: Utilize a microreactor equipped with a heat exchanger to maintain isothermal conditions and achieve high yields.
rules.reasoning: Maintaining precise temperature control enhances reaction efficiency and product yield.

rules.condition: WHEN using singlet oxygen in a microflow system
rules.recommendation: Employ a glass chip microreactor with a shallow optical path length and a high concentration of sensitizer for efficient photosensitization.
rules.reasoning: This configuration reduces heating, enhances photosensitization, and ensures safety by reducing the volume of reactive gases.
rules.quantitative: Optical path length: 50 µm, Sensitizer concentration: 5 mM

rules.condition: WHEN performing photosensitized oxidation reactions in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use LED light sources and microreactors with small channel dimensions to improve photonic efficiency and product yield.
rules.reasoning: LEDs provide focused light with specific wavelengths, enhancing reaction selectivity and efficiency.

rules.condition: WHEN conducting photocatalytic reductions using TiO2-coated microchannels
rules.recommendation: Use quartz microreactors with precise UV-LED settings for optimal reaction conditions and conversion efficiency.
rules.reasoning: Quartz allows proper transmission of UV light, and precise LED settings optimize light exposure for effective photocatalysis.

rules.condition: When conducting Friedel–Crafts alkylation of aromatic compounds with electrochemically generated carbocations.
rules.recommendation: Use a microflow system with a multilamination micromixer to achieve high monoalkylation selectivity.
rules.reasoning: Inefficient mixing leads to poor selectivity due to local deviations in reagent concentrations. A micromixer provides rapid mixing and precise control, enhancing monoalkylation.
rules.quantitative: Microflow yields: monoalkylation 92%, dialkylation 4%. Batch yields: monoalkylation 37%, dialkylation 32%.

rules.condition: When scaling up microreaction systems for controlled polymerization.
rules.recommendation: Utilize a microflow system with micromixers connected to microtube reactors to maintain tight molecular weight distribution during polymerization.
rules.reasoning: Rapid mixing and precise control of polymerization temperature result in tighter molecular weight distribution compared to batch systems.

rules.condition: When performing Swern oxidation at room temperature.
rules.recommendation: Maintain a short residence time between 2-5 seconds to ensure high efficiency and minimize side products.
rules.reasoning: Short residence times allow immediate transfer of unstable intermediates, preventing side reactions and decomposition.
rules.quantitative: Residence time: 2-5 seconds. Reaction sustained at room temperature with yields 70-95%.
rules.exceptions: Residence time might vary if extreme reaction conditions are used.

rules.condition: When performing a Grignard exchange reaction in a microflow system.
rules.recommendation: Employ a 'shell and tube' heat exchanger with multiple microtubes bundled together for efficient reaction performance.
rules.reasoning: The exchanger allows rapid heat removal and precise temperature control, which is necessary for highly exothermic reactions.
rules.quantitative: Internal diameter of microtubes: 490 μm; length: 200 mm.

rules.condition: When performing halogen–lithium exchange reactions at a small scale.
rules.recommendation: Use a microflow setup to maintain the temperature at −17°C for high yields and safety in handling highly reactive reagents.
rules.reasoning: Microflow setups offer efficient control over exothermic reactions, thus reducing safety hazards.
rules.quantitative: Reaction temperature: −17°C. Yield: 93%. Reaction time: 5 min.

rules.condition: When synthesizing phenyl boronic acid at industrial scales.
rules.recommendation: Implement a micromixer-tubular system capable of operating at higher temperatures with minimal side products formation.
rules.reasoning: Efficient mixing and temperature control mitigate side reactions during synthesis at elevated temperatures.
rules.quantitative: Micromixer width: 40 μm, depth: 300 μm. Yield: >80% at 22 or 50°C.

rules.condition: When synthesizing peptides using a macroporous polymer resin
rules.recommendation: Incorporate an in-line UV detector to constantly monitor the progress of the reaction.
rules.reasoning: UV detection helps in determining the completion of reactions and reduces the need for excess reagents.

rules.condition: When the reaction requires a larger amount of compound
rules.recommendation: Use a parallel setup of reactors for improvement over a single large reagent column.
rules.reasoning: Parallel reactors allow for scaling up without reducing the efficiency or purity of the reaction.
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for reactions that require serial processing

rules.condition: When using solid-supported reagents in column reactors
rules.recommendation: Use long columns and allow longer running times for reaction scaling.
rules.reasoning: Longer columns and extended reaction times enhance mass transfer and efficacy.
rules.exceptions: When recirculation suffices for mass transfer

rules.condition: Using systems with macroporous polymers for peptide synthesis
rules.recommendation: Backpressures encountered should be below 500-1000 psi, making metal-encased columns suitable.
rules.reasoning: High pressures can cause failure in non-metal systems.
rules.exceptions: If using specially reinforced polymer systems

rules.condition: When running a reaction that requires longer times than static flow allows
rules.recommendation: Use a recirculating setup to allow the reaction mixture to be continuously cycled through the reagent column.
rules.reasoning: Recirculation increases surface-to-substrate interactions and allows completion of slower reactions.
rules.exceptions: Reactions that degrade or decompose upon prolonged cycling

rules.condition: In use of polymer-supported catalysts for reactions like oxidation
rules.recommendation: The polymer can be regenerated to 99% of its original efficiency, though after several cycles degradation may occur.
rules.reasoning: Regeneration maintains cost-effectiveness and resource efficiency.
rules.exceptions: After excessive degradation beyond routine regeneration capability

rules.condition: Performing oxidation reactions using solid-supported reagents
rules.recommendation: Conduct the reaction at 40°C for 10 minutes residence time.
rules.reasoning: Established optimized conditions for oxidation reactions improve yield and efficiency.
rules.exceptions: If other temperature-sensitive substrates are involved

rules.condition: WHEN using PS-TBD columns for deprotonation of thiol-functionalized heterocycles
rules.recommendation: Use a column of PS-TBD and flow a solution of thiol-functionalized heterocycles through it to form a stable ion pair.
rules.reasoning: PS-TBD efficiently deprotonates thiol-functionalized heterocycles, allowing subsequent alkylation.

rules.condition: WHEN performing alkylation of thiolate in flow
rules.recommendation: Use alkyl bromide to flow through the column to react with the thiolate.
rules.reasoning: Alkyl bromide acts as an inexpensive alkylating agent to efficiently alkylate the thiolate.

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow reactors for heterocycle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use a glass chip heated to 60°C for mixing isocyanide and acid chloride.
rules.reasoning: This setup facilitates the formation of a reactive adduct for subsequent reactions.

rules.condition: WHEN using MFD reactors for reactions requiring longer residence times
rules.recommendation: Use a microcapillary flow disc (MFD) reactor to achieve longer residence times.
rules.reasoning: MFD reactors provide longer residence times, critical for certain reactions that require longer to go to completion.

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use larger columns and run the system for longer periods (~12h) to generate 10-20 g of compound.
rules.reasoning: Increasing column size and reaction time accommodates larger reaction scales.

rules.condition: When performing an oxidation of benzyl alcohol using PI-Ru catalyst
rules.recommendation: Use a mixed bed of PI-Ru and MgSO4, and ensure the system excludes moisture and air.
rules.reasoning: Moisture and air can degrade the catalyst, affecting conversion and yield.
rules.quantitative: 94% conversion and 92% yield were achieved over 8 hours.

rules.condition: When controlling product outcome in oxidation reactions using flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Adjust flow rates to control product selection: 650 µl/min for aldehydes, <50 µl/min for acids.
rules.reasoning: Different flow rates change the residence time, affecting the oxidation state of the product.
rules.quantitative: 650 µl/min (10 s residence) vs. <50 µl/min (>126 s residence)

rules.condition: When using N-propylammonium perruthenate (TPAP) for oxidations
rules.recommendation: Rejuvenate Ru(VII) by flowing NMO through the catalyst cartridge.
rules.reasoning: NMO helps regenerate the ruthenium species, maintaining catalyst activity.

rules.condition: During cross-coupling reactions using a palladium monolith
rules.recommendation: Apply recirculation to enhance yield and reusability of the catalyst.
rules.reasoning: Recirculation improves interaction between reactants and the catalyst, thereby increasing yield.
rules.quantitative: Yield decreased from 92% (second run) to 71% (seventh run)

rules.condition: For Heck reactions using imidazolium-functionalized monoliths
rules.recommendation: Use a flow reactor system at 200°C in DMF with a residence time of 3–4 min.
rules.reasoning: High temperature and controlled residence time ensure full conversion with low palladium loading.
rules.exceptions: Improper for reactions not requiring high temperatures or specific solvent compatibility.

rules.condition: To replace DMF with a more benign solvent in Heck reactions
rules.recommendation: Use EtOH with a 100 psi backpressure and a column temperature of 130°C.
rules.reasoning: EtOH is more environmentally friendly, and the setup maintains reaction efficiency.
rules.exceptions: May not apply if reaction conditions cannot accommodate EtOH without pressure regulation.

rules.condition: When employing microwave heating for Pd/SiO2 catalyzed Suzuki reactions
rules.recommendation: Use a thin gold film to improve heating efficiency and reduce substrate decomposition.
rules.reasoning: Gold films enhance microwave absorption and uniformity of heating.
rules.exceptions: If the gold film is not compatible with substrates or products.

rules.condition: In operations using compressed air to cool reactors in a microwave cavity
rules.recommendation: Continuously supply compressed air during heating phases to prevent overheating and maintain safety.
rules.reasoning: Air cooling prevents excessive temperature buildup, reducing risk of equipment failure or reaction hazards.
rules.quantitative: Temperatures and flow rates to ensure controlled conditions
rules.exceptions: If cooling is not feasible due to equipment constraints.

rules.condition: When using chiral oxazaborolidine systems for Diels–Alder reactions in a flow setup
rules.recommendation: Use an immobilized copolymerization of a sulfonamide-modified styrene monomer to produce polystyrene beads for the catalyst.
rules.reasoning: This setup improves asymmetric inductions compared to batch reactions.

rules.condition: When performing an enantioselective Diels–Alder reaction with catalyst 49
rules.recommendation: Cool the catalyst column to -30°C and use a 1:1.5 methyacrolein:cyclopentadiene solution.
rules.reasoning: This condition optimizes the yield and enantioselectivity of the desired product.

rules.condition: When using a monolithic polymer catalyst for Diels–Alder reactions
rules.recommendation: Apply the monolith in both one pass and 24 h recirculation conditions to compare yields.
rules.reasoning: This approach helps determine optimal conditions for yield improvement.
rules.quantitative: Yields observed: up to 55% with 23% ee over multiple passes

rules.condition: When using monoliths via grafting or copolymerization for Diels–Alder reactions
rules.recommendation: Expect 6-8 months of stability before regeneration is needed due to decreased activity.
rules.reasoning: Long-term performance may decline due to loss of bonding in catalytic complexes.

rules.condition: When milled catalyst 52 is used for Ene reactions
rules.recommendation: Cool the catalyst in a steel HPLC column to 0°C before starting the reaction.
rules.reasoning: Temperature control improves conversion and yield.

rules.condition: When running cyclopropanation with Ru-PyBox catalyst in flow
rules.recommendation: Test different starting material conditions, like CH2Cl2, neat, or scCO2, for optimal results.
rules.reasoning: Different solvents or neat conditions impact yield and selectivity.
rules.quantitative: Conversion: up to 92%; Yield: up to 72%

rules.condition: When doing asymmetric conjugate additions with Amberlyst 21
rules.recommendation: Conduct at 50°C with a flow rate of 120 μl/min and 6 h residence time.
rules.reasoning: These conditions are optimal for high yield and enantioselectivity.
rules.quantitative: Temperature: 50°C; Flow rate: 120 μl/min; Residence time: 6 h; Yield: 99%

rules.condition: When synthesizing β-lactams using an immobilized catalyst
