rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical oxidation of aromatic hydrocarbons to benzylamides
rules.recommendation: Use platinum cathode and stainless steel anode with methanesulfonic acid (MSA) as the optimum acid
rules.reasoning: MSA provides the best yields up to 63% and serves as proton source for counter reaction

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical amide synthesis to avoid decomposition
rules.recommendation: Neutralize the acid by mixing the electrochemical reactor outlet with a second stream containing ammonia in methanol
rules.reasoning: Acid neutralization prevents decomposition of the amide product
rules.quantitative: NH₃ (0.7 M) in MeOH at 0.5 mL min⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN performing quinone ketal formation from hydroquinone diether
rules.recommendation: Use C/PVDF anode, stainless steel cathode with potassium hydroxide as base and supporting electrolyte
rules.reasoning: This electrode combination provides the required oxidation potential for intramolecular alkoxylation

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic fluorination in flow electrochemistry
rules.recommendation: Use ionic liquids such as Et₃N·3HF in aprotic solvents instead of traditional solvents
rules.reasoning: Ionic liquids enable electrochemical fluorinations in aprotic conditions and provide better selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical fluorination with stoichiometric fluorine source
rules.recommendation: Use stoichiometric amounts of fluorine source to obtain monofluorinated product as main product
rules.reasoning: Stoichiometric amounts favor monofluorination, while excess fluorine leads to difluorinated products
rules.quantitative: Stoichiometric vs excess fluorine source affects product distribution
rules.exceptions: Doubling fluorine concentration increases difluorinated product formation

rules.condition: WHEN performing phenol-arene cross-coupling to avoid using HFIP solvent
rules.recommendation: Use methanol concentration higher than 20% v/v to overcome oxidation potential issues
rules.reasoning: Methanol acts as weak base, splits phenolic O-H bond, and lowers oxidation potential of deprotonated phenol form
rules.quantitative: >20% v/v methanol concentration, yields up to 44% with selectivities over 80%
rules.exceptions: Below 20% methanol concentration, cross-coupling efficiency decreases

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic substitution reactions with unstable carbocations
rules.recommendation: Use microflow systems to enable precise temperature control and very short residence times
rules.reasoning: Microflow systems prevent decomposition of unstable carbocations by minimizing residence time and enabling efficient temperature control
rules.quantitative: Very short residence times compared to batch reactions
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for reactions not involving unstable intermediates

rules.condition: WHEN using parallel laminar flow microreactors for electrochemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Ensure laminar flow by using very small reactor with separate inlet solutions pumped to anodic and cathodic sides
rules.reasoning: Laminar flow creates liquid-liquid interface where mass transfer occurs only via diffusion, preventing unwanted mixing and cross-contamination
rules.quantitative: Two separate inlet solutions, mass transfer only via diffusion
rules.exceptions: Must avoid mixing of streams to maintain oxidation potential separation

rules.condition: WHEN performing nucleophilic substitution in parallel laminar flow
rules.recommendation: Keep anolyte and catholyte separated - anolyte never reaches cathode, catholyte never reaches anode
rules.reasoning: Separation prevents nucleophiles (lower oxidation potential) from reaching anode where organic substrates are oxidized
rules.quantitative: Oxidation potential of nucleophiles < oxidation potential of organic substrates
rules.exceptions: Not applicable if oxidation potentials are not significantly different

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic C-C cross-coupling reactions in electrochemical flow
rules.recommendation: Use parallel laminar flow configuration to stabilize ionic liquid intermediates and enable reaction with nucleophiles before decomposition
rules.reasoning: The ionic liquid intermediates are stabilized by the parallel flow configuration, presenting a longer lifetime and being able to react with nucleophiles before they decompose

rules.condition: WHEN performing in situ electrogeneration of ortho-benzoquinones followed by reaction with nucleophiles
rules.recommendation: Connect the outlet of the microflow electrochemical reactor to a second stream containing the nucleophile
rules.reasoning: Ortho-quinones are too reactive and unstable to store, so they must be prepared in situ and reacted immediately with the desired reaction partner

rules.condition: WHEN both nucleophiles and catechols are present in an electrolysis cell
rules.recommendation: Ensure proper sequencing because nucleophiles will be oxidized first due to lower oxidation potentials
rules.reasoning: The oxidation potentials of nucleophiles are usually lower than the oxidation potentials of catechols, so if both reactants are mixed in an electrolysis cell, the nucleophile would be oxidized first

rules.condition: WHEN performing TEMPO-mediated oxidation of primary and secondary alcohols under flow conditions
rules.recommendation: Use C/PVDF anode and stainless steel cathode with 20 mA current, TEMPO (30 mol%), and maintain constant pH of 11.5 using carbonate buffer
rules.reasoning: TEMPO is oxidized at the anode to form the oxoammonium ion which is the active species that oxidizes alcohols to aldehydes or ketones
rules.quantitative: 20 mA current, 30 mol% TEMPO loading, pH 11.5, 0.1 mL min⁻¹ flow rate, 25°C

rules.condition: WHEN using TEMPO-mediated electrochemical oxidation
rules.recommendation: Maintain constant pH of 11.5 to avoid decomposition of the oxoammonium ion
rules.reasoning: A carbonate buffer is necessary to maintain constant pH and prevent decomposition of the oxoammonium intermediate

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical synthesis of isoindolinones using TEMPO mediator
rules.recommendation: Use graphite anode and platinum cathode in MeOH/H₂O (19:1) with BnNMe₃OH (0.5 eq.) and TEMPO (1.5 eq.)
rules.reasoning: This setup enables intramolecular hydroamination of benzamides to isoindolinones using TEMPO as both mediator and radical scavenger
rules.quantitative: 0.5 eq. BnNMe₃OH, 1.5 eq. TEMPO, MeOH/H₂O ratio 19:1

rules.condition: WHEN constructing electrochemical flow reactors for isoindolinone synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use graphite anode and platinum cathode with body consisting of aluminum plate or housing made from polymers using additive manufacturing (3D printing)
rules.reasoning: These materials provide suitable electrode surfaces and reactor construction for the electrochemical transformation

rules.condition: WHEN designing electrochemical flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Consider using FEP spacer with flow channel design as shown in reactor configurations
rules.reasoning: FEP spacers provide chemical inertness and define the flow channel geometry for proper electrolyte flow

rules.condition: WHEN performing post-electrochemical functionalization of TEMPO-containing products
rules.recommendation: Connect electrochemical reactor outlet to second reactor coil and use excess of base (BnNMe₃OH) and heat to eliminate TEMPO and form unsaturated compounds
rules.reasoning: The use of excess base and heat leads to elimination of TEMPO, giving unsaturated compounds as products

rules.condition: WHEN cleaving N-O bonds in TEMPO moiety from electrochemical products
rules.recommendation: Use acetic acid and zinc cartridge packed with zinc supported on silica to form free alcohol products
rules.reasoning: The N-O bond of the TEMPO moiety can be cleaved under these reducing conditions to form the corresponding free alcohol

rules.condition: WHEN performing N-heterocyclic carbene-mediated ester synthesis from aldehydes via anodic oxidation
rules.recommendation: Use Syrris flow cell with C/PVDF anode and stainless steel cathode, and achieve high productivity rates of 1.5-4.0 g h⁻¹
rules.reasoning: NHCs act as mediators for the oxidative process, enabling efficient ester formation from aldehydes with good to excellent yields

rules.condition: WHEN performing NHC-mediated ester synthesis with separate aldehyde and alcohol streams
rules.recommendation: Mix two streams where one contains aldehyde, alcohol, thiazolium salt and DMSO in THF, and the other contains DBU in THF, then introduce the mixed Breslow intermediate to the electrochemical cell
rules.reasoning: This setup prevents premature condensation of aldehyde with alcohol and enables proper formation of the Breslow intermediate before electrochemical oxidation
rules.quantitative: Flow rate: 0.5 mL min⁻¹ for both streams

rules.condition: WHEN performing NHC-mediated amide synthesis from aldehydes via anodic oxidation
rules.recommendation: Use three separate solutions to avoid condensation: thiazolium salt with aldehyde, DBU solution, and amine solution, all in DMSO
rules.reasoning: To avoid condensation of the aldehyde with the amine, both compounds are pumped separately, requiring three different solutions in DMSO

rules.condition: WHEN performing amide formation after electrochemical oxidation
rules.recommendation: Introduce the reactor outlet to a heating chip to speed up formation of the amide product
rules.reasoning: Heating accelerates the final amide formation step after the electrochemical oxidation has occurred

rules.condition: WHEN setting up NHC-mediated electrochemical synthesis with thiazolium salt
rules.recommendation: Use 850 mA current at anode/cathode with DBU (1.5 equiv), THF/DMSO solvent system at 0.5 mL min⁻¹
rules.reasoning: These conditions provide optimal current and base concentration for the NHC-mediated electrochemical transformation
rules.quantitative: 850 mA current, 1.5 equiv DBU, 0.5 mL min⁻¹ flow rate

rules.condition: WHEN performing NHC-mediated electrochemical synthesis of amides from aldehydes
rules.recommendation: Use flow rates of 4γ mL min⁻¹ with a heating chip in the electrochemical reactor
rules.reasoning: The synthesis requires controlled flow and heating to maintain reaction efficiency for amide formation

rules.condition: WHEN scaling out electrochemical reactions using multiple channels
rules.recommendation: Use 2-4 separate reaction channels with either a single set of electrodes or independent electrodes for each channel to increase productivity 2-4 fold
rules.reasoning: Multiple channels maintain reaction yield while increasing overall productivity proportionally to the number of channels

rules.condition: WHEN designing platinum electrodes for electrochemical flow cells
rules.recommendation: Use platinum foil electrodes with 8 mm width, 15 mm length, and 50 micron thickness
rules.reasoning: These dimensions provide optimal electrode surface area and current distribution for flow electrochemistry
rules.quantitative: 8 mm width × 15 mm length × 50 μm thickness

rules.condition: WHEN using PTFE spacers in electrochemical flow cells
rules.recommendation: Use PTFE spacers with equal dimension windows of 3 mm width and 21 mm length
rules.reasoning: Standardized spacer dimensions ensure consistent inter-electrode gap and flow characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN performing cathodic dimerization of 4-nitrobenzyl bromide
rules.recommendation: Use a flow rate of 0.04 mL min⁻¹ and current density of 2.4 mA cm⁻²
rules.reasoning: These conditions provide optimal electron transfer for the dimerization reaction while maintaining good conversion
rules.quantitative: 0.04 mL min⁻¹ flow rate, 2.4 mA cm⁻² current density

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical carboxylation to avoid metal ion contamination
rules.recommendation: Use flow microreactor with controlled carboxylate ion stabilization to achieve 95% yield
rules.reasoning: Flow control prevents metal ion contamination from sacrificial anodes (Mg, Al) and maintains carboxylate ion stability

rules.condition: WHEN performing electroreductive deprotection of iNoc protected groups
rules.recommendation: Use TBAI (50 mM) in DMF/H₂O (5:1) solvent system
rules.reasoning: This electrolyte and solvent combination provides optimal ionic conductivity for the deprotection reaction

rules.condition: WHEN performing electroreductive deprotection reactions
rules.recommendation: Use flow rate of 15 μL min⁻¹ with Pt anode/Pt cathode at 30 mA current
rules.reasoning: These conditions provide sufficient residence time and current for complete deprotection while maintaining good yields
rules.quantitative: 15 μL min⁻¹ flow rate, 30 mA current

rules.condition: WHEN designing paired electrolysis reactions
rules.recommendation: Utilize both anodic and cathodic reactions to obtain valuable products from both electrode transformations for higher combined electrochemical yield
rules.reasoning: Paired electrosynthesis maximizes atom economy by using both oxidation and reduction reactions productively instead of having sacrificial counter-reactions
rules.quantitative: Higher combined electrochemical yield compared to single electrode use
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when only one electrode reaction is desired

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing copper-NHC complexes in electrochemical flow
rules.recommendation: Use reactor dimensions of 50×50×1 mm separated by five PTFE spacers (1 mm thick) giving 4 mL total volume and 40 cm² surface area per electrode
rules.reasoning: These dimensions provide optimal surface area to volume ratio for metal-NHC complex formation
rules.quantitative: 50×50×1 mm plates, 1 mm PTFE spacers, 4 mL volume, 40 cm² electrode surface area

rules.condition: WHEN performing paired electrosynthesis requiring separate anolyte and catholyte introduction
rules.recommendation: Introduce anolyte and catholyte separately to achieve parallel laminar flow and avoid mixing of both streams
rules.reasoning: Separate introduction prevents premature mixing and maintains distinct reaction environments for each electrode, creating a stable liquid-liquid interface
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when intentional mixing is desired

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical propylene epoxidation
rules.recommendation: Use parallel plate flow electrochemical reactor with interdigitated electrodes on alumina substrate
rules.reasoning: Interdigitated electrode configuration on alumina provides optimal current distribution and heat dissipation for epoxidation reactions

rules.condition: WHEN designing electrochemical flow reactors for paired electrosynthesis of gluconic acid and sorbitol from glucose
rules.recommendation: Use parallel plate reactor configuration with 3 mm electrode separation for optimal yields
rules.reasoning: Parallel plate reactor gives higher yields (50% gluconic acid, 23% sorbitol) compared to packed bed reactors due to better current distribution and mass transfer
rules.quantitative: 3 mm electrode separation, 50% yield gluconic acid, 23% yield sorbitol

rules.condition: WHEN constructing packed bed electrochemical flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Fill cathodes with zinc shots (0.5 cm) and anodes with cylindrical shaped graphite chips (0.3 cm diameter)
rules.reasoning: These materials provide appropriate electrode surface area and conductivity for the electrochemical reactions
rules.quantitative: Zinc shots: 0.5 cm, graphite chips: 0.3 cm diameter

rules.condition: WHEN designing three different electrochemical flow reactor types
rules.recommendation: Use amalgamated zinc as cathode and graphite as anode material
rules.reasoning: These materials provide the necessary electrochemical properties for the paired electrosynthesis reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing paired electrolysis with unstable carbocations generated at the anode
rules.recommendation: Carry out the reaction at -78°C
rules.reasoning: Low temperature is needed to deal with unstable carbocations generated at the anode and prevent decomposition

rules.condition: WHEN performing sequential anodic and cathodic reactions in electrochemical flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use microflow electrochemical reactor with very small distance between electrodes (micrometer scale)
rules.reasoning: Small electrode distance enables rapid diffusion of electrochemically generated species from one electrode to the other, making sequential paired electrolysis feasible

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical conversion of dichlorophenol derivative to diaryl ether in microflow reactor
rules.recommendation: Achieve 41% yield and 100% selectivity for the desired diaryl ether product
rules.reasoning: The small electrode gap in microflow reactors enables efficient sequential electrochemical reactions with high selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN electrochemical synthesis of dibenzylamine from benzylamine requires high conversion efficiency
rules.recommendation: Use narrow interelectrode gap to ensure efficient diffusion between anode and cathode
rules.reasoning: Narrow gap prevents decomposition of intermediates and allows efficient conversion (74% yield in flow vs 3% yield in batch)
rules.quantitative: 74% yield in microflow vs 3% yield in batch

rules.condition: WHEN anodic and cathodic compartments need separation to prevent interference
rules.recommendation: Use divided cells with diaphragm or porous material to separate chambers while allowing current flow
rules.reasoning: Separation prevents products or electrode reactions from interfering with each other while maintaining electrical conductivity

rules.condition: WHEN using cation flow method for combinatorial organic syntheses
rules.recommendation: Perform reactions at low temperature by immersing reactor in dry ice bath and analyze products inline by FTIR spectroscopy
rules.reasoning: Low temperature stabilizes reactive cation intermediates, and inline analysis allows real-time monitoring of reaction progress

rules.condition: WHEN setting up cation flow electrochemical system
rules.recommendation: Use trifluoromethanesulfonic acid as sacrificial compound at the cathode to reduce protons and generate hydrogen gas
rules.reasoning: This compound effectively reduces protons to generate hydrogen gas as a sacrificial reaction at the cathode

rules.condition: WHEN designing electrochemical flow cells for dehalogenation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use a divided cell with carbon felt anode and wire coil cathode, separated by a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane
rules.reasoning: This configuration enables effective electrochemical transformations with proper electrode separation and ion transport
rules.quantitative: Carbon felt anode, wire coil cathode, PTFE membrane separator

rules.condition: WHEN designing modular electrochemical flow cells using Teflon blocks
rules.recommendation: Use two Teflon blocks (100×40×16 mm) with an opening (60×20×3 mm) where electrodes can be placed
rules.reasoning: This modular design allows for flexible electrode placement and thermal treatment for electrode positioning

rules.condition: WHEN positioning electrodes in Teflon-based electrochemical cells
rules.recommendation: Heat to 200°C to expand the Teflon, place electrode in cavity, then cool to contract and hold electrode, sealing the gap
rules.reasoning: Thermal expansion and contraction of Teflon provides a tight seal around the electrode without mechanical fasteners

rules.condition: WHEN designing electrochemical cells with variable spacing
rules.recommendation: Use a gasket/spacer with variable thickness between 0.12 and 2.0 mm to separate electrodes and serve as reaction channel
rules.reasoning: Variable spacing allows optimization of mass transfer and reaction conditions for different transformations

rules.condition: WHEN creating divided electrochemical cells
rules.recommendation: Sandwich a Nafion membrane between two spacers to create separate anodic and cathodic compartments
rules.reasoning: This configuration prevents cross-contamination while allowing ion transport between compartments

rules.condition: WHEN constructing electrochemical flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use stainless steel for structural components and tighten with eight screws for proper sealing
rules.reasoning: Stainless steel provides chemical resistance and mechanical strength, while multiple screws ensure uniform pressure distribution

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic oxidation followed by cathodic reduction in undivided cells
rules.recommendation: Use thin spacers (0.12 mm minimum) to ensure good mass transfer between anode and cathode
rules.reasoning: Product formed at the anode must diffuse to the cathode for the second transformation, requiring minimal distance

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up electrochemical dehalogenation from batch to flow
rules.recommendation: Flow reactors can improve yield from 41% (batch) to 63% (flow) for nitrile formation reactions
rules.reasoning: Flow-type reactors demonstrate clear advantages in mass transfer efficiency compared to batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical dehalogenation in flow
rules.recommendation: Use CuSn2Pd15 cathode at 0.6 mL min⁻¹ flow rate with 20 F charge, using DMF and [Et4NMe]O3SOMe as electrolyte
rules.reasoning: This electrode material and electrolyte system provides high yield (70%) and excellent enantioselectivity (99% ee)
rules.quantitative: Flow rate: 0.6 mL min⁻¹, Charge: 20 F, Yield: 70%, ee: 99%

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic oxidation of oximes to nitrile-N-oxides
rules.recommendation: Use MeCN/H2O (1:1:1) as solvent system for both oxidation and reduction steps
rules.reasoning: This solvent mixture provides appropriate conductivity and solubility for the electrochemical transformation

rules.condition: WHEN performing C-H functionalization reactions with highly reactive species (strong oxidants)
rules.recommendation: Use flow techniques to reduce the amount of chemicals subjected to reaction conditions at any given time, minimizing risk of fire or explosions
rules.reasoning: Flow techniques allow production of strongly reactive intermediates in small amounts that immediately react without accumulating
rules.quantitative: Small amounts produced continuously vs. large batch quantities

rules.condition: WHEN performing C-H functionalization reactions requiring high temperatures
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors to achieve better temperature control compared to batch alternatives
rules.reasoning: Higher degree of control of reaction temperature can be exerted in flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical C-H functionalization reactions
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors for better performance compared to batch due to high surface-area-to-volume ratio
rules.reasoning: High surface-area-to-volume ratio results in reduced path length and more efficient irradiation

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up C-H functionalization processes
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors to minimize time, resources and waste generation associated with process intensification
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors generally allow easier scale-up of processes

rules.condition: WHEN performing heterogeneous catalysis for C-H functionalization
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors packed with catalyst for easy reuse and increased turnover number
rules.reasoning: Allows easy reuse of catalyst and reduces metal contamination of products, simplifying purification and minimizing waste

rules.condition: WHEN performing oxidative coupling with ethers and peroxides at high temperatures (80-100°C)
rules.recommendation: Use flow protocol with two-feed approach to minimize explosion risk by avoiding preparation of large amounts of ether/peroxide mixtures
rules.reasoning: High temperatures with ethers and peroxides are associated with serious safety concerns due to explosion risk

rules.condition: WHEN performing oxidative coupling of β-ketoesters with ethers using Cu(OAc)₂ and TBHP
rules.recommendation: Mix two solutions (reactants + catalyst in one, TBHP in decane in other) just before entering flow reactor, heat at 130°C and maintain at 17 atm with back pressure regulator
rules.reasoning: Avoids preparation and use of large amounts of dangerous ether/peroxide mixtures while maintaining reaction efficiency
rules.quantitative: Temperature: 130°C, Pressure: 17 atm, Residence time: 20 minutes

rules.condition: WHEN performing photoredox C-H functionalization with secondary amines and vinyl azides
rules.recommendation: Use transparent perfluoroalkoxy (PFA) microreactor wrapped around white LED light source, fed through X-junction with three solutions
rules.reasoning: Enables efficient photochemical reaction with proper light exposure and controlled mixing
rules.quantitative: 1 mol% [Ru(bpy)₃][PF₆]₂ catalyst, 5 equivalents TBHP oxidant, residence time 44 minutes, yields 35-72%

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing photoredox C-H functionalization reactions
rules.recommendation: Adjust flow rates to achieve optimal catalyst concentration (1 mol%) and oxidant equivalents (5 equiv) in reactor with overall residence time of 44 minutes
rules.reasoning: Optimal conditions balance reaction completion with productivity, while flow enables shorter reaction times compared to batch
rules.quantitative: 1 mol% catalyst, 5 equivalents oxidant, 44 minutes residence time

rules.condition: WHEN performing Catellani reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use Pd(OAc)2 (2.5 mol%), XPhos (5 mol%), norbornene (0.6 equiv), TBAA (1.1 equiv) in DMF at 115°C with 2 hour residence time
rules.reasoning: These conditions enable successful Catellani coupling reactions in continuous flow with good yields
rules.quantitative: Pd(OAc)2: 2.5 mol%, XPhos: 5 mol%, norbornene: 0.6 equiv, TBAA: 1.1 equiv, 115°C, tR = 2 h, up to 91% yield

rules.condition: WHEN performing photocatalytic C-H arylation with CpMn(CO)3
rules.recommendation: Use CpMn(CO)3 (10 mol%) in DMSO at room temperature with 4 bar pressure, blue LEDs, and 30 minute residence time
rules.reasoning: Very short residence time enables efficient photocatalytic reactions on heteroaromatic substrates in flow
rules.quantitative: CpMn(CO)3: 10 mol%, DMSO solvent, r.t., 4 bar, blue LEDs, tR = 30 min, up to 85% yields

rules.condition: WHEN performing iridium-catalyzed C-H borylation in flow with heterogeneous catalyst
rules.recommendation: Use BPDCA-cat in methylcyclohexane at 80°C with flow rate 0.1-0.3 mmol h⁻¹
rules.reasoning: Heterogeneous iridium catalyst enables recyclable borylation reactions with minimal metal leaching
rules.quantitative: BPDCA-cat, methylcyclohexane solvent, 80°C, flow rate = 0.1-0.3 mmol h⁻¹, 67-89% yields, ≤7.9 ppm iridium leaching

rules.condition: WHEN using immobilized dirhodium catalyst for enantioselective C-H insertion
rules.recommendation: Pack [Rh2(S-p-BrTPCP)4]-Fiber catalyst in reactor at 40°C in DCM
rules.reasoning: Immobilized catalyst provides excellent enantioselectivity and recyclability for C-H insertion reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing heterogeneous C-H alkenylation of acetanilides
rules.recommendation: Use Pd/C, TsOH, BQ in GVL at 85°C with flow rate 0.5 mL min⁻¹ and 40 minute residence time
rules.reasoning: Heterogeneous palladium catalyst enables sustainable C-H functionalization in bio-derived solvent
rules.quantitative: Pd/C catalyst, TsOH, BQ, GVL solvent, 85°C, flow rate = 0.5 mL min⁻¹, tR = 40 min, 89-99% yields

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up heterogeneous C-H alkenylation reactions
rules.recommendation: Run continuously for extended periods (29 hours demonstrated) to achieve gram-scale production
rules.reasoning: Continuous operation enables high productivity and demonstrates catalyst stability under flow conditions
rules.quantitative: 29 hours continuous operation, 109 g product, 4 g h⁻¹ productivity, 500 mmol acetanilide processed

rules.condition: WHEN performing C-H functionalization of triazoles with heterogeneous Pd/C
rules.recommendation: Use Pd/C, MesCO2H, TBAA in GVL at 120°C with flow rate 0.25 mL min⁻¹ and 80 minute residence time
rules.reasoning: Heterogeneous catalyst avoids reactor clogging issues from poorly soluble bases like K2CO3
rules.quantitative: Pd/C, MesCO2H, TBAA, GVL, 120°C, flow rate = 0.25 mL min⁻¹, tR = 80 min, 78-93% yields
rules.exceptions: Avoid potassium trifluoroacetate or K2CO3 which are poorly soluble in GVL and cause reactor clogging

rules.condition: WHEN using heterogeneous Pd catalyst in triazole C-H functionalization
rules.recommendation: Expect only 0.0015% of total palladium to be released per mmol of substrate
rules.reasoning: Demonstrates excellent catalyst stability with minimal metal leaching compared to batch conditions
rules.quantitative: 0.0015% Pd release per mmol substrate in flow vs 0.16% in batch, >100 mmol substrate processed

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical aerobic oxidation of benzylic C-H bonds
rules.recommendation: Use RFT (10 mol%), Fe(ClO4)2 (5 mol%) with O2 in MeCN at 50°C under UV light (106 W) with 25 minute residence time
rules.reasoning: Photochemical conditions enable selective benzylic oxidation without overoxidation
rules.quantitative: RFT: 10 mol%, Fe(ClO4)2: 5 mol%, O2, MeCN, 50°C, UV (106 W), tR = 25 min, up to 79% yields

rules.condition: WHEN performing metal-free photocatalytic alkylation of C(sp³)-H bonds
rules.recommendation: Use Mes-Acr⁺ClO4⁻ (2 mol%) with HCl (5 mol%) in MeCN or MeCN/DCE at 50°C under blue LEDs for 24-48 hours
rules.reasoning: Metal-free photocatalyst enables alkylation reactions in stop-flow microtubing reactor
rules.quantitative: Mes-Acr⁺ClO4⁻: 2 mol%, HCl: 5 mol%, MeCN or MeCN/DCE, 50°C, blue LEDs, 24-48 h, 40-98% yield

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up metal-free photocatalytic reactions
rules.recommendation: Scale from SFMT to continuous-flow reactor or use larger SFMT reactor
rules.reasoning: Demonstrates feasibility of scaling photocatalytic processes to gram scale
rules.quantitative: Gram scale demonstrated for sample reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing chemoselective hydroarylation of alkenes
rules.recommendation: Use MnBr(CO)5 (10 mol%) with acetic acid (20 mol%) as cocatalyst for less than 20 minutes residence time
rules.reasoning: Enables selective C2-position functionalization of N-(2-pyridyl)indoles with good yields and short reaction times
rules.quantitative: MnBr(CO)5: 10 mol%, acetic acid: 20 mol%, <20 min residence time, 2.24 g product scale demonstrated

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with rapidly reacting monomers (e.g. acrylic acids or esters) in undiluted or concentrated solution
rules.recommendation: Terminate the reaction at low conversion, or run in a restricted approach by adding chain transfer agents (modifiers/regulators) with weak bonds (e.g. S-S bond in dodecyl mercaptan)
rules.reasoning: These monomers show auto acceleration or gel effect due to highly exothermic nature - more initiator becomes activated leading to more chain initiation

rules.condition: WHEN performing solution polymerisation
rules.recommendation: Use a solvent to reduce viscosity and prevent auto acceleration, with monomer concentration proportionate to desired decrease in polymerisation rate and degree
rules.reasoning: Solvent reduces viscosity and prevents reaction from achieving auto acceleration, but can cause chain transfer to solvent leading to decreased molecular weight
rules.exceptions: Issue with chain transfer to solvent

rules.condition: WHEN performing suspension polymerisation
rules.recommendation: Use water as inert bulk medium with surfactants as dispersion stabilisers, collect polymers as particulates or beads through induced high shear
rules.reasoning: Initiator must be soluble in monomer to form biphasic mixture with bulk media for industrial scale polymer preparation
rules.quantitative: High shear required for particle collection

rules.condition: WHEN performing emulsion polymerisation for diene and vinyl monomer polymerisations
rules.recommendation: Use water as solvent with water soluble initiator, immiscible monomer, and surfactant for droplet stabilisation
rules.reasoning: Most widely used commercial process, preferred for polyacrylates due to high exothermicity, viscosity increase, and likelihood of soft particle aggregation

rules.condition: WHEN Reynolds number is <2300 in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Expect laminar flow regime with constant smooth fluid motion and little mixing except via interlayer diffusion
rules.reasoning: Low velocity and viscous forces dominate, wall interactions create parabolic velocity flow profile with exterior fluid moving slower than interior laminae

rules.condition: WHEN Reynolds number is 2300 > Re < 4000 in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Expect transitional flow with medium velocity but only modest mixing induced
rules.reasoning: Transitional regime between laminar and turbulent flow with moderate mixing characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN Reynolds number is >4000 in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Expect turbulent flow with very efficient mixing, irregular and chaotic fluid motion with high velocities
rules.reasoning: Turbulent flow predominant when solution viscosity is low and flow rates are high, beneficial for better mixing in viscous reaction media

rules.condition: WHEN polymerisation leads to viscosity increase in flow
rules.recommendation: Add in-line mixing elements as architecture components of reactor channels or static mixing elements to facilitate turbulent mixing effects
rules.reasoning: As polymerisation increases viscosity, transition to more turbulent flow regime is beneficial for required mixing in viscous reaction media

rules.condition: WHEN polymerisation reactions result in very viscous solutions (gel) or generate extensive solids
rules.recommendation: Avoid running in flow as this will cause major challenges
rules.reasoning: Very viscous solutions or extensive solids create operational difficulties in flow systems
rules.exceptions: Can be performed with special processing equipment for precipitate or suspended particles

rules.condition: WHEN polymers stick to reactor walls affecting progression and distribution
rules.recommendation: Use static mixers and agitators, apply flow pulsing in straight smooth tubes, or use coiled tubing reactors
rules.reasoning: Prevents wall fouling and helps narrow residence time distribution while improving reaction through counter-rotating vortices and enhanced mass/heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN performing emulsion polymerisation
rules.recommendation: Ensure shear rates are distributed evenly and large heat exchange areas are available for homogeneous viscosity when different phases do not efficiently mix
rules.reasoning: Ensures droplet break-up is consistent which determines resultant particle size, and secondary flow patterns enhance mixing

rules.condition: WHEN using torus reactor for semi flow/batch emulsion polymerisation
rules.recommendation: Use mechanical stirrer to create dispersion flow, with high Reynolds number (Re) inherent to improved small dimensional mixing zone
rules.reasoning: High symmetry and forced circulation result in uniform particle size distribution as droplets are continuously broken up, with higher surface area for greater heat transfer
rules.quantitative: High Re required, improved small dimensional mixing zone
rules.exceptions: Potentially batch-like residence time and reactor volume

rules.condition: WHEN comparing torus reactors to stirred tank reactors for droplet size uniformity
rules.recommendation: Use torus reactors to achieve better droplet size uniformity with dispersity (σ/dp) values of 0.18-0.24 compared to 0.55-0.66 in stirred tank reactors
rules.reasoning: The rotatory stirrer in torus reactors induces gravitational forces creating a more even density gradient, resulting in more homogeneously distributed dispersed phase
rules.quantitative: Torus reactor dispersity: 0.18-0.24, Stirred tank reactor dispersity: 0.55-0.66

rules.condition: WHEN using Couette-Taylor vortex reactors for flow emulsion polymerisation
rules.recommendation: Operate near the critical Taylor number threshold to achieve desired flow characteristics and effective radial mixing
rules.reasoning: A threshold Taylor number must be overcome before the reactor will have the desired flow effect, with the Taylor number replacing the Reynolds number for rotating flow systems
rules.quantitative: Taylor number Ta = (ω·b·Ri/ν)·(b/Ri)^1/2, where Ri = inner cylinder radius (cm), b = radial clearance, ν = kinematic viscosity (cm²s⁻¹), ω = angular velocity (s⁻¹)

rules.condition: WHEN using Couette-Taylor reactors for emulsion polymerisation
rules.recommendation: Achieve particle size distribution standard deviations in the range of 15.2-13.7 nm by operating above critical Taylor number
rules.reasoning: Low Taylor numbers above the critical value make the residence time distribution narrow due to effective radial mixing
rules.quantitative: Standard deviations: 15.2-13.7 nm (vs 26.4 nm in batch reactor)

rules.condition: WHEN operating Couette-Taylor flow reactors for latex particle production
rules.recommendation: Avoid using physical stirrers to prevent coagulation and blockage issues with latex particles
rules.reasoning: Latex particles are very sensitive to mechanical shear which can cause blockage due to deposition of flocculated particles

rules.condition: WHEN using Couette-Taylor reactors for styrene polymerisation
rules.recommendation: Achieve particle diameter of 85 nm with dispersity of 1.31 by operating as a seeder unit feeding subsequent emulsion polymerisation reactors
rules.reasoning: The reactor can act as a pre-reactor producing consistent particles for further polymerisation while avoiding fouling or blockage
rules.quantitative: Average diameter: 85 nm, dispersity: 1.31 (optimized batch: 81 nm diameter, 1.08 dispersity)
rules.exceptions: Lower Reynolds number may produce some laminar flow characteristics reducing performance

rules.condition: WHEN adjusting Couette-Taylor reactor performance
rules.recommendation: Change flow pattern from perfectly mixed to plug flow by altering the rotational speed of the inner cylinder
rules.reasoning: Decreasing rotational speed decreases the Taylor number, allowing investigation of process limits and making the unit highly tunable
rules.quantitative: Taylor number varies with ω (angular velocity)

rules.condition: WHEN comparing pulse flow to continuous stirred tank reactors for styrene emulsion polymerisation

rules.reasoning: Pulse flow reactors provide better conversion efficiency compared to continuously stirred tank reactors
rules.quantitative: Pulse flow: ~60% conversion, Continuously stirred tank: ~40% conversion

rules.condition: WHEN performing vinyl acetate polymerisation in Couette-Taylor flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN considering controlled radical polymerisation (CRP) for industrial scale-up

rules.reasoning: CRP requires mediating or chain-transfer agents often in stoichiometric amounts, which are toxic/harmful and need removal, making industrial scale-up costly

rules.exceptions: ATRP has attracted most attention for substantial progress in increasing polymerisation rate and decreasing chain-transfer agent concentration

rules.condition: WHEN performing ATRP reactions in flow

rules.reasoning: If the ratio between kact and kdeact is small, control over the propagation step will be lost and a runaway reaction occurs

rules.condition: WHEN setting up ATRP reactions in flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN using low flow rates in ATRP packed bed reactors

rules.condition: WHEN designing sequential polymerization reactors for gradient polymers

rules.reasoning: Sequential injection enables preparation of tailored mixed polymers with different molecular weights and high correlation between predicted and experimental molecular weight distributions
rules.quantitative: Two reactor stages with sequential monomer injection capability

rules.condition: WHEN using immobilized TPP photocatalyst in flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN operating immobilized photocatalyst systems for extended periods
rules.recommendation: Achieve 10 h residence time under green light irradiation (λmax = 530 nm, 0.45 mW cm⁻²) to maintain 65% monomer conversion over 20+ hour runs
rules.reasoning: Extended operation demonstrates system stability and consistent polymer production with PDI > 1.20
rules.quantitative: 10 h residence time, λmax = 530 nm, 0.45 mW cm⁻², 65% conversion, PDI > 1.20, >20 h operation

rules.condition: WHEN designing enzymatic polymerization reactors with immobilized enzymes
rules.recommendation: Pack enzyme on solid beads in column reactors to achieve higher local enzyme concentrations than achievable in batch
rules.reasoning: Immobilization enables higher local concentrations and better performance compared to batch processes
rules.quantitative: Higher local concentration than batch achievable

rules.condition: WHEN operating enzymatic polymerization in packed bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Use residence time range of 15-240 s with flow rate coverage of 30-640 μL min⁻¹
rules.reasoning: These parameters provide suitable reaction time while maintaining adequate flow rates for enzymatic polymerization

rules.exceptions: Scale-up may be limited by small reactor volume

rules.condition: WHEN using microreactor systems for enzymatic polymerization
rules.recommendation: Fill internal capillary channels with immobilized enzyme to achieve >27 times larger apparent rate constant than batch
rules.reasoning: Microreactor configuration with immobilized enzyme provides dramatically improved reaction kinetics compared to batch processes
rules.quantitative: >27 times larger apparent rate constant than batch

rules.condition: WHEN designing packed bed enzymatic reactors with height to diameter (H/D) considerations
rules.recommendation: Investigate H/D ratios from 4.0-12.0, with optimal performance when H/D ratio >12.0 for increased polymerization rate
rules.reasoning: H/D ratio impacts contact time between enzyme and reactants, with higher ratios providing better reaction outcomes, though degradation rate may also increase
rules.quantitative: H/D ratio range 4.0-12.0, optimal >12.0, fixed H/D ratio of 12 recommended
rules.exceptions: When H/D ratio >12.0, degradation rate may increase faster than polymerization rate

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing packed bed enzymatic reactors for polycaprolactone production
rules.recommendation: Use 0.5-2.5 g of immobilized enzyme in columns varying from 4 to 20 cm height (1 cm ID) to achieve maximum monomer conversion (>98%) at 12 min residence time
rules.reasoning: This enzyme loading and reactor configuration provides optimal balance between polymerization and degradation rates

rules.condition: WHEN operating enzymatic flow processes for extended periods

rules.condition: WHEN conducting enzymatic synthesis of thiol-terminated poly(δ-valerolactone)

rules.condition: WHEN operating enzymatic flow synthesis with dual reactor configuration
rules.recommendation: Use 20 min residence time (flow rate 0.09 mL min⁻¹) to achieve 98% monomer conversion with Mn = 3120 g mol⁻¹ and Đ = 1.16

rules.condition: WHEN conducting direct in-line analysis of polymerization reactions
rules.recommendation: Prepare samples for analysis by dilution to known concentration and filtration before injection, as pure samples require significant preparation time
rules.reasoning: Sample preparation is often time-consuming, especially for GPC analysis requiring pure samples, making direct injection from flow lines challenging

rules.exceptions: Mass spectrometry can be used alternatively but may be problematic for high molecular weights due to detector limitations

rules.condition: WHEN conducting high-throughput polymer discovery in flow chemistry

rules.reasoning: Combinatorial and high-throughput approaches require rapid analysis to match the speed of synthesis and avoid creating bottlenecks in the discovery process

rules.condition: WHEN performing RAFT polymerization monitoring in microreactors

rules.condition: WHEN conducting RAFT polymerization of nBA in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Operate at 100°C with residence times between 1 and 5 minutes
rules.reasoning: These conditions allow obtaining molecular weights of 1100-2700 g mol⁻¹ for the polymerization reaction

rules.condition: WHEN performing aminolysis of RAFT polymer end groups
rules.recommendation: Operate at 60-80°C for the aminolysis reaction
rules.reasoning: This temperature range is suitable for the aminolysis of end group functionalities of RAFT polymers into corresponding thiol or thioether

rules.condition: WHEN preventing disulfide by-product formation during aminolysis

rules.reasoning: The packed column scavenges excess hexylamine to prevent formation of potential disulfide by-product

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing polymer nanoparticles via ATRP in tubular flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Tune reaction parameters (reagent amounts, flow rate, temperature) to generate Mn ranging from 2-20 kg mol⁻¹ with 0%-5% branching
rules.reasoning: These parameters allow control over molecular weight distribution and polymer properties in the continuous synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN isolating polymer from nanoparticle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use nano-precipitation followed by micromixers for colloidal suspension with non-solvent-water medium
rules.reasoning: This method facilitates simultaneous purification of polymer through separation from soluble residual monomer and catalyst
rules.quantitative: Nanoparticle diameters: 89-196 nm (average size distribution 41 nm)

rules.condition: WHEN performing RAFT polymerization in microreactors with ESI-MS monitoring
rules.recommendation: Operate at 20 bar back pressure
rules.reasoning: Back pressure regulation maintains proper flow conditions in the microreactor system

rules.condition: WHEN performing Cu(0) catalyzed polymerization of methyl acrylate via SET-LRP
rules.recommendation: Use toluene solvent with polar/coordinating additive (phenol/alcohol) and Me₆TREN ligand
rules.reasoning: Toluene ensures full solubility of monomer and polymeric product, while the additive promotes disproportionation of Cu(I) to Cu(0)

rules.condition: WHEN implementing autonomous self-optimization of polymers in flow
rules.recommendation: Integrate flow reactor with SEC for direct on-line molecular weight distribution measurements and use machine learning algorithms for optimization
rules.reasoning: This system can reproducibly target precise molecular weight with <2.5% deviation from preselected target
rules.quantitative: Precision: <2.5% deviation from target molecular weight

rules.condition: WHEN operating polymer synthesis mini-flow plant with integrated stages
rules.recommendation: Maintain GCP column at 35°C and use 60°C for polymer solution preparation
rules.reasoning: These temperatures are optimal for the integrated polymerization, analysis, and nanoparticle formation sequence
rules.quantitative: GCP column temperature: 35°C, polymer solution temperature: 60°C

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring high temperature ROP of ε-caprolactone
rules.recommendation: Use dielectric measurement as a simple and non-destructive method for determining progress
rules.reasoning: Dielectric measurement provides a calibrated measure of monomer conversion at high temperatures (100°C)

rules.condition: WHEN performing polymer purification in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider that purification is often the slowest step in converting monomer to polymer and obtaining analysis
rules.reasoning: Purification step creates a bottleneck in the overall polymer synthesis workflow

rules.condition: WHEN selecting polymer purification method
rules.recommendation: Choose method based on polymerization technique used, resulting chemical structure, and final application specification
rules.reasoning: Different applications have different purity requirements - medical applications need very high purity, while thickeners or food packaging may not

rules.condition: WHEN performing GPC analysis of polymers
rules.recommendation: Ensure clean sample, often of monomodal dispersity
rules.reasoning: Clean samples are preferable for accurate molecular weight and dispersity analysis

rules.condition: WHEN using anti-solvent purification
rules.recommendation: Find solvent or solvent mixture where either residual monomer or polymer is soluble but the other component is not
rules.reasoning: This is the most preferred methodology, especially at industrial scale, as it is easiest to perform
rules.exceptions: Difficult to be selective regarding molecular weight cut-off and yields can be variable

rules.condition: WHEN using anti-solvent purification
rules.recommendation: Acknowledge that addition of certain anti-solvents can add contamination issues depending on specific application (e.g. medical)
rules.reasoning: Anti-solvents may introduce impurities that are problematic for certain applications

rules.condition: WHEN using dialysis for polymer purification
rules.recommendation: Ensure tubing or membranes are compatible with the full range of organic solvents or polymers produced
rules.reasoning: Not all polymerization reactions can be purified using dialysis due to material compatibility issues

rules.condition: WHEN selecting dialysis membranes
rules.recommendation: Use membranes made of regenerated cellulose, cellulose acetate, polysulfone, polyethersulfone or collagen
rules.reasoning: These materials are commonly used for dialysis membrane construction

rules.condition: WHEN using dialysis for polymer separation
rules.recommendation: Separate polymers with various molecular weights by iteratively increasing the pore size of the membrane
rules.reasoning: Different pore sizes allow selective separation based on molecular weight

rules.condition: WHEN using dialysis tubing for purification
rules.recommendation: Expect purification to take in excess of 24 hours
rules.reasoning: Dialysis tubing has passive mode of action, making it very slow

rules.condition: WHEN handling dialysis storage solutions
rules.recommendation: Handle with care as storage solutions often contain toxic compounds (e.g. sodium azide)
rules.reasoning: Storage solutions contain preservatives that may be hazardous

rules.condition: WHEN processing large sample volumes with dialysis
rules.recommendation: Be aware that process becomes less efficient due to initial influx of water and equilibrium shifting
rules.reasoning: Large volumes become diluted and require significant amounts of water, reducing efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between dialysis and ultrafiltration
rules.recommendation: Choose ultrafiltration for faster purification compared to dialysis via tubing
rules.reasoning: Ultrafiltration is faster and includes ability to concentrate samples

rules.condition: WHEN using ultrafiltration
rules.recommendation: Consider it easier to recover residual monomers compared to dialysis due to smaller filtrate volume
rules.reasoning: Filtrate volume is considerably smaller making concentration, isolation and re-use easier

rules.condition: WHEN using ultrafiltration systems
rules.recommendation: Be aware of limited compatibility of membranes with various solvents
rules.reasoning: Membrane compatibility is a limiting factor like other membrane devices

rules.condition: WHEN using ultrafiltration with concentrated polymer samples
rules.recommendation: Add diluting solvent constantly to maintain sample concentration via external pump delivering solute to main tank at speed similar to withdrawal rate
rules.reasoning: Prevents polymer cluster formation and membrane degradation/blocking, balances system for continuous operation
rules.quantitative: Withdrawal rate similar to solute delivery rate

rules.condition: WHEN using Vivaflow 200 ultrafiltration system for polymer purification
rules.recommendation: Use membranes with surface area ~200 cm² fabricated from stabilized cellulose for MWCO 2000 Da
rules.reasoning: This configuration was successfully used for poly(acrylic acid) purification from free radical polymerizations

rules.condition: WHEN using integrated ultrafiltration system for polymer purification
rules.recommendation: Expect purification to be complete in 30 min, allowing new purified sample every working hour when combined with 5-20 min synthesis time
rules.reasoning: System enables rapid automated purification integrated with synthesis
rules.quantitative: 30 min purification time, 5-20 min synthesis time, ~1 hour total cycle

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing poly(acrylic acid) with 20-70% residual monomer content
rules.recommendation: Use ultrafiltration system to achieve complete purification and enable synthesis of compositions with Mn 158-378 kg mol⁻¹
rules.reasoning: Demonstrated successful purification of high residual monomer content samples

rules.condition: WHEN designing droplet formation devices for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use capillary tubed or micro-structured chip based systems as the two most common device types
rules.reasoning: These are the most established and effective designs for controlled droplet formation in flow
rules.quantitative: Two capillaries (usually glass or polymer) with different outer diameters, coaxial alignment

rules.condition: WHEN using capillary-based droplet formation systems
rules.recommendation: Choose between co-flow design (continuous and dispersed phase flowing in same direction) or flow focusing design (phases flowing in opposite directions)
rules.reasoning: Co-flow has both phases moving together, while flow focusing has output flow in same direction as dispersed phase for different droplet characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN using T-junction design for droplet formation
rules.recommendation: Keep flow rate of dispersed phase perpendicular to continuous phase low to allow slowly growing droplet to emerge and break off due to shear forces
rules.reasoning: Low perpendicular flow rate allows proper droplet formation through shear force breakup mechanism
rules.quantitative: Flow rate of dispersed phase should be low relative to continuous phase

rules.condition: WHEN forming polymeric particles in microfluidic devices
rules.recommendation: Use higher flow rate to form droplets through higher shear force, and ensure particles are smaller than channel diameter
rules.reasoning: Higher shear force prevents particles from contacting tube walls where they would deform or stick
rules.quantitative: Particles must be smaller than channel diameter

rules.condition: WHEN using intersection design for droplet formation
rules.recommendation: Introduce continuous phase perpendicular to output from two sides, with dispersed phase moving linearly towards output
rules.reasoning: This alignment produces droplets more in the middle of the channel with less wall interaction compared to T-junction

rules.condition: WHEN using flow focusing design
rules.recommendation: Include a narrow orifice at the exit of the channel for highest quality droplet formation
rules.reasoning: The narrow orifice is the most widely used configuration for high quality droplet production

rules.condition: WHEN creating uniform droplet size for polymer particle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use capillary instability driven break-up of the liquid for excellent control over emulsification
rules.reasoning: Capillary instability provides uniform droplet size which is key to the whole particle synthesis process

rules.condition: WHEN photopolymerizing freshly prepared droplets
rules.recommendation: Use UV irradiation at 360 nm with 200 mW m⁻² intensity at sample location
rules.reasoning: These specific conditions were demonstrated effective for continuous photopolymerization of droplets

rules.condition: WHEN controlling particle size and morphology in droplet synthesis
rules.recommendation: Change the flow rate of each liquid while maintaining the other two flow rates constant
rules.reasoning: This approach allows systematic control over particle size, liquid core thickness, shell thickness, and number of core droplets

rules.condition: WHEN performing photopolymerization in flow droplets
rules.recommendation: Use tripropylene glycol (TPGDA) or ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as monomer and 1-hydroxycyclohexyl phenyl ketone (HCPK) as initiator
rules.reasoning: These specific combinations have been demonstrated effective for generating polymer capsules with different shapes

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing Janus particles in microfluidic devices
rules.recommendation: Use aqueous solution of sodium dodecylsulfate to break up organic flow, then irradiate to induce polymerization
rules.reasoning: This approach allows control over Janus particle shape by adjusting volume fractions of the Janus droplets

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactor for self-assembly nanoparticle preparation
rules.recommendation: Use segmented gas-liquid flow with argon gas to assist in formation of spherical colloidal aggregates
rules.reasoning: Gas bubbles segment the liquid flow and help form spherical aggregates from polymers
rules.quantitative: 100 mm length mixing channel (100 μm wide × 150 μm depth), followed by 740 mm residence time channel (200 μm wide × 150 μm depth)

rules.condition: WHEN performing emulsion polymerization for polystyrene nanoparticles
rules.recommendation: Use mixed non-ionic (Triton X-100) and anionic (sodium dodecyl benzenesulfonate SDBS) emulsifiers
rules.reasoning: This combination was selected after investigating several emulsifiers to prevent unstable emulsion formation due to large unstable monomer droplets

rules.condition: WHEN using LED irradiation for photopolymerization in microfluidic droplets
rules.recommendation: Use 365 nm LED lamp for promoting photopolymerization of generated droplets
rules.reasoning: This wavelength is effective for initiating photopolymerization in the described droplet systems

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing monodispersed particles in two-stage process
rules.recommendation: First perform emulsification of monomer or liquid polymer in microfluidic device to obtain droplets with narrow size distribution, then conduct second step in batch for particle shell hardening
rules.reasoning: This two-stage approach combines the advantages of microfluidic control for narrow size distribution with batch processing efficiency for hardening
rules.quantitative: Size range: 100 μm-1 mm particles achievable

rules.condition: WHEN performing flow synthesis of poly(3-hexylthiophene)
rules.recommendation: Use 100°C in the first reactor section for 20 minutes residence time, then 150°C for 18 minutes
rules.reasoning: Sequential temperature increases optimize polymerization kinetics and molecular weight control

rules.condition: WHEN performing flow synthesis of poly(3-hexylthiophene) with temperature control

rules.reasoning: Higher pressure in heated sections prevents solvent vaporization and maintains liquid flow

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing conjugated polymers in flow

rules.reasoning: Polymers can be produced avoiding previously used chlorinated solvents for environmental benefits

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing PFO polymer via Suzuki coupling in flow

rules.reasoning: Altering reactor temperature and residence time impacts both Mn and Mw as well as final isolated yield

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up heteroaryl polymer synthesis beyond low flow rates (0.06 mL min⁻¹)
rules.recommendation: Add a second column connected in series to allow longer reactions and higher molecular weights
rules.reasoning: At low flow rates, short residence times give only low Mn (21 kg mol⁻¹), but series connection allows Mn of 33 kg mol⁻¹
rules.quantitative: 0.06 mL min⁻¹ flow rate limit, Mn improvement from 21 to 33 kg mol⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN pressure buildup occurs in packed bed reactors for polymerization
rules.recommendation: Substitute glass column with stainless steel reactor (12 mL internal volume) for safety
rules.reasoning: Safety issue resulting from pressure build in the reactor requires pressure-resistant materials

rules.condition: WHEN performing polycondensation setup for conjugated polymers

rules.reasoning: Maintains system pressure for high-temperature polymerization reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing heteroarylation polymerisation in flow with Cs₂CO₃/PivOH catalyst system

rules.recommendation: Use 8 bar back pressure regulator
rules.reasoning: Maintains pressure for high-temperature catalyst system operation

rules.condition: WHEN performing direct arylation polymerisation for PPDTBT synthesis

rules.reasoning: Temperature range required for effective Pd₂dba₃/P(o-anisyl) neodecanoic acid catalyzed polymerization

rules.reasoning: Maintains system pressure for high-temperature arylation polymerization

rules.condition: WHEN targeting higher molecular weight in Stille polymerization (Mn = 28 kDa)
rules.recommendation: Use 120 minute residence time at 120°C

rules.condition: WHEN increasing monomer concentration in flow polymerization from 150×10⁻³ M to 170×10⁻³ M

rules.reasoning: Higher monomer concentration increases molecular weight but requires careful control to manage polydispersity
rules.quantitative: 150×10⁻³ M to 170×10⁻³ M concentration range, Mn up to 60 kDa

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up bulk heterojunction polymer systems
rules.recommendation: Change residence time (1-72 h) and temperature (120-140°C) to control molecular weight; lower reagent concentration reduces Mn

rules.condition: WHEN designing MDMO-PVP synthesis in flow using microchips
rules.recommendation: Use 10 μL microchip for first reaction stage at 50°C, 5 min residence time, followed by 19.5 μL microchip at 195°C, 3.2 min residence time

rules.condition: WHEN performing ionic polymerization in flow

rules.reasoning: Precise volumetric control is essential for stoichiometric control in ionic polymerization reactions
