rules.condition: WHEN developing flow polymerization processes
rules.recommendation: Integrate process analytical technology (PAT) with flow synthesis machines to enable real-time optimization of polymer properties
rules.reasoning: Flow processing coupled with analytical technologies allows for streamlined and independent workflow for polymer design and property optimization

rules.condition: WHEN transitioning from batch to flow polymerization methods
rules.recommendation: Expect flow polymerization techniques to become the main synthesis approach surpassing traditional batch methods
rules.reasoning: Flow processing offers advantages in speed, efficiency, and integration with developing technologies for next-generation polymer chemists

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing nanomaterials in flow
rules.recommendation: Use segmented/slug flow regime in the microreactor for optimal synthesis
rules.reasoning: Segmented flow provides better mixing and control compared to homogeneous flow or droplet flow for nanomaterial synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN setting up flow synthesis equipment for nanomaterials
rules.recommendation: Include pumps, micromixer, residence time tubes/microreactors/flow reactors, connectors/fittings, pressure sensors, temperature sensors, and back pressure regulators
rules.reasoning: Complete system control requires monitoring and regulation of all process parameters to prevent channel blockage and ensure proper reaction conditions
rules.exceptions: Equipment varies based on reaction type (homogeneous vs multiphase)

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow systems for very slow synthesis reactions
rules.recommendation: Use continuous tank reactor in series to avoid settling of particles and clogging of channels/tubes
rules.reasoning: Long residence times in tubes can lead to particle deposition on tube/channel walls

rules.condition: WHEN particle deposition cannot be ruled out in flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use a two-phase system with particles suspended in dispersed phase for absolutely clean system
rules.reasoning: Two-phase systems prevent particle adhesion to reactor walls and enable cleaner operation

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing metal nanoparticles in continuous flow
rules.recommendation: Utilize enhanced heat and mass-transfer rates in microchannels due to short diffusion pathways from small characteristic lengths
rules.reasoning: Microfluidic devices provide improved efficiency through enhanced transport rates compared to batch synthesis
rules.quantitative: Short diffusion pathways from small cross-sectional distance

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing nickel nanoparticles using hydrazine reduction
rules.recommendation: Use 10 s residence time in continuous stainless steel coil reactor (ID 0.5 mm)
rules.reasoning: Specific conditions demonstrated for successful NiNP synthesis using modified polyol process
rules.quantitative: 10 s residence time, ID 0.5 mm reactor
rules.exceptions: Other metals may require different conditions

rules.condition: WHEN using segmented flow approach for nanoparticle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Utilize excellent local mixing within slugs through recirculation to achieve narrow particle size distribution
rules.reasoning: Slip velocity between fluids and internal mixing in continuous-phase slugs governs particle size distribution

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing silver nanoparticles to minimize channel deposition
rules.recommendation: Use various surfactants and ligands including sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and N-cetyl N,N,N,trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) to stabilize particles
rules.reasoning: Surfactants reduce deposition on channel walls and stabilize particles, affecting particle sizes and growth mechanism
rules.quantitative: Size reduction from 8.5 ± 6.9 nm to 4.1 ± 1.1 nm with increased surfactant concentration

rules.condition: WHEN using thermal reduction for silver nanoparticle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Operate in temperature range of 100-140°C using silver pentafluoropropionate as single-phase precursor
rules.reasoning: Thermal reduction provides alternative to citrate reduction but is less economically feasible
rules.exceptions: Economically less feasible than citrate reduction

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up palladium nanoparticle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Achieve ~10 L per day volumetric productivity in single-channel reactor using reduction-based approach with fluorinated oil co-injection
rules.reasoning: Large-scale, robust and non-fouling continuous synthesis demonstrated using annular flow regime
rules.quantitative: ~10 L per day productivity, single-channel reactor

rules.condition: WHEN creating segmented flow for nanoparticle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use gas as inert phase for creating segmented flow rather than another liquid
rules.reasoning: Gas inert phase prevents particle entrapment at interfaces compared to liquid-liquid systems where particles may remain trapped and difficult to separate

rules.condition: WHEN controlling nanoparticle growth rate and size
rules.recommendation: Control reaction medium pH as it plays vital role in controlling growth rate and eventual nanoparticle size
rules.reasoning: pH variation during reaction can result in variation in reaction rates and particle characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing CTAB-stabilized gold nanoparticles
rules.recommendation: Use 3 min residence time for particles with core diameters less than 8.0 nm with real-time optical analysis
rules.reasoning: Demonstrated successful synthesis conditions for gold nanoparticles in continuous flow
rules.quantitative: 3 min residence time, <8.0 nm core diameter

rules.condition: WHEN using microwave radiation for enhanced reaction rates in nanomaterial synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use rapid volumetric heating to eliminate thermal gradient and wall effects
rules.reasoning: Microwave radiation enhances reaction rates by rapid volumetric heating, eliminating thermal gradient and wall effects that occur in conventional heating
rules.quantitative: Higher selectivity and yields comparable to conventional heating methods
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when sparking occurs due to nanomaterial deposition

rules.condition: WHEN sparking occurs in single-flow reactor due to nanomaterial deposition on inner wall
rules.recommendation: Use segmented flow instead of single flow
rules.reasoning: Segmented flow prevents nanomaterial deposition on reactor walls that causes sparking in single-flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing CuInSe2 nanocrystals using continuous microwave-assisted segmented-flow reactor
rules.recommendation: Separate nucleation and growth stages by using burst nucleation followed by constant temperature bath
rules.reasoning: Separating nucleation and growth stages allows better control of reaction kinetics and particle quality
rules.quantitative: Burst nucleation in microwave heating zone, then constant temperature bath for NC growth

rules.condition: WHEN producing monodispersed CoSm nanoparticles
rules.recommendation: Allow nucleation and growth at 50°C, then use rapid cold quenching section (2°C, 10% acetone in THF)
rules.reasoning: Controlled temperature profile with rapid cooling suppresses growth and prevents Oswald ripening and oriented attachment processes
rules.quantitative: Nucleation at 50°C, quenching at 2°C with 10% acetone in THF

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing RhAg nanoparticles using two-phase continuous microwave irradiation
rules.recommendation: Use microwave at 120°C with residence time of 9 minutes
rules.reasoning: Specific temperature and residence time optimize the random alloying of Rh and Ag metals in ethylene glycol bath

rules.condition: WHEN using high molecular weight block polymers in 2D flow focusing devices for nanoprecipitation
rules.recommendation: Use 3D focusing in both horizontal and vertical dimensions instead of 2D focusing
rules.reasoning: High molecular weight polymers cause channel clogging and channelling in 2D devices; 3D focusing isolates precipitating polymer from channel walls preventing aggregation and clogging
rules.exceptions: Not needed for low molecular weight polymers

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing Au nanoparticles using borohydride reduction
rules.recommendation: Use rapid mixing to avoid pockets of nuclei formation during slow addition of reducing agent
rules.reasoning: Rapid mixing prevents formation of nuclei pockets that occur with slow addition, ensuring uniform particle formation

rules.condition: WHEN performing slightly exothermic nanomaterial synthesis reactions
rules.recommendation: Continuously remove heat during reaction to maintain constant temperature
rules.reasoning: For slightly exothermic reactions, continuous heat removal prevents temperature excursions if there are no heat losses
rules.exceptions: Not needed if significant heat losses are present

rules.condition: WHEN using microfluidic flow synthesis systems for nanomaterials
rules.recommendation: Take advantage of smaller diffusion paths and faster mixing compared to batch systems
rules.reasoning: In microfluidic systems, diffusion paths are smaller and mixing is faster, eliminating need for slow addition and enabling temperature profiles along reactor length

rules.condition: WHEN laminar flow causes deviation from ideal plug flow behavior and broader particle size distribution
rules.recommendation: Use segmented flow systems or oscillating flow system to overcome axial dispersion effects
rules.reasoning: Axial dispersion in laminar flow causes broader particle size distribution than expected; segmented or oscillating flow gives very narrow particle size distribution

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up continuous flow synthesis of nanomaterials for large-scale production (at least 1 ton per day)
rules.recommendation: Include storage vessels, dosing instruments, reactors based on reaction kinetics, separation units, process data acquisition, and control units
rules.reasoning: Large-scale production requires systematic approach with engineered plant components for consistent product properties and quality control
rules.quantitative: At least 1 ton per day production capacity

rules.condition: WHEN separating nanoparticles after synthesis in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use in-line separation methods (inertial microfluidics, filtration, electrostatic sieving) for dilute solutions; off-line centrifugal approaches for concentrated solutions
rules.reasoning: In-line methods are energy efficient for dilute solutions; off-line centrifugal methods become energy intensive at large scale but work for concentrated solutions
rules.exceptions: Magnetic separation works for magnetic nanoparticles regardless of concentration

rules.condition: WHEN producing Zn-Ce oxide nanomaterials using confined jet mixer
rules.recommendation: Operate at 450°C and 24.1 MPa for water-based large-scale production
rules.reasoning: These specific conditions enable effective supercritical water-based continuous synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN operating large-scale flow synthesis systems for many hours
rules.recommendation: Clean system periodically through gas-pressure shocks or solvent washing
rules.reasoning: Extended operation leads to fouling and requires periodic cleaning to maintain performance

rules.condition: WHEN transforming flow synthesis into manufacturing process
rules.recommendation: Develop systematic approach including: reaction kinetics, nucleation and growth rates, role of additives/surfactants, pH sensitivity, addition rate effects on functionality, and simultaneous reaction rates
rules.reasoning: Systematic approach is essential for reliable scale-up as general methodology does not necessarily apply to functional materials

rules.condition: WHEN designing a continuous scalable process for functional materials
rules.recommendation: Gather experimental data including residence time, reaction temperature, reaction kinetics, solubility data, heat of reaction and adiabatic temperature rise (for exothermic reactions)
rules.reasoning: These parameters are essential input data needed for proper reactor design and scale-up calculations
rules.quantitative: All experimental kinetic and thermodynamic data required

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow reactors for functional materials synthesis
rules.recommendation: Collect properties of reactants, solvents, products and byproducts including density, viscosity, specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, melting and boiling point, particle size and shape
rules.reasoning: Material properties are critical for mass and energy balance calculations and proper reactor sizing

rules.condition: WHEN using non-conventional energy sources in flow synthesis
rules.recommendation: Quantify the amount of energy used for specific synthesis and verify reactor dimensions meet the energy balance requirements
rules.reasoning: Energy balance must be satisfied for safe and efficient operation, especially with alternative heating methods
rules.quantitative: Energy balance calculations must be validated

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow processes for functional materials
rules.recommendation: Validate overall mass and energy balance before finalizing reactor design
rules.reasoning: Mass and energy conservation laws must be satisfied for successful scale-up
rules.quantitative: Complete mass and energy balance validation required

rules.condition: WHEN producing nanoparticles in single-phase microreactors
rules.recommendation: Consider that single-phase microreactors are prone to fouling that could limit their potential in NP production
rules.reasoning: Fouling is a common limitation in single-phase systems for nanoparticle synthesis

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing nanoparticles in microreactors
rules.recommendation: Use two-phase flow (gas-liquid/liquid-liquid) to overcome fouling limitations by confining reaction domain into dispersed phase slugs
rules.reasoning: Two-phase flow inhibits growth at reactor wall surface and reduces particle size distribution

rules.condition: WHEN using two-phase flow in microreactors for nanoparticle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Account for capping agents or stabilizers transferring from dispersed phase to continuous phase, which decreases their concentration in reaction media
rules.reasoning: Mass transfer of stabilizers can lead to uncontrolled nanocrystallization or foaming

rules.condition: WHEN separating nanoparticles from foams in two-phase flow
rules.recommendation: Ensure sufficient residence time is provided as separation will depend on foam stability and requires overcoming energy barriers
rules.reasoning: Foam separation is time-dependent and energy-intensive process
rules.quantitative: Very long time required due to energy barriers

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing quantum dots, ZnO, GaN, cesium lead halide perovskite nanocrystals, or carbon dots
rules.recommendation: Use ultraprecise colloidal synthesis with size-engineering to provide accurate control over particle size and size-dispersion
rules.reasoning: These materials require precise control over particle size and dispersion for optimal properties

rules.condition: WHEN implementing flow synthesis for nanomaterials
rules.recommendation: Consider that flow synthesis offers seamless and contactless measurement using miniaturized in-line and on-line measurement techniques
rules.reasoning: Flow systems enable real-time monitoring and control compared to batch methods

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up nanomaterial synthesis from flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Pay special attention to scale-up as small changes in nanomaterial size or shape (few nanometers) can result in significant deviation from expected properties
rules.reasoning: Nanomaterial properties are highly sensitive to size variations, making scale-up challenging
rules.quantitative: Few nanometer changes can cause significant property deviations

rules.condition: WHEN designing NMR microcoils for flow chemistry detection
rules.recommendation: Use microslot waveguide probes with widths of just 100 μm for high mass sensitivity
rules.reasoning: Due to small detection volume, commonly found in microslot resonators with just 188 nL volume, providing high mass sensitivity despite not very high concentration sensitivity
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when high concentration sensitivity is required

rules.condition: WHEN using Helmholtz coils for NMR microcoil applications
rules.recommendation: Use wire-bonded copper planar coils with connection between two coils for more homogeneous magnetic field distribution
rules.reasoning: Connection between two coils results in more homogeneous distribution of magnetic field than a single circular planar coil, showing line width of 1.79 Hz for active volume of 79 nL
rules.quantitative: Line width: 1.79 Hz, Active volume: 79 nL of deionized water
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when sample cleaning is extensive between samples

rules.condition: WHEN designing flat wire resonators for small-volume NMR samples
rules.recommendation: Position flat wire aligned to external magnetic field on top of capillary for close proximity to metal
rules.reasoning: Close proximity to metal affects field homogeneity but can be resolved by applying NMR pulse sequences that yield high resolution spectra in inhomogeneous field measuring sub-nL volumes
rules.exceptions: When field homogeneity is critical and cannot be corrected with pulse sequences

rules.condition: WHEN setting up on-flow reaction monitoring with microcoils
rules.recommendation: Use detection volumes in the lower μL or nL range with sample areas with diameters less than 1 mm, focus on high-field applications with cryogenically cooled magnet instruments
rules.reasoning: Exploiting cryogenically cooled magnet instruments provides better sensitivity for small volume detection
rules.quantitative: Detection volumes: lower μL or nL range, Sample area diameter: <1 mm
rules.exceptions: When room temperature magnets are sufficient for the application

rules.condition: WHEN choosing microcoil geometries for reaction monitoring
rules.recommendation: Use solenoids, planar spiral coils, and striplines as the most exploited geometries
rules.reasoning: These geometries have been proven most effective for reaction monitoring applications in flow chemistry
rules.exceptions: When specific geometric constraints require alternative designs

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring fast reactions (seconds) with microfluidic mixing devices
rules.recommendation: Position mixer close to detector and vary flow speed to detect various time points of reaction with channel volume separating mixer and detector determining lower reaction time limit
rules.reasoning: Allows for detecting various time points of a reaction, with the channel volume separating the mixer and detector determining the lower reaction time limit
rules.exceptions: When reaction kinetics are slower than mixing time scales

rules.condition: WHEN setting up continuous flow NMR with syringe pump system
rules.recommendation: Use micromixer and microcoil NMR cell with distance to coil after mixing determining measure reaction coordinate, control reaction time via flow rate
rules.reasoning: Distance to coil after mixing the two fluids together with flow rate determines the measure reaction coordinate for time-resolved NMR spectroscopy
rules.quantitative: Flow rate controls reaction time coordinate
rules.exceptions: When stopped-flow measurements are preferred over continuous flow

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring protein folding reactions with NMR microcoils
rules.recommendation: Mix solutions of ubiquitin in different methanol concentrations (20% methanol and 80% methanol) in micromixer and obtain different NMR spectra per flow rate value
rules.reasoning: Every spectrum shows different proportions of the two states of ubiquitin for times between 3.8 and 114 s, allowing measurement of folded/unfolded transition kinetics
rules.quantitative: Time range: 3.8 to 114 seconds, Methanol concentrations: 20% and 80%
rules.exceptions: When studying other protein systems or different denaturant conditions

rules.condition: WHEN using multicoil setups for reaction monitoring
rules.recommendation: Place microreactor in custom-built chip holder on top of stripline-probe very close to stripline resonator for multiple reaction time points
rules.reasoning: Elegant multicoil setup can provide different reaction time points, though complexity prevents straightforward use
rules.exceptions: When simple single-point monitoring is sufficient

rules.condition: WHEN accumulating NMR scans for improved signal-to-noise ratio in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Accumulate as many scans as required for a certain reaction time to obtain decent SNR
rules.reasoning: Additional advantage of on-flow NMR measurements is the option to accumulate as many scans as required for a certain reaction time to obtain decent SNR
rules.quantitative: Scan accumulation based on required SNR

rules.condition: WHEN designing NMR microcoil systems for on-line reaction monitoring
rules.recommendation: Connect NMR magnet and microfluidic chip using fused silica capillary with 4.5 μL volume to define the detection volume
rules.reasoning: This specific volume configuration allows for real-time monitoring while maintaining appropriate sample handling

rules.condition: WHEN using NMR microcoil systems for reaction monitoring
rules.recommendation: Design system to study reactions in time range from several seconds to 30 minutes
rules.reasoning: This time range is optimal for observing reaction kinetics and intermediate formation in microfluidic systems
rules.quantitative: Several seconds to 30 minutes residence time

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring photochemical reactions using photo-CIDNP effects
rules.recommendation: Use ESCARGOT-type microcoil with loosely wound microcoil solenoid and transparent PDMS for efficient light transfer to sample cavity
rules.reasoning: PDMS transparency allows efficient light penetration while the solenoid design maintains NMR sensitivity

rules.condition: WHEN using photosensitizers in hyperpolarization setups
rules.recommendation: Monitor degradable auxiliary compounds like sensitizers on-flow rather than under stationary conditions
rules.reasoning: On-flow monitoring prevents degradation of photosensitizers and maintains constant signal, while stationary conditions show dramatic signal decrease
rules.quantitative: FMN concentration: 1 mM under stopped flow, 5 mM on-flow

rules.condition: WHEN designing microwave-enhanced NMR monitoring systems
rules.recommendation: Use 1.6 μL reaction volume with microwave heater connected to microfluidic chip of 0.8 cm² with electroplated planar spiral NMR coil defining 6 nL detection volume
rules.reasoning: This configuration allows conversion curves to be obtained with just a few microliters of sample while maintaining sufficient NMR sensitivity
rules.quantitative: 1.6 μL reaction volume, 0.8 cm² chip, 6 nL detection volume

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing NMR microcoil systems for reaction monitoring
rules.recommendation: Optimize flow speeds and acquisition times to allow reaction times of the order of a few minutes and detect concentrations of 10-300 mM
rules.reasoning: These parameters provide optimal balance between temporal resolution and concentration sensitivity
rules.quantitative: Reaction times: few minutes, Concentration range: 10-300 mM

rules.condition: WHEN using small detector volumes relative to reactor volume in NMR monitoring
rules.recommendation: Adjust the number of scans to be accumulated on-flow to gain sufficient SNR, then determine volume blocks of reactor for conversion quantification
rules.reasoning: Small detection volumes require signal averaging to achieve adequate signal-to-noise ratio for quantitative analysis

rules.condition: WHEN using microwave heating in NMR microcoil systems
rules.recommendation: Switch on microwave heater element to achieve practically instantaneous temperature rise from room temperature to set temperature
rules.reasoning: Microwave heating provides rapid, uniform heating essential for isothermal reaction monitoring

rules.condition: WHEN performing microwave-enhanced isothermal monitoring with 100 accumulated scans per fraction at 0.2 μL/min flow speed
rules.recommendation: Expect eight separate sample fractions corresponding to 1-8 minutes of heating time
rules.reasoning: This specific configuration provides temporal resolution for kinetic analysis of first-order reactions
rules.quantitative: 0.2 μL/min flow speed, 100 scans per fraction, 1-8 minutes heating time

rules.condition: WHEN measuring conversion in microwave-enhanced NMR systems
rules.recommendation: Achieve mass sensitivity of 9 nanomol in accumulated 0.2 μL sample fraction volume
rules.reasoning: This sensitivity level is adequate for quantitative kinetic analysis in microfluidic systems
rules.quantitative: 9 nanomol mass sensitivity in 0.2 μL volume

rules.condition: WHEN using commercial heating devices (like Labtrix Start) with microfluidic NMR chips
rules.recommendation: Allow 1.5 minutes for heating plate to reach set temperature of about 100°C
rules.reasoning: Commercial heating plates have thermal mass that requires time to equilibrate, affecting reaction monitoring

rules.condition: WHEN designing non-isothermal reaction monitoring systems
rules.recommendation: Use 20 μL chip heating device hyphenated to home-built microfluidic chip with planar microcoil and 25 nL detection volume
rules.reasoning: This configuration allows discrimination of different temperature regimes during sample flow through the chip
rules.quantitative: 20 μL heating device, 25 nL detection volume

rules.condition: WHEN performing non-isothermal reaction monitoring
rules.recommendation: Discriminate four different regimes: fractions heated but not at final temperature, fractions heated during system warm-up, fractions entering while heating up, and fractions at final set temperature
rules.reasoning: Understanding these regimes is essential for proper kinetic analysis in non-isothermal conditions

rules.condition: WHEN analyzing non-isothermal reaction data
rules.recommendation: Expect sigmoidal shaped conversion plot rather than typical exponential curve, and use software like Maple 16 to solve differential equations with variable temperature numerically
rules.reasoning: Non-isothermal conditions produce different kinetic profiles that require specialized mathematical treatment

rules.condition: WHEN validating NMR microcoil methods for kinetic analysis
rules.recommendation: Obtain kinetic parameters from a single flow experiment and validate with library of compounds (isoxazoles and pyrazole derivatives)
rules.reasoning: Single experiment kinetic determination saves time and materials while library validation ensures method robustness

rules.condition: WHEN setting up photochemical reaction monitoring in microfluidic systems
rules.recommendation: Use microfluidic setups that require relatively low power devices and do not suffer from inefficient illumination
rules.reasoning: Microfluidic geometries provide excellent light penetration and require less power than larger-scale photochemical reactors

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical reactions with optically thick solutions
rules.recommendation: Use blue light (465 nm) provided by light-emitting diode (LED) lamps of low power (around 1 W) for 72 h for bulk reactions
rules.reasoning: Bulk photochemical reactions require extended irradiation times with appropriate wavelength and power to achieve complete conversion
rules.quantitative: 465 nm wavelength, 1 W power, 72 h duration

rules.condition: WHEN using microreactor coupled to planar microcoil detector for photochemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Reduce reaction times to only 30 min with in-line monitoring
rules.reasoning: Microfluidic setup with real-time monitoring dramatically reduces reaction times compared to bulk reactions while maintaining product distribution control
rules.quantitative: 30 min residence time vs 72 h for bulk

rules.condition: WHEN designing microcoil NMR devices for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use microfluidic connections to capillaries that allow not only on-flow measurements but also positioning of samples for stationary/stopped-flow experiments
rules.reasoning: Flexibility in measurement modes enables both continuous monitoring and detailed analysis of reaction intermediates

rules.condition: WHEN investigating supramolecular host-guest interactions requiring high concentrations
rules.recommendation: Use small volumes (50 nL detection volume) in microfluidic chips with planar microcoil for picomole sensitivity
rules.reasoning: Small volumes minimize material consumption while achieving necessary sensitivity for weak binding constants at high concentrations
rules.quantitative: 50 nL detection volume, picomole sensitivity, millimolar concentrations

rules.condition: WHEN designing NMR probe heads for flow chemistry with temperature control
rules.recommendation: Use FC-43 polymer for embedding microcoils to eliminate magnetic susceptibility differences and provide temperature regulation
rules.reasoning: FC-43 polymer has low melting and high boiling point, enabling operation between -40°C and 120°C while maintaining magnetic compatibility

rules.condition: WHEN performing esterification reactions in microfluidic NMR setups
rules.recommendation: Use liquid cooled micro-mixer to ensure efficient thermostating of reactants
rules.reasoning: Proper temperature control is critical for maintaining reaction kinetics and preventing thermal degradation in microscale systems

rules.condition: WHEN setting up photochemical reaction monitoring with fiber optics
rules.recommendation: Position light source to enter from opposite side of chip from where microcoil is located to avoid inefficient illumination
rules.reasoning: Proper light positioning ensures adequate illumination of the sample volume without interference from detection equipment

rules.condition: WHEN choosing light sources for microfluidic photochemistry
rules.recommendation: Use laser diodes rather than LEDs for most efficient power transfer to NMR chip
rules.reasoning: Laser diodes provide more efficient power transfer compared to LEDs in microfluidic setups

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemistry in microfluidic channels
rules.recommendation: Take advantage of small channel diameter to prevent significant absorption of light in only superficial parts of sample
rules.reasoning: Small channel dimensions allow for more uniform light penetration and higher sample concentrations compared to bulk systems

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring photochemical transformations in real-time
rules.recommendation: Use actinometer reactions (such as o-nitrobenzaldehyde to o-nitrobenzoic acid conversion) to determine light transfer efficiency
rules.reasoning: Actinometer reactions provide quantitative measure of light being transferred from source to sample for calibration purposes

rules.condition: WHEN designing microcoil NMR systems for flow chemistry applications
rules.recommendation: Prioritize mass sensitivity over spectral resolution for in-line reaction monitoring
rules.reasoning: For many flow chemistry applications, mass sensitivity is more important than spectral resolution, unlike traditional NMR where sub-Hz linewidths are prioritized
rules.quantitative: 5 mm probes with sub-Hz linewidths for traditional vs high-field instruments with microcoils for flow

rules.condition: WHEN selecting membrane materials for flow chemistry membrane reactors
rules.recommendation: Consider membrane permeability units of mol (or m³) m m⁻² Pa⁻¹ s⁻¹ for pressure gradient transport
rules.reasoning: Permeability defines the flux (molar or volumetric flow per unit membrane area) scaled with respect to membrane thickness and driving force
rules.quantitative: mol (or m³) m m⁻² Pa⁻¹ s⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN classifying porous membranes by pore size according to IUPAC
rules.recommendation: Use macroporous for mean pore diameters >50 nm, mesoporous for 2-50 nm, and microporous for <2 nm
rules.reasoning: IUPAC classification system provides standardized pore size categories for membrane selection
rules.quantitative: Macroporous: >50 nm; Mesoporous: 2-50 nm; Microporous: <2 nm

rules.condition: WHEN designing catalytic membrane reactors (CMRs) for equilibrium-limited reactions
rules.recommendation: Use CMRs to couple reactor and separator into single unit for continuous selective extraction of products
rules.reasoning: CMRs can improve yield by shifting thermodynamic equilibrium through continuous product removal

rules.condition: WHEN using packed bed membrane reactors
rules.recommendation: Place packed or fluidized bed of catalyst particles inside one of the compartments separated by the membrane
rules.reasoning: The membrane acts only as separation unit while reaction function is provided by the catalyst bed

rules.condition: WHEN designing CMRs where membrane is itself catalytic
rules.recommendation: Activate membrane by impregnation, grafting, or adsorption with active catalyst or enzyme
rules.reasoning: Provides both separation and reaction functions simultaneously within the membrane
rules.exceptions: Can also use inherently catalytically active membrane materials like metallic (Pd, Ag, Pt), alumina or zeolite membranes

rules.condition: WHEN performing alkane dehydrogenation in CMRs
rules.recommendation: Use dense membranes with Pt, Pd or alloys that are 100% selective towards hydrogen permeation
rules.reasoning: These metals provide selective hydrogen removal which shifts equilibrium and increases yield

rules.condition: WHEN manufacturing composite catalytic membranes to reduce cost
rules.recommendation: Deposit thin layer of noble metals on inorganic porous supports (alumina, silica, stainless steel, porous Vycor glass)
rules.reasoning: Reduces cost while maintaining catalytic activity and allows complex-shaped membrane reactor systems

rules.condition: WHEN performing ethane dehydrogenation in CMRs
rules.recommendation: Operate at 660 K with Pd-Ag alloy membranes deposited by magnetron sputtering
rules.reasoning: Reported conversion improvement from 2% (equilibrium) to 18% with CMR containing porous Vycor glass membrane

rules.condition: WHEN performing n-butane dehydrogenation in CMRs
rules.recommendation: Operate at 670 K with Pd-Ag alloy membranes
rules.reasoning: Reported 4- to 6-fold conversion enhancement above equilibrium

rules.condition: WHEN operating trans-membrane pressure difference in ethane dehydrogenation CMRs
rules.recommendation: Apply flow of inert sweep gas on low-pressure side of membrane to maintain hydrogen permeation gradient
rules.reasoning: Maintains good hydrogen permeation gradient in the permeate by removing hydrogen continuously
rules.quantitative: Experiments at 723-873 K with trans-membrane pressure difference

rules.condition: WHEN using less expensive catalytic metals for CMRs
rules.recommendation: Consider vanadium and titanium embedded into zeolite framework structures
rules.reasoning: Incorporating metals into membrane structure significantly influences physicochemical characteristics and permeation/adsorption properties

rules.condition: WHEN considering long-term alkane dehydrogenation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use packed or fluidized bed reactors rather than CMRs due to catalyst deactivation issues
rules.reasoning: Long-term dehydrogenation reactions result in catalyst deactivation, and reactivating catalyst outside membrane is easier than inside
rules.exceptions: CMR can be used if catalyst reactivation/recycling is feasible

rules.condition: WHEN operating high temperature dehydrogenation reactions in CMRs
rules.recommendation: Consider oxidation process hot-spots that can develop and compromise membrane structural integrity
rules.reasoning: High temperatures can cause thermal expansion differences and oxidation that damages membrane structure

rules.condition: WHEN performing cyclohexane dehydrogenation to benzene at low temperatures
rules.recommendation: Use dense polymeric membranes prepared by blending polyethylacrylate with 13X zeolite containing Ti or Ni dehydrogenation catalyst
rules.reasoning: Enables low temperature operation compared to traditional high temperature alkane dehydrogenation
rules.quantitative: 13X zeolite with Ti or Ni catalyst

rules.condition: WHEN performing alcohol dehydrogenation in catalytic membrane reactors (CMRs) with Pd membrane catalyst
rules.recommendation: Add oxygen in the feed to avoid catalyst deactivation by carbon monoxide/carbon coverage
rules.reasoning: Hydrogen permeation decreases with time due to growing carbon monoxide/carbon coverage of the membrane's surface, which can be avoided by adding oxygen

rules.condition: WHEN using CMRs for selective addition of reactants in hydrogenation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use membrane to allow selective diffusion of hydrogen on permeate side to react selectively with gaseous or liquid substrate, while separating hydrogen from retentate side where substrate flows
rules.reasoning: The membrane enables controlled addition of one reactant to maintain low partial pressure or concentration, enhancing selectivity by avoiding parallel or consecutive reactions

rules.condition: WHEN operating hollow-fiber CMR configurations with Pd/Pd-Co catalysts
rules.recommendation: Operate at temperatures around 313 K for kinetic-controlled regime with nearly complete catalyst utilization
rules.reasoning: At temperatures below 313 K, the rate is kinetically-controlled and catalyst utilization is nearly complete, whereas above 358 K indicates onset of transport limitations or heat effects
rules.quantitative: Below 313 K: kinetic-controlled; above 358 K: transport limitations begin
rules.exceptions: Above 358 K where transport limitations dominate

rules.condition: WHEN using polyamide-imide/TiO2/Pd composite membranes for propene hydrogenation
rules.recommendation: Achieve 100% conversion of propene at maximum yield of 98% propane at the permeate side
rules.reasoning: This configuration provides optimal selectivity and conversion for hydrogenation reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing selective hydrogenation of 5% propyne in propene using composite membranes
rules.recommendation: Achieve selectivity of 99% to propene at 100% conversion of propyne
rules.reasoning: This demonstrates the high selectivity achievable in membrane reactor configurations

rules.condition: WHEN designing three-phase CMRs for hydrogenation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use porous membranes to provide interface between gas (hydrogen), catalyst (supported on surface or inside membrane porosity), and liquid phase (containing molecule to be hydrogenated)
rules.reasoning: This provides a triple point of contact between three different phases while avoiding hydrogen diffusional problems through liquid layer wetting catalyst particles

rules.condition: WHEN comparing CMR and batch stirred tank reactors for methylene-cyclohexane hydrogenation at different temperatures
rules.recommendation: Operate at temperatures lower than 315 K where both reactors show similar rates, indicating same phenomenon controls the process
rules.reasoning: At higher temperatures, slurry reactor rates level off due to regime change from kinetic-controlled to diffusion-controlled, while CMR maintains kinetic-controlled regime throughout temperature range
rules.quantitative: Below 315 K: similar rates; above 315 K: CMR maintains kinetic control
rules.exceptions: Above 315 K where slurry reactors become diffusion-limited

rules.condition: WHEN performing catalytic oxidation of hydrocarbons in CMRs
rules.recommendation: Separate the feed of both reactants (oxygen and hydrocarbon) by membrane while allowing continuous permeation of oxygen through the CMR length
rules.reasoning: Control of oxygen concentration along reactor length increases selectivity towards intermediate oxidation products and provides safety by avoiding thermal runaway or explosions

rules.condition: WHEN performing partial oxidation reactions in CMRs
rules.recommendation: Separate oxidant and organic substrate in different CMR compartments to avoid possibility of thermal runaway or explosions
rules.reasoning: Separation increases safety of the process by preventing direct contact between oxidant and substrate, potentially broadening the range of feasible operation

rules.condition: WHEN using porous V2O5/γ-Al2O3 membranes for ethylene oxidation to acetaldehyde
rules.recommendation: Expect higher selectivity towards intermediate oxidation product compared to conventional reactor
rules.reasoning: The membrane configuration allows better control of reactant contact, leading to improved selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN using CMRs with organometallic catalyst incorporated into polydimethylsiloxane membrane for 3-pentene-2-ol epoxidation with H2O2
rules.recommendation: Expect very impressive increase in selectivity from ~20% to 100% when using CMR instead of free catalyst
rules.reasoning: The membrane reactor configuration provides better control over catalyst-substrate interaction

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow-through catalytic membrane reactors (FTCMRs) with catalyst immobilized within membrane
rules.recommendation: Recognize that contact between reactants and catalyst occurs during mass transfer process, with reactions taking place simultaneously with mass transfer through the membrane
rules.reasoning: This configuration reduces distance between catalyst and substrate, increasing probability of reaction and enabling better control of reaction through mass transfer rate

rules.condition: WHEN using FTCMRs for highly exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Recognize that heat removal from membrane is limited and isothermal operation can hardly be allowed
rules.reasoning: The membrane configuration limits heat transfer capability compared to conventional reactors

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pore size for CMRs
rules.recommendation: Choose pore size as a compromise between pressure drop and desired intensive contact, recognizing pore sizes can vary by several orders of magnitude (from microns to nanometers)
rules.reasoning: Pore size selection represents a fundamental trade-off between operational pressure requirements and reaction efficiency
rules.quantitative: Pore size range: microns to nanometers

rules.condition: WHEN comparing fixed-bed reactors and CMRs with same catalyst for methanol dehydrogenation to formaldehyde
rules.recommendation: Expect specific reaction rates (per gram of catalyst) to be 10 times higher in the CMR
rules.reasoning: The flow-through micro-CMR configuration provides enhanced contact between reactants and catalyst
rules.quantitative: 10x higher specific reaction rates in CMR

rules.condition: WHEN using flow-through micro-CMR with porous silicon membrane impregnated with Pd for CO oxidation to CO2
rules.recommendation: Optimize design to reach 100% conversion factor in the flow-through micro-CMR
rules.reasoning: The micro-reactor configuration enables complete conversion when properly designed

rules.condition: WHEN using catalytic membrane reactors (CMRs) for partial oxidation reactions
rules.recommendation: Increase convective flow from 70-130 cm³ min⁻¹ through catalytic porous glass membranes to enhance selectivity
rules.reasoning: Higher convective flow through membrane with immobilized catalyst effectively contributes to enhancement of product selectivity by improving mass transfer
rules.quantitative: Flow range: 70-130 cm³ min⁻¹, selectivity improvement from 18 to 41% at 483 K

rules.condition: WHEN performing oxidative coupling reactions in flow-through membrane reactors
rules.recommendation: Use short and controlled contact times to enhance selectivity towards desired products
rules.reasoning: Short contact times reduce side reactions between products and reactants (O₂, H₂), leading to better selectivity
rules.quantitative: 5 times selectivity enhancement compared to classical packed-bed reactor

rules.condition: WHEN operating CMRs under Knudsen diffusion regime vs mixed-regime
rules.recommendation: Accept 15-50 K temperature decrease for combustion efficiency when using Knudsen regime
rules.reasoning: Under Knudsen regime, molecule-catalyst surface collisions prevail over molecule-molecule collisions, reducing mass transfer resistance in pores and lowering combustion temperatures
rules.quantitative: 15-50 K temperature decrease, 350-450% pressure enhancement under Knudsen regime
rules.exceptions: Mixed permeation regime may be preferred after cost analysis

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow-through catalytic membrane reactors (FTCMRs)
rules.recommendation: Scale from laboratory to pilot unit using multi-capillary CMR modules (e.g., 27-capillary modules)
rules.reasoning: Multi-capillary configurations maintain enhanced space time yields while providing scalable reactor design

rules.condition: WHEN immobilizing enzymes in membrane reactors
rules.recommendation: Avoid high enzyme loading to prevent decreased biocatalytic activity
rules.reasoning: High enzyme loading causes crowding on the surface, resulting in blocking of active sites or multiple attachment points that decrease conformational flexibility and inhibit substrate accessibility
rules.quantitative: Activity decreases with enhanced enzyme loading

rules.condition: WHEN choosing enzyme immobilization methods for membrane reactors
rules.recommendation: Use covalent bonding (diazonium salts, carbodiimides, cyanogen bromide, glutaraldehyde, epichlorohydrin) to avoid biocatalyst leaching
rules.reasoning: Covalent bonding prevents enzyme leaching that can occur with weaker interactions like adsorption or Van der Waals forces
rules.exceptions: Some immobilization methods like entrapment can cause diffusional limitations

rules.condition: WHEN designing enzymatic membrane reactors (EMRs) for oil hydrolysis
rules.recommendation: Use hollow-fiber configuration to achieve high surface-volume ratios and long contact times
rules.reasoning: Hollow-fiber EMRs offer relatively long contact times due to high surface-volume ratios, making them efficient for oil hydrolysis without emulsion formation

rules.condition: WHEN performing triglyceride hydrolysis in EMRs
rules.recommendation: Design membrane to separate aqueous phase (where glycerol solubilizes) from oily phase to avoid emulsion formation
rules.reasoning: Phase separation prevents emulsion formation and allows lipase enzyme to act spontaneously at the hydrophilic-hydrophobic interface

rules.condition: WHEN performing enzymatic reactions in organic solvents with EMRs
rules.recommendation: Use ceramic membranes instead of polymer membranes for solvent resistance
rules.reasoning: Ceramic membranes are solvent resistant, while polymers can swell in organic solvents

rules.condition: WHEN using supercritical CO₂ (scCO₂) in EMRs
rules.recommendation: Use ceramic membranes for stability and to enable easy product recovery without organic solvent traces
rules.reasoning: Ceramic membranes are stable under scCO₂ conditions and scCO₂ allows easy product recovery without organic solvent contamination
rules.quantitative: Enhanced activity and selectivity in scCO₂ compared to classical organic solvents

rules.condition: WHEN degrading pharmaceuticals using EMRs
rules.recommendation: Use grafted enzymes rather than free biocatalyst for equivalent enzyme amounts
rules.reasoning: Grafted enzymes are more efficient than free biocatalyst and maintain catalytic activity for extended periods
rules.quantitative: 119 mg tetracycline degraded per hour per m² for more than 200 h without losing activity

rules.condition: WHEN selecting membrane pore size for enzyme immobilization
rules.recommendation: Use larger pore sizes (1.4 μm vs 0.2 μm) for enzyme immobilization inside membrane porosity
rules.reasoning: Larger pore size allows enzyme immobilization both on surface and inside porosity, enhancing reaction rates
rules.quantitative: 80% enhancement in depletion rate with 1.4 μm vs 0.2 μm pore size at 8 L h⁻¹ m⁻² flux

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactors for flow chemistry applications
rules.recommendation: Use channel diameters typically in the range of 10 to 500 micrometers
rules.reasoning: This size range defines continuous flow microreactors and enables the generation of thin lamellas of fluids for fast mass transfer while maintaining small internal structures
rules.exceptions: Production scale reactors may use larger dimensions up to 1mm or above for specific performance requirements

rules.condition: WHEN constructing microreactors for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use glass, quartz, silicon, polymers, or stainless steel as construction materials
rules.reasoning: These materials are compatible with microreactor fabrication and can withstand the operating conditions of flow chemistry processes
rules.exceptions: For specific applications, materials like glassy carbon, titanium, and hastelloy may be preferred

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactors for lab-scale investigation
rules.recommendation: Design for throughput range of 0.1-1 litres per hour
rules.reasoning: Small structures down to 40 micrometers are suitable for lab-scale investigation rather than production scale
rules.exceptions: Production scale applications require different throughput considerations

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up microreactors for higher throughput
rules.recommendation: Use larger structure dimensions around 600 μm or channel dimensions around 1 mm or above
rules.reasoning: Larger throughput ranges require bigger structures while maintaining the benefits of microreactor technology
rules.quantitative: ~600 μm structure dimension, ≥1 mm channel dimension

rules.condition: WHEN working at higher temperature settings in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Build up system pressure to avoid gas formation and work above the boiling point of the used solvent
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors enable working at higher temperatures to accelerate reaction speed while managing reaction enthalpy through excellent heat transfer capabilities

rules.condition: WHEN working with increased concentrations in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Reduce solvent usage by leveraging excellent heat transfer capabilities of flow reactors
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors enable the reduction of solvent that is sometimes used to mitigate heat release in batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN working with hazardous elements in flow processes
rules.recommendation: Utilize the small reactor hold-up in an intensified process to safely work with hazardous elements like diazo methane, hydrogen azide, or peroxides
rules.reasoning: Small reactor hold-up reduces the inventory of hazardous materials present in the system at any given time

rules.condition: WHEN generating unstable intermediates in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Couple the direct further conversion in a continuous process to handle unstable intermediates
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors enable the generation and immediate consumption of unstable intermediates without isolation

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow reactor processes from lab to production
rules.recommendation: Keep critical reactor dimensions constant when moving to larger reactors for higher throughputs to reduce scale-up risks
rules.reasoning: This approach minimizes the risks associated with transferring a process from laboratory to production scale

rules.condition: WHEN selecting 3D printing materials for microfluidic devices using stereolithography (SL)
rules.recommendation: USE photo-curable polymers containing acrylate and/or epoxy functional groups with resolution of 27×27×0.25 μm
rules.reasoning: SL provides low price and high resolution for 3D printers
rules.exceptions: Limited material selection, high price of resin, low viscosity requirement, poor solvent resistance

rules.condition: WHEN selecting 3D printing materials for microfluidic devices using digital light projection (DLP)
rules.recommendation: USE proprietary photoresins (Formlabs, Watershed, Somos, Ormocer, Pototec, etc.) with resolution of 50×50×50 μm
rules.reasoning: DLP offers good resolution and low equipment cost
rules.exceptions: Limited material selection, high resin price, low viscosity requirement, poor solvent resistance

rules.condition: WHEN selecting 3D printing materials for microfluidic devices using multiple jet modelling (MJM)
rules.recommendation: USE proprietary photoresins (Visijet, Vero, FullCure, etc.) with resolution of 34×34×16 μm
rules.reasoning: MJM provides higher resolution than other 3D printing techniques and enables multimaterial printing
rules.exceptions: Limited material selection, high price of resins and printers, low viscosity requirement, poor solvent resistance

rules.condition: WHEN selecting 3D printing materials for microfluidic devices using fused deposition modelling (FDM)
rules.recommendation: USE thermosoftening plastics (ABS, PLA, PP, PMMA, PET, PDMS, etc.) with resolution of 100×100×80 μm
rules.reasoning: FDM offers diverse selection of thermoplastics as open source materials
rules.exceptions: Low resolution and need for post-treatment for smooth surfaces

rules.condition: WHEN selecting 3D printing materials for microfluidic devices using selective laser sintering (SLS)
rules.recommendation: USE metals (stainless steel, titanium, cobalt-chrome, aluminium, nickel, etc.) with resolution of 30×30×20 μm
rules.reasoning: SLS provides superior resistance and mechanical strength of metals
rules.exceptions: High price of 3D printer and long process time required

rules.condition: WHEN designing 3D-printed metallic flow reactors using selective laser melting (SLM)
rules.recommendation: Use SLM for creating chemical reactors resistant to harsh reaction conditions (temperature, pressure, and corrosiveness) required in chemical production
rules.reasoning: SLM allows processing of metals as construction materials, providing superior resistance to harsh conditions compared to polymers

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between selective laser sintering (SLS) and selective laser melting (SLM)
rules.recommendation: Use SLM for pure metal alloys and SLS for polymers; SLM fully melts materials while SLS uses thermal treatment (sintering)
rules.reasoning: SLM provides complete fusion while SLS may leave remaining porosity from incomplete particle fusion, potentially leading to leakages at elevated pressures

rules.condition: WHEN designing tube-in-tube reactors with 3D-printed fluid guiding elements
rules.recommendation: Use 3D-printed inserts with wall thickness of 150 μm to minimize flow obstruction and pressure drop while maintaining heat exchange performance
rules.reasoning: Thin wall thickness (150 μm) causes only marginal flow obstruction while facilitating dedicated subdivision of flow into partial flows that alternately contact heat transferring walls

rules.condition: WHEN using 3D-printed inserts in tube-in-tube reactors
rules.recommendation: Use inserts in standard pipes and fittings to ensure safe operation at high pressure
rules.reasoning: Standard pipes and fittings provide structural integrity for high-pressure operation while inserts provide enhanced performance

rules.condition: WHEN using 3D-printed flow guiding inserts in heat exchange applications
rules.recommendation: Expect more than doubling of thermal power transfer compared to empty tube-in-tube reactor configuration
rules.reasoning: Flow guiding elements facilitate subdivision of flow into partial flows that alternately contact heat transferring walls, maintaining high local heat transfer along reactor length

rules.condition: WHEN fabricating 3D-printed metallic flow guiding elements using SLM
rules.recommendation: Use copper tube (12 mm outer diameter × 1 mm wall thickness) as inner tube and stainless steel tube (18.0 mm × 1.2 mm) as outer tube
rules.reasoning: These dimensions provide appropriate gap for flow guiding elements while maintaining structural integrity
rules.quantitative: Inner tube: 12 mm OD × 1 mm wall; Outer tube: 18.0 mm × 1.2 mm

rules.condition: WHEN designing 3D-printed flow guiding elements
rules.recommendation: Fill gap between inner and outer tube along a length of 28.8 cm with flow guiding elements providing specific surface area of >2000 m⁻¹
rules.reasoning: High specific surface area enhances heat and mass transfer performance
rules.quantitative: Length: 28.8 cm; Specific surface area: >2000 m⁻¹; Void fraction: 83-85%

rules.condition: WHEN using SLM for stainless steel powder fabrication
rules.recommendation: Use 316 L stainless steel powder with SLM125 machine settings: powder layer height 30 μm, laser spot size ≥25 μm
rules.reasoning: These parameters ensure proper layer formation and structural integrity
rules.quantitative: Powder layer height: 30 μm; Laser spot size: ≥25 μm

rules.condition: WHEN fabricating structures using SLM within size constraints
rules.recommendation: Structures within a cube of 12 cm × 12 cm × 20 cm can be realized with realization time of inserts made by SLM assumed to be less than 50 min (10 cm³ per hour)
rules.reasoning: These are typical manufacturing constraints and timeframes for SLM processes
rules.quantitative: Build volume: 12 cm × 12 cm × 20 cm; Build rate: 10 cm³ per hour; Time: <50 min

rules.condition: WHEN using 3D-printed metal foam reactors for continuous chemical production
rules.recommendation: Consider metal foam filled reactors for better plug flow behavior, improved mass transfer and heat exchange compared to empty tubes
rules.reasoning: Metal foam structures provide advantages comparable to commercial static mixing elements with high specific surface area for heterogeneous catalyzed reactions
rules.quantitative: Porosity: 84% (tetrahedral spheres diameter 2.9 mm in cylinder diameter 7 mm)
rules.exceptions: Limitations include corrosion resistance and machining/dimensioning challenges

rules.condition: WHEN using 3D-printed foam-like heat exchanger structures in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use foam structures to increase heat transfer by a factor of 10 compared to empty tubes
rules.reasoning: The foam-like structures provide increased surface area and heat transfer capability
rules.quantitative: Heat transfer increased by factor of 10

rules.condition: WHEN using fully sintered foam reactors with direct wall connection
rules.recommendation: Expect 30% increase in pseudo-convective heat transfer rate on average
rules.reasoning: Direct wall connection of foam structures enhances heat transfer through improved thermal conductivity

rules.condition: WHEN designing 3D-printed heat exchangers for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use effective length of 20 cm for heat exchanger with heating core wrapped around

rules.condition: WHEN operating foam-based heat exchangers in flow systems

rules.reasoning: This flow rate range provides optimal heat transfer performance

rules.condition: WHEN using 3D-printed catalytic static mixers with Pd coating
rules.recommendation: Use inserts of 150 mm × 6 mm coated with Pd via electroplating in temperature-controlled housing
rules.reasoning: This configuration provides adequate catalyst surface area and temperature control

rules.condition: WHEN designing CSM (Catalytic Static Mixer) reactors
rules.recommendation: Use reactor volume of 39 mL with 2.4 g of Pd catalyst loading
rules.reasoning: This provides adequate catalyst loading for effective conversion

rules.condition: WHEN using optimized CSM reaction conditions
rules.recommendation: Expect full conversion with only minor traces of side product (<0.1%)
rules.reasoning: Proper optimization of CSM conditions minimizes side reactions

rules.condition: WHEN designing CSM reactors for production
rules.recommendation: Target productivity of half a kilogram of product per day
rules.reasoning: This represents feasible production scale for CSM technology

rules.condition: WHEN comparing CSM to fixed bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Expect cost reduction by factor of 10-100 compared to other current technologies
rules.reasoning: CSM designs offer higher flow rates, lower pressure drop, and reduced clogging risk

rules.condition: WHEN designing 3D-printed reactors for difluoromethylation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use single reaction channel with 0.8 mm inner diameter and 2.4 mm outer diameter, slightly oval cross section
rules.reasoning: Oval shape reduces printing area overhangs and provides better surface quality
rules.quantitative: 0.8 mm inner diameter, 2.4 mm outer diameter

rules.condition: WHEN integrating heat exchanger with 3D-printed reactor

rules.reasoning: This configuration provides adequate cooling capacity and integration

rules.condition: WHEN designing meandering channels for mixing enhancement

rules.quantitative: Three-dimensional meandering channel, 4 m length, 1.9 mL reaction volume

rules.condition: WHEN operating difluoromethylation reactions in 3D-printed reactor

rules.condition: WHEN operating difluoromethylation in optimized reactor

rules.condition: WHEN 3D printing reactors using SLM (Selective Laser Melting)

rules.reasoning: This particle size provides good printability and surface finish

rules.condition: WHEN designing 3D-printed reactors for powder removal

rules.condition: WHEN using EOS system for 3D printing reactors

rules.condition: WHEN designing commercial 3D-printed flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN selecting materials for 3D-printed reactors

rules.reasoning: These materials provide chemical resistance and structural integrity

rules.condition: WHEN fabricating 3D-printed heat exchangers using SLM
rules.recommendation: Achieve wall thicknesses of 340-650 μm with surface roughness of 30-50 μm
rules.reasoning: These specifications represent achievable precision with standard SLM processes

rules.reasoning: The devices are specifically designed to handle high-pressure conditions for chemical process applications

rules.recommendation: Use reaction channel cross-sections of 2000 μm × 500 μm and heat exchanger channels of 2000 μm × 700 μm, with wall thickness between heat exchanger and reaction layer of 500 μm
rules.reasoning: These dimensions provide optimal balance between heat transfer efficiency and structural integrity while allowing proper fluid flow

rules.condition: WHEN fabricating modular microreactors for high-temperature and high-pressure applications
rules.recommendation: Use stainless steel type 1.4404 as the material for 3D-printed reactor modules
rules.reasoning: This steel grade provides necessary corrosion resistance and mechanical properties for chemical processing under harsh conditions

rules.condition: WHEN operating modular microreactor systems for organic chemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Design for operation up to 150°C and 50 bar depending on gasket material
rules.reasoning: These are the operational limits for safe and effective chemical processing in the modular system design

rules.condition: WHEN designing single microreactor modules
rules.recommendation: Target internal reaction volume of approximately 7 cm³
rules.reasoning: This volume provides adequate residence time for most chemical reactions while maintaining good heat and mass transfer

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up modular microreactor systems
rules.recommendation: Add additional core reaction modules to extend reaction channels and achieve modular assembly with total volumes up to 21 cm³ for three modules
rules.reasoning: Modular design allows for easy scale-up by connecting multiple modules while maintaining individual temperature control

rules.condition: WHEN designing reactors for Grignard reagent formation using magnesium turnings
rules.recommendation: Use cartridge inner diameter of about 2 cm with total inner volume of about 5 cm³, filled with 15-20 g of magnesium turnings
rules.reasoning: This configuration provides adequate surface area for heterogeneous reaction while allowing proper fluid flow and heat transfer
rules.quantitative: Inner diameter: ~2 cm; Volume: ~5 cm³; Mg loading: 15-20 g

rules.condition: WHEN operating Grignard reagent formation reactors
rules.recommendation: Operate at 20-60°C at atmospheric pressure with liquid flow rate of organic halide (1-2 M in organic solvent) in the range of 0.5-5.0 cm³ min⁻¹
rules.reasoning: These conditions provide optimal reaction kinetics while maintaining safety and preventing side reactions
rules.quantitative: Temperature: 20-60°C; Pressure: atmospheric; Flow rate: 0.5-5.0 cm³ min⁻¹; Concentration: 1-2 M

rules.condition: WHEN designing heat exchanger structures for Grignard reagent reactors
rules.recommendation: Use heat exchanger cross-section of approximately 3 mm × 1 mm with remaining wall thickness of about 1 mm
rules.reasoning: These dimensions provide effective heat removal for the highly exothermic Grignard formation reaction
rules.quantitative: Heat exchanger cross-section: ~3 mm × 1 mm; Wall thickness: ~1 mm

rules.condition: WHEN selecting additive manufacturing methods for flow chemistry reactors
rules.recommendation: Use SLM (Selective Laser Melting) for metallic devices requiring harsh process conditions (high pressures, high temperatures, challenging material stability)
rules.reasoning: SLM allows realization of metallic devices that can address challenges of chemical production under harsh process conditions, unlike other methods that mainly deliver polymeric devices with limitations
rules.exceptions: Other methods (SL, DLP, MJM, FDM, SLS) mainly suitable for polymeric devices with process limitations

rules.condition: WHEN using external service providers for SLM fabrication
rules.recommendation: Expect delivery of devices in less than ten working days
rules.reasoning: SLM by external service providers offers fast assembly and realization compared to conventional manufacturing methods

rules.condition: WHEN categorizing continuous tubular reactors (CTRs) by scale
rules.recommendation: Use micro-scale reactors for cross-sectional diameters less than 1 mm, milli-scale reactors for 1-10 mm range, and meso-scale reactors for interim scale-up position up to macro-scale production in litres
rules.reasoning: Different scales provide different advantages and are suited for different production volumes and process development stages
rules.quantitative: Micro-scale: <1 mm diameter; Milli-scale: 1-10 mm diameter; Meso-scale: interim to macro-scale (litres)

rules.condition: WHEN operating microfluidic reactors at low Reynolds numbers
rules.recommendation: Expect laminar flow conditions with smooth and constant flow stream, as viscous forces dominate over inertial forces
rules.reasoning: At low Reynolds numbers, viscous forces are more dominant and characterized by smooth flow as opposed to chaotic patterns at higher Reynolds
rules.quantitative: Reynolds number Re = ρ·u·L/μ, where ρ is fluid density, u is velocity, L is characteristic length, μ is dynamic viscosity
rules.exceptions: Does not apply at higher Reynolds numbers where turbulent flow occurs

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing diffusive mixing in microfluidic systems
rules.recommendation: Maximize interfacial surface area and concentration gradient, and increase temperature to enhance diffusion coefficient
rules.reasoning: Diffusive flux equals D·A·∇c, where enhancing diffusion coefficient requires appropriate temperature increase
rules.quantitative: Diffusive flux = D·A·∇c (D=diffusion coefficient, A=interfacial surface area, ∇c=concentration gradient)
rules.exceptions: Temperature increases must be compatible with reaction chemistry and materials

rules.condition: WHEN designing mixing strategies for low Reynolds number flows
rules.recommendation: Use splitting and recombining methods to maximize interfacial surface area and concentration gradients
rules.reasoning: Since mixing is dependent on intermolecular diffusion at low Reynolds, methods to increase contact area are essential

rules.condition: WHEN using CTRs for synthetic processes requiring temperature control
rules.recommendation: Take advantage of enhanced heat transfer capabilities compared to stirred tank reactors for maintaining reaction temperature control
rules.reasoning: CTRs provide significant advantages in maintaining control of heat transfer, mass transport and extreme reaction conditions

rules.condition: WHEN selecting between CTR scales for process development
rules.recommendation: Use lab-scale CTRs for safer handling, waste minimization, efficient reagent use, and isolation of moisture-sensitive compounds
rules.reasoning: CTRs are advantageous across scales for safer synthetic processes, efficient reactant use, and better control compared to stirred tank reactors

rules.condition: WHEN designing reactors requiring precise mixing and selective reagent contact
rules.recommendation: Consider microfluidic reactors as they are specifically beneficial for applications requiring precise ordering and selective contact between reagents
rules.reasoning: Microfluidic reactors excel at precise control and selective interactions due to their scale and flow characteristics

rules.condition: WHEN using FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling) for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Achieve fabrication resolution between 0.35 to 0.1 mm with fabrication accuracy of around ±0.25 mm
rules.reasoning: FDM resolution depends on materials and machine specification, and typical accuracy ranges provide guidance for design tolerances

rules.condition: WHEN using Robocasting for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Achieve resolution of 30-500 μm for deposited ceramic, metal or polymer parts
rules.reasoning: Robocasting allows controlled deposition of paste-like suspensions after drying or firing

rules.condition: WHEN using FDM parts for flow reactors with smaller channel diameters and sharp corners
rules.recommendation: Account for staircase effect and implement strategies to create leak-free reactors including proper material selection, reactor geometry optimization, and layer height control
rules.reasoning: FDM parts have poorer physical properties perpendicular to the build plane, making leaks likely to occur between layers, especially at sharp corners

rules.condition: WHEN using vat photopolymerisation (VP) for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Use UV-curable polymers based on acrylates, urethanes and epoxides
rules.reasoning: VP platforms are typically limited to these polymer types for proper curing and mechanical properties

rules.condition: WHEN using stereolithography (SLA) for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Achieve layer thicknesses as low as 25 μm to 150 μm with laser spot sizes typically around 0.15 mm
rules.reasoning: SLA allows production of fully dense parts with high precision using laser drawing of 2D patterns
rules.quantitative: Layer thickness: 25-150 μm, Laser spot size: ~0.15 mm

rules.condition: WHEN using VP materials (Formlabs Clear resin FLGPCL02) for flow reactors
rules.recommendation: AVOID prolonged exposure to Isopropanol (i-PrOH), Ethanol (EtOH) and Acetonitrile (MeCN) as these cause degradation of the chemical structure after 24 hours at room temperature
rules.reasoning: TGA and IR analysis showed changes to degradation profile and chemical structure when exposed to these solvents
rules.quantitative: Exposure time: 24 hours at room temperature

rules.condition: WHEN using VP materials (Accura 60 resin) for flow reactors
rules.recommendation: AVOID prolonged exposure to THF and dichloromethane as significant swelling occurs after 24 hours
rules.reasoning: These solvents cause physical degradation through swelling of the VP material

rules.condition: WHEN using material jetting (MJ) for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Achieve layer resolution of 25 to 100 μm at deposition rates of 8 pl to 1 nL per droplet using acrylate-based photopolymer resins
rules.reasoning: MJ allows precise droplet deposition similar to inkjet printing for building parts layer by layer
rules.quantitative: Layer resolution: 25-100 μm, Deposition rates: 8 pl - 1 nL per droplet

rules.condition: WHEN using MJ parts for flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Remove sacrificial materials by water jetting or soaking in sodium hydroxide solution, and consider post-curing under UV light to enhance rigidity
rules.reasoning: Sacrificial materials support overhanging structures during fabrication and must be removed, while UV curing improves mechanical properties
rules.exceptions: Cleaning channels with numerous corners and active mixers is challenging

rules.condition: WHEN using laser sintering (LS) for polymer flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Heat powder bed close to material melt temperature and use layer thicknesses of 0.1 mm or 0.15 mm, typically using polyamides (PA12 and PA11)
rules.reasoning: LS requires precise temperature control for proper consolidation, and polyamides provide good solvent compatibility
rules.exceptions: Solid state sintering is not achieved with polymers due to insufficient diffusion time

rules.condition: WHEN using LS for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Account for potential leakages at high pressures due to roughness and porosity from consolidation, and consider heat treatment to influence physical and mechanical properties
rules.reasoning: LS consolidation can create porous structures that may leak under pressure, but parts have good solvent compatibility

rules.condition: WHEN using selective laser melting (SLM) for metal flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Use high-power fiber laser of 100 to 400 W to produce thin layers of 0.02 to 0.1 mm with titanium, aluminium and stainless steel materials
rules.reasoning: SLM creates completely dense parts using full melting, and these metals are chemically stable across broad conditions
rules.quantitative: Laser power: 100-400 W, Layer thickness: 0.02-0.1 mm

rules.condition: WHEN using SLM parts for flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Take advantage that remaining powder can be fully reused without mixing with virgin powder, unlike thermoplastics
rules.reasoning: SLM metal powder processing allows complete reuse of unfused material

rules.condition: WHEN using electron beam melting (EBM) for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Process materials at high temperatures (>870 K) using Ti grade 2, Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718, CoCrMo and optimize beam scanning velocity, beam power, beam focus, plate temperature, and pre-heat temperature
rules.reasoning: EBM requires vacuum processing and precise parameter control for proper consolidation

rules.condition: WHEN using powder bed fusion methods for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Plan for post-processing to separate un-fused powder from fabricated parts and consider air or powder jetting for cleaning, plus potential CNC machining for surface finish improvement
rules.reasoning: All powder bed methods require removal of unused powder and may need surface finishing

rules.condition: WHEN using directed energy deposition (DED) or sheet lamination for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: AVOID these methods as their resolution is not suitable for flow reactors and will require post machining to achieve required surface finish and feature sizes
rules.reasoning: These AM methods lack the precision needed for flow reactor applications

rules.condition: WHEN using FDM 3D printing for flow reactor fabrication with polypropylene and nylon materials
rules.recommendation: Fabricate on sheets of similar materials or design features into the reactor design to control warping, as these materials shrink and warp when cooling
rules.reasoning: Polypropylene and nylon materials do not easily adhere to the build platform, and as layers cool down, they shrink, pulling inwards and warping the structure

rules.condition: WHEN using FDM 3D printing for flow reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Avoid platforms or materials restricted to larger layer thicknesses as they will lead to flow devices with ridged or 'scalloped' patterns
rules.reasoning: Surface roughness from larger layer resolutions can produce results that vary largely from the intended design due to ridged patterns affecting flow

rules.condition: WHEN functionalizing nylon surface of FDM reactor for enzyme immobilization

rules.reasoning: This treatment functionalizes the internal wall surface of the FDM reactor to immobilize enzymes, enabling optimized contact between reagents and enzymes under continuous flow

rules.condition: WHEN manufacturing flow reactors using extrusion-based methods
rules.recommendation: Incorporate active and passive mixing methods to improve heat and mass transfer, as producing leak-free devices is challenging
rules.reasoning: Extrusion-based methods have limitations in producing leak-free devices, and advanced mixing capabilities are required for enhanced heat and mass transfer

rules.condition: WHEN designing 3D mixers using vat photopolymerization (VP) with split-and-recombine geometry
rules.recommendation: Use geometry that folds fluid at repeated 3D bends and re-combines at intervals to create larger area for fast diffusion-based mixing
rules.reasoning: VP allows highly beneficial 3D structure manufacturing that would be very hard to manufacture by traditional methods, enhancing mixing by folding fluid streams

rules.condition: WHEN comparing 3D mixers to tubular reactors using fluorescein (0.1 μg mL⁻¹) and rhodamine B (1 μg mL⁻¹) solutions
