rules.reasoning: 3D mixers demonstrate superior mixing performance compared to conventional tubular reactors under identical test conditions
rules.quantitative: Fluorescein: 0.1 μg mL⁻¹, Rhodamine B: 1 μg mL⁻¹

rules.condition: WHEN using meso-scale oscillatory baffled flow reactor (mCOBR) with embedded baffles
rules.recommendation: Control mixing patterns by changing the frequency and amplitude of oscillation of bulk fluid inside the reactor
rules.reasoning: When bulk fluid oscillates inside reactor, vortices form between inter-baffle zones during forward and backward stroke, creating varied mixing patterns

rules.condition: WHEN designing mCOBR with embedded baffles for particle synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use periodically spaced baffles with scalable dimensions including baffle thickness, inner and outer diameter and spacing
rules.reasoning: mCOBR resembles tubular reactor with embedded baffles serving as static mixers, and scalable dimensions allow for controlled mixing patterns

rules.condition: WHEN using Dean flow-based trapezoidal reactor for magnetic nanoparticle separation
rules.recommendation: Use helical channel design to create secondary flow formation with asymmetric trapezoidal channels for size-sorting effect
rules.reasoning: Helical reactor creates secondary flow with two generated vortices, and asymmetric trapezoidal channels result in size-sorting where larger materials migrate towards outer channel wall

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing AgNPs using mCOBR platform with HPLC pumps
rules.recommendation: Use flow rates of 0.25 mM AgNO₃ in CH₃CN and 0.75 mM NaBH₄ with 0.75 g PVP in CH₃CN, connect to UV spectrometer, light source and flow cell
rules.reasoning: The mCOBR showed improved stability during synthetic process, reduced fouling and narrower size distribution of particles compared to tubular reactors
rules.quantitative: 0.25 mM AgNO₃ in CH₃CN, 0.75 mM NaBH₄, 0.75 g PVP in CH₃CN, reactor volume: 2.7 mL

rules.condition: WHEN using AM fabricated mCOBR compared to tubular reactor
rules.recommendation: Expect more evenly distributed residence time distribution in mCOBR
rules.reasoning: AM fabricated mCOBR demonstrated superior residence time distribution characteristics compared to similar tubular reactor at equivalent volume

rules.condition: WHEN operating open cell foam reactors under low Reynolds number regime
rules.recommendation: Operate at Reynolds number Re < 50 for optimal performance
rules.reasoning: Open-cell foams are considered promising candidates for structured catalyst supports under low Reynolds number conditions

rules.condition: WHEN designing open cell foam reactors using additive manufacturing
rules.recommendation: Target cell size of approximately 4 mm and porosity of 0.8
rules.reasoning: AM enabled fabrication of complex foam structures with these dimensions, which provide large surface area to impinge catalyst and enhance mass transport

rules.condition: WHEN using vat polymerization (VP) for foam reactor fabrication
rules.recommendation: Expect dimensional deviation of 1-5% depending on strut size and foam porosity
rules.reasoning: VP fabricated geometry was measured and compared to CAD file, with denser packing creating more area for un-polymerised resin to form a skin

rules.condition: WHEN designing packed bed reactors with open cell foam structures
rules.recommendation: Verify close correlation between pressure drop and cell diameter, porosity and strut geometry
rules.reasoning: Experimental work showed correlation between pressure drop and these geometric parameters, enabling predictive design

rules.condition: WHEN fabricating catalytic static mixers using EBM
rules.recommendation: Use TiAl64V or cobalt-chrome powders cold sprayed or electroplated with required catalysts
rules.reasoning: EBM based fabrication enables combining improved mass transfer benefits of static mixers with catalyst functionality

rules.condition: WHEN coating AM catalytic flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Coat with nickel or platinum catalyst after fabrication for specific synthetic processes
rules.reasoning: The mixers were designed for specific synthetic processes and coated with appropriate catalysts to serve dual function as mixers and catalysts

rules.condition: WHEN manufacturing complete flow reactors with heat exchanger functionality using SLM

rules.condition: WHEN operating low temperature reactions in AM flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN designing future AM flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Consider applications beyond chemical reactions including separations, electrochemical transformations, and crystallisations under continuous flow

rules.condition: WHEN using SLM for flow reactor fabrication

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up AM flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreaction systems for chemical synthesis
rules.recommendation: Divide microreaction systems into three main categories: (1) system integrated microreactor with stirred tank reactor (MSTR); (2) system integrated microreactor with conventional tubular reactor (MCTR); (3) system integrated microreactor with static mixer (MSM)
rules.reasoning: Different physicochemical properties of chemical processes require different system configurations for optimal performance
rules.quantitative: Three distinct categories based on physicochemical properties

rules.condition: WHEN reactant solubility is poor or suspension and emulsion are needed

rules.reasoning: Stirred tank reactor can handle poor solubility conditions and provide necessary mixing for suspensions and emulsions

rules.condition: WHEN reaction time is too long for tube reactors or solid intermediates and products are generated
rules.recommendation: Use MSTR-II configuration (microreactor integrated with stirred tank reactor for aging or reactions)
rules.reasoning: Long reaction times are impractical in tube reactors due to excessive length requirements, and solid handling requires stirred systems

rules.condition: WHEN reaction rate is relatively slow or heat exchange acceleration is needed for highly exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Use MCTR configuration where conventional tubular reactor increases residence time and chemical conversion or accelerates heat exchange
rules.reasoning: Conventional tubular reactors provide extended residence time for slow reactions and better heat management for exothermic processes

rules.condition: WHEN multiphase flow patterns need to be maintained with heterogeneous reactions
rules.recommendation: Use MSM configuration (microreactor with static mixer) to intensify mixing of reactants and maintain stability of multiphase flow patterns
rules.reasoning: Static mixers provide intensive mixing while maintaining stable multiphase flow conditions required for heterogeneous reactions

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up microreactor technology for industrial applications
rules.recommendation: Consider integrated systems with conventional chemical equipment as an important alternative to parallel numbering-up, consecutive numbering-up, and dimension enlargement strategies
rules.reasoning: Conventional equipment is mature, can offer short residual time compensation, and can mitigate microreactor limitations like clogging and solid handling

rules.condition: WHEN designing new microreaction processes
rules.recommendation: Carry out extensive lab-scale work to ensure the design of the new microreaction system is reliable
rules.reasoning: Reaction kinetics, transfer properties, physical properties, mixing performances, and multiphase flow conditions determine the size of reaction systems and must be well understood

rules.condition: WHEN microreactors cannot deal with solid reactants, intermediates, or products
rules.recommendation: Use conventional chemical equipment integration to compensate for microreactor disadvantages and handle solid materials
rules.reasoning: Microreactors have inherent limitations with solid handling that conventional equipment can overcome

rules.condition: WHEN building laboratory-scale microreaction systems for chemists

rules.reasoning: Chemists focus on chemistry rather than engineering, so systems need to be accessible and cost-effective while meeting process requirements

rules.condition: WHEN preparing nanoparticles in flow using microreactors

rules.reasoning: Microreactors offer advantages for nanoparticle preparation but there are limited applications for large-scale production

rules.condition: WHEN preparing calcium carbonate nanoparticles using CO2 bubbling
rules.recommendation: Control the supersaturation ratio using equation s = [Ca2+][CO32-]/Ksp to enhance nucleation and restrict growth

rules.condition: WHEN preparing calcium carbonate via CO2 bubbling
rules.recommendation: Use bicarbonate ion formation (k1 = 8×103 L mol-1 s-1 at 25°C) and carbonate ion formation (k2 = 6×109 L mol-1 s-1 at 25°C) rate constants
rules.reasoning: Bicarbonate ion can be formed rapidly by direct reaction of CO2 and hydroxyl ions, and will then be quickly transformed to carbonate ion

rules.condition: WHEN conducting gas-liquid reactions for calcium carbonate precipitation

rules.reasoning: Although calcium hydroxide dissociation and calcium carbonate precipitation are ultrafast, the mass transfer of CO2 is slow in gas-liquid two phase reactions

rules.condition: WHEN designing membrane dispersion microreactor (M1) for calcium carbonate preparation

rules.condition: WHEN selecting pumps for laboratory-scale microreaction systems with dispersions

rules.reasoning: Peristaltic pumps are better suited for laboratory-scale research with dispersions compared to piston pumps

rules.condition: WHEN monitoring calcium carbonate precipitation reaction

rules.reasoning: pH monitoring provides real-time feedback on reaction progress for calcium carbonate precipitation

rules.condition: WHEN achieving uniform calcium carbonate nanoparticle size

rules.condition: WHEN using stirred tank reactor for calcium hydroxide dissolution

rules.reasoning: Stirred tank reactor keeps high concentration of calcium hydroxide in solution during most of the reaction

rules.condition: WHEN applying microreaction system to solid-liquid mixture cycling
rules.recommendation: Carefully apply microreaction system only if chemical reaction is strict with stoichiometric ratio in cycling microreaction system
rules.reasoning: Mass of solid-liquid mixture varies with time at the end of reaction, requiring careful application
rules.exceptions: When reaction is not strict with stoichiometric ratio

rules.condition: WHEN reactions are not fast enough and require additional equipment for high reaction conversions

rules.reasoning: Some processes are not fast enough for microreactors alone, and multiphase systems with solid products present configuration challenges
rules.exceptions: For fast chemical processes that can be completed in microreactors

rules.condition: WHEN synthesizing polyvinyl butyral (PVB) with target acetalization degree

rules.condition: WHEN PVB synthesis involves complex phase behavior transitioning from liquid-liquid to liquid-liquid-solid phases

rules.condition: WHEN conducting PVB aging reaction in stirred reactor

rules.reasoning: Higher temperature accelerates liquid-liquid-solid reaction, but excessive temperature causes PVB softening and aggregation

rules.condition: WHEN conducting PVB synthesis in traditional batch reactor

rules.reasoning: This prevents premature precipitation and allows uniform mixing before acceleration of AD growth

rules.condition: WHEN controlling PVB batch reaction process
rules.recommendation: Control reaction by mass transfer of mixing acid catalyst and highly viscous emulsion of PVA/n-butanal, and the liquid-liquid-solid reaction
rules.reasoning: The reaction process is limited by mass transfer in the complex multiphase system

rules.condition: WHEN designing integrated microreaction systems for PVB preparation with phase transformation
rules.recommendation: Use MSTR-II (microreactor with stirred reactor) to divide the process into two stages: microreactor for reaction rate control and stirred reactor for mass transfer problems
rules.reasoning: The microreactor solves mass transfer problems in stage I while the stirred reactor handles long reaction time requirements in stage II
rules.quantitative: Temperature between 33°C and 35°C for delayed tube connection

rules.condition: WHEN using integrated microreaction system for PVB preparation
rules.recommendation: Use 4 hour aging time in stirred reactor after microreaction processing
rules.reasoning: Required aging time transforms residual materials to PVB with AD value greater than required 78%

rules.condition: WHEN reaction processes show characteristics of solid contained, phase change, or long reaction time required

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow systems for high viscous polymer reactions like BIIR preparation

rules.condition: WHEN working with bromine and hydrogen bromide in microreaction systems

rules.condition: WHEN selectivity of BIIR1 in total substituted bromides is required for industrial applications

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreaction systems

rules.recommendation: Fabricate reactors with heated aluminum cylinders, embedded thermocouples, and protect with aluminum shell for safe operation

rules.condition: WHEN conducting flow chemistry reactions requiring rapid synthesis
rules.recommendation: Operate at high temperatures (130°C-180°C) and pressures (~1.7 MPa)
rules.reasoning: High temperature and pressure enable rapid reaction kinetics compared to batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN conducting reactions in automated flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use highly concentrated conditions to minimize waste production
rules.reasoning: Concentrated conditions reduce solvent waste and improve process efficiency in continuous manufacturing

rules.condition: WHEN designing compact flow chemistry platforms
rules.recommendation: Design systems within dimensions of 1.0 m (w) × 0.7 m (l) × 1.8 m (h) for ~100 kg unit capacity
rules.reasoning: Compact design enables efficient use of space while maintaining manufacturing capability
rules.quantitative: Dimensions: 1.0 m × 0.7 m × 1.8 m, capacity: ~100 kg

rules.condition: WHEN designing second-generation API manufacturing platforms
rules.recommendation: Include in-line evaporator suitable for flow rates up to 5 mL/min in reaction module
rules.reasoning: In-line evaporation enables solvent removal and concentration without stopping flow

rules.condition: WHEN designing modular upstream units for API synthesis
rules.recommendation: Include 20 auxiliary devices such as pressure sensors and separators in reaction module
rules.reasoning: Multiple sensors and separators enable precise monitoring and control of reaction conditions

rules.condition: WHEN designing pump modules for flow chemistry platforms
rules.recommendation: House 21 compact Vici Milligat pumps in middle section of upstream unit
rules.reasoning: Multiple pumps enable precise flow control for multiple reagent streams

rules.condition: WHEN designing solvent modules for flow platforms
rules.recommendation: Design to hold 10×1 L and 13×500 mL solvent bottles
rules.reasoning: Multiple solvent containers enable automated solvent switching without manual intervention

rules.condition: WHEN manufacturing neostigmine methylsulfate (0.5 mg per dose)
rules.recommendation: Achieve throughput of 9450 doses per day using compact flow system
rules.reasoning: Flow system enables efficient production of low-dose APIs with high throughput

rules.condition: WHEN manufacturing ciprofloxacin hydrochloride and rufinamide
rules.recommendation: Achieve throughput of 9600 doses per day (0.6 mg per dose) and 75 doses per day (200 mg per dose), respectively
rules.reasoning: Different APIs require different throughput optimization based on dosage requirements
rules.quantitative: Ciprofloxacin: 9600 doses/day at 0.6 mg/dose, Rufinamide: 75 doses/day at 200 mg/dose

rules.condition: WHEN increasing throughput for APIs with large prescribed dosages like rufinamide
rules.recommendation: Scale-up custom vessels or operate multiple units in parallel
rules.reasoning: High-dose APIs require larger production capacity that may exceed single unit capability
rules.quantitative: Example: increasing crystallizer volume from 60 mL to 350 mL increases rufinamide throughput to ~1200 doses/day

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow reactors that need to prevent cross-contamination
rules.recommendation: Use PFA tubing that can be replaced between API syntheses
rules.reasoning: PFA tubing replacement prevents cross-contamination between different API production runs

rules.condition: WHEN switching between API production runs in automated systems
rules.recommendation: Flush upstream and downstream units with solvent, swap reagent and solvent feeds, and adjust fluid manifolds
rules.reasoning: Complete system flushing and reconfiguration ensures no cross-contamination between different products

rules.condition: WHEN manufacturing Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) using continuous flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider that materials are simultaneously charged and discharged from the process, unlike batch chemistry where materials are charged before processing starts and discharged at the end
rules.reasoning: This represents the fundamental difference between batch and continuous API manufacturing systems

rules.condition: WHEN considering flow chemistry for pharmaceutical manufacturing
rules.recommendation: Utilize flow chemistry for improved quality of compounds, reduced scale-up safety risks, and economic advantages
rules.reasoning: Flow processes provide multiple benefits over traditional batch processing including better control and safety

rules.condition: WHEN performing Diels-Alder reactions in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Consider using high temperature and/or pressure conditions with low-cost reactors and infrastructure for more efficient energy consumption
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry allows utilization of conditions that would be problematic in batch due to safety concerns

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with highly unstable reactive intermediates and/or reagents in pharmaceutical synthesis
rules.recommendation: Use flow synthetic processes instead of discarding reagents due to batch safety concerns
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry provides better control and safety for handling unstable materials that would be too dangerous in batch

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up Diels-Alder reactions from laboratory to industrial scale
rules.recommendation: Be aware of challenges including formation of diastereomer mixtures, reactivity of dienes and dienophiles with polymerization, exothermic reactions, need for high pressures, and expensive catalysts
rules.reasoning: These are the primary obstacles that have limited industrial adoption of DA reactions in batch mode

rules.condition: WHEN performing Diels-Alder reactions with myrcene and different dienophiles
rules.recommendation: Use intensified continuous-flow reactors of both tubular and plate types
rules.reasoning: Both reactor types have been demonstrated effective for DA reactions in flow chemistry applications

rules.condition: WHEN performing intramolecular photodehydro Diels-Alder reactions
rules.recommendation: Use flow chemistry to achieve improvements in both efficiency and selectivity compared to batch processes
rules.reasoning: Flow photochemistry provides better control of light exposure and reaction conditions leading to improved outcomes

rules.condition: WHEN performing Diels-Alder reactions in flow chemistry with temperature-sensitive compounds
rules.recommendation: USE temperatures in the range of 100-160°C for diene-dienophile cycloaddition reactions
rules.reasoning: DA reactions require elevated temperatures to achieve reasonable reaction rates, and this temperature range balances reaction kinetics with thermal stability
rules.exceptions: Temperature may vary based on specific substrate reactivity

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical Diels-Alder reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: USE DCM as solvent and apply UV irradiation with appropriate wavelength for the specific chromophore
rules.reasoning: DCM is transparent to UV light and provides good solvation for most organic substrates in photochemical DA reactions
rules.exceptions: Other solvents may be used if DCM is incompatible with substrates

rules.condition: WHEN performing Diels-Alder reactions requiring high pressure in flow systems
rules.recommendation: USE pressures up to 30 bar with appropriate back-pressure regulation
rules.reasoning: Higher pressure can accelerate DA reactions and improve selectivity by favoring the more compact transition state
rules.exceptions: Pressure limits depend on reactor material and safety considerations

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing rapid Diels-Alder reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: USE residence times as short as 30 seconds at elevated temperatures (120°C) for fast DA reactions
rules.reasoning: Short residence times at high temperatures can achieve complete conversion while minimizing side reactions and thermal degradation
rules.exceptions: Slower reactions may require longer residence times

rules.condition: WHEN performing Diels-Alder reactions in flow systems
rules.recommendation: USE dry DCM at 0°C for moisture-sensitive DA reactions, or diglyme with AcOH for acid-catalyzed reactions
rules.reasoning: Dry conditions prevent hydrolysis of reactive intermediates, while acid catalysis can enhance reaction rates and selectivity
rules.exceptions: Solvent choice depends on substrate solubility and reaction requirements

rules.condition: WHEN setting up tubular reactors for flow chemistry optimization
rules.recommendation: ADOPT a diameter to length ratio of 1/10,000 to obtain optimal laminar flow using Stainless Steel 316L or Fluorinated Ethylene Propylene (FEP) tubes
rules.reasoning: This specific ratio ensures proper laminar flow characteristics for consistent residence time distribution and heat transfer
rules.exceptions: Ratio may be adjusted based on specific reaction requirements and Reynolds number considerations

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up flow reactors from test to production scale
rules.recommendation: SCALE from 1/16″ o.d., 1/32″ i.d., 7.94 m, 3.93 mL (test) to 1/8″ o.d., 1/16″ i.d., 15.88 m, 31.42 mL (scale-up) to 6 mm o.d., 4 mm i.d., 40.00 m, 502.6 mL (pilot) to 6 mm o.d., 4 mm i.d., 7.96 m, 100.0 mL (production)
rules.reasoning: Systematic scaling maintains similar flow characteristics while increasing throughput capacity at each scale
rules.quantitative: Specific tube dimensions and volumes: Test: 1/16″ o.d., 1/32″ i.d., 7.94 m, 3.93 mL; Scale-up: 1/8″ o.d., 1/16″ i.d., 15.88 m, 31.42 mL; Pilot: 6 mm o.d., 4 mm i.d., 40.00 m, 502.6 mL; Production: 6 mm o.d., 4 mm i.d., 7.96 m, 100.0 mL
rules.exceptions: Dimensions may need adjustment based on specific reaction kinetics and heat transfer requirements

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up from flow chemistry optimization to industrial production
rules.recommendation: TARGET production capacity of 1 kg h⁻¹ using twin reactors of 50 L (35 L usable capacity) with continuous distillation
rules.reasoning: Twin reactor configuration provides redundancy and continuous operation capability for industrial-scale production
rules.quantitative: 1 kg h⁻¹ production rate, 50 L reactors with 35 L usable capacity
rules.exceptions: Capacity may be adjusted based on market demand and process economics

rules.condition: WHEN working with cyclopentadiene (CPD) in flow systems
rules.recommendation: IMPLEMENT proper cooling and storage systems due to CPD's low boiling point (39-43°C) and highly exothermic spontaneous dimerization (30-36 kJ mol⁻¹)
rules.reasoning: CPD's instability and exothermic dimerization pose significant safety risks including the potential for runaway reactions and explosions
rules.quantitative: Boiling point: 39-43°C, dimerization energy: 30-36 kJ mol⁻¹
rules.exceptions: Special handling procedures required regardless of scale

rules.condition: WHEN preparing CPD for use in DA reactions
rules.recommendation: PREPARE CPD by thermal cracking from dicyclopentadiene (DCPD) at high temperatures (250-300°C)
rules.reasoning: Thermal cracking is the standard method to generate monomeric CPD from the more stable dimer form
rules.exceptions: Alternative preparation methods may be used if thermal cracking is not feasible

rules.condition: WHEN selecting solvents for aDA reactions in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Avoid halogenated solvents such as dichloromethane (DCM) according to green chemistry principles
rules.reasoning: Halogenated solvents are environmentally harmful and should be avoided for sustainable processing

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow chemistry processes for aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Avoid extreme temperatures (-78°C) which are widely used in batch but cause high energy costs
rules.reasoning: Extreme low temperatures result in high energy costs and reduced process efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN performing aDA reactions in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Maintain good solubility during all reaction phases to ensure homogeneous mixtures
rules.reasoning: Good solubility during flow process is essential to achieve reproducible and scalable processes

rules.condition: WHEN choosing solvents for flow chemistry aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Provide optimal selectivity of the target molecule to obtain better isomer ratio without being detrimental to overall yield
rules.reasoning: Solvent selection directly impacts product selectivity and yield in flow processes

rules.condition: WHEN using alcoholic solvents (methanol, ethanol, isopropanol) at low temperatures
rules.recommendation: Avoid alcoholic solvents as they yield low solubility and non-homogeneous reaction mixtures
rules.reasoning: These solvents cause substrate precipitation and poor mixing, leading to non-homogeneous reactions
rules.quantitative: Below -20°C, reaction mixture becomes very viscous and crystallization occurs

rules.condition: WHEN temperatures drop below -20°C in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Avoid conditions that cause crystallization of imine intermediate to prevent clogging
rules.reasoning: Crystallization leads to clogging of the flow system and process failure

rules.condition: WHEN using toluene as solvent for aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Use concentration of 2.5 M of imine in toluene (approximately 1 L toluene per 1 kg imine) for optimal balance
rules.reasoning: This concentration provides perfect balance between low solvent consumption and homogeneous liquid state
rules.quantitative: 2.5 M concentration, 1 L toluene per 1 kg imine

rules.condition: WHEN selecting between ethyl acetate and dimethylformamide (DMF) for aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Discard ethyl acetate and DMF due to instability against acidic reaction conditions
rules.reasoning: These solvents are unstable under acidic conditions and generate undesired impurities

rules.condition: WHEN performing aDA reactions in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Take advantage of working at extreme temperatures due to high surface-to-volume ratio and efficient heat transfer
rules.reasoning: Small reactor size provides advantage of working at extreme temperatures with low energy cost due to high efficiency in heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN temperature drops to -40°C during aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Avoid -40°C as mixture becomes viscous and iminium salt starts to crystallize
rules.reasoning: At -40°C the mixture becomes too viscous and crystallization occurs, making flow processing difficult

rules.condition: WHEN selecting acids for aDA reaction activation
rules.recommendation: Choose acids with pKa values < 1 to ensure formation of iminium salt without water traces
rules.reasoning: Low pKa ensures complete activation and prevents hydrolysis of the imine by trace water
rules.exceptions: Mineral acids except HCl can be generated in situ from TMSCl/MeOH but were discarded due to difficulty of dry form handling

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and methanesulfonic acid for aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Use trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, pKa = 0.23) as it gives homogeneous reaction mixture at tested temperatures
rules.reasoning: TFA provides homogeneous reaction conditions while methanesulfonic acid shows solubility problems yielding heterogeneous mixtures
rules.quantitative: pKa = 0.23 for TFA, pKa = -1.9 for methanesulfonic acid

rules.condition: WHEN using excess CPD in aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Avoid excess of CPD over 3 equivalents to prevent polymerization impurities
rules.reasoning: Excess CPD may cause appearance of impurities related to its polymerization
rules.quantitative: > 3 equivalents of CPD should be avoided

rules.condition: WHEN using high amounts of acid in aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Avoid acid amounts over 2.5 equivalents to prevent CPD polymerization and imine degradation
rules.reasoning: High acid amounts produce polymerization impurities and cause imine degradation through retro-condensation
rules.quantitative: > 2.5 equivalents of acid should be avoided

rules.condition: WHEN storing crude aDA reaction mixture after completion
rules.recommendation: Store at room temperature for maximum 1 hour, at 0°C for 2.5 hours, or at -10°C for more than 12 hours
rules.reasoning: Prevents degradation of the product while allowing time for work-up and purification
rules.quantitative: 1 hour at RT, 2.5 hours at 0°C, >12 hours at -10°C

rules.condition: WHEN aDA reaction reaches completion
rules.recommendation: Buffer reaction mixture at pH = 6 after completion to minimize degradation
rules.reasoning: Buffering prevents degradation and shows no signs of product loss after 12 hours at room temperature

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing aDA reaction conditions
rules.recommendation: Use molar ratio of imine/CPD/TFA = 1/1.5/1.1 in toluene at -20°C for 15 min residence time
rules.reasoning: These conditions provided optimal results with average isolated yield of isomer 2 of 13-15%

rules.condition: WHEN performing aDA reactions at room temperature in flow
rules.recommendation: Use 15 min residence time for completion, extend to 30 min at 0°C and -10°C
rules.reasoning: Reaction completion time varies with temperature, requiring longer residence times at lower temperatures
rules.exceptions: At -20°C, reaction did not reach completion even with extended times

rules.condition: WHEN starting flow chemistry tests for aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Use FEP tube with outer diameter 1/16", inner diameter 0.032", and diameter/length ratio of 1/10000
rules.reasoning: These dimensions provide appropriate flow characteristics for the reaction system

rules.condition: WHEN performing aza-Diels-Alder reactions at -20°C in flow
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 60 minutes to achieve favorable target isomer ratio at -10°C
rules.reasoning: At -10°C the target isomer ratio is more favored but requires longer residence time for completion
rules.exceptions: Higher temperatures reduce selectivity even with longer RT

rules.condition: WHEN attempting reactions at -40°C in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Avoid temperatures of -40°C due to system clogging
rules.reasoning: Clogging of the system occurs at -40°C, preventing proper flow operation

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing aza-Diels-Alder reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use optimal ratio of imine/CPD/TFA 1/1.9/2 in toluene at -20°C for 30 min RT
rules.reasoning: This ratio provides optimal reaction completion while minimizing impurities

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up from small reactors to larger volumes
rules.recommendation: Maintain diameter/length ratio when scaling: use 1/8" (1/16" i.d.) FEP reactor with 1/10000 ratio (15.9 m long, 31 mL capacity) for first phase, then 6 mm (4 mm i.d.) FEP with 1/10000 ratio (40 m long, 502 mL capacity)
rules.reasoning: Maintaining geometric similarity ensures consistent flow characteristics during scale-up
rules.quantitative: Diameter/length ratio 1/10000; Phase 1: 15.9 m long, 31 mL; Phase 2: 40 m long, 502 mL

rules.condition: WHEN using acetic anhydride as water scavenger in aza-Diels-Alder reactions
rules.recommendation: Use ≥7.5% molar ratio of Ac2O relative to imine to maintain amine below 10%
rules.reasoning: Ac2O reacts with water and forms acetic acid as by-product; acetamide formation when amine reacts with Ac2O
rules.quantitative: ≥7.5% Ac2O: amine <10%; 2.5% and 5.0% Ac2O: amine 14% and 11% respectively
rules.exceptions: Lower ratios (2.5%, 5.0%) result in higher amine content

rules.condition: WHEN using acetic anhydride in aza-Diels-Alder reactions
rules.recommendation: Expect ≥70% desired isomer ratio and ~45% product recovery after isolation
rules.reasoning: Ac2O improves selectivity and provides consistent product recovery
rules.quantitative: ≥70% desired isomer vs 58-60% without Ac2O; 45% average yield after 60 min collection
rules.exceptions: Results without Ac2O show lower selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN scaling to 500 g production using optimized conditions
rules.recommendation: Use 1 L of 2.5 M imine in toluene, 10 mol% Ac2O, 1.9 mol/mol TFA, 2 mol/mol CPD at -15°C with 30 min RT
rules.reasoning: These conditions provide 46.3% yield of target isomer with 20% improvement over non-Ac2O conditions
rules.quantitative: 2.5 M imine, 10 mol% Ac2O, 1.9 mol/mol TFA, 2 mol/mol CPD, -15°C, 30 min RT, 46.3% yield

rules.condition: WHEN using T-shaped mixers are insufficient for viscous solutions
rules.recommendation: Add static mixers after T-shaped mixers to achieve homogeneous mixing
rules.reasoning: Imine salt solution in toluene is very viscous when compared with CPD, creating biphasic mixture requiring 30 min to homogenize
rules.quantitative: 30 min required for biphasic mixture to enter homogeneous phase without static mixer
rules.exceptions: T-shaped mixers alone sufficient for less viscous solutions

rules.condition: WHEN designing static mixers for improved mixing
rules.recommendation: Use helical PTFE static mixer (TYPE #2) requiring only 5 cm length vs rod-filled mixer (TYPE #1) requiring >20 cm length
rules.reasoning: Helical PTFE mixer achieves homogeneous reaction mixture more efficiently than rod-filled mixer
rules.quantitative: TYPE #1: SS 316L tube 6 mm diameter, 4 mm i.d., filled with 1 mm diameter rods 1.5-2.0 mm long, >20 cm length; TYPE #2: helical PTFE, 5 cm length
rules.exceptions: Rod-filled mixers can be used but require much longer length

rules.condition: WHEN using improved reactor configuration with static mixers
rules.recommendation: Achieve full imine conversion in 1 minute residence time instead of 30 minutes
rules.reasoning: Static mixers dramatically improve mixing efficiency, reducing required reaction time
rules.quantitative: 1 min RT vs previous 30 min RT requirement
rules.exceptions: Without static mixers, 30 min RT still required

rules.condition: WHEN using stoichiometric conditions (1.1 eq TFA, 1.5 eq CPD) in flow vs batch
rules.recommendation: Use additional reagent amounts in flow compared to batch conditions
rules.reasoning: Flow reactions do not work the same way as batch reactions under identical stoichiometric conditions
rules.quantitative: 1.1 equivalents TFA and 1.5 equivalents CPD insufficient in flow
rules.exceptions: Batch conditions work with these stoichiometric ratios

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing for production scale with 1 min RT
rules.recommendation: Increase production from 12 kg/day (30 min RT) to 360 kg/day (1 min RT) - a factor of 30 improvement
rules.reasoning: Reduced residence time dramatically increases throughput for the same reactor volume

rules.condition: WHEN implementing an aDA reaction flow system for pharmaceutical production
rules.recommendation: Use a 100 mL reactor capacity with three pump feeds: Imine at 60.22 mL/min (from 500 L tank), CPD at 23.66 mL/min (from continuous distilling system), and TFA at 16.13 mL/min (from 200 L tank)
rules.reasoning: This configuration achieved successful continuous production with proper reagent stoichiometry and manageable reactor size for industrial implementation
rules.quantitative: Reactor: 100 mL, Imine: 60.22 mL/min, CPD: 23.66 mL/min, TFA: 16.13 mL/min

rules.condition: WHEN designing continuous distillation for CPD production in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use twin distilling reactors capable of reaching up to 60 mL/min of CPD output
rules.reasoning: Twin reactor configuration provides sufficient CPD supply rate for the main reaction while maintaining continuous operation
rules.quantitative: Maximum CPD output: 60 mL/min per twin reactor system

rules.condition: WHEN scaling from laboratory to production for aDA reactions
rules.recommendation: Expect production capacity scaling from 4.7 kg/day (lab) to 26.2 kg/day (pilot) to 1000 kg/day (production) with proportional flow rate increases
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry enables linear scaling with maintained reaction efficiency and yield across different scales
rules.quantitative: Lab: 4.7 kg/day, 18 mL/min, 18 mL reactor; Pilot: 26.2 kg/day, 100 mL/min, 100 mL reactor; Production: 1000 kg/day, 4200 mL/min, 4200 mL reactor

rules.condition: WHEN operating continuous flow systems for pharmaceutical production
rules.recommendation: Target production rates of 1.09 kg/h with 47% overall yield, operating continuously for 102 hours to achieve 26.2 kg per day and 110 kg per week
rules.reasoning: These rates demonstrate economically viable continuous production with acceptable yields for pharmaceutical manufacturing
rules.quantitative: Production rate: 1.09 kg/h, Yield: 47%, Operating time: 102 h continuous, Daily output: 26.2 kg, Weekly output: 110 kg

rules.condition: WHEN running continuous flow chemistry under cGMP conditions for pharmaceuticals
rules.recommendation: Use small scale reactors to safely handle high pressures and temperatures that would be inaccessible under large scale batch conditions
rules.reasoning: Small reactors provide better containment and safety control for intensified process conditions while maintaining access to wider range of chemistry, reagents and solvents

rules.condition: WHEN implementing continuous manufacturing for pharmaceutical production
rules.recommendation: Telescope process steps to avoid intermediate isolations and purifications, allowing each step to flow seamlessly into the next
rules.reasoning: Telescoping reduces costly turnaround times, eliminates equipment cleaning/changeover between steps, reduces factory footprint and overall energy consumption
rules.exceptions: May not apply when very slow reagent additions are required or long reaction/crystallization desupersaturation times are needed

rules.condition: WHEN comparing flow vs batch production capacity for similar reactions
rules.recommendation: Consider that flow systems can achieve significantly higher production rates - flow achieved 1000 kg/day vs batch 103 kg/day for similar aDA reaction
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry enables continuous operation without batch turnaround times, leading to much higher throughput in the same timeframe
rules.quantitative: Flow: 1000 kg/day production capacity vs Batch: 103 kg/day for similar compound
rules.exceptions: Comparison based on literature data for similar compound, actual results may vary

rules.condition: WHEN transitioning from batch to continuous processing under cGMP
rules.recommendation: Invest in new technology, facilities, and develop expertise to run continuous processes according to cGMP guidelines
rules.reasoning: Continuous manufacturing requires specialized knowledge and equipment different from batch processing, while maintaining the same cGMP standards
rules.quantitative: Same cGMP guidelines apply to both batch and continuous manufacturing

rules.condition: WHEN designing continuous pharmaceutical processes
rules.recommendation: Address unique challenges including process control, data management, material tracking, and equipment design for both solution phase chemistry and solids handling (crystallizations and filtrations)
rules.reasoning: Continuous manufacturing presents specific challenges not found in batch processing that must be addressed for successful implementation

rules.condition: WHEN operating multi-step continuous processes
rules.recommendation: Operate within the design space where multiple steps are telescoped and large numbers of unit operations run simultaneously
rules.reasoning: The number of input variables and process parameters is large, making the associated design space small and challenging for manufacturing
rules.quantitative: Large number of input variables and process parameters

rules.condition: WHEN achieving state of control in continuous manufacturing
rules.recommendation: Use active feedback control where manipulated variables (such as flowrates) are adjusted to maintain controlled variables (such as product CQAs) within allowable ranges
rules.reasoning: This approach allows more flexibility in process conditions and rapid correction of perturbations while maintaining product quality, though it requires high process understanding and advanced engineering controls
rules.exceptions: Depends on high degree of process understanding and advanced engineering controls

rules.condition: WHEN designing material tracking systems for continuous processes
rules.recommendation: Understand residence time distribution (RTD) and mean residence time (τ) to implement precise material tracking strategies
rules.reasoning: RTD describes the amount of time fluid elements spend inside a reactor - some spend more than mean residence time, some less, due to dispersion effects
rules.quantitative: RTD and τ (mean residence time) parameters

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between plug flow reactors (PFR) and continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTR)
rules.recommendation: Use PFRs to minimize dispersion and simplify material tracking; PFRs have very narrow RTDs while CSTRs have broad RTDs
rules.reasoning: Narrow RTDs reduce material dispersion between unit operations and simplify material tracking requirements
rules.quantitative: PFRs have very narrow RTDs, CSTRs have broad RTDs

rules.condition: WHEN implementing material tracking in multi-step continuous processes with large numbers of feed inputs
rules.recommendation: Use a dynamic mass balance model that incorporates RTD data to track material from raw material inputs to final batch
rules.reasoning: Multiple raw materials with several batch numbers continuously flow into the system, requiring sophisticated tracking beyond simple batch number assignment
rules.quantitative: RTD data used in mass balance model

rules.condition: WHEN implementing automation in continuous processing
rules.recommendation: Use feedback control on pump flows based on closed loop logic to maintain precise mass flowrates and stoichiometry
rules.reasoning: Stoichiometry is the keystone of continuous flow chemistry, and automation ensures precision and repeatability over prolonged periods

rules.condition: WHEN using Process Analytical Technology (PAT) for on-line HPLC monitoring
rules.recommendation: Consider sampling frequency relative to residence time - if sampling provides result every hour and residence time is one hour, only one sample per system volume is obtained
rules.reasoning: Insufficient sampling frequency may not provide adequate samples to accurately monitor the reaction in that particular unit operation
rules.quantitative: Sampling frequency should be higher than residence time for adequate monitoring

rules.condition: WHEN implementing on-line HPLC in continuous manufacturing
rules.recommendation: Use engineering controls such as delay loops to hold the sample upstream of the next step while waiting for HPLC results, then use results to adjust process or execute diversion
rules.reasoning: HPLC methods take time to generate results, and the process material will have moved downstream by the time results are available
rules.quantitative: HPLC analysis time must be considered in control strategy

rules.condition: WHEN using surge vessels in continuous processes
rules.recommendation: Use surge vessels to de-couple unit operations, simplify automation, reduce equipment redundancy needs, allow intermediate quality assessment, and provide mass balance checks
rules.reasoning: Surge vessels provide operational flexibility and control, allowing one step to be stopped for maintenance while others continue running
rules.quantitative: Multiple surge vessels provide greater surge capacity than single vessels
rules.exceptions: Surge vessels add equipment, expense, factory footprint, and increase overall system dispersion

rules.condition: WHEN operating surge vessels in duty standby mode
rules.recommendation: Use multiple surge vessels to allow off-line analysis for intermediate quality assessment and in-process control where homogeneous sections are sampled and tested before forward processing
rules.reasoning: This allows for quality control and accurate flowrate setting for intermediate non-isolated solutions that would otherwise require sophisticated automation control
rules.quantitative: Multiple surge vessels operating in duty standby mode

rules.condition: WHEN designing manufacturing processes using surge vessels
rules.recommendation: Consider surge vessels for operational advantages during process design for manufacturing
rules.reasoning: Surge vessels provide operational advantages offered by storage capacity and material tracking capabilities

rules.condition: WHEN designing continuous manufacturing processes
rules.recommendation: Account for material tracking purposes and total start-up time during surge vessel filling during transition start-up
rules.reasoning: These considerations provide operational advantages versus batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN implementing diversion strategies during process start-up
rules.recommendation: Start each unit operation in series using PAT and RTD understanding until it reaches desired concentration, then forward process to next unit operation
rules.reasoning: Allows controlled transition from solvent to fully concentrated process solution

rules.condition: WHEN process upset or perturbation occurs that impacts downstream quality
rules.recommendation: Implement diversion strategy to isolate and reject out-of-specification material
rules.reasoning: Prevents contamination of good product and maintains process control

rules.condition: WHEN determining extent of material diversion during process upsets
rules.recommendation: Base decision on duration, frequency and severity of disturbance and mixing patterns of the system
rules.reasoning: These factors determine how much material is impacted and needs to be isolated

rules.condition: WHEN implementing diversion strategy
rules.recommendation: Establish capability to detect disturbance and quantify amount of impacted material using real-time process monitoring
rules.reasoning: Required for decision-making based on process control strategy to minimize impact

rules.condition: WHEN selecting diversion point location
rules.recommendation: Locate diversion point after unit operation with narrow RTD (such as PFR) rather than after unit operation with broad RTD (such as CSTRs in series)
rules.reasoning: Minimizes amount of diverted material due to smaller impact of dispersion

rules.condition: WHEN conducting iridium-catalyzed reductive amination at industrial scale
rules.recommendation: Use pipes-in-series bubble flow reactor at 20°C and 50 bar hydrogen pressure with 12 h residence time
rules.reasoning: Allows hydrogen and nitrogen vapor to travel through reactor concurrently for effective mass transfer
rules.quantitative: 20°C, 50 bar H2 pressure, 12 h residence time, 100 kg/day production rate

rules.condition: WHEN designing pipes-in-series reactor for hydrogenation
rules.recommendation: Choose number of pipes in series to maximize mass transfer and minimize dispersion within reactor
rules.reasoning: Important for reaction conversion and material tracking purposes
rules.quantitative: 360 L plug flow reactor volume used in manufacturing case

rules.condition: WHEN conducting hydrogenations at large scale
rules.recommendation: Locate hydrogen feed cylinder storage, hydrogen addition and VLS outside manufacturing building to ensure >99.9% of hydrogen does not enter facility where staff are located
rules.reasoning: Hydrogenations at large scale are typically high risk safety operations, continuous process dramatically reduces safety risk

rules.condition: WHEN implementing duty/standby feed vessel pairs
rules.recommendation: Use pairs of duty/standby feed vessels to achieve continuous feed flow into reactor
rules.reasoning: Ensures uninterrupted flow and provides operational flexibility

rules.condition: WHEN using surge vessels for continuous reaction collection
rules.recommendation: Operate surge vessels in tandem collection and feed mode to ensure uninterrupted flow and provide control point for testing large homogeneous sections
rules.reasoning: Provides quality control checkpoint before forward processing to next step

rules.condition: WHEN implementing material tracking in pipes-in-series reactor
rules.recommendation: Control feed switch times at reactor inlet and surge collection vessel switch times at reactor outlet, then calculate batch-to-batch carryover based on RTD and τ
rules.reasoning: Maintains material traceability through continuous hydrogenation process

rules.condition: WHEN conducting gas-liquid reactions where one reagent needs to migrate by diffusion from one phase to another

rules.condition: WHEN conducting photocatalytic reactions with gaseous light hydrocarbons (methane, ethane, propane, isobutane) using hydrogen atom transfer photocatalysis

rules.condition: WHEN conducting C(sp³)-H carbonylation with gaseous carbon monoxide

rules.condition: WHEN switching from batch to flow for reactions where mixing time is longer than reaction time

rules.condition: WHEN conducting extremely fast reactions that require highly controlled conditions to produce desired compounds with high selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN calculating mixing performance in microreactors

rules.reasoning: Reynolds number determines the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces, which affects mixing efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN determining characteristic mixing time

rules.condition: WHEN requiring optimal heat transfer properties

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time in flow reactors

rules.condition: WHEN calculating molar flow rates in flow chemistry

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with slug flow in microreactors

rules.condition: WHEN using laminar flow in microchannels

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow reactors for controlled residence time

rules.reasoning: Real flow reactors have velocity profiles that create a distribution of residence times, affecting product distribution
rules.quantitative: Parabolic velocity profile creates residence time distribution

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow reactors for reactions with competing pathways (A + B → C + B → S)

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between different mixer types for consecutive reactions

rules.condition: WHEN the Damköhler number is greater than 1 (Da > 1)

rules.reasoning: When reaction rate is faster than mixing rate (Da > 1), poor mixing leads to concentration gradients that can favor unwanted side reactions
rules.quantitative: Da = reaction rate/mixing rate; Da > 1 indicates mixing-limited regime

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing flow rates for nucleophilic addition reactions at -65°C

rules.exceptions: Very low pump rates (rate 1) give poor conversion (55%)

rules.condition: WHEN designing two-stage flow reactions with mixing followed by reaction

rules.reasoning: Short residence times prevent unwanted side reactions while allowing sufficient time for the desired transformation

rules.condition: WHEN batch reactions show poor selectivity due to inefficient mixing

rules.reasoning: Flow reactors can achieve much faster mixing than batch reactors, revealing the intrinsic selectivity of chemical reactions that may be masked by mixing limitations

rules.condition: WHEN both nucleophile and intermediate can react with the same substrate

rules.reasoning: Fast mixing ensures that the primary nucleophile reacts before secondary reactions can occur with intermediate products

rules.condition: WHEN performing anionic Fries rearrangement reactions in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use residence times between 377-628 ms to maximize rearranged product formation (74-91% yield) and minimize unrearranged products (0-20%)
rules.reasoning: Longer residence times allow the intramolecular rearrangement to proceed to completion, favoring the desired rearranged product over the unrearranged starting material
rules.quantitative: 628 ms: 0% unrearranged, 91% rearranged; 377 ms: 20% unrearranged, 74% rearranged; 220 ms: 45% unrearranged, 51% rearranged
rules.exceptions: Very short residence times (0.33-55 ms) favor unrearranged products (73-91%)

rules.condition: WHEN residence time is reduced to very short durations (0.33-55 ms) in anionic Fries rearrangement
rules.recommendation: Expect predominantly unrearranged products (73-91%) with minimal rearranged products (4-21%)
rules.reasoning: Insufficient time for the intramolecular rearrangement mechanism to proceed, resulting in quenching of the intermediate before rearrangement occurs
rules.quantitative: 0.33 ms: 91% unrearranged, 0% rearranged; 4 ms: 87% unrearranged, 4% rearranged; 55 ms: 73% unrearranged, 21% rearranged
rules.exceptions: Longer residence times (>200 ms) favor rearranged products

rules.condition: WHEN calculating worst-case adiabatic temperature rise in exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Use the formula ΔT_ad = (c_s,0(-ΔH_R))/(ρC_p) where c_s,0 is inlet concentration, ΔH_R is reaction enthalpy, ρ is fluid density, and C_p is specific heat
rules.reasoning: This formula provides the maximum possible temperature increase assuming no heat removal, giving a conservative safety estimate for reactor design
rules.exceptions: Only valid for adiabatic conditions; actual temperature rise will be lower with heat exchange

rules.condition: WHEN evaluating heat exchange efficiency in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Calculate the ratio of heat exchange β = (-rΔH_R d_h)/(6h_in ΔT_ad) = (heat generation rate)/(heat removal rate)
rules.reasoning: This dimensionless parameter indicates whether heat generation can be balanced by heat removal; β >> 1 indicates poor heat removal leading to hot spots
rules.quantitative: β = (-rΔH_R d_h)/(6h_in ΔT_ad) where r is reaction rate, d_h is hydraulic diameter, h_in is heat-transfer coefficient
rules.exceptions: Formula assumes steady-state conditions and uniform heat generation

rules.condition: WHEN designing microreactors for heat management
rules.recommendation: Increase surface area to volume ratio by decreasing channel diameter to improve heat exchange efficiency
rules.reasoning: Smaller diameters provide higher surface area per unit volume, enhancing heat transfer and reducing temperature gradients within the reactor
rules.quantitative: Heat transfer coefficient h increases as diameter d_h decreases (inverse relationship shown in graph)
rules.exceptions: Very small diameters may increase pressure drop and cause flow instabilities

rules.condition: WHEN heat generation rate exceeds heat removal rate (β >> 1)
rules.recommendation: Reduce channel diameter, increase heat transfer coefficient, or decrease reaction concentration to prevent thermal runaway
rules.reasoning: High β values indicate insufficient heat removal capacity, leading to temperature gradients and potential hot spot formation that can cause side reactions or safety issues
rules.quantitative: β = (heat generation rate)/(heat removal rate); aim for β ≤ 1 for good heat management
rules.exceptions: Some applications may tolerate moderate temperature gradients if selectivity is maintained

rules.condition: WHEN temperature gradients exist in flow reactors with exothermic reactions that are faster than heat transfer (βB = 6.3)
rules.recommendation: Design for effective heat removal to prevent degradation of Curtin-Hammett selectivity
rules.reasoning: Temperature gradients cause different reaction rates at different positions, leading to loss of selectivity control when the exothermic reaction rate exceeds heat transfer rate
rules.quantitative: βB = 6.3 (exotherm faster than heat transfer), βF = 0.2 (heat removed effectively)
rules.exceptions: When heat transfer is faster than reaction rate (βF = 0.2)

rules.condition: WHEN performing Diels-Alder reactions in flow

rules.condition: WHEN performing nucleophilic aromatic substitution in flow

rules.condition: WHEN designing gas-liquid reactions where mass transfer is rate-determining

rules.condition: WHEN choosing reactor type for gas-liquid reactions

rules.quantitative: 5 mL RB Flask: 141 m²/m³, 50 mL RB Flask: 66 m²/m³, 250 mL RB Flask: 38 m²/m³, tube reactors: 50-700 m²/m³, gas-liquid microchannel: 3400-18000 m²/m³

rules.condition: WHEN increasing gas flow rate in gas-liquid flow

rules.quantitative: Flow regime depends on gas and liquid flow rates

rules.condition: WHEN performing biphasic reactions with aqueous and organic phases
rules.recommendation: Increase aqueous:organic volume ratio to improve interfacial area and mass transfer - higher ratios give shorter slug lengths and higher interfacial areas
rules.reasoning: Smaller slug lengths create more interfacial area for mass transfer between phases
rules.quantitative: 1.0 v/v: 467 μm slugs, 3000 m²/m³ area; 2.3 v/v: 330 μm slugs, 4500 m²/m³ area; 4.0 v/v: 295 μm slugs, 5100 m²/m³ area; 6.1 v/v: 265 μm slugs, 5900 m²/m³ area

rules.condition: WHEN gas diffusion through liquid film is rate-limiting (Ha < 0.3)

rules.reasoning: Slow reaction occurs primarily in bulk liquid phase, limited by gas diffusion rate

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is instantaneous at gas-liquid interface (Ha > 3)

rules.condition: WHEN calculating Hatta number for gas-liquid reactions
rules.recommendation: Use Ha = √((2/(m+1)) × (km,n(εA,i)^(m-1)(cB,bulk)^n)DA) / kL to determine if reaction or mass transfer is rate-limiting
rules.reasoning: Hatta number compares reaction rate in liquid film to diffusion rate through the film
rules.quantitative: Ha formula with km,n = reaction rate constant, εA,i = enhancement factor, cB,bulk = bulk concentration, DA = diffusivity, kL = mass transfer coefficient
rules.exceptions: Formula may vary for different reaction orders and mechanisms

rules.condition: WHEN calculating photon penetration depth in flow photochemistry reactors
rules.recommendation: USE Beer-Lambert Law (A = εcl) where A = absorbance, ε = extinction coefficient, c = concentration, l = path length to determine light penetration
rules.reasoning: Beer-Lambert Law governs photon transport and determines how far light can penetrate into the reaction mixture

rules.condition: WHEN using 0.02 inch tubing for photochemistry with 2.5 mM concentration

rules.condition: WHEN handling hazardous reagents like fluorine, chlorine, diazomethane, or phosgene in flow chemistry
