rules.recommendation: IMPLEMENT continuous generation of hazardous reagents from safer precursors rather than storing large quantities
rules.reasoning: Continuous generation eliminates the need to store large quantities of dangerous materials

rules.condition: WHEN conducting organocatalyzed aldol reactions in flow using proline-derived catalysts
rules.recommendation: Use 20 minutes residence time at 60°C instead of 40 hours at room temperature
rules.reasoning: Flow conditions with elevated temperature provide similar yields (79% vs 77%) and enantioselectivity (75% vs 74% ee) in dramatically reduced time
rules.quantitative: 20 min vs 40 hours reaction time, 60°C vs RT, 79% vs 77% yield, 75% vs 74% ee

rules.condition: WHEN comparing batch vs flow reactors for exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Use flow reactors to achieve better heat transfer rates (βF = 4 x 10^-4) compared to batch reactors (βB = 1 x 10^-2)
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors provide superior heat transfer capabilities due to higher surface-area-to-volume ratios, enabling better temperature control

rules.condition: WHEN dealing with potentially exothermic reactions
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry to minimize adiabatic temperature rise (ΔTad = 1.3°C in flow vs 61.3°C worst case in batch)
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors provide better heat dissipation and smaller reaction volumes, reducing thermal runaway risks
rules.quantitative: ΔTad = 1.3°C in flow, worst case batch = 61.3°C

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is unsafe in batch
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry as an alternative approach
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry can provide safer operating conditions through better heat and mass transfer

rules.condition: WHEN batch reaction performance is unacceptable with respect to yield, scale, and reaction time
rules.recommendation: Evaluate flow chemistry as an alternative
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry can provide improved process metrics through enhanced heat and mass transfer
rules.exceptions: If reaction already performs well in batch

rules.condition: WHEN one of the reagents is a gas
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for improved gas-liquid contact
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors can provide better mixing and mass transfer for gas-liquid reactions

rules.condition: WHEN precipitate formation drives equilibrium in desired direction
rules.recommendation: Consider batch chemistry over flow
rules.reasoning: Batch reactors can better accommodate precipitation without clogging issues

rules.condition: WHEN one reagent/catalyst is a solid
rules.recommendation: Consider batch chemistry over flow
rules.reasoning: Solid handling can be challenging in flow systems due to potential clogging

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is very fast (<1 min)
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for better control
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors provide precise residence time control for fast reactions

rules.condition: WHEN selectivity is affected by temperature
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for better temperature control
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors provide superior temperature control through enhanced heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN reaction needs high heat to achieve reasonable reaction rate
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for better heat transfer capabilities
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors can more safely and efficiently provide high temperatures due to better heat transfer

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is photochemically driven
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for better photon delivery
rules.reasoning: Flow reactors can provide more uniform photon flux and better light penetration

rules.condition: WHEN making decisions about flask vs flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Avoid comparing apples with oranges and inferring conclusions based on simple end-point analysis without time-course data
rules.reasoning: Proper comparison requires comprehensive kinetic analysis rather than just final yields

rules.condition: WHEN selecting between batch and flow processes
rules.recommendation: Recognize that the decision is highly context specific
rules.reasoning: Each reaction system has unique requirements that must be evaluated individually

rules.condition: WHEN implementing flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Focus on reducing concentration and temperature gradients to potentially provide higher yield and selectivity
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry's main advantage is improved mass and heat transfer leading to more uniform reaction conditions

rules.condition: WHEN considering flow chemistry benefits
rules.recommendation: Recognize that flow chemistry does not improve every process
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry has specific advantages but is not universally superior to batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN choosing reactor material for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Select silicon (conductivity 150 W/mK) for best heat transfer, stainless steel (10 W/mK) for moderate heat transfer, or PTFE (0.1 W/mK) for minimal heat transfer requirements
rules.reasoning: Thermal conductivity directly affects heat transfer efficiency - higher conductivity materials provide better temperature control
rules.quantitative: Silicon: 150 W/mK, Stainless steel: 10 W/mK, PTFE: 0.1 W/mK

rules.condition: WHEN designing high-temperature flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Choose reactor material based on heat transfer requirements to reduce temperature gradients between inlet and outlet sections
rules.reasoning: Material thermal conductivity plays a role in heat transfer efficiency and needs to be chosen accordingly to reduce temperature gradients
rules.quantitative: Temperature gradients between inlet and outlet sections

rules.condition: WHEN scaling microwave reactions from batch to flow
rules.recommendation: Use pressurized flow reactors to eliminate headspace and maintain uniform reagent concentrations
rules.reasoning: Vaporization of reagents into headspace decreases concentration in solution; pressurized flow reactors eliminate headspace

rules.condition: WHEN scaling preparative microwave reactions
rules.recommendation: Limit preparative scales to ~30 mL due to microwave penetration limitations
rules.reasoning: Microwave scale preparations are generally limited to 50 mL reaction volumes but are largely limited by penetration of microwaves

rules.condition: WHEN light transmission is critical for photochemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Use inner diameter of 0.02 inch tubing to maintain >1% light transmission at path length 0.25 mM for [Ru(bpy)₃]Cl₂ in methanol
rules.reasoning: Light attenuation follows Beer-Lambert law - smaller path lengths maintain higher light transmission
rules.quantitative: 0.02 inch tubing inner diameter, >1% transmission at 0.25 mM concentration

rules.condition: WHEN conducting photochemical reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Reduce catalyst concentration to <1% of incident light to avoid serious scaling implications
rules.reasoning: Even after 10-fold reduction (0.25 mM), there would be <1% incident light transmitted to center of reaction vessel

rules.condition: WHEN designing flow reactors for photochemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Ensure flow reactor dimensions fall within region for sufficient light exposure - use 2.5 mM solution of [Ru(bpy)₃]Cl₂ in methanol with >1% incident light transmission through tubing with inner diameter 0.002 mm
rules.reasoning: Photochemical reactions require sufficient light exposure, and flow reactor dimensions must accommodate this requirement
rules.quantitative: 2.5 mM concentration, >1% light transmission, 0.002 mm inner diameter

rules.condition: WHEN conducting electrochemical reactions requiring supporting electrolytes
rules.recommendation: Reduce distance between electrodes by 10-fold to reduce conductivity requirements and enable removal of supporting electrolytes
rules.reasoning: Small dimensions of flow reactors permit removal of supporting electrolytes since conductivity is inversely proportional to distance

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up electrochemical reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Account for undesirable heat evolution when scaling up due to larger distances between electrodes
rules.reasoning: Batch scale up can lead to undesirable evolution of heat caused by larger distances between electrodes

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is not safe in batch
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry as an alternative approach
rules.reasoning: Flow chemistry can provide safer conditions for hazardous reactions

rules.condition: WHEN one of the reagents is a gas
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for improved handling and reaction control
rules.reasoning: Flow conditions offer advantages for gas-liquid reactions

rules.condition: WHEN using heterogeneous catalysts
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for improved catalyst utilization and reaction control
rules.reasoning: Flow conditions can provide better contact and handling of solid catalysts

rules.condition: WHEN reaction involves an emulsion
rules.recommendation: Be cautious as scaling could be problematic in flow
rules.reasoning: Emulsions can present challenges in flow systems during scale-up

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is fast (<1 min)
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for better control and mixing
rules.reasoning: Fast reactions benefit from the precise control available in flow systems

rules.condition: WHEN selectivity is affected by temperature
rules.recommendation: Use flow chemistry for better temperature control and selectivity
rules.reasoning: Flow systems provide better temperature control which can improve selectivity

rules.condition: WHEN heating speeds up the reaction
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for controlled heating and reaction acceleration
rules.reasoning: Flow systems can provide controlled heating for reaction acceleration

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is photochemically driven
rules.recommendation: Use flow chemistry for uniform irradiation and better light exposure
rules.reasoning: Flow conditions offer more uniform irradiation due to small reactor dimensions

rules.condition: WHEN reaction is electrochemically driven
rules.recommendation: Consider flow chemistry for improved electrode contact and electrolysis control
rules.reasoning: Flow conditions can provide better electrode utilization and control

rules.condition: WHEN using low and medium pressure applications (<30 bar)
rules.recommendation: Use inert perfluorinated polymers (PTFE, PFA, PEEK, and FEP) as adequate materials
rules.reasoning: These materials provide adequate chemical compatibility and pressure resistance for most flow chemistry applications
rules.exceptions: High-pressure processes requiring reactions above boiling point of reaction medium or supercritical solvents

rules.condition: WHEN using high-pressure processes (reactions above boiling point or supercritical solvents)
rules.recommendation: Use more robust materials such as stainless steel or special alloys
rules.reasoning: Standard polymers are inadequate for high-pressure and high-temperature conditions
rules.quantitative: Above boiling point temperatures, supercritical solvent conditions

rules.condition: WHEN designing fluid delivery systems
rules.recommendation: Ensure the delivery system can surpass the pressure set by the pressure regulation module
rules.reasoning: Pressure control is achieved by pressure-driven flow techniques where pressure difference between inlet and outlet creates flow
rules.quantitative: System pressure must exceed regulation module setting

rules.condition: WHEN using HPLC pumps for flow rates higher than 0.1 mL min⁻¹
rules.recommendation: Use HPLC pumps (single and dual piston reciprocating) for low to high-pressure applications
rules.reasoning: HPLC pumps are commonly utilized and effective for these flow rate ranges
rules.exceptions: When periodic pressure pulses must be avoided or when volatile solvents are used

rules.condition: WHEN mixing is highly important or well-defined biphasic flow patterns need to be generated
rules.recommendation: Avoid single piston reciprocating pumps due to periodic pressure pulses
rules.reasoning: Pressure pulses can disrupt critical mixing or biphasic flow patterns

rules.condition: WHEN using volatile solvents (Et₂O, DCM, CHCl₃) with HPLC pumps
rules.recommendation: Circumvent pumping problems by degassing and/or prepressurization of liquids
rules.reasoning: Volatile solvents can cause cavitation and pumping issues in HPLC systems
rules.quantitative: Applies to Et₂O, DCM, CHCl₃ and similar volatile solvents

rules.condition: WHEN accurate pumping at lower flow rates is needed
rules.recommendation: Use simple 'single shot' syringe pumps that dose a predefined amount of liquid
rules.reasoning: Syringe pumps provide more accurate flow control at lower rates and avoid pressure pulse issues
rules.quantitative: Lower flow rates, predefined liquid amounts

rules.condition: WHEN continuous operation is required with syringe pumps
rules.recommendation: Use two independent syringes where one delivers liquid while the second is simultaneously being filled, with role switching for continuous operation
rules.reasoning: Allows for truly continuous operation without interruption

rules.condition: WHEN pumping well-suspended slurries
rules.recommendation: Use peristaltic pumps with movement of a central rotor pressing on flexible tubing
rules.reasoning: Peristaltic pumps can handle suspended particles without clogging issues

rules.condition: WHEN feeding small quantities of substrate/reagent or moisture/oxygen sensitive materials
rules.recommendation: Use sample loops with 6-way valve systems to create bypass flow streams that can be switched to introduce samples
rules.reasoning: Allows for precise introduction of small amounts and protection of sensitive materials

rules.condition: WHEN utilizing gases in flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Connect gas bottles to flow reactor via pressure regulator, use thermal mass flow controllers (MFC) for precise control
rules.reasoning: Provides precise control over gas flow rates and maintains safety
rules.exceptions: Simple gas addition without precise control requirements

rules.condition: WHEN fast mixing is crucial for the reaction (reaction time <10 min)
rules.recommendation: Use micromixers or specialized mixing units rather than simple T- or Y-shaped connections
rules.reasoning: Fast reactions require rapid mixing to achieve desired reaction outcomes and prevent side reactions
rules.exceptions: Slow reactions where mixing time is not critical

rules.condition: WHEN reaction time is greater than 10 min
rules.recommendation: Simple mixing devices are acceptable as mixing is not critical
rules.reasoning: Longer reaction times allow sufficient time for adequate mixing even with simple devices
rules.exceptions: When reaction is extremely sensitive to mixing conditions

rules.condition: WHEN using T-mixers with high flow rates
rules.recommendation: Combine with high flow rates to achieve more efficient mixing
rules.reasoning: Higher flow rates improve mixing efficiency in simple T-mixer configurations
rules.quantitative: High flow rates enhance T-mixer performance
rules.exceptions: When flow rate is limited by other process constraints

rules.condition: WHEN using split and recombine (SAR) micromixers
rules.recommendation: Utilize multiple segmentation cycles by rotation and recombination to create small segments with effective mixing distance
rules.reasoning: SAR technique creates multiple small segments that enhance mixing through reduced diffusion distances

rules.condition: WHEN one fluid is significantly faster than the other in multiphase reactions
rules.recommendation: Use specialized designs such as falling-film microreactors and gas permeable membrane reactors
rules.reasoning: Prevents annular regime formation and maintains proper mixing in multiphase systems
rules.quantitative: Significant flow rate differences between phases

rules.condition: WHEN using tube-in-tube gas addition systems
rules.recommendation: Use gas-permeable Teflon AF-2400 membrane tubing (inner tube) fixed within larger impermeable tubing (outer tube)
rules.reasoning: Allows selective gas permeation while maintaining liquid phase separation
rules.exceptions: When direct gas injection is preferred

rules.condition: WHEN selecting coil reactor materials for different temperature and pressure ranges
rules.recommendation: For low T/p (<50°C, <5 bar): use PTFE; for high T/p (<150°C, <20 bar): use PFA or PEEK; for very high T/p (>150°C, >20 bar): use SST
rules.reasoning: Material selection based on temperature and pressure compatibility and chemical resistance
rules.quantitative: Low: <50°C, <5 bar; High: <150°C, <20 bar; Very high: >150°C, >20 bar
rules.exceptions: UV-vis applications require different material considerations

rules.condition: WHEN using UV-vis irradiation applications
rules.recommendation: Use FEP material as it has excellent transmission properties
rules.reasoning: FEP provides optimal light transmission for photochemical applications

rules.condition: WHEN using corrosive reagents
rules.recommendation: Check material compatibility - some materials marked with concerns for corrosive reagent exposure
rules.reasoning: Prevents reactor degradation and contamination from material incompatibility

rules.condition: WHEN using coil reactors
rules.recommendation: Use common outer diameters of 1/8" or 1/16" with various inner diameters (0.01", 0.02", 0.03", 0.04", 1/16")
rules.reasoning: Standard dimensions provide good heat transfer characteristics and are readily available
rules.quantitative: 1/8" or 1/16" outer diameter, multiple inner diameter options

rules.condition: WHEN using packed bed reactors with heterogeneous materials
rules.recommendation: Consider particle size impact - big particles have low surface-to-volume ratio and reaction occurs on surface, small particles may cause high back pressure and clog filter unit
rules.reasoning: Balances reaction efficiency with practical flow considerations
rules.quantitative: Particle size optimization between surface area and pressure drop

rules.condition: WHEN reactions generate solids during mixing
rules.recommendation: Submerge the mixing unit in an ultrasonic bath or use mechanical agitation to avoid clogging
rules.reasoning: Prevents blockage from solid formation during reaction
rules.exceptions: Reactions that do not generate solids

rules.condition: WHEN designing electrochemical flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use undivided cells with one inlet and one outlet, where the electric current flow and the liquid flow are parallel, permitting conversion of the starting material without the supporting electrolyte
rules.reasoning: This design allows for parallel flow of current and liquid while maintaining electrochemical conversion
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for divided cell applications

rules.condition: WHEN operating agitated cell reactors to avoid clogging
rules.recommendation: Operate in a temperature range from -40 up to 140°C
rules.reasoning: Temperature range allows for proper mixing and prevents material freezing or degradation

rules.condition: WHEN initial mixing unit itself acts to an extent as a reactor unit for highly reactive species
rules.recommendation: Use micromixing devices such as microreactor units to enable otherwise inaccessible control over highly reactive intermediates
rules.reasoning: Efficient mixing enables control over highly reactive intermediates that would otherwise be inaccessible

rules.condition: WHEN using tube-in-tube units for gas/liquid reactions
rules.recommendation: Use tube-in-tube units for carboxylation of Grignard reagents with CO2 where rapid gas/liquid reaction occurs and immediate introduction of the gas
rules.reasoning: Allows for immediate introduction of gas during the reaction phase for optimal reaction control
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for reactions not requiring gas introduction

rules.condition: WHEN terminating flow reactions for accurate control of reaction time
rules.recommendation: Use appropriate quenching procedure since most reactions (or side reactions) can otherwise continue in the collection flask leading to unreliable results
rules.reasoning: Reactions can continue after leaving the reactor if not properly quenched, leading to unreliable results
rules.exceptions: Not necessary for packed bed catalysts where reaction stops immediately when mixture leaves the column

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Use instantaneous quenching once the reaction mixture leaves the reactor unit due to separation from the electrode materials or photon source
rules.reasoning: Electrochemical and photochemical reactions exhibit instantaneous quenching when separated from their energy sources

rules.condition: WHEN controlling reaction time for homogeneous transformations
rules.recommendation: Control either by thermal or chemical quenching. For thermal reactions, use rapid cooling due to enhanced heat transfer in micro- or meso-flow environment
rules.reasoning: Enhanced heat transfer characteristics in micro/meso-flow environments allow for effective thermal quenching
rules.exceptions: Chemical quenching required when thermal quenching is insufficient

rules.condition: WHEN chemical quenching of the reactant is required
rules.recommendation: Add a quenching reagent via a mixing unit to achieve accurate control of reaction time for extremely fast reactions
rules.reasoning: Allows control of extremely fast reactions which cannot be done in conventional batch systems
rules.exceptions: Not needed when thermal quenching is sufficient

rules.condition: WHEN working with elevated pressures
rules.recommendation: Install back pressure regulators (BPR) to operate at a constant downstream system pressure and allow processes above the boiling point of reaction media
rules.reasoning: Working at elevated pressures allows processes above boiling point and enables superior control and rate enhancement when volatile or gaseous reagents are employed
rules.exceptions: Not needed for atmospheric pressure reactions

rules.condition: WHEN using back pressure regulators for high pressure reactions
rules.recommendation: Install pressure sensors within the flow setup for actual system pressure measurement, as many BPR units do not measure actual system pressure
rules.reasoning: BPR units often cannot accurately measure system pressure, requiring additional sensors for precise monitoring
rules.exceptions: Not needed if BPR includes integrated pressure measurement

rules.condition: WHEN choosing between preset and adjustable BPR types
rules.recommendation: Use preset BPRs for predetermined pressure values; use adjustable BPRs with reference pressure against diaphragm for capability of adjusting system pressure via mechanical forces or gas pressurization
rules.reasoning: Different BPR types offer different capabilities for pressure control and flexibility
rules.quantitative: Pressure range up to 70 bar for most models
rules.exceptions: Processing of reaction mixtures involving solid particles or viscous materials at elevated pressure regimes best realized via pressurized collection vessel

rules.condition: WHEN processing biphasic (gas/liquid, liquid/liquid) reactions
rules.recommendation: Use back pressure regulators that can handle biphasic systems
rules.reasoning: Standard BPRs may not be suitable for multiphase systems

rules.condition: WHEN performing liquid/liquid separation in flow
rules.recommendation: Use PTFE membrane sandwiched between two flow channels, where the organic phase can pass through the hydrophobic membrane
rules.reasoning: Hydrophobic membrane allows selective passage of organic phase while retaining aqueous phase

rules.condition: WHEN converting gas/liquid reactions to gas separation
rules.recommendation: Connect gas-miscible phase separation device to a vacuum line for simple conversion to gas separator
rules.reasoning: Membrane technologies can be adapted for gas separation by vacuum connection
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when gas removal is not desired

rules.condition: WHEN removing ethylene generated during olefin metathesis reactions
rules.recommendation: Use membrane separation to remove ethylene, as this technique has been successfully used during olefin metathesis reactions
rules.reasoning: Removal of ethylene byproduct drives the reaction equilibrium forward
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for reactions where ethylene removal is detrimental

rules.condition: WHEN performing inline purification using scavenger cartridges
rules.recommendation: Use packed bed reactors filled with suitable material (acidic, basic, etc.) positioned in the flow path to remove excess reagents or impurities
rules.reasoning: Packed bed scavenger cartridges provide inline purification without manual workup
rules.exceptions: Not applicable when scavenger materials interfere with desired products

rules.condition: WHEN calculating residence time for single-phase transformations
rules.recommendation: Use the equation t_res = V/ν where V is reactor volume and ν is flow speed
rules.reasoning: Prediction of residence time is relatively simple for single-phase transformations since reactor volume and flow rate are set by the user
rules.exceptions: Less trivial for liquid/solid reactions in packed bed reactors due to dead volume and system pressure effects

rules.condition: WHEN regulating residence time
rules.recommendation: Control residence time as a nontrivial task since it strongly depends on the respective chemical transformation
rules.reasoning: Residence time control is critical for reaction optimization and depends heavily on the specific chemical transformation being performed

rules.condition: WHEN calculating pressure drop in flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Use the Hagen-Poiseuille equation: Δp = 32μLν/d² for laminar flow in continuous flow reactors
rules.reasoning: Pressure difference is important as it is always higher at the beginning of the reactor unit than at the end
rules.quantitative: Δp = 32μLν/d² where μ is dynamic viscosity, L is length, ν is flow rate, d is diameter
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for non-laminar flow conditions

rules.condition: WHEN choosing reactor dimensions and pumping systems
rules.recommendation: Keep pressure phenomenon in mind to avoid malfunctions such as stalling or bursting
rules.reasoning: Pressure buildup throughout the reactor can cause system failures if not properly managed

rules.condition: WHEN using gaseous HCl for chlorodehydroxylation of alcohols in flow
rules.recommendation: Take special care to eliminate moisture from the gas delivery system to avoid corrosion and purge the system with nitrogen
rules.reasoning: Moisture in HCl gas delivery systems causes corrosion and system degradation
rules.quantitative: Heat neat alcohols in ETFE coil reactor at 120°C for 10-15 min

rules.condition: WHEN working with gaseous HCl in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use ETFE coil reactor materials and cool reaction mixture to room temperature before depressurization
rules.reasoning: ETFE provides chemical compatibility with HCl gas and cooling prevents rapid depressurization issues
rules.quantitative: Cool to room temperature, depressurize slowly, 1.2 equiv of HCl controlled via MFC

rules.condition: WHEN performing asymmetric hydrogenation in continuous flow using heterogeneous catalysts
rules.recommendation: Use Ubiphox catalyst at 2.5 mol % at 10 bar back pressure with 80 min residence time
rules.reasoning: Ubiphox catalyst proved best for asymmetric hydrogenation with 76% diastereomeric ratio and full conversion
rules.quantitative: 2.5 mol % catalyst, 10 bar pressure, 80 min residence time, 76% dr

rules.condition: WHEN conducting palladium-catalyzed cross-coupling of aryl iodides with gaseous ethylene
rules.recommendation: Use tube-in-tube gas loading tool combined with coil reactor made of PFA
rules.reasoning: This setup allows gaseous reagent delivery while maintaining reaction control and prevents Pd black formation
rules.quantitative: 20 bar pressure, 20 min residence time, 88-95% yields
rules.exceptions: Initial experiments with Pd(OAc)2 suffered from Pd black formation

rules.condition: WHEN using NH3 in continuous synthesis via Paal-Knorr reaction
rules.recommendation: Use gaseous NH3 and recognize that uptake varies significantly depending on solvent and residence time
rules.reasoning: NH3 solubility and reactivity are highly solvent-dependent, affecting reaction efficiency

rules.condition: WHEN using NH3 gas loading in tube-in-tube systems
rules.recommendation: Monitor temperature effect on NH3 uptake, with uptake varying from ~1.0 to 4.0 mmol/min/g depending on temperature and solvent
rules.reasoning: Temperature significantly affects NH3 solubility and mass transfer rates in different solvents
rules.quantitative: Temperature range -20°C to 80°C, uptake varies 1.0-4.0 mmol/min/g, residence time 300s
rules.exceptions: Effect varies by solvent (toluene, DME, MeOH show different patterns)

rules.condition: WHEN needing to separate products during diazomethane generation and utilization
rules.recommendation: Use PDMS membrane reactor with dual channels - one for diazomethane generation, one for product reaction
rules.reasoning: PDMS membrane allows selective permeation of diazomethane while blocking polar solvents and reagents
rules.quantitative: 37% conversion, 2.88 mmol of product per day output
rules.exceptions: Many nonpolar solvents are not compatible with PDMS due to swelling

rules.condition: WHEN working with diazomethane in flow systems
rules.recommendation: Use continuous flow devices to avoid accumulation and minimize safety risks from this explosive reagent
rules.reasoning: Diazomethane is highly toxic, explosive, and sensitive to heat, light, and friction
rules.quantitative: Residence times less than 20s, temperatures kept constant at 20°C

rules.condition: WHEN performing diazomethane methylation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use highly polar solvents such as water in outer tube of gas-permeable membrane reactor
rules.reasoning: Gas-permeable Teflon AF-2400 membrane does not suffer from swelling problems with polar solvents like PDMS
rules.quantitative: Various transformations: cycloadditions, cyclopropanations of alkenes, Arndt-Eistert homologations

rules.condition: WHEN using phosgene for carboxylic acid activation in amide couplings
rules.recommendation: Operate first mixing unit at 20°C and second at 20°C with residence time of 0.5s and 4.3s respectively
rules.reasoning: Controlled temperature prevents side reactions and maintains phosgene reactivity for selective amide formation
rules.quantitative: 20°C, 0.5s residence time (first mixer), 4.3s residence time (second mixer), yields 74-99%

rules.condition: WHEN using phosgene activation strategy for amide synthesis
rules.recommendation: Keep residence times very short: 0.5s for acid activation and 4.3s for final amide formation
rules.reasoning: Short residence times prevent decomposition of reactive intermediates and minimize phosgene exposure
rules.quantitative: 0.5s first stage, 4.3s second stage, high-excellent yields obtained

rules.condition: WHEN performing Pd-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in packed bed reactors with high substrate concentrations (0.5M)
rules.recommendation: Expect significant catalyst leaching (12-28%) during long run experiments at elevated temperatures
rules.reasoning: High substrate concentrations cause steady decrease in chemical conversions over time due to Pd leaching from support
rules.quantitative: 0.5M substrate concentration, 110-170 min experiments, 100-120°C, 12-28% Pd leaching

rules.condition: WHEN using FiberCat 1001 catalyst in Suzuki-Miyaura reactions
rules.recommendation: Monitor for gradual Pd migration through the packed bed during continuous operation
rules.reasoning: Pd gradually moves through packed bed during continuous process, leading to visible change in catalyst homogeneity
rules.quantitative: Pd concentrations: 31 μg Pd mg⁻¹ to 3 μg Pd mg⁻¹ gradient observed

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing SilaCat DPP-Pd catalyst for Suzuki-Miyaura coupling
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 5 min at 60°C for substrate concentrations of 0.15M
rules.reasoning: These conditions provide sufficient reaction time while minimizing Pd leaching to <1%
rules.quantitative: 5 min residence time, 60°C, 0.15M substrate concentration, <1% Pd leaching

rules.condition: WHEN using dendrimer-encapsulated Pd nanoparticles in microreactor systems
rules.recommendation: Expect only 0.12 μg Pd leaching within the system for long-term stability studies
rules.reasoning: Dendrimer encapsulation provides excellent Pd retention compared to other immobilization methods
rules.quantitative: 0.12 μg Pd total, 10% decrease in conversion over 7 days, TON 39,650

rules.condition: WHEN performing copper-catalyzed Huisgen-type cycloaddition in packed bed reactor
rules.recommendation: Use inductive heating at 70°C with 12g copper turnings for optimal results
rules.reasoning: Electromagnetic induction provides direct heating of metal catalyst, leading to better temperature control and yields

rules.condition: WHEN performing Fukuyama reductions using Pd/XAD-4 catalyst
rules.recommendation: Use process at 100 bar pressure and 100°C or alternatively room temperature process for similar yields
rules.reasoning: High pressure system provides benefits but room temperature alternative using DIPEA and AcOH gives similar results
rules.quantitative: 100 bar, 100°C, 1.5 min residence time OR room temperature with DIPEA/AcOH
rules.exceptions: Both high pressure and room temperature processes give similar yields

rules.condition: WHEN using packed bed reactors for catalytic reactions
rules.recommendation: Heat the packed bed reactor to 90°C with a back pressure of 6.9 bar for reactions requiring elevated temperatures
rules.reasoning: Higher temperatures and pressures improve reaction rates and conversion in heterogeneous catalysis while preventing solvent evaporation
rules.exceptions: Not applicable for temperature-sensitive substrates or catalysts

rules.condition: WHEN performing Huisgen cycloaddition reactions in copper coil reactors
rules.recommendation: Use residence times of 2.5 hours to achieve >99% yield at 100°C
rules.reasoning: Sufficient residence time is needed for complete cycloaddition reaction at elevated temperatures
rules.exceptions: Milder continuous flow conditions may outperform heated conditions for some substrates

rules.condition: WHEN preventing residence time distribution phenomena in slug flow
rules.recommendation: Use automated continuous flow reactor systems with slug flow technology and carrier solvent delivery
rules.reasoning: Slug flow prevents axial dispersion and ensures uniform residence time distribution, improving reaction reproducibility
rules.exceptions: Not suitable for reactions requiring homogeneous mixing

rules.condition: WHEN operating copper coil reactors at elevated temperatures
rules.recommendation: Apply back pressure of 6.9 bar to prevent solvent boiling and maintain liquid phase
rules.reasoning: Back pressure keeps solvents in liquid phase at elevated temperatures, ensuring consistent reaction conditions
rules.exceptions: Not needed for low-boiling point reactions or ambient temperature conditions

rules.condition: WHEN using immobilized catalysts in packed bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Ensure proper catalyst loading and test for leaching by analyzing reaction mixtures
rules.reasoning: Catalyst leaching reduces activity over time and can contaminate products, requiring monitoring for system stability
rules.quantitative: Monitor for >93 hours of operation without significant activity loss
rules.exceptions: Single-use reactions may not require extensive leaching studies

rules.condition: WHEN immobilizing metal catalysts on polymer supports
rules.recommendation: Use polystyrene cross-linked with divinylbenzene for catalyst immobilization
rules.reasoning: This polymer support is hydrophilic, insoluble in organic solvents, and has nitrogen content suitable for metal coordination
rules.exceptions: Other supports may be needed for specific metal catalysts or reaction conditions

rules.condition: WHEN performing multistep synthesis in copper coil reactors
rules.recommendation: Use residence time of 6.2 minutes at 110°C for SN2 substitution reactions
rules.reasoning: Optimized residence time balances reaction completion with throughput for nucleophilic substitution reactions
rules.quantitative: 6.2 min residence time at 110°C, 6.9 bar pressure
rules.exceptions: Different residence times may be needed for other reaction types

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up packed bed reactor processes
rules.recommendation: Maintain flow rate of 1 mL/min and residence time of 1 min for quantitative formation
rules.reasoning: These conditions provide optimal balance between throughput and conversion for carbene insertion reactions
rules.quantitative: 1 mL/min flow rate, 1 min residence time
rules.exceptions: May need adjustment for different reaction kinetics

rules.condition: WHEN using heterogeneous catalysts in packed bed reactors
rules.recommendation: Monitor catalyst stability over extended operation periods and check for deactivation
rules.reasoning: Catalyst deactivation can occur due to sintering, poisoning, or mechanical degradation over time
rules.quantitative: Test stability over 8.5 days of operation
rules.exceptions: Short-term reactions may not require extended stability testing

rules.condition: WHEN designing packed bed reactors for continuous synthesis

rules.quantitative: 48 W LED, 4 h residence time, 80% yield in flow vs 50% in batch

rules.condition: WHEN using dual photoredox nickel catalysis for C(sp²)-C(sp³) coupling in flow
rules.recommendation: Maintain residence time of 50 min using 420 nm LEDs as light source and operate at elevated temperatures (40-60°C) for optimal coupling
rules.reasoning: Moderate residence time allows complete catalytic turnover, 420 nm matches photoredox catalyst absorption, elevated temperature improves nickel catalyst activity
rules.quantitative: 50 min residence time, 420 nm LEDs, 40-60°C, 20-40 min for coupling reactions

rules.condition: WHEN performing gas-liquid photochemical reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use MFC-controlled addition and install back pressure regulator to increase gaseous reagent amount in liquid phase while enabling better residence time control
rules.reasoning: Controlled gas addition ensures consistent stoichiometry while back pressure increases gas solubility in the liquid phase

rules.condition: WHEN designing photochemical flow reactors
rules.recommendation: Consider sensitizers can be immobilized on reactor channel wall or on dedicated supports for continuous operation
rules.reasoning: Immobilized sensitizers prevent product contamination and allow for easier separation and catalyst recovery

rules.condition: WHEN scaling up artemisinin synthesis from laboratory to preparative scale
rules.recommendation: Isolate 1.56 g (76%) of API within 4 hours using photochemical flow techniques, demonstrating large scale viability
rules.reasoning: Flow photochemistry enables rapid scale-up with consistent yields and shorter reaction times compared to batch processes

rules.condition: WHEN utilizing gaseous reagents in photoredox processes
rules.recommendation: Generate superoxide (O₂⁻•) as electron acceptor and utilize reactive species to oxidize thiols and disulfides efficiently
rules.reasoning: Oxygen acts as terminal oxidant in photoredox cycles and can generate reactive oxygen species for selective oxidations
rules.quantitative: Reaction time reduced from 2-16 h (batch) to 20 min (flow)

rules.condition: WHEN performing photocatalytic aerobic oxidation of thiols to disulfides using homogeneous photoredox catalysts
rules.recommendation: Use a solution of substrate in THF pumped through a tube-in-tube gas loading unit at 8 bar back pressure within 3.33 h residence time
rules.reasoning: Higher activity compared to other PRCs and enables synthesis of carbazoles in good-to-excellent yields
rules.quantitative: 8 bar back pressure, 3.33 h residence time, ~1 g d⁻¹ productivity

rules.condition: WHEN performing trifluoromethylation using CF₃I as reagent
rules.recommendation: Control CF₃I by MFC and mix with liquid phase before irradiation with blue light at room temperature
rules.reasoning: CF₃I is particularly interesting due to high atom economy and relatively low cost

rules.condition: WHEN using TiO₂ nanoparticles for disulfide formation in packed bed reactor
rules.recommendation: Use reaction times of 3-5 min for triphasic transformation, batch reactions need up to 8 h
rules.reasoning: TiO₂ has relatively high energy band gap (3.2 eV for anatase) requiring UV irradiation, but surface interactions enable visible light excitation
rules.quantitative: 3-5 min reaction times vs 8 h for batch
rules.exceptions: When amines such as TMEDA are present

rules.condition: WHEN performing photoredox catalysis with TiO₂ nanoparticles and glass beads
rules.recommendation: Use 28 h experiment with yield not decreasing during high potential of semiconducting material for heterogeneous photocatalysis
rules.reasoning: Shows high potential of semiconducting material for heterogeneous photocatalysis catalysis

rules.condition: WHEN performing continuous deoxyafluorination of allylic alcohols using SF₆
rules.recommendation: Use 6.8 bar pressure with blue light and 15 min residence time to achieve 45-79% yields
rules.reasoning: SF₆ enables efficient deoxyafluorination reactions in continuous flow
rules.quantitative: 6.8 bar pressure, 15 min residence time, 45-79% yields

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing photochemical reactions in batch vs continuous flow
rules.recommendation: Achieve continuous version by mixing gaseous reagent with liquid producing slug flow regime prior to irradiation with small residence time unit at 6.8 bar
rules.reasoning: Enables better mixing and increases solubility of gaseous fluorine source
rules.quantitative: 6.8 bar pressure for slug flow regime

rules.condition: WHEN performing carboxylation of amines with CO₂ using continuous photoredox catalysis
rules.recommendation: Use potassium trifluoroacetate (CF₃CO₂K) with two-phase gas-liquid photoflow setup at 3.4 bar and residence time of 10 min
rules.reasoning: CO₂ (E₀ = -2.21 V vs SCE) is too high for common PRCs which absorb visible light, combination with trifluoroacetate overcomes this limitation
rules.quantitative: 3.4 bar system pressure, 10 min residence time

rules.condition: WHEN developing sustainable photoredox processes using expensive transition metal based PRCs
rules.recommendation: Implement nanofiltration recycling strategy using liquid-liquid separator equipped with nanofiltration membrane
rules.reasoning: Expensive PRCs are difficult to recycle and necessitate additional purification steps, recovery strategies are important for sustainability
rules.exceptions: When catalyst material decomposes in presence of acids

rules.condition: WHEN using polyisobutylene-tagged fac-Ir(ppy)₃ complex for continuous recycling
rules.recommendation: Use thermomorphic solvent system with heptane at 90°C, allow biphasic mixture separation upon cooling
rules.reasoning: Enables continuous recycling and reuse with thermomorphic solvent system, constant E/Z ratios maintained over entire experiment
rules.quantitative: 90°C reaction temperature, loss of catalyst in heptane phase only observed at beginning

rules.condition: WHEN using luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) for flow photochemistry
rules.recommendation: Use polymeric materials or glass waveguides with luminophore dispersion to concentrate light by total internal reflection toward device edge
rules.reasoning: Light penetrates surface where absorbed by luminophore, re-emitted light is guided and concentrated by total internal reflection

rules.condition: WHEN performing photochemical reactions using PDMS chip reactor with Lumiogen Red 305
rules.recommendation: Use fluorescent dye that absorbs visible light from ~400-600 nm and re-emits at ~600-700 nm with methylene blue photosensitization
rules.reasoning: Perfectly overlaps with absorption spectrum of methylene blue, common photosensitizer for singlet oxygen cycloaddition

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic oxidation of amides using N-acyl iminium ions
rules.recommendation: Use several undivided electrochemical flow devices as operationally straightforward reaction forming new carbon-carbon bond with H₂ as only byproduct
rules.reasoning: Among most studied electrochemical reactions in organic synthesis, operationally straightforward with clean byproduct formation

rules.condition: WHEN performing dehydrogenative methoxylation of N-formylpyrrolidine in flow
rules.recommendation: Use carbon/polymer anode (C-Anode) and stainless steel cathode (SS-Cathode) with different reactor geometries including 5mm×5cm microchannel or round cell design with star-shaped or spiral channel pattern
rules.reasoning: Different geometries enable optimization of flow rate and cell currents for desired productivity
rules.quantitative: Flow rate 16 mL min⁻¹, cell currents 12 A, 353 was produced in high yields (84%) within residence time of 19 s, productivity of 207 g h⁻¹
rules.exceptions: Pitting issue is detrimental to long-term experiments at cell currents above 10 A

rules.condition: WHEN performing Shono oxidation of N-protected cyclic amines
rules.recommendation: Use stainless steel or platinum-coated anode with excellent selectivity (95%) at current density of 49 mA cm⁻²
rules.reasoning: Enables access to natural product building blocks and related congeners
rules.quantitative: 95% selectivity, 49 mA cm⁻² current density, 89-99% yields, 14 h experiment duration

rules.condition: WHEN performing Kolbe electrolysis for trifluoromethylation reactions
rules.recommendation: Use electrochemical reactor consisting of cathode and anode made of Pt foil with FEP flow channel, use TFA as CF₃ source due to high economic potential
rules.reasoning: Generates CF₃ radical from trifluoroacetic acid for trifluoromethylation reactions with high economic potential as CF₃ source
rules.quantitative: 50 mA cell current, 66 s residence time, 67% yield for trifluoromethyl acetamidation
rules.exceptions: When trifluoroacetic acid is used instead of TFA

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic coupling of creosol with 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene
rules.recommendation: Use 2.8 mA cm⁻² current density with Ni-cathode and BDD-anode to minimize homocoupling while using methanol to reduce homocoupling
rules.reasoning: Reaction suffers from large amount of homocoupling, methanol helps reduce this issue

rules.condition: WHEN performing in situ electrogeneration of ortho-benzoquinone
rules.recommendation: Use catechol 359 in reaction with thiophenols by Michael-type addition to result in unsymmetrical aromatic disulfides
rules.reasoning: Initial experiments showed this reaction using batch electrolysis cell gave low yields (13%) due to similar oxidation potentials of both substrates
rules.quantitative: 13% yield in batch, improved yields in flow setup
rules.exceptions: When oxidation potentials of both substrates are similar

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic oxidation using Breslow intermediate for esterification and amidation
rules.recommendation: Use cell current of 20 mA at 10 mA for significantly lower conversion (56%) compared to higher currents, apply conditions to primary and secondary alcohols including benzylic, allylic, and aliphatic species
rules.reasoning: Most benzylic and allylic alcohols result in good-to-excellent isolated yields, aliphatic alcohols somewhat less reactive
rules.quantitative: 20 mA vs 10 mA cell current, 56% conversion at lower current, residence time less than 1 min
rules.exceptions: Overoxidation observed at longer residence times

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical mimicking of metabolic oxidation processes
rules.recommendation: Achieve productivity of 1.5-4.3 g h⁻¹ showing potential of electrochemical flow process for drug metabolism studies
rules.reasoning: Can be used to simulate metabolism of drugs, drug oxidation by cytochrome P450 crucial for development processes
rules.quantitative: 1.5-4.3 g h⁻¹ productivity, 10-100 mg respective metabolites for full characterization

rules.condition: WHEN using TEMPO-mediated electrochemical oxidation of alcohols
rules.recommendation: Use 30 mol% TEMPO and mixture of tert-butanol and carbonate-bicarbonate buffer for oxidation of cyclohexanol with good balance between conversion (86%) and selectivity (99%)
rules.reasoning: Stoichiometric oxidants such as NaOCl in TEMPO-mediated oxidation can be substituted by anodic oxidation on preparative scales

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical oxidation of tolbutamide in divided cell
rules.recommendation: Use carbon anode and platinum cathode separated by spectra/por membrane with most redox-active site governing oxidation
rules.reasoning: Flow electrosynthesis can complement biosynthetic methods due to ability to oxidize at most redox-active site

rules.condition: WHEN performing cathodic reduction in flow electrochemistry for reduction of functional groups
rules.recommendation: Use metal hydrides (LAH, NaBH4, and DIBAL), transition metal catalyzed hydrogenation/hydrogenolysis reactions, or electron-reducing agents such as sodium
rules.reasoning: Electrochemistry offers a sustainable alternative via cathodic reduction which overcomes the economic and environmental implications associated with traditional procedures
rules.quantitative: 93% isolated yield achievable on multigram scale

rules.condition: WHEN performing anodic dehalogenation in a divided cell reactor with Me4NSO3Me as electrolyte
rules.recommendation: Use LB-cathode and C-anode with flow rate and applied electricity optimization to achieve 70% yield in scalable continuous procedure
rules.reasoning: Allows for electrolyte and solvent recycling to improve sustainability of the dehalogenation process

rules.condition: WHEN performing deprotection of tNoc protected phenols in electrochemical flow reactor using TBAI/DMF/H2O
rules.recommendation: Use platinum cathode and anode under optimized conditions for 43-61% yield of respective unprotected phenol derivatives within 92 s reaction time
rules.reasoning: Continuous electrochemical deprotection varied significantly in batch versus flow, with batch giving slightly lower yield but requiring 6.5 h for full conversion
rules.quantitative: 43-61% yield, 92 s reaction time, 6.5 h required for batch conversion
rules.exceptions: No deprotection occurred with tNoc protected thiols, respective disulfides were obtained instead

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrogeneration of 2-pyrrolidone anion for trichloromethylation of benzaldehyde
rules.recommendation: Feed solution containing 2-pyrrolidone into reactor near cathodic part where reductive generation occurs, preventing anion oxidation at anode by stream separation
rules.reasoning: The separation of the two streams prevents the reactive anion from being reoxidized at the anode
rules.quantitative: Bu4NClO4 (20 mol%) DMF, 25 mA cm⁻², 1.5 A, 378 52% yield

rules.condition: WHEN performing electrochemical transformations that require laminar flow regime
rules.recommendation: Use laminar flow to mimic a divided cell reactor, allowing reactive species to be fed into specific reactor regions
rules.reasoning: With laminar flow regime, a solution containing 2-pyrrolidone can be fed into the reactor near the cathodic part where reductive generation occurs
rules.quantitative: Less than 15 s reaction time achievable

rules.condition: WHEN reaction gives significantly lower yield (20%) without laminar flow
rules.recommendation: Create laminar flow conditions, as no reaction occurred if benzaldehyde and chloroform are present during the initial electrochemical step
rules.reasoning: The reaction is highly selective in flow at room temperature, whereas the same experiment in a divided cell batch reactor required cooling to −70°C for high selectivity
rules.quantitative: 20% lower yield without laminar flow, room temperature vs -70°C for batch

rules.condition: WHEN using automated optimization for flow reactions
rules.recommendation: Control delivery system with syringe pumps or HPLC pumps, varying flow rate or stoichiometric feeds to control time, stoichiometry, and concentration
rules.reasoning: LabVIEW program controls the delivery system and by varying the flow rates of the respective reagent or solvent feeds, it controls the time, stoichiometry, and concentration of the reaction
rules.quantitative: Temperature and pressure control available (Table referenced)

rules.condition: WHEN setting up automated optimization systems for flow chemistry
rules.recommendation: Use feedback loops with online analysis to enable real-time reaction optimization methods due to establishment of online and inline flow analysis
rules.reasoning: High-throughput experimentation (HTE) has led to rapid, cost-effective identification of optimal conditions for new transformations, facilitating swift screening of discrete variables
rules.exceptions: Less effective at scanning continuous variables like temperature, reaction time, and concentration

rules.condition: WHEN optimizing discrete variables in flow setups
rules.recommendation: Use droplet-based reaction design using liquid handler for rapid self-optimization, particularly promising for discrete and continuous variables optimization
rules.reasoning: Since flow setups struggle to efficiently scan discrete variables, the majority of examples only optimize continuous parameters due to setup limitations where stock solutions are usually delivered by single syringe or static lines
rules.exceptions: Stock solutions are usually delivered by a single syringe or static lines for each input stream, limiting discrete variable optimization

rules.condition: WHEN performing Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling optimization with precatalyst and ligand screening
rules.recommendation: Use droplet screening system enabling discrete variable screening, with optimal conditions showing trialkyl/triarylphosphine ligands work best at high temperatures with short residence times, whereas dialkylbiarylphosphine ligands were best at lower temperatures and longer residence times
rules.reasoning: Between 0.2 and 0.8 equiv of ligand were ideal, and the yield decreased significantly with 2.0 equiv, enabling optimization of classes of ligands
rules.quantitative: 0.2-0.8 equiv ligand optimal, yield decreased significantly with 2.0 equiv

rules.condition: WHEN forming droplets of 4-methoxybenzyl chloride in different solvents using nitrogen-regulated Parr bomb at 6.9 bar
rules.recommendation: Control temperature carefully (50-120°C for 1-10 min) and use refractive index sensor for droplet detection, with optimization yielding 62% at 7.5 min, 78°C, and 4-methoxybenzyl chloride concentration of 100 mM in DMSO
rules.reasoning: Continuous stream of acetic acid and acetonitrile was used as a quench, and a third refractive index sensor was used to time the sampling for analysis by FI-HPLC. Increasing temperature too high led to overalkylation
rules.quantitative: 50-120°C, 1-10 min, 62% yield at 7.5 min, 78°C, 100 mM concentration, 6.9 bar pressure
rules.exceptions: Temperature too high leads to overalkylation

rules.condition: When performing photochemical reactions in continuous flow
rules.recommendation: Use photosensitizer-immobilized microfluidic devices to enable efficient photosynthesis with improved selectivity
rules.reasoning: Immobilized photosensitizers provide better control over photochemical processes and can be reused, improving efficiency and reducing waste

rules.condition: When designing continuous flow reactors for organic synthesis
rules.recommendation: Consider practical flow reactor designs that balance efficiency with manufacturability
rules.reasoning: Practical reactor designs are essential for successful implementation and scale-up of flow processes
rules.quantitative: Reference 156 discusses practical flow reactor design considerations

rules.condition: When using singlet oxygen in continuous flow oxidation reactions
rules.recommendation: Employ continuous oxidation methods using singlet oxygen for selective organic transformations
rules.reasoning: Singlet oxygen provides selective oxidation pathways that can be well-controlled in flow systems
rules.quantitative: Reference 157 covers continuous oxidation using singlet oxygen

rules.condition: When conducting flow photochemistry reactions
rules.recommendation: Use appropriate light sources and reactor designs to ensure uniform light distribution and efficient photochemical conversion
rules.reasoning: Proper light distribution is critical for achieving consistent photochemical reactions in flow systems

rules.condition: When using palladium catalysts in continuous flow cross-coupling reactions
rules.recommendation: Optimize catalyst loading, temperature, and residence time for efficient cross-coupling reactions while minimizing metal leaching
rules.reasoning: Palladium catalysts require careful optimization to balance activity, selectivity, and catalyst stability in flow systems
rules.quantitative: Reference 159 discusses palladium catalysts for continuous-flow cross-coupling reactions

rules.condition: When designing microfluidic systems for electrochemical reactions
rules.recommendation: Use electrochemical microflow systems with appropriate electrode configurations and flow patterns
rules.reasoning: Electrochemical reactions in microfluidic systems require specific electrode arrangements and flow control for optimal performance

rules.condition: When using tube-in-tube gas permeable membrane reactors
rules.recommendation: Select appropriate membrane materials and configurations for gas-liquid reactions in continuous flow
rules.reasoning: Gas permeable membranes enable controlled gas introduction and removal in flow systems, improving reaction control
rules.quantitative: Reference 162 discusses tube-in-tube gas permeable membrane reactors

rules.condition: When conducting high-temperature and high-pressure reactions in flow
rules.recommendation: Use appropriate reactor materials and safety measures for elevated temperature and pressure conditions
rules.reasoning: High-temperature and high-pressure conditions require special considerations for reactor design and safety
rules.quantitative: Reference 164 discusses high-temperature and high-pressure chemical reactions

rules.condition: When designing continuous flow systems for heterogeneous catalysis and metathesis reactions
rules.recommendation: Optimize reactor design for solid catalyst contact and minimize catalyst deactivation
rules.reasoning: Heterogeneous catalysis requires proper catalyst-reactant contact and strategies to maintain catalyst activity
rules.quantitative: Reference 165 discusses tube-in-tube reactors for heterogeneous catalysis and metathesis

rules.condition: When scaling up microchemical synthesis processes
rules.recommendation: Use multistep microchemical synthesis approaches that maintain the advantages of microscale reactions
rules.reasoning: Multistep microchemical synthesis allows for precise control and integration of multiple reaction steps
